skills/research/research-paper-writing/t.description: This folder contains a complete ICML 2026 LaTeX submission template set used by the research-paper-writing skill: style files (.sty), a BibTeX style (.bst), a sample .tex and .bib, plus compiled PDF outputs. It exists to provide a ready-to-use, standards-compliant example and assets for generating ICML-format papers; modifications will affect document compilation, citation formatting, and any tooling that consumes these template filenames or macros.
key_behaviors: Provide LaTeX macros and package configuration that define the ICML document class/format (layout, metadata, preprint/accepted toggles).
Define algorithm floating and algorithmic environments used by example papers and user documents.
Supply a BibTeX style and sample bibliography that control citation formatting and bibliography generation via natbib/BibTeX.
Serve as source + artifacts: example TeX/BIB sources are compiled into canonical PDFs which are used as references or shipped with the skill.
Act as a consumable template by other code or user workflows that programmatically copy/include these files to produce ICML-compliant submissions.

insights.title: PDF outputs are generated artifacts that can become stale
insights.problem: example_paper.pdf and icml2026_conference.pdf are compiled outputs; editing .sty/.tex/.bst without regenerating PDFs creates mismatch between source and shipped binaries.
insights.root_cause: PDF files are stored alongside source without an enforced build step that keeps them synchronized.
insights.solution: When changing any source (.sty, .tex, .bst, .bib), treat PDFs as generated; plan to run the appropriate LaTeX+BibTeX compilation sequence and replace regenerated PDFs so consumers see consistent artifacts.

insights.title: Macros and option names are stable public surface
insights.problem: Renaming or removing macros (e.g., \icmltitle, \icmlauthor, 'accepted'/'preprint' options) will break any documents or external code that include this template.
insights.root_cause: The style file defines the canonical macros other files rely on; other files and possibly external scripts expect those names.
insights.solution: Preserve macro names and option semantics or provide backward-compatible aliases; document any intentional breaking changes and update dependent example files.

insights.title: Tight coupling between .bib and .bst for natbib/BibTeX output
insights.problem: Changing the .bst may change expected .bib fields or ordering; incorrect changes produce compile errors or incorrect citation output.
insights.root_cause: BibTeX styles implement formatting rules and often assume specific entry fields and natbib usage.
insights.solution: When editing icml2026.bst or example_bib entries, validate by running bibtex (or biber if used) and compiling the sample .tex to verify citations render correctly under natbib conventions.

insights.title: LaTeX toolchain required to validate changes
insights.problem: Edits cannot be fully validated without a TeX distribution (pdflatex/xelatex, bibtex/biber) and required LaTeX packages (e.g., algorithms package).
insights.root_cause: LaTeX files depend on an external toolchain and third-party packages that are not part of the repo.
insights.solution: Run a local or CI LaTeX build using an appropriate TeX Live (or equivalent) environment to confirm compilation; ensure required packages are available in the build environment.

insights.title: Files are referenced by fixed filenames in examples
insights.problem: Renaming or moving files (icml2026.sty, icml2026.bst, algorithmic.sty) will break include lines in example_paper.tex and any automation that copies/includes these assets.
insights.root_cause: Example documents and consuming tooling reference exact paths/filenames to include the styles and bibliography.
insights.solution: Maintain filenames or update all referents (example .tex and any external code) to match new names; search for string occurrences if renaming.

key_files.why: Central LaTeX style; defines the document format, macros (icml2026, icmltitle, icmlauthor) and submission toggles that other files and documents rely on.
BibTeX style controlling citation and bibliography formatting for ICML output; tightly coupled with example_paper.bib and natbib usage.
Canonical usage example that includes the style files, demonstrates macros and algorithms, and is the primary source to validate template behavior.
Provides algorithmic environment macros referenced by example_paper.tex; changes affect rendering of pseudocode and may require corresponding float definitions.
Compiled reference artifact used by humans or tooling as the expected output; must be kept in sync with source files to avoid confusion.

construct_relationships: example_paper.tex includes icml2026.sty to obtain core macros (\icmltitle, \icmlauthor) and layout options (accepted/preprint).
example_paper.tex references algorithmic.sty and algorithm.sty to provide floating algorithm environments and pseudocode formatting used within the sample paper.
example_paper.tex uses example_paper.bib together with icml2026.bst (BibTeX style) and natbib to produce the bibliography and citation formatting.
icml2026.sty and icml2026.bst form the public formatting contract: .sty controls visual/layout macros while .bst controls bibliography rendering; example files demonstrate their combined behavior.
Generated PDFs (example_paper.pdf, icml2026_conference.pdf) are downstream artifacts produced by compiling example_paper.tex with the provided .sty/.bst/.bib and toolchain.
notable_relationships: The research-paper-writing skill (parent folder) likely programmatically copies or references these templates by exact path/name; edits may affect skills that scaffold new papers or compile templates.

External workflows that produce ICML submissions (scripts, CI or user build processes) will depend on the presence of a TeX toolchain; repository-level CI may not exercise LaTeX builds unless explicitly configured.
Other template folders (e.g., for different conferences) may share conventions (macro names, filenames). Preserve cross-template compatibility expectations if other skills assume similar macro names.
recommendations: Treat .sty and .bst files as public API: preserve macro names, option flags, and filename stability unless also updating all consumers and example files.

When changing any source (.sty, .bst, .tex, .bib, algorithmic files), run a full LaTeX compilation (pdflatex/xelatex + bibtex/biber as appropriate, repeated runs for cross-references) to validate output and regenerate example PDFs.
Verify natbib/BibTeX flows after any .bst change by compiling example_paper.tex against example_paper.bib; confirm citation order, formatting, and absence of BibTeX errors.
Do not assume the repository contains the full TeX toolchain; ensure the execution environment used for validation has required LaTeX packages and binaries available (TeX Live or equivalent).

If renaming or relocating files, update all include statements in example_paper.tex and any code in the research skill that references these filenames; perform a repository-wide search for hard-coded paths.
Preserve file encodings and line endings (UTF-8 preferred) to avoid introducing LaTeX compilation issues; validate after edits on the same platform(s) used by consumers.
If PDFs are kept in the repo, update them to match source changes to avoid stale documentation artifacts and ensure downstream consumers see consistent examples.

skills/research/research-paper-writing/t.description: This folder contains an ACL conference paper LaTeX template and supporting bibliography and guidance files used by the research-paper-writing skill. It provides the style (.sty), a natbib-compatible .bst, a LuaLaTeX/XeLaTeX example document, sample and anthology BibTeX references, and human-facing formatting guidance so the skill can produce compile-able ACL-formatted manuscripts and bibliographies.
key_behaviors: Compose a LaTeX document that loads the ACL style (acl.sty) and the ACL natbib bibliography style (acl_natbib.bst).
Provide an engine-targeted example (LuaLaTeX/XeLaTeX) with language/Unicode support and a final-copy compile option.
Supply canonical BibTeX inputs: an anthology guide, and a custom.bib for non-anthology sources, to be used during BibTeX bibliography generation.
Explain formatting rules and usage via README/formatting docs for downstream consumers or automated generation tools.

insights.title: LaTeX engine and bibliography tooling must match template expectations
insights.problem: The example uses LuaLaTeX/XeLaTeX and the supplied .bst indicates BibTeX-based bibliography processing; mismatch of engine or bibliography tool will break compilation or bibliographies.
insights.root_cause: Template was authored with specific engine and BibTeX workflow (natbib/.bst); substitute engines or biber/backends are not equivalent without adjustments.
insights.solution: Ensure edits preserve engine-targeted constructs and natbib/BibTeX usage; any change that affects engine or bibliography backend requires validating a full compile with the intended LaTeX toolchain.

insights.title: Final-copy option toggles presentation and must remain consistent
insights.problem: acl.sty exposes a \ifacl@finalcopy toggle and a \DeclareOption{final} that affect layout and possibly author anonymity; altering or removing it can change compilation results and policy compliance.
insights.root_cause: Presentation and submission behaviors are encoded in style options rather than document-level flags.
insights.solution: Treat the final option and its flag as a public contract; changes must preserve the flag behavior or provide explicit guidance and tests showing equivalent results.

insights.title: Anthology and custom BibTeX sources must remain consistent with .bst expectations
insights.problem: Custom entries or anthology-guidance text that don't conform to fields expected by acl_natbib.bst can render incorrect citations or break bibliography formatting.
insights.root_cause: The .bst styles expect certain ENTRY fields and author formatting; mismatched entries lead to formatting or missing-field failures.
insights.solution: Keep sample BibTeX entries aligned with the .bst's expected fields and document the required fields in formatting.md/README; validate by running BibTeX on the example document.

insights.title: Guidance and examples must match template behavior
insights.problem: Discrepancies between formatting.md/README and actual .sty/.tex behavior lead to user confusion and incorrect generated documents.
insights.root_cause: Documentation can drift from implementation during edits if not updated together.
insights.solution: When modifying templates or style behavior, ensure READMEs and formatting instructions reflect the change and test the documented workflow end-to-end.

key_files.why: Primary ACL style file that defines layout, the 'final' option, and selects the ACL bibliography behavior — central to formatting and submission semantics.
Engine-targeted example document (LuaLaTeX/XeLaTeX) demonstrating how to load the style and use the templates in a compile pipeline; useful for validation.
BibTeX style used by the template to format citations and references; defines expected BibTeX fields and output formatting.
Holds non-anthology example BibTeX entries that are consumed by example builds and expected by the .bst; edits here affect citation outputs.
Human-facing formatting instructions that describe how the template should be used and what the output should look like; used by users and calling code for guidance.

construct_relationships: acl_lualatex.tex includes \usepackage{acl} which loads acl.sty — the example document depends on the style file for layout and options.
acl.sty sets the bibliography style to acl_natbib.bst (\bibliographystyle{acl_natbib}), so the TeX document + BibTeX workflow depends on the .bst file's formatting rules.
custom.bib and anthology.bib.txt are intended input sources for the bibliography; their entry fields must align with the ENTRY and format.authors expectations in acl_natbib.bst.
formatting.md and README.md document how to use acl_lualatex.tex and the .sty options (including the final option) so documentation must remain synchronized with the implementation.
notable_relationships: Upstream toolchain: templates require a LaTeX environment with LuaLaTeX/XeLaTeX and BibTeX available (or compatible pipeline) — CI or Docker images that build papers must have these installed.

Downstream consumers: the research-paper-writing skill and any automation that generates manuscripts will invoke these files; template edits affect generated paper validity and any automated compile/test steps.
Cross-folder dependency: other skills or tools that assemble papers (e.g., citation management utilities) may assume natbib/BibTeX workflow and the presence of custom.bib/anthology references.
Packaging/CI: changes here can break automated validation (compile + BibTeX); CI pipelines that test compilation must be considered when editing template files.
recommendations: Preserve the public-facing style option names and behaviors (e.g., 'final' flag) unless you will also update documentation and validate equivalence.
When modifying formatting or bibliography behavior, run a full compile of acl_lualatex.tex with the intended engine (LuaLaTeX/XeLaTeX) and BibTeX to verify PDF and reference output.
Ensure any edits to .bst or .sty remain compatible with the fields present in custom.bib and anthology examples; validate bibliography formatting for typical entry types (author lists, venue fields).
Keep README.md and formatting.md in sync with any behavioral changes; treat them as part of the public contract for template usage.
Respect file encodings and LaTeX-special characters in .bib and .tex files; avoid accidental introduction of characters that break Unicode or engine-specific compilation.

skills/research/research-paper-writing/t.description:

It provides the style layer (.sty), citation/natbib integration, math macros, a sample bibliography, a shell .tex that wires the pieces together, and a placeholder PDF; these files are used to produce submission-ready PDFs and to guide any automated paper-generation workflows.",

skills/mlops/evaluation/lm-evaluation-ha.description: This folder contains reference documentation for integrating and running the lm-evaluation-harness within the project's MLOps/skills evaluation subsystem. The markdown files cover advanced usage, API-driven evaluation, benchmarking practices, custom task creation, and distributed evaluation — serving as the canonical how-to and operational constraints for evaluators and automation that invoke the harness.
key_behaviors: Documenting how to configure and invoke lm-evaluation-harness tasks (CLI/flags, environment variables, credentials).
Describing recommended benchmarking workflows, metrics collection, and reproducibility practices for model evaluations.
Explaining how to implement and wire custom tasks into the harness (schema, task interface, example code snippets).
Outlining API-based evaluation flows and authentication/rate-limit considerations when calling remote model endpoints.
Detailing distributed evaluation patterns: orchestration, parallelism, resource constraints, and fault tolerance considerations.

insights.title: Docs must track harness and model-adapter API surface
insights.problem: Procedural examples, flags, and code snippets can become stale relative to evolving harness or model-adapter APIs.
insights.root_cause: Documentation exists separately from implementation and references CLI flags, function signatures, or config keys that change over time.
insights.solution: When editing these docs, ensure examples, flag names, and code snippets match the current harness and adapter interfaces; validate by running the referenced commands or sample scripts in a matching dev environment.

insights.title: Benchmark reproducibility depends on environment and versions
insights.problem: Reported benchmark procedures omit or underspecify environment, dependency versions, seeds, and tokenization/model metadata necessary to reproduce results.
insights.root_cause: Benchmarks rely on external model providers, tokenization differences, and optional extras that affect outputs.
insights.solution: Preserve or clearly state exact environment variables, package versions, model identifiers, tokenizer settings, and random seeds used by benchmarks; note required extras and system-level dependencies.

insights.title: Custom task contracts must be explicit
insights.problem: Implementers of custom tasks may mis-wire task metadata, dataset splits, or expected evaluation metrics because of under-specified interfaces.
insights.root_cause: Custom-tasks guidance provides examples but may not enumerate required hooks, return schemas, or registration steps against the harness.
insights.solution: When updating custom-tasks doc content, ensure it maps fields to the project’s task interface (input/output shapes, metadata keys) and indicates where code lives that the harness will import or call.

insights.title: Distributed eval introduces orchestration and state concerns
insights.problem: Distributed evaluation guidance can underplay failure modes (partial results, retries, inconsistent seeds) and shared-state handling (logging, metrics aggregation).
insights.root_cause: Distributed execution requires coordination layers and explicit idempotency/aggregation strategies which are orthogonal to single-node instructions.
insights.solution: Ensure distributed-eval docs mention how tasks should be partitioned, how results are aggregated and stored, and what external systems (object store, message queue, cluster scheduler) are assumed available.

insights.title: API-driven evaluation depends on credentials and rate limits
insights.problem: API evaluation examples may assume available keys, ignore rate limits, or not document error handling and cost implications.
insights.root_cause: Remote provider behavior and rate limits vary by provider and by the project's credential-pool usage.
insights.solution: Keep API-evaluation guidance explicit about which environment variables or credential management hooks to use, recommended throttling/backoff, and how to avoid accidental billing or hitting provider quotas.

insights.title: Metrics aggregation and output formats matter for downstream tooling
insights.problem: Benchmark guide may not specify output formats or metadata required by downstream reporting or CI checks.
insights.root_cause: Multiple consumers (CI, dashboards, experiment tracking) expect consistent artifact formats but docs are silent or ambiguous.
insights.solution: Document the expected result artifact formats, filenames, and metadata fields required by any downstream pipeline or CI job that will consume benchmark outputs.

key_files.why: Describes complex or non-default invocation patterns and serves as the primary reference for power users and automation that exercise advanced harness features.
Specifies how to run evaluations against remote model endpoints and details credential, throttling, and error-handling expectations tied to API-driven flows.
Captures recommended benchmarking workflows, expected outputs, and reproducibility notes used by CI and reporting pipelines.
Explains the contract and registration pattern for adding custom evaluation tasks — directly informs code that implements new tasks.
Contains guidance for parallelizing and orchestrating evaluations at scale; touches on resource management and aggregation which affect runtime components.

construct_relationships: Custom task docs map to the harness task interface and dataset schema; readers will implement code that registers tasks with the evaluation harness.
API evaluation documentation references credential and client configuration used by model-adapter components (credential pool / provider adapters) for remote calls.
Benchmark guide describes metrics and artifact formats which downstream CI, experiment tracking, or reporting tooling will parse and ingest.
Distributed evaluation guidance assumes an orchestration/parallelism layer (cluster scheduler, queue, or multiprocessing) and shared storage for aggregated results.
Advanced usage ties together prompt/task configuration, prompt-caching behavior, and batching/tokenization settings that influence runtime model invocation.
notable_relationships: These docs are upstream of code in the evaluation harness and model-adapter layers: changes to adapters or CLI flags elsewhere must be reflected here to keep examples runnable.

API evaluation guidance relies on the project's credential management (credential pool / environment variables) and thus has cross-cutting dependency on global secrets/configuration patterns documented elsewhere (e.g., .env, Dockerfile, CI).
Benchmark outputs and formats are consumed by CI and any reporting dashboards; modifying expected artifact names or schemas will affect test and ingest pipelines.
Distributed evaluation guidance interacts with system-level concerns (Docker, cluster configuration, shared HERMES_HOME or object stores) and with tooling under the project packaging/deploy surfaces.
recommendations: When editing, verify every CLI command and code snippet against the current harness and adapter code paths; run examples in a matching dev environment.
Preserve or explicitly state version, dependency, and environment requirements (package versions, Python >= 3.11, required extras) referenced in examples.
Keep credential names and environment variable references consistent with the project's credential/credential-pool conventions; do not assume secret availability in CI.
Maintain and document output artifact names, formats, and metadata keys expected by CI or downstream consumers; update CI/test fixtures if those change.
Ensure distributed-eval examples document required external services (object storage, schedulers) and describe failure/retry semantics so implementers can reproduce behavior.
Avoid editing these docs without validating sample runs (or indicating expected divergence) because stale documentation can lead to misuse, unexpected costs, or failed CI jobs.

skills/research/research-paper-writing/t.description: This folder contains a unified AAAI 2026 LaTeX paper template set (main template, style file, supplementary template, bibliography, and README). It is used to generate submission-ready and camera-ready documents that must conform to AAAI 2026 formatting rules and compilation expectations, so any edits here can change compile behavior, anonymization, and bibliographic tooling.
key_behaviors: Produce a compilable LaTeX paper: the main .tex file composes document structure, loads the aaai2026 style, and references the provided .bib.
Toggle between anonymized submission and camera-ready output: templates/supplementary coordinate anonymization and author metadata with the style file.
Supply formatting rules and macros via the aaai2026.sty style package which enforces appearance, spacing, and class-level options used by templates.
Provide and reference bibliography entries via aaai2026.bib; bibliography processing (bibtex/biber) and citation macros must match the template's expectations.
Document usage and compilation instructions in README.md for authors and automation tools that assemble or compile papers.

insights.title: Template depends on style package for visual rules
insights.problem: The .tex templates assume macros and layout provided by aaai2026.sty; changing template content without checking the style may produce invalid formatting or compilation errors.
insights.root_cause: Formatting rules, macros and class-like behavior are implemented in aaai2026.sty and the template references them directly.
insights.solution: Any code or automated edits must preserve calls to the style file and validate the combined .tex + .sty compile; treat the style file as the authoritative source of formatting constraints.

insights.title: Submission vs camera-ready behavior encoded across files
insights.problem: Anonymization (author metadata, acknowledgments) and supplementary material handling live in both the main and supp templates and may rely on conditional macros in the style file.
insights.root_cause: Templates and style file coordinate via macros/flags to produce anonymous or non-anonymous outputs.
insights.solution: When modifying templates or automations that populate metadata, ensure anonymization flags and corresponding placeholders are preserved and tested in both submission (anonymous) and camera-ready modes.

insights.title: Bibliography and package dependencies affect build
insights.problem: The template references a .bib and loads packages; changes may require a specific LaTeX engine (pdflatex/xelatex/lualatex) or bibliography processor (bibtex vs biber).
insights.root_cause: Package selection, bibliography style, and encoding expectations are not explicit only in the .tex but also enforced by the .sty file and .bib format.
insights.solution: Automated edits should preserve bibliography commands and verify the intended compilation pipeline; CI or local tests must run a full LaTeX build (multiple passes and bibliography processing) to validate changes.

insights.title: File names and package identity matter for inclusion
insights.problem: Renaming the style file or template paths will break \usepackage or \input references in templates and external tooling.
insights.root_cause: Templates reference aaai2026.sty and expect specific filenames; external build scripts or tools may hardcode these names.
insights.solution: Do not change filenames or \ProvidesPackage identifiers without updating all references and re-validating build scripts and any automation that programmatically manipulates these files.

insights.title: Non-ASCII content can cause silent failures
insights.problem: Introducing Unicode or special characters may cause compilation or bibliography issues depending on the LaTeX engine and package set.
insights.root_cause: Engine and inputenc/font packages expected by the template may not support arbitrary encodings by default.
insights.solution: Ensure edits preserve or explicitly declare input encoding and test with the intended LaTeX engine; maintain .bib encoding consistency.

key_files.why: Primary main-paper template that composes document structure, loads the style, and demonstrates expected usage and citation commands.
Style package implementing AAAI 2026 formatting rules and macros; authoritative for layout and behavior the templates rely on.
Supplementary material template which must remain consistent with the main template for anonymization and formatting of appendices or supplemental files.
Bibliography data referenced by templates; affects citation rendering and requires compatible bibliography processing.
Usage and compilation instructions for authors and automation; may document required engine, bibliography commands, and anonymization instructions.

construct_relationships: aaai2026-unified-template.tex uses \usepackage{aaai2026} which imports aaai2026.sty; the template expects macros and layout defined by that style.
aaai2026-unified-template.tex and aaai2026-unified-supp.tex both reference aaai2026.bib for bibliography entries and must be compiled with the same bibliography processing strategy.
aaai2026-unified-supp.tex mirrors anonymization and metadata conventions in the main template and therefore relies on the same flags/macros exposed by aaai2026.sty.
README.md describes the intended compile workflow and file interdependencies (style file, bibliography, and engine), informing users and automation that assemble these files.
notable_relationships: Upstream: any skill or automation that programmatically fills author, title, abstract, or inserts content into these templates must respect anonymization flags and filename references to aaai2026.sty and aaai2026.bib.

Downstream: CI, build scripts, or external submission tooling that compiles PDFs need a LaTeX toolchain (pdflatex/xelatex/lualatex) and bibliography processor; changes here can break automated builds.
External constraint: AAAI submission rules and PDF compliance requirements are enforced via the style file; altering formatting can cause submission rejection by AAAI systems or reviewers.
Cross-folder: other research skill templates or generator code may import or copy these files; name or API changes will ripple to those generators.
recommendations: Preserve filenames, \ProvidesPackage identifier in aaai2026.sty, and \usepackage/\input references in templates unless you update all callers and verify builds.
When editing templates or the style file, run a full LaTeX build (multiple passes) including bibliography processing for both main and supplement to ensure no regressions.
Respect anonymization flags and author metadata placeholders; validate both anonymous submission and camera-ready outputs after any modification.
Do not change package selections, encoding declarations, or bibliography commands without confirming the intended LaTeX engine and that the resulting PDF meets AAAI formatting constraints.

If an automation or skill will programmatically edit these files, ensure it updates both the main and supplement templates consistently and re-runs compilation/tests to detect formatting or compile failures.

skills/research/research-paper-writing/t.description: This folder contains LaTeX template artifacts for the COLM 2025 conference used by the research-paper-writing skill. It provides style definitions, header/footer configuration, math macro shorthands, and a local BibTeX bibliography to produce consistent PDF output for papers that use these templates. These files are authoritative for visual and citation formatting and will affect any generated or user-submitted LaTeX documents that reference them.
key_behaviors: Define and enforce document appearance and structure (title, sections, headers/footers) via .sty style files.
Expose math macro shorthands and semantic commands for paper authors to use consistently across documents.
Provide citation compatibility and a local bibliography for BibTeX-based citation resolution.
Interact with LaTeX build toolchain (pdfLaTeX/XeLaTeX/LuaLaTeX and BibTeX/biber) during PDF generation.
Serve as input assets for automated paper-generation or templating workflows in the research skill.

insights.title: Style files declare common LaTeX commands (e.g., \maketitle, \section) that can clash with document classes or packages
insights.problem: Overriding or redefining core commands inside conference style files can cause unexpected behavior when combined with other LaTeX classes or packages.
insights.root_cause: colm2025_conference.sty defines document-level macros and structural commands that may shadow or replace standard class definitions.
insights.solution: When modifying any .sty, preserve the original macro names or provide backward-compatible wrappers; verify load order and that the style does not unintentionally override class-level semantics. Risk: breaking downstream documents that rely on the current macro behavior.

insights.title: Math macro shorthands risk colliding with author-defined or package macros
insights.problem: math_commands.tex introduces short commands (e.g., \newterm, \ceil) that may already exist in user documents or other packages, causing compilation errors or altered output.
insights.root_cause: Common/common-word macro names without package namespace or guards are used for convenience.
insights.solution: Any edits must retain original command names or add clear namespacing while ensuring backwards compatibility; document the macro set and test sample papers for symbol conflicts. Risk: renaming will break existing papers that import these macros.

insights.title: Custom natbib compatibility and local .bib can create build-toolchain assumptions
insights.problem: natbib.sty and the included colm2025_conference.bib assume a BibTeX-style bibliography workflow and specific citation macros; switching to biber/biblatex or changing citation commands may break citation rendering.
insights.root_cause: Provision of a natbib shim and an embedded .bib file ties templates to BibTeX-style compilation flows.
insights.solution: Maintain compatibility with natbib/BibTeX expectations when editing; if changing citation macros or bibliography format, ensure the build pipeline and documentation reflect the new toolchain. Risk: citations may disappear or format incorrectly if build steps differ.

insights.title: Fancyhdr customization affects page layout cross-cuttingly
insights.problem: fancyhdr.sty sets global page header/footer definitions (\fancyhead/\fancyfoot) which can interfere with other packages that alter page style or with multi-style documents.
insights.root_cause: Global redefinition of page styles without conditional scoping to document sections or environments.
insights.solution: Respect existing fancyhdr settings when editing; test multi-page and float-heavy documents to ensure headers/footers remain correct. Risk: changing these will alter pagination and may violate conference formatting expectations.

key_files.why: Primary conference style that defines title/section behavior and is the main point of visual formatting control for COLM 2025 papers.
Defines math shorthands and semantic macros used across papers; edits here impact notation and potential macro collisions.
Provides citation compatibility layer and controls bibliography macro behavior, tying template usage to a specific citation toolchain.
Controls global headers and footers; changes propagate across all pages and affect compliance with formatting guidelines.
Local bibliography resource used during example builds and for in-template citation examples; important for reproducible builds and citation tests.

construct_relationships: colm2025_conference.sty loads or expects math_commands.tex for math macro definitions referenced in templates.
colm2025_conference.sty and fancyhdr.sty jointly determine page-level presentation (title formatting + header/footer) and must be applied in a compatible load order.
natbib.sty and colm2025_conference.bib form the citation/bibliography workflow; natbib.sty defines citation macros that the .bib file will be processed against by BibTeX.
math_commands.tex provides macros that are referenced by author content and therefore transitively referenced by colm2025_conference.sty through example documents.
notable_relationships: These files assume an external LaTeX toolchain (pdfLaTeX/XeLaTeX/LuaLaTeX and BibTeX/biber) and will be impacted by upstream packaging or CI that compiles PDFs.
Any change here affects downstream generated papers or templates produced by the research-paper-writing skill and therefore impacts users who rely on the COLM 2025 format.
Changes may interact poorly with third-party LaTeX packages used by authors; compatibility with common packages (hyperref, amsmath, babel, fontspec) should be considered when testing.
recommendations: Preserve macro names and their semantics unless you also update all dependent documents; if renaming is unavoidable, provide backward-compatible aliases.
When editing .sty or macro files, test by compiling at least one representative sample document with the intended LaTeX engine and run BibTeX/biber as appropriate to validate citations.
Do not assume biber/biblatex unless the template and natbib shim are updated together; validate both BibTeX and biber workflows if changes touch citations.
Validate headers/footers and title formatting across multi-page, one-column and two-column scenarios after changes to fancyhdr.sty or colm2025_conference.sty.
Keep changes encoding- and engine-neutral (avoid engine-specific primitives) or document engine requirements; ensure line endings and file encodings are compatible with common LaTeX toolchains.

skills/mlops/inference/outlines/referenc.description: Reference outlines for MLOps inference guidance: backend choices, production examples, structured JSON output patterns, and troubleshooting notes. These markdown outlines define operational expectations, output contracts, and common failure modes that downstream runtime code and operational runbooks must honor.
key_behaviors: Documenting and comparing inference backends (Transformers, llama.cpp, vLLM, OpenAI) to guide runtime selection and deployment trade-offs
Defining production-ready example flows for generating structured outputs (JSON generation, extraction, classification) that act as canonical contracts for consumers
Specifying schema-driven output patterns (Pydantic, JSON Schema) and recommended patterns for robust parsing and validation
Capturing troubleshooting heuristics mapping observed failures to backend/configuration/format issues for operational debugging

insights.title: Backend behavioral and capacity differences impact runtime expectations
insights.problem: Different inference backends have varying latency, token limits, streaming semantics, and feature support, which can lead to mismatches between documented behavior and deployed behavior.
insights.root_cause: A single documentation outline trying to cover heterogeneous runtimes without explicitly binding examples or contracts to each backend.
insights.solution: When editing these references, surface precise backend constraints (limits, streaming/no-streaming, concurrency) and ensure examples call out which backend they target; note downstream code that must adapt to those constraints.

insights.title: Structured output contracts must be explicit and machine-checkable
insights.problem: Examples and guidance that show JSON outputs without an authoritative schema increase risk of parsing errors and silent failures in production.
insights.root_cause: Narrative examples may deviate from the Pydantic/JSON Schema patterns used by runtime validators.
insights.solution: Keep canonical Pydantic models / JSON Schema definitions aligned with examples; any change to example output must be reflected in the corresponding schema and vice versa, and sample payloads should validate against the schema.

insights.title: Example flows are used as de facto integration tests and developer expectations
insights.problem: Inaccurate or ambiguous examples (JSON, extraction, classification) can lead implementers to produce incompatible outputs or assume unsupported features.
insights.root_cause: Examples sometimes omit edge cases, failure modes, or required metadata (timestamps, ids, confidence scores).
insights.solution: Maintain explicit, complete example payloads including metadata and error cases; ensure examples reference backend capabilities and pick defaults that map to implemented runtime behavior.

insights.title: Troubleshooting guidance must map symptoms to configuration and code-level causes
insights.problem: High-level troubleshooting notes without mapping to config flags, logs, or metrics reduce operational usefulness.
insights.root_cause: Outlines list common failures but don't link them to where to look in logs, which config or metric to inspect, or which backend-specific setting to change.
insights.solution: When modifying troubleshooting content, link symptoms to exact config keys, expected log messages, and backend-specific diagnostics; keep it consistent with implemented logging/metrics in the inference server.

key_files.why: Central summary of runtime backend trade-offs and constraints that other reference materials and runtime choices will rely on.
Canonical production examples (JSON, extraction, classification) that downstream code and tests may be expected to implement and validate against.
Defines schema-driven output patterns (Pydantic, JSON Schema) and validation approaches that form the output contract between models and consumers.
Operational playbook for diagnosing inference failures; maps real-world issues to likely root causes and remediation steps.

construct_relationships: backends.md -> examples.md: Backend capability and semantic details in backends.md should determine which example patterns in examples.md are feasible (e.g., streaming vs batch outputs).
json_generation.md -> examples.md: Pydantic/JSON Schema patterns in json_generation.md are the authoritative definitions for the structured example outputs shown in examples.md.
troubleshooting.md -> backends.md: Diagnosed failure modes in troubleshooting.md reference backend-specific behaviors and configuration options described in backends.md.
examples.md -> troubleshooting.md: Example payloads and edge cases in examples.md should be reflected as test cases and failure scenarios in troubleshooting.md to ensure operational coverage.
notable_relationships: These reference outlines serve as upstream documentation for runtime inference implementations elsewhere in the repository (inference server code, model adapters, tools that call models) and therefore must stay consistent with implemented interfaces and logging/metrics.
JSON schema and Pydantic definitions referenced here are likely consumed by consumer code (skills, API adapters) and test suites; changes here can affect serialization/parsing across the system.

Backend recommendations implicate deployment/configuration artifacts (Docker images, resource limits, systemd/docker runtime flags) and third-party dependency versions (transformers, vLLM, llama.cpp, OpenAI client libraries).
recommendations: Preserve explicit bindings between examples, schemas, and backend constraints: when editing an example, check json_generation.md and backends.md for compatibility and update all referenced artifacts.
Validate any sample JSON or Pydantic snippets against real validators; ensure examples included in docs are syntactically valid and pass the stated schema.

When changing troubleshooting text, reference exact config keys, log message patterns, and backend-specific diagnostics so operators can act on it; verify those logs/metrics exist in the running inference code.
Do not alter example semantics or schema shapes without coordinating with runtime code and tests — update unit/integration tests and any schema consumers if needed.

Run documentation linting / build and the repository's integration/e2e tests that exercise inference examples after edits, and be mindful of external dependency version constraints called out for specific backends.

skills/mlops/inference/gguf/references.description: This folder contains reference documentation for GGUF-based model inference used by the MLOps skills: operational guidance (advanced usage), artifact handling, and troubleshooting notes. These docs capture expected interactions between GGUF files, Llama/LlamaCpp model loaders, and downstream artifact/embedding pipelines and therefore act as the authoritative behavioral contract that engineers and runtime code rely on when loading, running, and storing GGUF models.
key_behaviors: Describe and prescribe runtime configuration and usage patterns for GGUF models (e.g., speculative decoding, batching, quantization), including recommended parameters and trade-offs.
Define how model artifacts and derived outputs (embeddings, metadata) should be packaged, stored, and referenced by MLOps pipelines.
Document known failure modes and deterministic workarounds for GGUF model loading and inference (e.g., duplicate metadata key handling).

insights.title: Duplicate metadata keys are observed during GGUF loads
insights.problem: Duplicate keys in GGUF metadata lead to ambiguous/overwritten fields during model loading, resulting in potentially unexpected model behavior or lost metadata.
insights.root_cause: GGUF artifact generation or merging steps can produce repeated metadata entries; the loader/process currently resolves duplicates by keeping one instance without explicit reconciliation.
insights.solution: Ensure consumers and artifact producers agree on canonical metadata keys and that loaders either validate & report duplicates or apply a documented deterministic merge policy; update both documentation and loader behavior to remain consistent.

insights.title: Quantized GGUF artifacts and runtime compatibility
insights.problem: Quantized model files (various bit widths / formats) may be incompatible with certain Llama/LlamaCpp builds or runtime flags, causing inference failures or degraded quality if not matched correctly.
insights.root_cause: Mismatch between artifact quantization parameters and the capabilities/compile-time options of the chosen model loader and backend.
insights.solution: Document the supported quantization formats and required loader/runtime flags; when editing docs or artifacts, ensure the artifact metadata explicitly contains quantization details and that example commands align with the loader's supported options.

insights.title: Batching and speculative decoding interactions
insights.problem: Advanced inference modes (batching, speculative decoding) can significantly change latency and output behaviors; misconfiguration may produce incorrect decoding or throughput regressions.
insights.root_cause: These features require coordinated settings across prompt composition, model call parameters, and loader backend capabilities (threading, token cache), and examples are sensitive to slight API mismatches.
insights.solution: Reference implementations and examples in the docs must match the exact runtime API of the model adapter (Llama/LlamaCpp) and include required prerequisites; maintain parity between documentation parameters and actual code expectations.

key_files.why: Primary guidance for runtime inference patterns (speculative decoding, batching, quantization) that callers and examples will rely on.
Defines artifact schema and expectations for embeddings and model packaging consumed by MLOps pipelines and storage subsystems.
Documents known failure modes and deterministic workarounds (e.g., duplicate key behavior) which must reflect actual loader/runtime behavior.

construct_relationships: GGUF artifacts contain metadata used by model loaders (Llama/LlamaCpp) to configure quantization and runtime options; the docs define which metadata keys are required.

Advanced inference modes (speculative decoding, batching) described in advanced-usage.md map to specific flags and APIs exposed by the Llama/LlamaCpp adapters; example parameter sets must align with adapter implementations.
Artifact guidance (artifacts.md) specifies how embeddings and derived outputs should be produced and stored, which downstream indexing/search or evaluation pipelines will consume.
Troubleshooting notes describe how loader behavior (e.g., deduplication of duplicate metadata keys) affects artifact ingestion and therefore how artifact producers should structure files.
notable_relationships: These reference docs are upstream of code in the inference adapters (agent model adapters, Llama/LlamaCpp integration) and must remain consistent with their public APIs and runtime behaviors.
Artifact specifications influence storage/persistence subsystems and any tools that generate or consume GGUF files (exporters, embedding services, artifact registries).
Performance and decoding guidance ties into CI/integration tests and any benchmark suites that validate throughput/latency for different quantization and batching settings.
Troubleshooting guidance may overlap with error messages surfaced by model loaders; changing docs without aligning loader messages or behavior will create mismatches for operators and automation.
recommendations: When editing files here, verify that examples, flag names, and parameter values exactly match the current Llama/LlamaCpp adapter APIs and runtime flags used in the codebase.
If you change documented artifact schemas or metadata expectations, also update any model artifact producers/consumers and run downstream tests that read/write GGUF artifacts and embeddings.

Treat troubleshooting entries (e.g., duplicate key handling) as behavioral contracts: confirm loader behavior before changing text; if loader behavior changes, coordinate doc and code updates together.

Run any relevant inference integration tests or small end-to-end checks (model load, quantized inference, embedding generation, batching) after modifying these docs to ensure examples remain reproducible.
Preserve filenames and linkable anchors used elsewhere (other docs or README references); if renaming files or anchors, update cross-references in the codebase and documentation set.

skills/mlops/inference/guidance/referenc.description: This folder contains reference documentation for using the Guidance framework within the MLOps inference subsystem: backend configuration, generation constraint patterns, and production-ready examples. These documents codify runtime choices, constraint techniques, and runnable examples that downstream code (model adapters, prompt builders, deployment scripts) will rely on for correct inference behavior in production.
key_behaviors: Documenting backend configuration options and trade-offs for API-hosted vs. local models, including provider-specific settings that affect runtime behavior.
Describing constraint strategies applied at generation time (regex, grammar-based constraints, token healing) to reduce hallucinations and enforce structured outputs.
Providing production-ready example recipes (JSON extraction, structured outputs, agent patterns) that combine backend and constraint patterns to produce deterministic, deployable inference flows.

insights.title: Backend choices materially change inference behavior
insights.problem: Configurations that work for one provider (e.g., API-hosted models) may produce different outputs or fail when used with local runtimes because of differing defaults, tokenization, or decoding implementations.
insights.root_cause: Provider and runtime differences (tokenizer behavior, sampling defaults, API semantics) are not transparent and require explicit mapping in docs and runtime code.
insights.solution: Treat backend docs as authoritative for mapping Guidance patterns to provider-specific flags; ensure examples explicitly show provider-dependent settings and note where behavior diverges.

insights.title: Constraint mechanisms are fragile across providers
insights.problem: Regex/grammar/token-healing constraints described in Guidance may behave inconsistently across different decoder implementations and tokenization schemes, leading to broken parsing or ineffective constraint enforcement.
insights.root_cause: Constraints are often expressed at the text level but enforcement happens at token/decoder level, and token boundaries differ between providers and models.
insights.solution: Document constraint limitations and recommended approaches per backend: state where token-level healing is necessary, indicate which constraint types are robust per runtime, and include example edge cases to validate enforcement.

insights.title: Examples must tie together config + constraints to be reproducible
insights.problem: Standalone examples (JSON extraction, agent sequences) can be misleading if they omit backend configuration, seed/temperature settings, or postprocessing steps necessary to reproduce results.
insights.root_cause: Examples assume implicit runtime defaults that vary by environment or provider; missing operational context causes divergence in behavior when examples are executed elsewhere.
insights.solution: Ensure examples pair each recipe with the exact backend configuration and constraint settings required for reproducibility; include notes about expected tokens/outputs and failure modes to validate correctness.

key_files.why: Authoritative mapping of Guidance usage to specific backends (API vs local), which determines runtime flags and capability expectations that other docs and code must honor.
Catalog of constraint patterns (regex, grammar, token-healing) that implementers will follow to enforce structured outputs and reduce hallucination at generation time.
Production-ready recipes showing how to compose backends and constraints into runnable patterns; used as reference tests and integration examples by engineers deploying inference pipelines.

construct_relationships: Examples reference and depend on backend configuration choices from backends.md to reproduce outputs reliably.

Constraint patterns in constraints.md are applied within examples.md recipes; the effectiveness of these constraints depends on backend properties described in backends.md (tokenization and decoder behavior).
All three documents form a cohesive guidance layer: backends.md defines runtime surfaces, constraints.md defines enforcement techniques, and examples.md demonstrates concrete integrations of both.
notable_relationships: These docs are upstream of runtime components like agent/prompt_builder.py, agent/anthropic_adapter.py, and credential_pool: changes in backend recommendations must remain compatible with provider adapters and credential management.

Constraint recommendations affect parsing and postprocessing code elsewhere (JSON extraction tools, skill invocation handlers); mismatch can cause downstream failures in tools/* and skills/* that expect structured outputs.
Examples serve as informal integration tests for inference behavior; modifying examples without updating CI/integration tests or model-adapter behavior risks regressions in inference pipelines.
recommendations: When editing files here, preserve explicit mappings between Guidance techniques and provider-specific settings; update corresponding model-adapter code and tests if you alter recommended flags or assumptions.

Validate any changed constraint patterns against the tokenization and decoding behaviors of intended backends; run inference integration tests (or minimal local runs) with representative models to confirm enforcement.

Keep example recipes paired with the exact backend config, temperature/seed, and postprocessing steps required to reproduce outputs; update downstream example consumers and CI/integration tests when examples change.
Avoid changing semantic intent of documented patterns without coordinating changes in adapter code, parsing logic used by skills/tools, and CI/integration tests that rely on these examples.

Ensure formatting and any code snippets remain syntactically accurate for the Guidance version in use; if provider-specific APIs are referenced, confirm they match the versions used by agent/model adapters.

skills/mlops/inference/llama-cpp/referen.description: This folder contains operational reference documentation for running llama.cpp-based inference within the mlops/inference pipeline: optimization tuning, model quantization/format guidance, and server deployment with an OpenAI-compatible API. These references matter because production behavior (throughput, memory usage, model fidelity) depends on tight coordination between model format, quantization choices, hardware targets, and the server implementation that exposes inference to the rest of the system.
key_behaviors: Describes how model format and quantization (GGUF and related formats) affect load-time, memory footprint, and numeric fidelity for llama.cpp inference.
Prescribes CPU/GPU and batching/configuration trade-offs to tune throughput and latency for different hardware profiles.
Documents deployment and runtime expectations for an OpenAI-compatible llama.cpp server (API surface, model loading behavior, and configuration flags).
Serves as the single source of truth for operators about supported model artifacts, required toolchain steps, and runtime interactions between model binary, inference engine, and serving layer.

insights.title: GGUF model format is a canonical interchange for quantized models
insights.problem: Server and inference tooling must agree on supported model file formats and metadata (quantization type, tensor layout, tokenizer compatibility).
insights.root_cause: Multiple model formats and quantization pipelines exist; mismatches cause load failures or silent fidelity regressions.
insights.solution: Keep explicit, versioned statements of supported GGUF variants, the required tokenizer files, and validation steps; ensure any changes respect backward compatibility and note format-specific caveats (e.g., certain quantization types requiring specific CPU flags or GPU support).

insights.title: Batching, context size and hardware targeting drive throughput/latency tradeoffs
insights.problem: Default or inconsistent tuning across examples can lead to poor latency or out-of-memory errors in production.
insights.root_cause: Optimizations (threading, mmap vs. eager load, tensor core use, batch size) are hardware- and model-dependent and not universally safe.
insights.solution: Document hardware-specific recommended flag sets and their failure modes; when adjusting tuning recommendations, keep explicit pruning of unsafe defaults and include guidance on empirical tests to validate memory/latency behavior.

insights.title: OpenAI-compatible API semantics must be preserved for downstream consumers
insights.problem: The server exposes an API that downstream components expect; mismatches in parameter semantics, streaming behavior, or error codes break integrations.
insights.root_cause: Differences in how llama.cpp-based servers interpret generation parameters and how the rest of the system (gateway, adapters, agents) expects OpenAI semantics.
insights.solution: Keep migration notes and a clear mapping of OpenAI request fields to llama.cpp runtime flags; validate examples against client code paths that call the server and include expected response shapes and error conditions.

key_files.why: Defines GGUF/quantization expectations that determine which model artifacts the inference engine and server can load and serve.
Captures the hardware- and runtime-level tuning guidance that operators must follow to achieve predictable performance and avoid resource failures.
Specifies deployment and API compatibility details for the llama.cpp server which downstream components rely on for inference requests.

construct_relationships: quantization.md specifies GGUF formats and quantization tradeoffs that optimization.md assumes when recommending memory and threading flags (quantized models allow smaller memory but may change compute patterns).

server.md depends on the model-format expectations in quantization.md to declare valid model artifacts for runtime load; server deployment guidance also relies on optimization.md for recommended runtime flags to attain target latency/throughput.

All three documents together form a chain: model artifact (quantization) -> runtime configuration (optimization) -> serving surface and integration (server), so inconsistencies across any document will impact end-to-end behavior.
notable_relationships: Downstream: The server API expectations in server.md must align with higher-level components (gateway, agent model adapters, and acp_adapter) that invoke inference via an OpenAI-compatible interface — any divergence can break request/response handling.

Upstream: Model generation/quantization tooling (external scripts or CI that produce GGUF files) must produce artifacts conforming to quantization.md; embargoed or versioned GGUFs will affect deployment artifacts and Docker images used by runtime.

Operational: Dockerfile, orchestration scripts, and systemd/unit files that start the llama.cpp server must honor the optimization flags and file paths described here (HERMES_HOME / model volume mounts), so editing these docs should be consistent with deployment manifests elsewhere.
recommendations: When editing any document ensure all model format names, quantization types, and example command-lines remain consistent across files (quantization -> optimization -> server).
Preserve versioning and explicit compatibility notes for GGUF and tokenizer requirements; if any supported variant is removed or added, update server.md and optimization.md together.

Avoid changing example API request/response shapes or semantics in server.md without verifying client codepaths (gateway/platform adapters, agent model adapters) that call the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

Any adjustments to recommended runtime flags or resource assumptions should be validated on representative hardware (CPU-only and GPU) and include observed memory/latency characteristics; expect co-changes to Docker/entrypoint scripts and CI/deployment manifests.

Maintain clear failure-mode descriptions and validation steps (how to detect mismatched formats, out-of-memory, or degraded quality) so operators and automated checks can detect regressions after edits.

website/docs/user-guide/features.description: This folder contains user-facing documentation pages that describe Hermes Agent features, integrations, and configuration knobs. It is the canonical reference users consult to configure provider routing, credential pools, the API server, context file conventions (SOUL.md/AGENTS.md), automation (cron/batch), and platform integrations (ACP, MCP, browser automation). These docs must remain consistent with code-level names, environment variables, and behavioral constraints in the runtime.
key_behaviors: Describe feature intent and user configuration steps for runtime components (API server, credential pools, provider routing).
Map docs to concrete code constructs and environment variables (e.g., API_SERVER_ENABLED, API_SERVER_KEY, credential_pool).
Explain data and security considerations for context files, batch-processing, and credential handling.
Provide operational guidance for integrations (ACP editor, MCP toolservers, browser backends, cron jobs) including expected file/format conventions (trajectories, jobs.json).
Surface limits and runtime parameters that affect concurrency and delegation (MAX_CONCURRENT_CHILDREN, MAX_ITERATIONS).

insights.title: Docs reference code-only identifiers and env vars
insights.problem: Multiple pages expose exact environment variables and internal identifiers (API_SERVER_ENABLED, API_SERVER_KEY, credential_pool, rotation_strategies) which can drift from implementation.
insights.root_cause: Documentation is written to reference internal config names rather than abstract concepts; no enforced synchronization between docs and code.
insights.solution: When editing documentation, verify referenced config keys, endpoint paths, and constants against the corresponding runtime modules and configuration files to prevent mismatches; ensure examples remain syntactically valid for copy-paste.

insights.title: Credential handling and exposure
insights.problem: Docs describe credential pools, rotation strategies, and API server keys which could be misused if examples include real secrets or poor guidance.
insights.root_cause: Feature docs include operational examples and instructions for credential setup without automatic redaction or safety checks.
insights.solution: Treat code snippets and examples as potentially sensitive; do not include real keys or file paths. Ensure examples clearly mark placeholders and warn about storing secrets in plain text, and validate that any sample config.yaml / jobs.json is secure and minimal.

insights.title: Behavioral constraints and limits are documented
insights.problem: Pages explain limits (e.g., delegation/max iterations, cron lifecycle) that affect runtime concurrency; if docs change without code changes, users will be misled about safe parameters.
insights.root_cause: Implementation-enforced limits and defaults live in agent/cron/delegation code, while docs state these as guidance.
insights.solution: Ensure documentation changes that alter stated limits, defaults, or suggested settings are reconciled with the source constants and runtime logic so users are not given configurations that cause failures or unexpected resource usage.

insights.title: Context file injection & reference syntax
insights.problem: Features like context references (@file:, @diff) and context files (SOUL.md, AGENTS.md) interact with prompt composition and may expose sensitive data or exceed token limits if misused.
insights.root_cause: Docs provide powerful inline injection mechanisms tied to prompt building and file loading without automated validation in the docs.
insights.solution: When updating guidance or examples, emphasize file size/range limits, show safe examples, and ensure any sample commands produce outputs that an operator can reasonably audit for PII or large payloads.

insights.title: Batch processing and trajectory format expectations
insights.problem: Batch-processing docs reference required input formats (prompts.jsonl, Trajectory format) that batch runners expect; mismatches cause large-scale failures.
insights.root_cause: The batch runner and trajectory serializer have precise schema expectations not enforced by the docs.
insights.solution: Validate that example files and schemas in the docs match the data loader and batch_runner.py expectations; document schema fields explicitly and ensure examples are valid JSONL/JSON that conform to the implementation.

insights.title: Adapter and protocol descriptions (ACP/MCP/API server)
insights.problem: Docs describe integration endpoints and adapter names (hermes-acp, mcp_servers, OpenAI-compatible API endpoints) which must match the adapter entrypoints and server implementations.
insights.root_cause: Multiple integration points across codebase depend on exact naming and endpoint contracts described in docs.
insights.solution: Confirm naming and endpoint examples in the docs align with the corresponding adapter entry points and server flags so deployed integrations connect successfully.

key_files.why: Entry page that orients users to the features collection and links to all detailed feature docs; edits here affect discoverability and navigation.
Explains AGENTS.md and SOUL.md conventions and security rules that directly affect prompt construction and persistent personality; high impact for safe operation.
Documents the OpenAI-compatible API surface and environment flags that users enable to expose Hermes as an API; must match runtime server behavior.
Describes credential pool configuration and rotation strategies used by provider routing; relevant to security and cost controls.
Details batch-run workflows and trajectory formats relied on for offline training and scale generation; small format mismatches have large downstream effects.

construct_relationships: api-server.md references endpoints (GET /v1/chat/completions, GET /v1/models) and env flags (API_SERVER_ENABLED, API_SERVER_KEY) that must align with the API server implementation in the runtime gateway module.

credential-pools.md and provider-routing.md both reference credential_pool and rotation_strategies which correspond to the credential pool implementation and routing logic in agent/credential_pool and agent/smart_model_routing.

context-files.md, personality.md and context-references.md describe AGENTS.md, SOUL.md and the build_context_files_prompt behavior used by prompt composition logic (PromptBuilder and context compressor).
batch-processing.md depends on the trajectory format and batch_runner.py behavior—examples and schema in docs should match the batch data loader and serializer.
acp.md and mcp.md describe adapters (hermes-acp, mcp_servers) that must match adapter registry names and server behaviors in acp_adapter and MCP integration modules.
notable_relationships: These docs are authoritative for operators and refer to runtime code in agent/, gateway/, and tools/; any doc text that names config keys, endpoints, or file formats implicitly depends on the code under those folders.

Security-sensitive docs (credential pools, API server) have downstream operational impact on deployments (Docker, systemd, HERMES_HOME), so changes could affect CI, Docker images, and installers referenced elsewhere.

Integration docs (ACP, MCP, browser automation) implicate external dependencies (editor plugins, FAL.ai, CAMOFOX_URL) and any change must consider the external protocol contracts and CLI entrypoints mentioned in the repository packaging / pyproject scripts.
recommendations: When editing any page, verify referenced environment variables, endpoint paths, constants, and file formats against the corresponding runtime modules to avoid doc-code divergence.
Avoid embedding real secrets in examples; mark placeholders clearly and ensure sample config snippets are syntactically valid for copy-paste into YAML/JSON.
Run the site/build/link checker and markdown linter after edits to catch broken links and malformed code blocks; validate JSON/JSONL examples against the actual parser expectations.

If updating behavioral descriptions (defaults, limits, rotation strategies), cross-check the source of truth in code (constants or config parsing) and update all pages that mention the same values to keep guidance consistent.
Preserve category/frontmatter formatting (_category_.json) and internal anchors so generated site navigation and cross-linking remain intact.

website/docs/user-guide/messaging.description: This folder contains user-facing documentation for messaging gateway integrations and how to connect various chat/messaging platforms to the Hermes agent. It explains per-platform authentication, delivery methods (webhooks, socket mode, bridges), and the operational prerequisites (API server, keys, public endpoints) that the gateway and platform adapters rely on. These docs are the authoritative guide for operators and integrators and must stay consistent with runtime flags, environment variables, and gateway behavior.
key_behaviors: Describes platform-specific onboarding flows: how to obtain credentials/IDs, configure callbacks/endpoints, and register bots or webhooks.
Specifies delivery and event models per platform (webhook vs socket mode vs long polling/bridge), and how those map to Hermes' API server and gateway adapters.
Defines security and configuration requirements for externally accessible endpoints (API_SERVER_ENABLED, API_SERVER_KEY, webhook secrets, TLS/public URL).
Explains message and attachment handling expectations (text, media, threading, mentions) and any platform-specific nuances affecting the agent runtime.
Surfaces operational caveats and dependencies (local network access, third-party binary constraints, tunneling tools) needed for reliable integration.

insights.title: Docs reference runtime flags and env vars that gate WebUI/API connectivity
insights.problem: Open WebUI and similar integrations depend on specific runtime flags and environment variables to be enabled and configured correctly.
insights.root_cause: Platform guides assume an Hermes API server that may be disabled by default or behind a different config name in runtime code; mismatches between docs and code cause failed connections.
insights.solution: When editing docs or code ensure the documented config keys (e.g., API_SERVER_ENABLED, API_SERVER_KEY, OPENAI_API_BASE_URL) match the runtime flag/env names and behavior; keep warnings about enabling and securing the API server prominent.

insights.title: Multiple authentication surfaces increase risk of secret exposure
insights.problem: Platform integrations require tokens, webhook secrets, or socket-mode tokens; inconsistent handling in docs can lead users to expose secrets or misconfigure access controls.
insights.root_cause: Each messenger has its own auth model and the docs enumerate many different credential types without a unified guidance on secret storage and rotation.
insights.solution: Edits must preserve explicit guidance about where tokens are used, how to keep them out of source control, and any platform-specific token lifecycles or rotating-key recommendations; ensure references to runtime secret locations are accurate.

insights.title: Platform-specific operational caveats (e.g., matrix and native libs)
insights.problem: Some platforms depend on optional or platform-specific native dependencies or long-lived connection models that are fragile (e.g., matrix client native wheels, Home Assistant network exposure).
insights.root_cause: The repository exposes many optional integrations with differing dependency and runtime constraints; native build/e2e issues exist for certain platforms.
insights.solution: Documentation edits must continue to call out optional extras, platform limitations, and any CI/OS caveats so users understand when additional platform-specific steps or binary dependencies are required.

insights.title: Public endpoint and tunneling requirements for webhook-based integrations
insights.problem: Webhooks and some platform callbacks require a reachable public URL; failing to document tunneling/SSL setup leads to nonfunctional integrations during local development.
insights.root_cause: Local dev setups lack a public endpoint by default; many platform docs assume the reader will set up a tunnel or host the API server publicly.
insights.solution: Edits must retain or update instructions for obtaining a public URL, configuring TLS, and alternatives (tunnels, reverse proxies), and clearly link to where the gateway expects callback URLs to be configured.

insights.title: Mapping between Hermes gateway behavior and platform docs must remain aligned
insights.problem: If the gateway’s event model or adapter APIs change, docs can become stale, misguiding integrators about expected message formats, threading, or tool invocation behavior.
insights.root_cause: Documentation is distributed per platform and references runtime behavior implemented in gateway/platform adapters; disparate updates risk drift.
insights.solution: When modifying docs, ensure documented message semantics (e.g., how replies map to session/thread IDs, how attachments are represented) reflect the current gateway adapter contracts and link to the authoritative adapter code or API server docs.

key_files.why: Primary overview of the messaging gateway architecture and quickstart; anchors platform-specific pages and references gateway-level configuration.
Documents WebUI-to-Hermes connectivity and references critical runtime flags/env vars (API_SERVER_ENABLED, API_SERVER_KEY, OPENAI_API_BASE_URL) that tie into the API server and model routing.
Covers Slack-specific integration modes (e.g., Socket Mode) and authentication flows that differ from webhook-driven platforms; sensitive to runtime adapter changes.
General guidance for webhook-based delivery, public endpoint requirements, and event wiring that applies to multiple platform docs.
One of the widely used platform guides; documents bot registration and token use and is often used as the exemplar for other messaging integrations.

construct_relationships: Open WebUI configuration (API_SERVER_ENABLED, API_SERVER_KEY, OPENAI_API_BASE_URL) is documented here and must correspond to the Hermes API server/runtime flags and agent model adapter configuration.
Webhooks page describes how external platform events map to Hermes' internal session/agent endpoints; platform-specific docs (Telegram, Slack, Twilio/SMS, etc.) are specializations of that mapping.
Slack docs emphasize socket mode vs webhook; this maps to different gateway adapter implementations and to the need for persistent connections or public endpoints documented in the webhook guidance.
Home Assistant and other local integrations require local network access and long-lived connections, which intersects with gateway runtime and deployment guidance (public vs local hosting).
Platform credential and token handling described across files ties into the gateway's authentication checks and the API server's expected headers/keys.
notable_relationships: This documentation folder is directly upstream of gateway/platform adapters and the Hermes API server: any change to adapter APIs, config names, or expected webhook routes must be reflected here.
Open WebUI docs depend on model-provider configuration (OPENAI_API_BASE_URL) and therefore relate to agent model adapters and credential handling in the core agent.
Matrix-related docs may be affected by broader dependency management decisions (native wheels, optional extras) documented in pyproject.toml and the project README.

Webhook and public endpoint guidance has downstream operational implications (Docker/host networking, reverse proxy, TLS) and must remain consistent with deployment scripts and Dockerfile expectations.
recommendations: When editing these docs, verify that every referenced config key, env var, endpoint path, and token name exactly matches the runtime flag and code-level configuration names used by the gateway and API server.

Preserve and/or validate security instructions for credential handling, webhook secrets, and API keys; ensure no secrets are added to docs and that guidance about secure storage and rotation remains intact.

If changing platform-specific behavioral language (threading, attachment semantics, message formats), update corresponding gateway/platform adapter code or add explicit pointers so integrators can reconcile behavior.
Keep operational prerequisites (public URL, TLS, tunnel tools, native deps) clearly stated; before removing or renaming such notes, confirm equivalent behavior in the runtime environment and CI docs.

After edits, run the site/docs build and link/anchor checks and, for any changes to env/config names, run or trigger the relevant gateway integration tests or a manual verification against a representative platform adapter to avoid documentation-code drift.

skills/research/research-paper-writing/r.description: This folder holds canonical reference material for the research-paper-writing skill: templates, guidelines, and bibliographic resources used to produce, review, and evaluate ML conference papers. The documents encode editorial rules (narrative, clarity, checklists), reviewer/evaluation criteria, experiment and human-evaluation patterns, and a central bibliography/citation workflow that other parts of the skillset will rely on. These resources are authoritative text artifacts rather than executable code, and changes to them affect downstream automated workflows, skill outputs, and any tooling that parses or cites them.
key_behaviors: Provide authoritative editorial guidance (narrative principles, abstract/introduction formulas) consumed by writing flows and templates.
Define normative reviewer and checklist criteria used for pre-submission checks and reviewer-simulation tooling.
Centralize bibliographic and citation-handling guidance to prevent hallucinated references and to standardize citation APIs/tools.
Surface experimental design and human-evaluation protocols to ensure reproducible experiments and valid downstream evaluations.
Map paper types to structural and stylistic expectations which downstream generators or prompts may use to tailor outputs.

insights.title: Single source of truth for citations and bibliographic references
insights.problem: Multiple documents reference literature/citation workflows but there is one sources.md that should be authoritative; if documents diverge, citation inconsistency and hallucination risk rises.
insights.root_cause: Decentralized edits to guidance files without synchronizing the central bibliography and citation workflow guidance.
insights.solution: Treat sources.md and citation-workflow.md as canonical for bibliographic entries and citation handling; any content edits that add or reference sources must update the central bibliography and citation guidance so downstream consumers remain consistent.

insights.title: Conference checklists can become stale as official guidelines change
