insights.problem: checklists.md codifies conference-specific rules that are periodically updated by conferences; stale checklists will mislead pre-submission checks.
insights.root_cause: External dependency on conference policies and deadlines not tracked inside the repository.
insights.solution: Document provenance and timestamps for checklist items and ensure consumers verify against official conference pages; when editing checklists, update provenance metadata so readers and automated checks know freshness.

insights.title: Structured headings and constructs may be parsed by tools
insights.problem: Reviewer-simulation, pre-submission automation, or skill prompts may parse specific headings/construct names (e.g., 'Universal Evaluation Dimensions'), so free-form edits can break parsers.
insights.root_cause: Human-readable Markdown is also used as a light-weight machine-readable resource without explicit schema enforcement.
insights.solution: Preserve canonical headings, key construct names, and predictable section structure where tooling expects them; if reorganization is necessary, ensure parsers/consumers are updated in tandem.

insights.title: Human evaluation and experiment patterns need tight coupling
insights.problem: human-evaluation.md and experiment-patterns.md encode complementary procedures; inconsistency between them undermines reproducibility and experimental validity.
insights.root_cause: Separate documents authored independently without explicit cross-references or shared templates for protocols and analysis.
insights.solution: Link protocol steps and expected artifact formats across both documents; when updating experimental steps or scoring rubrics, update the companion file so evaluation pipelines remain aligned.

insights.title: Paper types impose different structural and stylistic constraints
insights.problem: writing-guide.md and paper-types.md provide distinct but related guidance; conflicting advice (e.g., style vs theory-paper expectations) will confuse automated generation and human authors.
insights.root_cause: Overlap between generic writing advice and type-specific recommendations without explicit precedence rules.
insights.solution: When referencing stylistic rules from writing-guide.md in a paper-type context, call out precedence (type-specific overrides) and ensure downstream generators know which guidance to prioritize.

insights.title: Reviewer guidance is used both for humans and simulation tooling
insights.problem: reviewer-guidelines.md contains evaluation dimensions and simulation steps that may be consumed by reviewer-simulation or pre-submission review features; misaligning language or removing dimensions will break expectations and comparisons.
insights.root_cause: The same document is both a human-readable guideline and an informal contract for tooling without an explicit API or schema.
insights.solution: Keep reviewer-guidelines.md stable for the enumerated evaluation dimensions and document any structural changes with migration notes so dependent tooling or tests can adapt.

key_files.why: Central bibliography and listing of citation APIs/tools referenced by other guide documents; authoritative source for all bibliographic entries.
Defines the anti-hallucination citation workflow and how citations are created/verified; informs generation and verification steps in automated writing flows.
Contains conference-specific pre-submission checklists used by pre-submission validation and reviewer-simulation processes.
Enumerates evaluation dimensions and reviewer advice that pre-submission simulations and review-assistant features consume.
Provides the narrative, clarity, and stylistic rules that writing generators and templates use to shape text output.

construct_relationships: The bibliography in sources.md is referenced by citation-workflow.md for anti-hallucination verification and by writing-guide.md when giving citation examples.
Reviewer-guidelines.md supplies 'Universal Evaluation Dimensions' which checklists.md maps into conference-specific acceptance/review criteria for pre-submission checks.
Experiment-patterns.md and human-evaluation.md share protocol constructs (protocol steps, scoring rubrics) that must remain aligned for reproducible experiments and evaluation analysis.
paper-types.md provides structural expectations that the writing-guide.md must reconcile with (e.g., theoretical vs empirical sections) so templates and narrative rules apply correctly.
Autoreason methodology (autoreason-methodology.md) and writing-guide.md may be used together by generation or refinement flows to drive iterative improvement of manuscript drafts.
notable_relationships: Downstream skills and runtime: The research-paper-writing skill and any automated writing or reviewer-simulation subskills likely read these Markdown files to produce content or checks; altering content/structure impacts runtime behavior.
External dependencies: checklists.md and sources.md implicitly depend on external authoritative conference pages and citation APIs/tools; their correctness depends on those upstream resources.

Cross-skill dependencies: citation-workflow.md mentions Citation APIs & Tools that likely overlap with platform-level tooling under tools/ or other skills that handle bibliographic APIs—coordination may be required when changing names or expected formats.

Persistence and reproducibility: Changes to experiment-patterns.md and human-evaluation.md affect artifacts expected by analysis pipelines or stored experiment metadata elsewhere in the repo or HERMES_HOME.
recommendations: Preserve canonical headings and construct names (e.g., 'Universal Evaluation Dimensions', 'The Narrative Principle') unless you update all consumers that parse these headings.
When adding or editing citations, update sources.md and ensure citation-workflow.md reflects any new verification steps or API/tool references.
If checklist items or reviewer dimensions are modified, note provenance (source URL and date) in the file to make it clear whether the guidance matches current official conference rules.

Cross-check human-evaluation and experiment-patterns edits for protocol and rubric consistency; ensure expected artifact names/formats used by evaluation tooling remain unchanged or are migrated together.

Run link-checking and basic Markdown linting after edits, and search the repository for references to changed headings/filenames so downstream consumers (skills, automation scripts, or tests) are updated or validated.
Avoid renaming or moving files without searching for internal references and update any code or other markdown that imports/parses these files to prevent runtime breakage.

skills/red-teaming/godmode/scripts.description: This folder implements an offline/interactive red‑teaming "godmode" toolkit: automated jailbreak discovery and locking, a multi‑model racing/scoring engine, an input obfuscation (parseltongue) generator, and a small loader that imports these helper scripts into a shared namespace. These scripts integrate with model provider clients, scoring heuristics, and on‑disk persistence (config/prefill) so they are high‑impact for model querying flows and for any persisted jailbreak artifacts.
key_behaviors: Detect model family and iterate model‑specific jailbreak strategies, test them against canary queries, score results, and optionally persist a chosen jailbreak/prefill.
Dispatch parallel queries across a tiered model list, score responses for refusal/hedging/detail, and select the best non‑refusal answer (racing engine).
Generate large numbers of obfuscated input variants by applying a registry of transformation techniques and trigger detection utilities to evade input‑side classifiers.
Dynamically load multiple godmode script files into the running process and expose their callables/constants for interactive/exec use, while mutating loader process state (e.g., sys.argv).

insights.title: Jailbreak prompts and prefills are written to disk
insights.problem: Auto‑jailbreak can persist discovered jailbreak prompts/prefills (config.yaml and prefill.json) which may contain sensitive or policy‑violating content.
insights.root_cause: Scripts intentionally write chosen prompt/prefill artifacts to local files to 'lock in' working jailbreaks for reuse.
insights.solution: Treat file writes as sensitive operations: ensure clear, configurable target paths, atomic writes, appropriate permissions, and that callers are aware these artifacts may leak if committed or mounted into shared volumes.

insights.title: Model provider network calls are central and environment‑sensitive
insights.problem: Functions perform real model queries (OpenRouter/OpenAI clients) and will fail, be rate‑limited, or behave differently when API keys are missing or blanked (CI pattern).
insights.root_cause: Scripts rely on live model clients and credentials; CI blanks keys to avoid external calls.
insights.solution: When editing or testing, assume model clients must be mocked and that timeouts, backoff, and credential lookup behavior are important to preserve for reliable unit/integration testing.

insights.title: Scoring heuristics and refusal/hedge detection drive selection
insights.problem: Small changes to scoring heuristics or refusal/hedge detection will change which model outputs are chosen or classified as failures.
insights.root_cause: Selection and persistence logic depend on heuristics (is_refusal, score_response) used across racing and auto‑jailbreak flows.
insights.solution: Maintain deterministic scoring interfaces and be mindful of side effects when adjusting thresholds or text classification logic; verify behavior with representative fixtures.

insights.title: Loader expects specific exported symbols and mutates process state
insights.problem: load_godmode executes and exports callables/UPPERCASE constants from other scripts and temporarily adjusts sys.argv; changes to exported names or loader side effects will break interactive exec use.
insights.root_cause: The loader relies on naming conventions (callables and UPPERCASE constants) and on being able to exec modules into the current namespace.
insights.solution: Preserve exported symbol names and be cautious about loader side effects (sys.argv, globals) when renaming or refactoring functions/constants.

insights.title: Parseltongue provides many transforms and escalation encodings
insights.problem: A large technique registry (33 transforms) and escalation strategies can produce exponential variant sets; changes can alter coverage and may affect reproducibility.
insights.root_cause: Technique registry and generation utilities are the single source for variant production used by red‑teaming pipelines.
insights.solution: Treat the technique registry as a stable contract: changing technique names, ordering, encoding escalation, or trigger detection will affect downstream tests and automated pipelines that expect specific variants.

key_files.why: Entry point for automated jailbreak discovery and persistence; coordinates model detection, strategy iteration, scoring, and writing config/prefill artifacts.
Parallel multi‑model racing and scoring engine used to get the best non‑refusal model response; contains refusal detection and scoring heuristics reused by other flows.
Obfuscation/variant generator and trigger detector used to produce input variants for testing and escalation; central registry of transformation techniques.
Dynamic loader that exec() imports the above scripts into a shared namespace for interactive usage; alters process state (sys.argv) during load.

construct_relationships: auto_jailbreak uses model detection utilities (_detect_model_family/_get_current_model) and iterates MODEL_STRATEGIES to produce candidate jailbreak prompts.
auto_jailbreak invokes model querying and scoring logic (heuristics compatible with godmode_race.score_response / is_refusal) to evaluate candidate outputs before persisting.
godmode_race issues parallel model requests via external clients, applies scoring heuristics and refusal detection, and returns the top non‑refusal response for callers such as auto_jailbreak.
parseltongue.generate_variants and obfuscate_query produce input variants that auto_jailbreak and the racing engine use to test canary queries and bypass input‑side filters.
load_godmode.exec loader imports functions/constants from parseltongue, godmode_race, and auto_jailbreak into the current namespace so interactive sessions can call their APIs directly.
notable_relationships: These scripts make network calls to model provider clients (OpenRouter/OpenAI) and therefore depend on the project’s credential/configuration subsystems (credential lookup, environment variables) present elsewhere in the codebase.

Persisted artifacts (config.yaml, prefill.json) are written locally and may intersect with the broader HERMES_HOME/data persistence strategy and packaging/deployment (Docker volume mounts, backups, or CI snapshots).
CI and test environments that blank API keys will cause live queries to fail; maintainers must rely on mocks or recorded fixtures for unit tests.
Loader behavior (exec/imports, sys.argv mutation) can interact poorly with test harnesses or CLI tooling if not respected when invoked from other parts of the system.
recommendations: When editing, preserve exported symbol names, function signatures, and UPPERCASE constant names consumed by load_godmode and other callers to avoid breaking dynamic imports.
Assume model queries will be mocked in CI/tests; avoid introducing hard dependencies on live network calls in unit tests and preserve existing timeout/backoff behavior for realistic integration runs.

Treat file writes (config.yaml, prefill.json) as sensitive: respect configurable write locations, ensure atomic writes and appropriate permissions, and avoid writing secrets into repository working trees or shared volumes.
Be cautious when changing scoring/refusal detection logic: tests or downstream flows rely on deterministic heuristics—run scoring/unit tests with representative fixtures after changes.

Account for concurrency and resource usage in the racing engine: maintain thread/process model assumptions, explicit timeouts, and model client compatibility to avoid deadlocks or excessive parallelism in production.
When modifying parseltongue, preserve technique registry identities and trigger detection semantics because pipelines depend on variant coverage and deterministic escalation ordering.

skills/productivity/google-workspace/scr.description: This folder contains two CLI-focused scripts that provide Google Workspace integration for the Hermes Google skill: a runtime thin client (google_api.py) that authenticates and issues Gmail/Calendar/Drive/Sheets/Docs calls, and a non-interactive provisioning helper (setup.py) that performs OAuth2 PKCE flows, token lifecycle management, and optional dependency installation. Both scripts operate against persistent credentials and token files stored under a Hermes profile (HERMES_HOME) and are intended to be invoked by the Google Workspace skill or by operators for setup and troubleshooting.
key_behaviors: Authenticate using stored OAuth2 credentials (load, refresh, persist tokens) and build Google service clients
Map CLI subcommands to Google Workspace API operations (Gmail search/send/reply, list/create calendar events, drive search/create, read/update Sheets and Docs) and return JSON results
Provision and manage OAuth2 client secrets and tokens: generate PKCE auth URLs, exchange auth codes for tokens, refresh tokens, revoke tokens, and persist PKCE state
Install required Python dependencies for Google APIs when requested by the provisioning helper
Operate against a Hermes profile directory (HERMES_HOME), relying on repo/package import paths and persisted files for state

insights.title: Scripts assume package import context and can fail when run directly
insights.problem: Direct execution of setup.py or google_api.py from the repository root failed with ImportError related to hermes_constants (missing module in sys.path).
insights.root_cause: Scripts import shared project modules (e.g., hermes_constants) using package-relative or project-root paths; invoking them as standalone scripts changes Python import resolution and omits the project root from sys.path.
insights.solution: When modifying or invoking these scripts, ensure the execution environment provides the project package on sys.path (run via package entry points, set PYTHONPATH, or preserve the existing repo-root sys.path fallback behavior) so imports of hermes_constants and other project modules continue to resolve.

insights.title: Persistent tokens and client_secret.json are written to the Hermes profile and must be protected
insights.problem: OAuth tokens and client_secret.json are persisted to the Hermes home/profile directory and used directly by the Google skill and scripts.
insights.root_cause: Persistent OAuth flows require local storage of tokens and client secrets to allow refresh and non-interactive operation.
insights.solution: Any edits must preserve token file formats, storage paths (HERMES_HOME/profile), and not leak secrets; ensure file permissions and locations remain compatible with the agent that consumes them.

insights.title: Token refresh and PKCE state handling are central to non-interactive operation
insights.problem: The provisioning script and client expect ability to refresh tokens and to persist PKCE state between auth steps; broken refresh handling will prevent the skill from functioning without manual re-auth.
insights.root_cause: OAuth2 flows (PKCE authorization code exchange) require transient state persistence and reliable refresh token storage and refresh logic.
insights.solution: Maintain correct token refresh semantics, PKCE state persistence and revocation behavior; be aware of potential race conditions if multiple processes may refresh the same token files concurrently.

insights.title: setup.py can install Python dependencies which has operational risk
insights.problem: setup.py includes the ability to install required Google API dependencies which may modify the host environment and requires network access and permissions.
insights.root_cause: Convenience to bootstrap Google API client libs from the script itself couples provisioning with environment mutation.
insights.solution: When editing or executing the provisioning helper, account for environment isolation (virtualenvs), permission boundaries, and that CI/test environments may not allow package installation; maintain explicit, opt-in behavior for dependency installation.

key_files.why: Runtime CLI client used by the Google Workspace skill to authenticate and call Gmail/Calendar/Drive/Sheets/Docs APIs and return JSON responses.
Non-interactive OAuth2 provisioning helper that creates/stores client_secret.json, generates PKCE auth URLs, exchanges codes for tokens, refreshes/revokes tokens, and can install Python dependencies.

construct_relationships: setup.py writes client_secret.json, token files and persisted PKCE state into the Hermes profile directory which google_api.py then reads via get_credentials to build_service clients.
get_credentials in google_api.py performs token loading and refresh; build_service constructs per-API service clients used by gmail_search, calendar_list and other operation functions.

CLI subcommands in google_api.py map directly to Google API operations (Gmail, Calendar, Drive, Sheets, Docs) and return structured JSON; these outputs are consumed by the Google skill layer that invoked the script.
The dependency-install capability in setup.py is orthogonal to runtime calls but affects the runtime environment that google_api.py depends on (Google client libraries).
notable_relationships: Both scripts depend on hermes_constants / project package modules and expect to run with the project on Python's import path (HERMES_HOME and other constants are shared across the repo).
Persisted token and client secret files are part of the agent's persistent state (HERMES_HOME) and therefore changes here impact security, backups, and migrations for the whole agent runtime.
Network calls to Google APIs and pip install operations make these scripts sensitive to CI/production environment policies (credentials must not be present in CI; installation may be blocked).

The Google Workspace skill (skills folder) invokes these scripts; changing CLI signatures, JSON output structure, or token locations will affect upstream skill code and possibly gateway/agent flows that expect stable outputs.
recommendations: Preserve token file formats, storage paths (Hermes profile/HERMES_HOME), and PKCE state semantics so the skill and other scripts continue to locate and use credentials.

Maintain import compatibility: edits must not break imports of hermes_constants or other project modules when scripts are executed either as package entry points or directly; ensure the execution environment provides the project root on sys.path if necessary.
Do not expose client_secret.json or persisted tokens in logs or repository; respect file permission and secret handling when changing read/write behaviors.
When editing any code that refreshes or writes tokens, consider concurrent access (potential race conditions) and preserve atomic write patterns to avoid token corruption.

Be mindful that setup.py can install packages — avoid executing install actions in CI or constrained environments; any test that touches dependency installation should be run in controlled/local contexts.

If you change CLI outputs or subcommand semantics, update any callers in the Google Workspace skill and run integration validation where credentials are available; run unit tests (excluding integration/e2e) and verify that scripts work when invoked via the packaged entrypoints used by the agent.

skills/creative/p5js/scripts.description: This subtree provides small utilities to develop, run, and render p5.js sketches into deterministic image frames and MP4 movies. It ties together a Node-based Puppeteer exporter, a shell-based ffmpeg encoding pipeline, a lightweight local file server for development, and a dependency checker; these scripts orchestrate external system tooling (Chromium/Puppeteer, ffmpeg, Python/Node) and rely on conventions in the sketch implementation.
key_behaviors: Serve a local sketch directory over HTTP so browser-based p5.js sketches can load assets during development
Launch headless Chromium (via Puppeteer) to load a p5.js sketch and capture deterministic frame-by-frame PNGs by invoking redraw() and relying on sketch-side readiness signaling
Provide a shell pipeline that runs the frame exporter, manages temporary frame directories, and encodes captured frames into an MP4 using ffmpeg
Check for the presence and viability of external dependencies (node/npm/Puppeteer/ffmpeg/python) and report actionable guidance to the developer

insights.title: Heavy reliance on external system binaries and downloaded Chromium
insights.problem: Scripts invoke Puppeteer/Chromium and ffmpeg which may not be present or may be configured differently across environments (CI, developer machines, containers).
insights.root_cause: The exporter and render pipeline perform browser-based rendering and video encoding; these capabilities are provided by external tools rather than pure-Python/JS libraries.
insights.solution: Edits must preserve detection/usage of these external tools and respect environment variants (missing ffmpeg, missing Chromium, Puppeteer install flags). Any change should keep or update the diagnostic messages, exit codes, and fallbacks so callers can detect missing tools and avoid silent failures.

insights.title: Capture depends on sketch-side contract for deterministic redraw
insights.problem: export-frames.js requires the sketch to call noLoop() and set window._p5Ready (or the fallback timed capture) to guarantee frame-accurate redraw(). If sketches do not adhere, exported frames may be missing, duplicated, or non-deterministic.
insights.root_cause: The exporter operates by invoking redraw() and reading canvas output; p5.js sketches that use continuous looping or do not expose a readiness flag break the expected flow.
insights.solution: When editing scripts, preserve the expectation and any logging/errors about window._p5Ready and noLoop; ensure the fallback timed capture remains explicit and clearly reported so callers can understand nondeterministic captures.

insights.title: Frame directories and temporary files are created and removed by the shell pipeline
insights.problem: render.sh creates and removes temporary frame directories; mistakes in path handling or error paths can leave large temporary data or accidentally remove user files.
insights.root_cause: Shell scripts perform filesystem operations and subprocess orchestration; improper quoting, globbing, or error handling can cause data loss or leftover artifacts.
insights.solution: Any edits must keep safe directory creation, explicit temporary directory names, and deliberate cleanup semantics. Maintain clear default locations and avoid destructive operations that could target non-temporary directories; ensure consistent exit codes and error logging so automated callers can respond.

insights.title: Multiple fallbacks for serving and browser tooling
insights.problem: serve.sh prefers python3 http.server but falls back to npx serve; Puppeteer may require a pre-downloaded Chromium. Platforms lacking one tool need a fallback to continue functioning.
insights.root_cause: Different developer environments have different toolchains installed; the scripts try to be pragmatic by probing alternatives.
insights.solution: Preserve the probe-and-fallback logic and the printed guidance so users know which tool was chosen. Keep output of the final server URL and working directory stable for scripts that rely on parsing that output.

insights.title: Setup script provides essential diagnostics but can become stale
insights.problem: setup.sh reports presence/absence of tools and installation hints; if changes alter how tools are invoked or which env vars are required, the guidance can mislead developers.
insights.root_cause: Documentation and checks diverge as scripts evolve and rely on environment variables or upstream package behavior.
insights.solution: When modifying scripts, ensure setup.sh reflects the accurate invocation patterns and environment variables (e.g., Puppeteer download flags, required ffmpeg options). Preserve clear, actionable messages and non-zero exit codes on fatal missing dependencies.

key_files.why: Primary Node/Puppeteer exporter that drives deterministic frame capture from p5.js sketches; other scripts orchestrate around this file.
Shell entrypoint that runs the exporter and pipes frames into ffmpeg; contains temp-dir management and is the canonical end-to-end render pipeline.
Local HTTP server wrapper used during development and by the exporter to host sketches and assets; chooses between python and npx-based servers.
Environment checker that scripts and contributors rely on to validate required tools and provide installation guidance.

construct_relationships: render.sh invokes Node to run export-frames.js to produce numbered PNG frames, then invokes ffmpeg to encode those frames into an MP4.

export-frames.js expects the sketch loaded by the local server to expose a readiness contract (set window._p5Ready and call noLoop()); serve.sh provides the HTTP endpoint used to host that sketch during headless capture.
setup.sh detects the presence and versions of Node/npm, Puppeteer (and implicitly Chromium), ffmpeg, and Python which are prerequisites for export-frames.js, serve.sh, and render.sh to succeed.
serve.sh's printed URL and working directory are consumed implicitly by developers and by the export pipeline as the target URL to load the sketch into Puppeteer.
notable_relationships: These scripts depend on system-level tools (Chromium via Puppeteer, ffmpeg, python3, npx) outside the repository; CI or container environments may need those installed or configured (Chromium may be downloaded by Puppeteer or skipped via env flags).

Sketch files elsewhere in the p5js skill must follow the export contract (noLoop + window._p5Ready) for deterministic exports; changes to sketch conventions in other folders will affect the behavior of export-frames.js and render.sh.

Output artifacts (numbered PNG frames and MP4 files) are produced under temporary or output directories; external consumers (other skills, CI artifacts, or user scripts) may expect specific filename patterns and ordering.

Platform differences (macOS, Linux, CI runners, Docker images) influence availability and behavior of python3, npx, Puppeteer downloads, and ffmpeg; the scripts encode fallbacks and diagnostics to handle this variability.
recommendations: Preserve the command-line interfaces, option names, and default behaviors of export-frames.js and render.sh so existing invocations and automation keep working; if arguments are changed, update setup.sh and any callers accordingly.

Do not remove or weaken the sketch readiness contract (noLoop and window._p5Ready) without coordinating with the sketches that will be captured; ensure any messaging about missing readiness remains intact.

Maintain safe temporary directory creation/cleanup semantics in render.sh; avoid patterns that could delete non-temporary user data. Ensure proper quoting and error handling in shell code to be robust across environments.

Keep or update detection and fallback logic in serve.sh and setup.sh for python3 vs npx serve and for Puppeteer/Chromium vs system Chromium, and ensure the output server URL format remains parseable by tools or documented for users.

When testing changes, run an end-to-end scenario with a minimal compliant p5.js sketch: start the server, run export-frames.js to produce frames, and run render.sh to produce the MP4 (only if ffmpeg and headless Chromium are available). Also validate behavior when dependencies are missing to ensure clear diagnostics and non-zero exit codes.

skills/productivity/powerpoint/scripts.description: This subtree contains CLI scripts and XML-processing helpers that operate on unpacked Office package directories (primarily PPTX and DOCX). The scripts perform creation, cleanup, transformation and repacking of Office files by manipulating slide/doc XML, relationship (.rels) files, and [Content_Types].xml; helpers implement delicate XML canonicalization tasks (run merging and tracked-change simplification) used by packing/validation flows. These utilities are critical because they must maintain Office package integrity and compatibility with downstream Office applications while being safe to run on user worktrees.
key_behaviors: Manipulate an unpacked Office package: create/duplicate slide XML, update presentation.xml slide IDs, maintain .rels entries and [Content_Types].xml.
Prune unreachable artifacts by computing transitive reachability from presentation manifest and removing orphaned slides, media and rels iteratively.
Pack an unpacked Office directory into a .pptx/.docx/.xlsx by condensing XML, running validators/auto-repair, and producing a ZIP archive suitable for Office applications.

Perform XML-level cleanup operations on Word documents: merge adjacent <w:r> runs with identical formatting and collapse adjacent tracked-change elements by author while preserving whitespace semantics.
Emit deterministic and machine-parsable outputs (e.g., printing a <p:sldId> element) so other tools or scripts can integrate with these utilities.

insights.title: Slide and relationship identifiers must remain unique and synchronized across multiple manifests
insights.problem: New slide files or removals can leave inconsistent slideId values, duplicated rId targets, or stale .rels entries that make the package unreadable by Office.
insights.root_cause: Separate updates are performed to slide files, presentation.xml, .rels and [Content_Types].xml; failure to update every related manifest or to choose globally-unique IDs causes mismatch.
insights.solution: Ensure any change that adds/removes slides updates presentation.xml slideId entries, relationship rId targets in the correct .rels file, and content-type overrides atomically; generate IDs deterministically and check for collisions before writing.

insights.title: Cleaning must be conservative and idempotent to avoid data loss
insights.problem: Prune operations that remove files based solely on a single pass of reachable targets risk deleting assets referenced indirectly or by nonstandard rels, causing unrecoverable loss.
insights.root_cause: Packages can have multiple rel chains, external relationships, or metadata that are not covered by a naive traversal; one-pass removal misses transitive references added by other package parts.
insights.solution: Perform iterative, transitive reachability analysis; treat unexpected rel targets as potential external references, optionally back up pruned content, and make the operation idempotent so repeated runs converge to the same state.

insights.title: Merging runs and collapsing redlines must preserve xml:space and visible whitespace
insights.problem: Aggressive consolidation of text nodes or runs can change rendering (spaces removed/condensed) or corrupt tracked-change semantics if xml:space attributes and whitespace-only nodes are mishandled.
insights.root_cause: Text consolidation routines that ignore xml:space, surrounding markup, or whitespace-only separators between tracked-change elements incorrectly normalize content.
insights.solution: When merging runs or tracked-change wrappers, preserve xml:space attributes, honor whitespace-only nodes as separators, and only merge when formatting properties and whitespace semantics match exactly.

insights.title: Stripping rsid / revision metadata is lossy and changes provenance
insights.problem: Removing rsid and other revision attributes simplifies downstream processing but permanently removes revision provenance, which other tools or users may rely on.
insights.root_cause: Helpers like merge_runs explicitly strip revision metadata to reduce fragmentation without preserving an audit trail.
insights.solution: Document the lossy nature clearly; where necessary offer an option to back up original files or store removed metadata in a separate location before stripping.

insights.title: File-system writes and zip packing can leave packages partially updated
insights.problem: Interruption during multi-file updates, cleanup, or zipping can leave the unpacked directory in an inconsistent state or produce a corrupt archive.
insights.root_cause: Operations span multiple files (XML, .rels, [Content_Types].xml) and the final packaging step; lacking atomic replace/transaction semantics causes partial state exposure.
insights.solution: Use temporary files/directories and atomic rename for final swaps; when packing, create the archive in a temp path and validate before replacing the original; optionally create backups prior to destructive operations.

insights.title: CLI outputs and pack formats are relied upon by external callers
insights.problem: Scripts print machine-parsable data (e.g., <p:sldId>) and generate Office files consumed by other systems; changing output format or zip contents can break integrations.
insights.root_cause: External tooling or automation expects stable outputs and Office-compatible packaging conventions.
insights.solution: Preserve CLI output formats and ensure packed archives conform to Office ZIP/container expectations (file ordering, relationships, content types) and validate produced files against Office readers.

key_files.why: Primary entry for creating or duplicating slides in an unpacked PPTX; updates presentation manifests and emits the <p:sldId> element expected by callers.
Responsible for iterative reachability analysis and safe removal of orphaned slides, media and rels; crucial for keeping packages minimal and consistent.
Packs an unpacked Office folder into a ZIP-based Office file, runs validators/auto-repair and condenses XML — the final step that must produce Office-compatible archives.
Implements run-merging logic used to reduce run fragmentation in DOCX documents; affects text node consolidation and whitespace semantics.
Handles tracked-change merging by author and author inference; impacts provenance and redline presentation in DOCX.

construct_relationships: add_slide.py modifies slide XML files and must update presentation.xml, the slide-level .rels (e.g., ppt/slides/_rels/slideX.xml.rels), and the root [Content_Types].xml so the new slide is recognized.

clean.py computes transitive reachability by walking .rels chains starting from the presentation manifest and then removes files and rels not reachable; it must update presentation relationships and content-type overrides after pruning.

pack.py consumes an unpacked directory (potentially after modifications from add_slide.py or clean.py), optionally invokes validators and helpers (merge_runs/simplify_redlines) to condense/repair XML, and produces a ZIP archive conforming to Office container rules.

merge_runs.py and simplify_redlines.py operate on word/document.xml structures; their normalization output can be used by pack.py to reduce size and complexity before zipping and may be called independently by other scripts that manipulate DOCX.
Helpers in office/helpers are shared utilities: their transformations affect downstream packaging and any script that expects particular XML structures or rsid presence.
notable_relationships: These scripts operate on unpacked Office directories that may be produced or consumed by other parts of the productivity skillset; changes here affect any higher-level code that automates slide generation or document repair.
Outputs (packed .pptx/.docx files and printed <p:sldId> elements) are externally visible contract points — other processes or user workflows may consume them, so format stability is important.

The pack/clean workflows interact with Office application expectations (content types, rels, and file ordering) and therefore indirectly depend on Office compatibility requirements rather than only internal conventions.
These scripts will operate on user file trees (HERMES_HOME or user-supplied paths); file permissions, backups, and platform differences (path separators, symlinks) are cross-cutting concerns.
recommendations: Preserve CLI output formats and printed artifacts (e.g., the exact <p:sldId> string) because external tools/scripts may parse them.

When changing code that updates multiple manifests (.rels, presentation.xml, [Content_Types].xml), ensure operations are atomic: write to temp files and rename to avoid partial updates; add backups before destructive edits.
Maintain deterministic ID generation and check for collisions across the entire package before committing writes (slideId, relationship rId, content-type overrides).
Respect xml:space, existing whitespace, and tracked-change semantics when merging runs or collapsing redlines; verify render equivalence on sample documents.

Treat clean operations as conservative and idempotent: run iterative reachability analysis, skip deletion of unexpected/unknown rel targets unless explicitly confirmed, and provide safeties for external references.

Run pack-time validators/auto-repair on any changed package and verify the resulting archive opens in Office; unit/integration tests should include small sample PPTX/DOCX fixtures to assert no regressions in structure and content.
Be mindful of large files and memory use when processing media or zipping; prefer streaming I/O for packing and avoid loading large binary resources fully into memory.
Consider cross-platform filesystem differences (paths, symlinks, permissions) and ensure any path-manipulating logic normalizes inputs. Ensure tests exercise these edge cases where possible.

skills/mlops/training/grpo-rl-training.description: This folder contains a focused GRPO-based RL fine-tuning training skill that uses TRL and PEFT primitives to prepare data, define reward signals, configure a model with LoRA/PEFT, and launch a GRPOTrainer run. It is a self-contained training template (code + docs) intended as a reproducible starting point for RL training workflows and matters because it codifies dataset/prompt shape, reward definitions, and trainer configuration that downstream model artifacts and CI/dev workflows will rely on.
key_behaviors: Load and prepare a dataset and assemble conversational prompts (system + examples) into the format expected by the trainer.
Define and compose reward signals (correctness, format, incremental) that score generation outputs for GRPO optimization.
Configure model and tokenizer objects, including LoRA/PEFT wrappers and any adapter layers required by TRL.
Instantiate and run a GRPOTrainer loop that consumes prepared data and rewards, produces checkpoints, and persists a fine-tuned model.
Persist training artifacts and document usage via accompanying README/SKILL docs for reproducibility and handoff.

insights.title: Heavy runtime and extra-package requirements
insights.problem: The template relies on TRL, PEFT, tokenizer/model backends and possibly large optional extras that are not part of a minimal install.
insights.root_cause: RL fine-tuning stacks (TRL, PEFT) and large model/tokenizer binaries require optional extras and GPU-enabled runtime support which may not be present in default developer environments or CI.
insights.solution: Edits must preserve explicit import points and feature gates; assume the code will be executed only when the appropriate extras and hardware (GPU / CUDA) are available and validate via a smoke training run in that environment.

insights.title: Dataset/prompt format is an implicit contract
insights.problem: Downstream reward functions and trainer expect a specific dataset structure and prompt composition; changing the prompt or dataset shape can break reward computation or batching.
insights.root_cause: Reward functions and trainer input pipeline are tightly coupled to the exact tokenization/prompt layout produced by get_dataset and prompt construction.
insights.solution: When modifying code, preserve or explicitly adapt dataset output schema (fields, text templates, tokenization markers) and ensure reward functions are updated consistently and validated with sample inputs.

insights.title: Reward functions can introduce bias or instability
insights.problem: Incorrect reward scaling or mis-implemented incremental rewards can destabilize policy updates and produce poor training outcomes.
insights.root_cause: Reward composition (multiple signals) and their numeric ranges are not normalized by default and are applied directly by GRPOTrainer.
insights.solution: Treat reward functions as a contract: keep their signatures stable, document numeric ranges/expectations in SKILL.md, and validate reward outputs on unit examples before a full run.

insights.title: Checkpointing and saved format are important for downstream consumption
insights.problem: Saved adapters / LoRA / PEFT artifacts must be compatible with downstream load paths; mismatches break deployment or later fine-tuning.
insights.root_cause: Different PEFT/TRL versions or saving conventions produce incompatible artifact layouts.
insights.solution: Ensure saving/loading calls adhere to the expected PEFT/TRL API; preserve save paths and metadata, and if changing them, update README/SKILL.md and any consumers that load these artifacts.

key_files.why: The single training template that encodes dataset preparation, reward functions, model/tokenizer setup, LoRA/PEFT configuration, and the GRPOTrainer orchestration — the operational core of the folder.
Explains structure, prerequisites, and usage notes that are essential context for running or modifying the training template.
Documents workflow-level assumptions, reward semantics, and reproducibility concerns that editors must respect when changing behavior.

construct_relationships: get_dataset -> prepares examples and prompt strings consumed by the trainer's data loader and the reward functions.
prompt construction (system + examples) -> directly influences tokenization and correctness/format reward computations.
correctness_reward_func and format_reward_func -> produce scalar rewards per example that are composed/normalized and supplied to GRPOTrainer for policy updates.
setup_model_and_tokenizer -> returns a model and tokenizer (possibly wrapped with LoRA/PEFT) that GRPOTrainer uses for forward/backward passes and checkpointing.
main -> orchestrates dataset preparation, model setup, reward composition, trainer configuration, training loop execution, and saving artifacts.
notable_relationships: Relies on external libraries TRL and PEFT (and their installed versions) which are managed via project extras in pyproject.toml; mismatches in installed versions can break runtime behavior.

Produces model/adapter artifacts that are likely consumed by other components or deployment flows (tools, gateway integrations, or packaging); saved artifact layout must remain compatible with those consumers.
Expectations about resource availability (GPU, CUDA drivers) and large-model memory footprint imply this code interacts indirectly with Dockerfile/CI setup and developer environment scripts.
Dataset inputs and prompt templates may come from or be validated by other repo subsystems (data engineering or tests); altering them affects reproducibility and any downstream evaluation tooling.
recommendations: Preserve public function signatures (get_dataset, reward functions, setup_model_and_tokenizer, main) or adjust callers and documentation together when changing them.
When editing, validate locally with the same extras and hardware configuration intended for training (install TRL/PEFT extras, run a small smoke training to exercise full pipeline).
Do not change prompt/text schema or reward numeric scaling without updating SKILL.md/README and unit tests that assert expected reward behavior.
Keep save/load semantics and artifact directory conventions stable; if changing them, update documentation and any consumers that load these artifacts.
Run a minimal end-to-end smoke test (single-batch training step) after edits to catch tokenization, reward, or checkpointing regressions before larger runs.

skills/productivity/ocr-and-documents/sc.description: This folder contains CLI-style helper scripts that perform OCR and document extraction for the productivity/ocr-and-documents skill: one model-backed, layout-aware wrapper around marker-pdf and one lightweight extractor using pymupdf/pymupdf4llm. These scripts are entry-level utilities that produce machine-friendly outputs (markdown or JSON), extract and persist images and tables, and therefore act as bridges between on-disk documents, optional model artifacts, and other agent components that consume parsed document content.
key_behaviors: Model-backed OCR pipeline: verify disk/model requirements, invoke marker-pdf model(s) for OCR and layout-aware extraction, optionally run LLM-assisted parsing, persist extracted images, emit structured output as markdown or JSON.

Lightweight extraction pipeline: open documents via pymupdf, produce per-page text or markdown, extract tables to pandas DataFrames (and render as markdown), extract page images to an output directory, and report document metadata.
Shared output and persistence behavior: both scripts save embedded images to a specified directory and emit human- and machine-readable textual outputs intended for downstream parsing or display.

Dependency and environment checks: both scripts expect optional external packages and check resources (disk/installed models) before performing heavy operations, providing a lower-overhead path when those are absent.

insights.title: Heavy optional dependencies and alternate lightweight fallback
insights.problem: marker-pdf and model-backed flows require large model files and non-default Python packages which may not be present in all environments.
insights.root_cause: The model-backed script relies on optional, heavy dependencies and local model artifacts; the repo uses extras for large features.
insights.solution: Treat marker-pdf/model usage as optional at runtime; ensure code paths clearly detect and gate on missing packages/models and provide the lightweight pymupdf alternative or descriptive error messaging.

insights.title: Disk space and model-installation risks
insights.problem: Downloading/installing marker models or saving many extracted images can exhaust disk space or fail silently.
insights.root_cause: Marker-backed extraction can trigger model downloads and both scripts persist potentially large image assets without global quotas.
insights.solution: All flows must check available disk capacity before initiating model installs or large saves and surface deterministic failure modes rather than partial writes.

insights.title: Stable, machine-parseable outputs are consumed downstream
insights.problem: Downstream components expect predictable JSON/markdown structures and image layout; ad-hoc changes can break consumers.
insights.root_cause: Scripts emit both human-facing markdown and structured JSON and save images using directory/name conventions that other code assumes.
insights.solution: When editing, preserve or intentionally version the output schema and image naming/directory conventions; any change must consider downstream parsers and skill integrations that read these artifacts.

insights.title: Long-running model/OCR operations and environment constraints
insights.problem: Model-backed OCR can be slow and may run into runtime limits in CI, containers, or interactive sessions.
insights.root_cause: Large model inference and PDF parsing of big documents are resource- and time-intensive operations.
insights.solution: Edits must respect that operations may be long and should not assume synchronous, quick completions; callers or tests should mock or guard heavy operations and consider streaming/progress semantics.

insights.title: Persisted extracted content may contain sensitive data
insights.problem: Extracted text, tables, and saved images are written to disk and could be inadvertently exposed or persisted across runs.
insights.root_cause: Scripts default to saving extracted artifacts to directories on the host filesystem.
insights.solution: Treat output directories and saved artifacts as sensitive: preserve existing naming/location behavior, document where outputs go, and ensure callers or orchestration layers handle retention, permissions, and backups appropriately.

key_files.why: Primary model-backed OCR and layout-aware extraction wrapper; performs model checks/downloads, LLM-assisted extraction, image saving, and emits markdown/JSON output—central for heavy extraction workflows.

Lightweight document extraction utilities using pymupdf/pymupdf4llm; provides fast per-page text, markdown conversion, table extraction to pandas, image extraction, and metadata reporting as a fallback or quick path.

construct_relationships: Both scripts produce textual output (markdown and/or JSON) that downstream skills or tools will parse; they should maintain compatible output shapes.
Both scripts extract and write images to an output directory using predictable naming/directory semantics; consumers likely read those image paths alongside the textual output.
extract_marker.py includes preflight disk/model checks before performing heavy work; extract_pymupdf.py is used as a lighter-weight path when heavy dependencies or models are absent.
Table extraction in extract_pymupdf.py produces pandas DataFrames which are formatted to markdown for output; this implies a dependency on pandas or an alternative formatting path.
notable_relationships: These scripts depend on optional third-party libraries and model artifacts (marker-pdf, local model files, pymupdf, pymupdf4llm, pandas) which are installed via extras in the project; CI and packaging may omit them by default.

Output artifacts (markdown/JSON and image files) are likely consumed by higher-level skills, tools, or the agent UI/gateway; changes to output schemas or file locations can affect downstream consumers across the skill and tools directories.
Disk usage and HERMES_HOME/data layout matter: model downloads and saved images will interact with the project's configured data directory and container mounts (Dockerfile, deployment scripts).

Runtime environment constraints (CI blanks API keys and optional extras; Docker images may not include optional model binaries) mean these scripts must be tolerant to missing credentials/artifacts and operate in degraded mode or fail with clear messages.
recommendations: Preserve the public output formats (markdown/JSON schema) and image naming/directory conventions unless downstream consumers are updated in lockstep; treat output schema changes as breaking.
When adding or modifying model-backed flows, retain explicit disk/model preflight checks and ensure operations fail fast with clear messages if models/dependencies are unavailable.
Guard heavy operations behind dependency checks or feature flags so edits do not cause CI or lightweight environments to attempt model downloads or long-running inference.
Keep table extraction behavior compatible with pandas-based formatting; if pandas becomes optional, maintain a clear fallback and ensure tests mock DataFrame outputs.

Treat saved artifacts as potentially sensitive and respect configured data paths (HERMES_HOME or configured output dirs); do not hardcode global paths and ensure permission/retention implications are considered in changes.
Tests touching these scripts should mock external libraries, model downloads, and file-system side effects (large files, image writes); avoid running heavy model inference in unit test runs.

website/src/pages/skills.description: This folder implements the Skills Hub page for the Docusaurus-powered website: a client-side React UI that lets users browse, search, filter, paginate and inspect skill metadata and installation hints. It couples presentational CSS modules with interactive components (dashboard, cards, stat widgets) and is intended to run as a static site page with client-side interactivity enabled after hydration.
key_behaviors: Client-side loading and rendering of a skills catalog into a dashboard view with cards and statistics.
Interactive, incremental filtering and keyword search with result highlighting and ranked presentation.
Category and source-based faceted filtering via sidebar controls and source pills.
Pagination of skill results with UI controls and internal state to manage current page and page size.
Skill detail preview and generation of installation hints/commands shown on cards or detail panels.
Styling via a CSS module to scope page-specific styles (hero, searchInput, card).

insights.title: Client-side filtering over full catalog can be expensive
insights.problem: Filtering, searching, highlighting and pagination are all performed in the browser, which can be slow or memory-heavy for large skill datasets.
insights.root_cause: The page performs all data transformations and search/highlight logic client-side on the full skills array rather than server-side or using incremental loading/virtualization.
insights.solution: When modifying filtering or search, preserve or introduce strategies to limit CPU/memory impact (e.g., batch processing, debounce input, pagination limits, or virtualized lists) and test with large catalogs to ensure responsiveness.

insights.title: Search highlighting risks incorrect rendering or XSS if not escaped
insights.problem: Highlighting matches likely injects markup or wraps matched substrings; if match content comes from untrusted metadata it could result in unsafe HTML or broken escape sequences.
insights.root_cause: Highlighting implementation manipulates skill text at render time without guarantees about escaping vs innerHTML usage.
insights.solution: Any changes to highlightMatch or rendering must ensure matches are escaped and use safe React text nodes rather than injecting raw HTML; test with atypical/edge metadata values.

insights.title: CSS module class coupling with JSX
insights.problem: Component markup depends on specific class names exported by styles.module.css; renaming classes breaks layout and visual behavior.
insights.root_cause: Styles are scoped via a CSS module and referenced directly in component code; there is a tight coupling between the class keys used in TSX and the CSS file.
insights.solution: Any edits to class names or file structure must keep the referenced keys in sync; when refactoring styles, update all references in components and verify the static site build and styles on hydration.

insights.title: Pagination and filters are maintained in component state
insights.problem: Current pagination and filter state may not be reflected in the URL, so reloads or deep links are not preserved and may break expected user flows.
insights.root_cause: Filtering and pagination are implemented as client-side state without explicit URL sync or deep-linking support.
insights.solution: When changing pagination/filter behavior, be aware of whether state should persist across reloads; if so, ensure state serialization (query params or local storage) is preserved and tested during SPA and initial render.

insights.title: Install hints are presentation-only but represent side effects
insights.problem: Install command text or 'install hint' UI may imply interactions with the CLI or package manager that live elsewhere; broken or stale command strings can mislead users.
insights.root_cause: The page sources static or generated install hints from skill metadata; logic that formats those hints must stay consistent with CLI tooling and package naming conventions.
insights.solution: When editing or reformatting install hints, ensure the string format matches downstream tooling expectations and verify sample flows (copy/paste) and any link targets are correct.

key_files.why: Primary React page implementing the Skills Dashboard: search, filters, skill cards, highlight logic, pagination and install hints — the main entry point to modify UI/behavior.
Scoped CSS module that defines the visual layout and component classes (hero, searchInput, card) used by the React components; changes affect page styling and layout.

construct_relationships: SkillsDashboard (index.tsx) renders a collection of SkillCard components and StatCard widgets to present metadata and statistics.
SkillCard instances use highlightMatch to emphasize query matches within skill title/description and also display install hints derived from skill metadata.
Index-level pagination and filter controls update the dashboard's local state which in turn selects which SkillCard elements are rendered.
JSX markup in index.tsx imports class names from styles.module.css and relies on those keys for layout and styling.
notable_relationships: This Docusaurus page is a static site route and must be compatible with static build + client-side hydration semantics of the overall website.

Skill metadata shown here is likely supplied by a centralized source (static JSON, MDX frontmatter, or generation pipeline) — changes to display expectations should consider that upstream source format.
Install hints and any links shown should stay consistent with the CLI and packaging conventions elsewhere in the repository (downstream consumer: hermes CLI and packaging scripts).
CSS module changes may interact with global theme CSS or Docusaurus layout wrappers; ensure class scopes do not conflict with site-level styles.
recommendations: Preserve component exports and the default page export so Docusaurus continues to recognize the route.
Maintain the CSS module keys referenced by TSX or update all references together; run the site build/dev server to verify styles after edits.

When modifying search/filter/highlight logic, validate against large datasets and with edge-case metadata values (special characters, HTML-like content) to avoid performance regressions and XSS risks.
If pagination or filters are changed, confirm expected behavior on reloads and deep links (test SPA navigation and initial renders).
Test copy/paste or link behaviors of install hints to ensure they remain accurate with CLI tooling; verify any visible command strings for correctness.
Run the Docusaurus dev server and a production build after edits to ensure hydration, static export, and styling behave as expected.

skills/mlops/cloud/modal.description: This folder contains the Modal-specific MLOps skill documentation used by the skills subsystem to guide serverless GPU deployments on the Modal cloud platform. It centralizes an entrypoint guide (SKILL.md) plus focused reference material on advanced usage patterns (containers, volumes, web endpoints) and troubleshooting, and therefore acts as the canonical operational playbook for deploying and debugging Modal-hosted workloads.
key_behaviors: Provide a step-by-step deployment walkthrough for running GPU workloads on Modal (entrypoint usage and expectations).
Document advanced runtime configuration: container image choices, persistent volumes, and exposing web endpoints / ingress on Modal.
Capture operational failure modes and troubleshooting workflows tied to Modal platform interactions, resource constraints, and runtime logs.

insights.title: Single authoritative skill guide
insights.problem: SKILL.md serves as the primary user-facing guide but depends on more detailed reference docs; inconsistencies between them can confuse operators.
insights.root_cause: Split content model: high-level steps are separated from advanced configurations and troubleshooting, so parallel edits can drift.
insights.solution: Ensure cross-references remain accurate and that the entrypoint succinctly points to the advanced and troubleshooting references; treat SKILL.md as the canonical flow while keeping details in references.

insights.title: GPU and resource constraints
insights.problem: Modal deployments rely on serverless GPU availability and specific resource configurations which may fail or behave differently across regions and instance types.
insights.root_cause: Cloud platform resource limits, preemption, or mismatched instance type/driver requirements are not always visible from deployment commands.
insights.solution: Documentation must explicitly surface required GPU types, driver/runtime compatibility, and the operational implications (cold starts, quotas); troubleshooting should map symptoms to resource-related causes.

insights.title: Container images, volumes and persistence assumptions
insights.problem: Advanced usage sections describe containers and volumes; incorrect assumptions about volume persistence and container layering can break deployments or data availability.
insights.root_cause: Serverless container semantics and ephemeral filesystem behavior differ from traditional VMs; volumes and mounts require explicit configuration.
insights.solution: Reference material must clarify which filesystems are ephemeral vs persisted and include the exact configuration knobs required to mount volumes and retain data between invocations.

insights.title: Web endpoint and networking risks
insights.problem: Expose-on-web-endpoint instructions risk public exposure or mistaken authentication assumptions.
insights.root_cause: Web endpoint examples may default to open or incomplete security configurations without emphasizing access controls and secrets management.
insights.solution: Docs should call out authentication, ingress restrictions, and secrets handling expectations so operators know which credentials/config files to secure and rotate.

insights.title: Environmental drift & observable signals
insights.problem: Troubleshooting guidance must map errors to actionable signals (logs, exit codes, Modal dashboard events) otherwise reproducing issues is difficult.
insights.root_cause: Lack of explicit mapping between symptoms and platform-specific observability sources results in ad hoc debugging.
insights.solution: Troubleshooting references should enumerate where to find logs, what common error messages mean, and the minimal reproducible steps to surface telemetry.

key_files.why: Primary user-facing guide that outlines the canonical serverless GPU deployment flow on Modal and acts as the skill entrypoint.
Contains detailed instructions on non-trivial configurations (containers, volumes, web endpoints) that operators will follow after the entrypoint guide.
Captures common failure modes and debugging workflows tied to Modal infrastructure and is essential for operational recovery and support.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references and delegates detailed configuration and failure diagnostics to the files in references/ (advanced-usage.md and troubleshooting.md).
advanced-usage.md prescribes runtime constructs (container images, mounted volumes, web endpoints) whose failure modes are diagnosed in troubleshooting.md.
Troubleshooting guidance expects the deployment configurations described in SKILL.md/advanced-usage.md to be present and consistent (log locations, config keys, secrets).
notable_relationships: External Modal cloud platform (APIs, GPU instance types, quotas, dashboards) — documentation assumes availability and semantics of Modal's runtime and must remain aligned with platform changes.
Credentials and secrets management used by deployments — docs must align with the project-wide secret handling patterns and any skills/credential stores.

Impact on skills subsystem and deployable artifacts — changes to these docs affect how skills are packaged and deployed by the agent and operators, and may require coordination with tooling or CI that performs builds.
recommendations: When editing, preserve and verify cross-references between SKILL.md and the reference docs so the high-level flow and detailed steps remain consistent.
Validate any example commands, runtime flags, and resource/type names against the current Modal API/CLI versions and include the expected platform context (region, GPU type) in examples.
Be explicit about where operators should obtain logs, error messages, and telemetry; edits should not remove mappings from symptoms to observability sources.
Retain or clearly state security guidance around web endpoints and secrets; do not leave credential-handling instructions ambiguous or with insecure defaults.

Consider the downstream consumers of these docs (skills packaging, CI deploys, and operators) — ensure edits don't change required configuration keys or artifact names without coordinating corresponding code/tests.

skills/mlops/evaluation/lm-evaluation-ha.description: This folder contains the skill-level documentation and operational guidance for integrating and running the external lm-evaluation-harness within the project's MLOps/evaluation subsystem. It codifies CLI/flag usage, environment and credential expectations, custom task schema, benchmarking procedures, API-driven invocation patterns, and distributed evaluation practices. These artifacts are authoritative guidance relied on by automation, CI, scheduled jobs, and humans running reproducible model evaluations.
key_behaviors: Documenting how to invoke the lm-evaluation-harness (CLI flags, environment variables, credentials) so automation and humans run evaluations consistently
Defining the contract and schema for custom evaluation tasks so they can be discovered, loaded, and executed by harness-driven pipelines
Describing benchmarking metrics collection, result reporting, and reproducibility practices used by downstream tooling and CI
Prescribing API-driven evaluation patterns and constraints (authentication, rate limits, request/response shape) for programmatic runners
Explaining distributed evaluation orchestration concerns (sharding of tasks, node coordination, resource constraints, and aggregation of results)

insights.title: Custom task contract is a fragile integration point
insights.problem: Custom task definitions and example snippets are the canonical contract other code and automation consume; mismatches cause runtime failures or silent evaluation errors.
insights.root_cause: Multiple codepaths and automation rely on a small, implicit task schema documented only in this folder; there is high coupling between docs examples and actual loader/runner behavior.
insights.solution: Treat the files as the authoritative specification: any changes to task formats, example code, or CLI flags must be reconciled with code that discovers/loads tasks and with automation that invokes the harness.

insights.title: Credential and environment variable handling is risk-prone
insights.problem: Docs show how to configure API keys and credentials; incorrect edits could reveal sensitive values or change names expected by automation/CI.
insights.root_cause: Environment variable names, credential file paths, and CLI flags are referenced here and used by automated runners and CI; they are a source of coupling.
insights.solution: Keep env var names, examples that show placeholders (not real keys), and credential handling guidance stable and ensure any edits preserve the same variable names and semantic expectations used by automation.

insights.title: Reproducibility depends on pinned invocation patterns
insights.problem: Benchmarks and reproducible runs rely on specific CLI flags, seed handling, and environment set-up; changes to documented invocation or omitted config details will break reproducibility.
insights.root_cause: Operational guidance (flags, seed, data paths, dependency/version notes) lives only in these docs and is referenced by CI and scheduled jobs.
insights.solution: Ensure invocation examples and benchmark workflows explicitly state required flags/vars and align with actual runner behavior; preserve these invocation patterns unless coordinated with automation owners.

insights.title: Distributed evaluation introduces coordination and result-aggregation risks
insights.problem: Distributed guidance involves sharding tasks, node coordination, and merging results; inconsistencies cause partial or duplicated results and resource contention.
insights.root_cause: Distributed workflows span multiple machines/processes and depend on shared conventions (naming, checkpointing, metrics export) documented here.
insights.solution: When working around distributed eval docs, always verify that orchestration tooling and aggregation pipelines adhere to the documented sharding and checkpointing conventions.

insights.title: API-driven evaluation must handle provider constraints
insights.problem: API evaluation guidance must account for rate limits, retry/backoff, and response variability; failing to follow these constraints leads to failed runs or unexpected costs.
insights.root_cause: The docs show patterns for programmatic invocation; they implicitly assume particular provider behaviors and limits which are enforced outside the repository.
insights.solution: Respect documented API patterns, rate-limiting guidance, and authentication flows when invoking or modifying API evaluation examples; treat them as operational contracts with external providers.

key_files.why: Primary entrypoint document for the skill — summarizes usage, operational workflows, and links to all reference guides that automation and engineers will consult.
Defines the schema and examples for custom tasks; changes here affect task discovery, loaders, and any automation that dynamically imports tasks.
Describes benchmarking workflows and metrics collection that downstream reporting and CI expect to consume in a stable format.
Explains API-driven invocation patterns and auth requirements used by programmatic runners and integrations; impacts runtime behavior when followed.
Covers sharding, orchestration, and aggregation conventions that distributed runners and jobs must adhere to for correct results.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md aggregates and references the detailed guides in the references/ directory and acts as the canonical entrypoint for human and automated consumers.
custom-tasks.md defines the task schema that API-driven evaluation examples (api-evaluation.md) and benchmark workflows (benchmark-guide.md) assume when invoking the harness.
benchmark-guide.md expects reproducible invocation patterns described in advanced-usage.md (CLI flags, seed handling) and references custom task output formats for metrics collection.
distributed-eval.md depends on conventions from advanced-usage.md for environment setup and on benchmark-guide.md for how partial results should be aggregated into final reports.
api-evaluation.md maps to operational constraints (credentials and rate-limit handling) referenced in advanced-usage.md; together they form the runtime contract for programmatic evaluators.
notable_relationships: This documentation is a dependency for automation elsewhere in the repo (CI fixtures, scheduled cron jobs, and MLOps pipelines) that invoke lm-evaluation-harness; changes here can break those callers.

The content must remain compatible with the external lm-evaluation-harness package and any pinned upstream versions referenced by project packaging; mismatch between docs and installed harness behavior is a risk.
Credential names and environment variable conventions referenced here cross-cut with the repository's global credential/secret management and CI settings (CI blanks API keys by design).
Benchmark outputs and metrics described here are consumed by downstream telemetry/reporting systems and may be expected by CI or result-aggregation tooling in other folders.
Distributed evaluation guidance touches infrastructure concerns (compute nodes, GPUs, cluster orchestration) that are configured outside this subtree (deployment scripts, Dockerfile, scheduler/cron).
recommendations: Treat the markdown files as authoritative operational contracts: when editing, verify any change against automation, CI, and orchestration that reference these docs.
Preserve environment variable names, CLI flags, and example task signatures unless you coordinate corresponding code/automation updates.
Avoid embedding real credentials or secrets in examples; ensure placeholders remain clearly non-sensitive and consistent with repository-wide naming.
When altering examples that show invocation, include explicit flags/seed/version notes because downstream reproducibility and CI depend on them.
If changing any distributed evaluation conventions or output formats, validate that aggregation and reporting pipelines (and any scheduled jobs) still consume the altered outputs.

skills/mlops/evaluation/weights-and-bias.description: This folder contains the Weights & Biases (W&B) skill documentation and focused reference guides for the MLOps evaluation skill. It centralizes operational guidance for experiment tracking, artifact management, framework integrations, and hyperparameter sweeps that other MLOps skills and runtime code may rely on. These docs are authoritative for how the agent and user-facing skills should interact with the W&B service and its SDK.
key_behaviors: Describes end-to-end experiment tracking workflows (initialization, logging, run lifecycle, and teardown) for W&B within MLOps evaluation tasks.
Defines artifact management patterns (creating, storing, versioning, and retrieving artifacts) tied to model training/evaluation outputs.
Documents framework-specific integrations and code snippets to wire W&B into common ML frameworks (Transformers, PyTorch Lightning, etc.).
Explains hyperparameter sweep configuration and execution semantics, including sweep strategies, concurrency, and results aggregation.
Acts as a canonical reference surfaced by the MLOps skill for runtime behavior and operator/user instructions related to W&B usage.

insights.title: API key handling is a cross-cutting operational concern
insights.problem: W&B usage requires API keys and/or login tokens which, if mishandled, can cause failed runs or secret leaks.
insights.root_cause: Documentation often includes examples of initializing W&B without emphasizing secure credential handling or CI sanitization.
insights.solution: Documentation must consistently reference secure patterns (environment variables, CI blanking) and avoid embedding secrets; any code examples should assume keys are injected by environment/config and note CI behavior where keys are deliberately blanked.

insights.title: Docs depend on specific W&B SDK and framework versions
insights.problem: Integration snippets (Transformers, Lightning) can break when SDK or framework versions change.
insights.root_cause: Integration guidance encodes API-level usage that evolves across package versions, but the docs do not self-validate against a pinned dependency matrix.
insights.solution: When editing text or examples, verify the examples align with the project's declared extras/pyproject or the pinned W&B SDK versions used elsewhere; flag versioned instructions where appropriate.

insights.title: Artifact lifecycle expectations affect downstream retrieval and storage
insights.problem: Artifact handling guidance (naming, paths, versioning) can be inconsistent with code that stores/retrieves artifacts, leading to failures to locate artifacts at runtime.
insights.root_cause: Docs and runtime code may evolve separately, producing mismatched conventions for artifact names, storage backends, or paths (e.g., local vs. cloud-backed artifact storage).
insights.solution: Treat artifact naming and storage conventions in these documents as authoritative for skills that interact with artifacts; check any edits against the codepaths and storage defaults (local paths, HERMES_HOME, container volumes) used by runtime components.

insights.title: Sweep guidance assumes certain compute and orchestration capabilities
insights.problem: Hyperparameter sweep instructions may assume the availability of parallel workers, distributed compute, or a W&B agent, which may not exist in target deployments.
insights.root_cause: Sweeps are documented at a conceptual level without explicit runtime constraints (e.g., concurrency limits, resource quotas, required background services).
insights.solution: Ensure any changes preserve explicit caveats about concurrency/resource assumptions and reference the mechanism by which sweeps are launched (local agent, cloud agents, CI), so downstream automation will honor available resources.

insights.title: Multiple reference docs form a single behavioral contract
insights.problem: SKILL.md references multiple sub-guides (artifacts, integrations, sweeps); inconsistent edits can create contradicting instructions for a single workflow.
insights.root_cause: Documentation is split across files by topic but used together in workflows, increasing the risk of diverging instructions when files are edited independently.
insights.solution: When modifying any one file, reviewers/agents must verify consistency across SKILL.md and the three reference files so the guidance remains coherent end-to-end.

key_files.why: Primary entrypoint that defines the skill purpose and links/anchors the more detailed reference topics; authoritative for how the skill is presented to users and other subsystems.
Contains artifact lifecycle conventions that are directly consumed by codepaths that persist or retrieve trained models, checkpoints, and evaluation outputs.
Holds framework-specific examples and integration notes that correspond to runtime adapters or example code in other folders (Transformers, Lightning, etc.).
