Documents sweep configuration and execution semantics which can affect orchestration code, CI jobs, and resource planning for distributed experiments.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references and aggregates the three reference guides (artifacts.md, integrations.md, sweeps.md) to present a unified W&B skill, so edits to SKILL.md must remain aligned with the sub-guides' content.

Integrations.md contains framework-specific code snippets that correspond to runtime adapters or example scripts elsewhere; the docs therefore implicitly map framework hooks to W&B SDK calls described in artifacts.md and sweeps.md.

Artifacts.md defines naming/versioning and storage conventions that sweeps.md expects when collecting run outputs and integrations.md assumes when logging model checkpoints; these conventions form a contract across the three references.
notable_relationships: This documentation subtree is upstream of runtime code that interacts with W&B (SDK calls in training scripts, skills that launch experiments, and tools that upload/download artifacts). Changes here can alter the expected behaviors of those code paths.

The instructions in these docs must be consistent with project packaging/extras (pyproject.toml) for optional dependencies such as wandb; mismatches may cause example code to fail during local development or CI.

CI and workflow configurations (which blank provider API keys in CI) are relevant to these docs: examples should reflect the CI pattern and callers should not assume keys are present in automated environments.

Deployment artifacts and container images (Dockerfile, HERMES_HOME mounts) determine where artifacts persist; the artifact docs must be coherent with those deployment defaults so runtime artifact retrieval works in containerized setups.
recommendations: When editing any file in this folder, check for and preserve cross-file consistency between SKILL.md and the three reference files (artifacts, integrations, sweeps).
Validate examples against the project's declared extras and pinned/expected W&B SDK versions; ensure snippet assumptions match installed dependency versions used by CI or developer environments.
Do not include or expose secrets/API keys in text or example code; assume credentials are provided via environment variables or CI/GitHub secrets and note CI blanking behavior where relevant.
Confirm artifact naming/storage conventions align with runtime defaults (container volumes, HERMES_HOME, and any code that persists artifacts) to avoid breaking retrieval paths.

When making changes that affect sweep behavior or resource assumptions, verify compatibility with available orchestration (local vs. cloud agents) and mention concurrency/resource expectations wherever relevant.

After edits, run any repository checks that validate docs/examples (link checks, linting) and, if examples are executable, run them in a controlled environment with appropriate dependencies to avoid regressions.

skills/mlops/inference/gguf.description: This folder contains operational documentation and guidance for using GGUF-formatted models within the MLOps/inference subsystem, with a focus on quantization for llama.cpp and the runtime behaviors engineers must follow when loading, running, and storing GGUF models. It serves as the authoritative behavioral contract for model conversion, runtime configuration (e.g., batching/speculative decoding), artifact packaging (embeddings/metadata), and known failure modes that downstream code and pipelines rely on.
key_behaviors: Guiding model conversion and quantization steps for llama.cpp-compatible GGUF artifacts (commands, expected inputs/outputs, and compatibility notes).
Prescribing runtime inference patterns and parameter trade-offs: speculative decoding, batching, and quantization settings used by Llama/LlamaCpp loaders.
Defining artifact packaging and metadata conventions for storing model binaries, embeddings, and derived outputs so downstream pipelines can locate and interpret them.
Documenting deterministic workarounds and failure modes (e.g., duplicate metadata key handling) that loading code and pipelines must implement or expect.

insights.title: Quantized GGUF and llama.cpp version tight-coupling
insights.problem: Quantized GGUF artifacts created for one llama.cpp/tooling version may not load or may perform incorrectly on other versions.
insights.root_cause: Format and loader/decoder assumptions change between llama.cpp releases and quantization toolchain revisions.
insights.solution: Treat version and conversion command details in the docs as part of the contract: record exact tool version, conversion flags, and expected loader version; ensure runtime code either pins or detects compatible versions before loading artifacts.

insights.title: Performance-sensitive parameters (speculative decoding, batching) have concrete tradeoffs
insights.problem: Incorrect or inconsistent runtime parameter choices can cause severe latency or degraded output quality when serving GGUF models.
insights.root_cause: Speculative decoding, batch sizes, and quantization settings interact with model size and hardware in non-obvious ways; defaults in docs must reflect tested ranges.
insights.solution: Documented recommended parameter sets and explicit trade-offs should be considered authoritative; any code or UI that exposes these parameters must align with the documented recommendations and fallback behaviors.

insights.title: Artifact schema and embedding/metadata expectations
insights.problem: Downstream pipelines can fail or misinterpret stored artifacts or embeddings if metadata keys, file layout, or naming conventions differ.
insights.root_cause: Lack of a strictly enforced schema or mismatched expectations between producers (conversion/packaging) and consumers (loaders/embedding pipelines).
insights.solution: The artifacts reference doc functions as the contract: edits must preserve schema semantics (key names, file locations, and embedding formats) or be coordinated with consumers so they can parse updated layouts.

insights.title: Duplicate metadata key failures during model load
insights.problem: Model loading can fail or behave inconsistently when GGUF artifacts contain duplicate metadata keys.
insights.root_cause: GGUF artifact generation or packaging can create duplicate keys; some loaders assume deduplication while others error or keep the first/last occurrence.
insights.solution: Troubleshooting guidance (e.g., 'kept once') is the expected behavior: loading code and packaging pipelines must implement or tolerate a deterministic deduplication policy matched by the docs.

key_files.why: Primary guide for GGUF quantization and llama.cpp conversion commands; this is the entrypoint engineers will read to produce compatible quantized artifacts.
Defines runtime patterns (speculative decoding, batching, quantization trade-offs) that inference runtimes and model loaders are expected to follow.
Specifies artifact layout, metadata keys, and embedding packaging conventions relied upon by storage and downstream embedding/consumption pipelines.
Contains known failure modes and deterministic workarounds (e.g., duplicate-key handling) that runtime loaders and CI/integration checks should mirror.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md prescribes the conversion and quantization commands that produce GGUF artifacts which are consumed by Llama/LlamaCpp model loaders referenced in advanced-usage.md.
advanced-usage.md maps runtime parameter settings (speculative decoding, batching) to expected runtime behaviors; inference code must implement those behaviors or adhere to the documented defaults.
artifacts.md defines metadata keys and file layout that embedding pipelines and persistence code expect when reading/writing GGUF artifacts and derived embeddings.
troubleshooting.md documents failure modes (e.g., duplicate metadata keys) and specifies deterministic handling (deduplication/keep-once) that model loaders and packaging scripts should mirror.
notable_relationships: Downstream dependency on agent model loaders (Llama/LlamaCpp adapters) — docs must reflect the loader capabilities and version constraints used by the runtime agent.

Artifact storage/persistence subsystem (HERMES_HOME or configured data directories) is an upstream/downstream concern: artifact naming and layout here must match storage and backup processes elsewhere in the project.
Conversion and packaging tools (external binaries or in-repo scripts) are implicit dependencies — documented commands assume particular tool versions and flags that affect runtime compatibility.
Embedding and downstream ML pipelines that consume artifacts depend on the metadata schema in artifacts.md; changes here ripple to embedding indexing, search, and model-serving components.
recommendations: When editing any document, verify and keep explicit the tool and loader version constraints (conversion tool, llama.cpp, and any helper scripts) so runtime code can match those versions.
Preserve documented parameter names, default values, and trade-offs for speculative decoding, batching, and quantization; any mismatch between docs and runtime code must be reconciled together.
Do not change artifact metadata keys, file layout, or embedding formats without coordinating code changes in loaders and downstream pipelines; update both docs and consumers atomically.

Ensure troubleshooting semantics (e.g., duplicate-key handling) match the behavior implemented by model loaders and packaging scripts — tests or validation tooling should assert the expected deduplication policy.

After edits, run or trigger the inference-related validation: load a representative GGUF artifact via the Llama/LlamaCpp adapter, run the documented runtime parameter sets (batching/speculative decoding), and validate artifact consumption by embedding/indexing pipelines.

skills/mlops/inference/obliteratus.description: This subtree contains the OBLITERATUS skill documentation and experiment templates for ablation/analysis of model refusals. It functions as a documentation-first experiment spec library: high-level guidance (SKILL.md), detailed methodological notes, analysis module descriptions, and YAML templates that are likely consumed by external experiment runners or orchestration tooling. It matters because these artifacts define experiment contracts, safety guidance, and configuration that downstream automation will rely on to run sensitive model-altering studies.
key_behaviors: Provide authoritative human-readable guidance for an ablation-style study that targets model refusals (SKILL.md, methods and analysis docs).
Supply machine-consumable experiment configurations as YAML templates that encode parameters, inputs, and analysis study descriptions.
Describe analysis modules and processing steps that downstream pipelines or experiment runners will need to implement or invoke.
Act as the single source of truth for experiment semantics, naming, and safety/ethical cautions associated with the OBLITERATUS study.

insights.title: Docs drive experiments and serve as informal API
insights.problem: The markdown guides and textual descriptions are used as the authoritative source for how experiments should be run, but they are not machine-enforced.
insights.root_cause: Separation between human-readable guidance and executable code: templates are provided, but there is no guarantee an external runner enforces or validates the documented semantics.
insights.solution: Treat the markdown and templates as specification documents; any edits must preserve semantics, naming, and required fields because downstream tooling expects them. Maintain alignment between prose and YAML schemas.

insights.title: YAML templates are implicit contracts for runners
insights.problem: Templates encode experiment parameters and structure; breaking changes can silently break automation or produce invalid runs.
insights.root_cause: No local executables or schema files in this folder that formally declare expected keys/types; templates are standalone examples.
insights.solution: When changing templates, assume they are parsed by external experiment orchestration; ensure backwards-compatible keys, preserve required fields, and document any semantic changes clearly in SKILL.md and methods guide.

insights.title: Study actively targets model refusal behaviors
insights.problem: The content aims to remove or ablate refusals — this can create safety, policy, or compliance hazards if executed without controls.
insights.root_cause: Research objective conflicts with safety/guardrails; the repo provides operational templates which could be executed in non-controlled environments.
insights.solution: Any edits must preserve existing safety warnings and clearly surface constraints and required safeguards. Consumers of these artifacts should be expected to run studies in isolated, auditable environments with appropriate approvals.

insights.title: Templates and methods must jointly define reproducible experiments
insights.problem: If templates and method descriptions diverge, results will not be reproducible or comparable across runs.
insights.root_cause: Documentation and config live in separate files and may drift independently.
insights.solution: Keep parameter names, defaults, and methodological steps aligned. If editing either prose or templates, update the other to retain a reproducible mapping from description to concrete configuration.

insights.title: External pipelines likely parse these artifacts
insights.problem: There is no indication in this folder of the exact runner or parsing logic, so changes may break consumer pipelines elsewhere in the repo.
insights.root_cause: This folder is focused on content rather than implementation; orchestration and parsers live outside the subtree.
insights.solution: Assume downstream code expects specific filenames, keys, and semantics. Preserve filename conventions and high-level structure or coordinate with the consumers when making changes.

key_files.why: Primary high-level description and guidance for the OBLITERATUS experiment; defines purpose, scope, and safety considerations that downstream users and automation will rely on.
Detailed methodological instructions for ablation strategies; maps experiment intents to concrete parameter choices that should match YAML templates.
Describes the analysis modules and expected processing steps (metrics, transformations) that external analysis code must implement or reference.
Machine-consumable config template for running an Abliteration experiment; likely parsed by experiment runners and therefore a contract artifact.
YAML template that specifies the structure of an analysis study; used to configure analysis pipelines and must remain compatible with downstream parsers.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references the methods-guide and analysis-modules documents to explain intent and safety expectations for experiments.
methods-guide.md supplies parameter semantics and expected procedure that map to keys and sections in the abliteration-config.yaml template.
analysis-modules.md enumerates analysis steps and outputs that analysis-study.yaml is intended to configure or trigger in downstream analysis pipelines.
The two YAML templates act as the machine-readable embodiment of the prose in SKILL.md and methods-guide.md and therefore must remain semantically aligned.
notable_relationships: Downstream experiment runners and orchestration code (outside this subtree) likely parse the YAML templates; breaking changes here can impact CI, scheduled jobs, or external MLOps tooling.
Analysis modules may depend on telemetry, stored trajectories, or experiment outputs produced by the core agent or persistence subsystem elsewhere in the repo (data directories, HERMES_HOME).

Safety, compliance, and review processes external to the codebase (organizational policies) are relevant because the study concerns removing model refusals; artifacts here must surface required approvals to operators.
recommendations: Preserve semantic compatibility: do not rename or remove YAML keys, template sections, or filenames without confirming consumers or adding explicit migration notes in SKILL.md.
Retain and surface all safety and ethical warnings present in SKILL.md and methods-guide.md; any edits must keep them prominent and unambiguous.
When adjusting prose that describes template parameters, update the corresponding YAML templates (and vice versa) to maintain reproducibility and avoid drift.

Assume external parsers validate template schemas; run any available validation or CI that exercises experiment parsing after edits, and identify where the templates are consumed before making breaking changes.

Coordinate with downstream MLOps/orchestration owners if you must change experiment contracts, and ensure experiment outputs remain auditable and reproducible (record seeds, versions, and provenance).

skills/mlops/inference/outlines.description: This folder holds the structured outline and reference guidance for MLOps inference patterns used by the project—covering backend trade-offs, production examples for structured output, schema-driven JSON generation patterns, and troubleshooting notes. These markdown outlines act as the canonical specification and operational runbook that downstream inference runtime code, validators, tests, and deployment documentation must honor.
key_behaviors: Define and document backend selection trade-offs (latency, memory, model compatibility) to guide runtime routing and deployment decisions.
Specify canonical production examples and input/output contracts (JSON extraction, classification, structured generation) that downstream code must implement and validate against.
Establish schema-driven output patterns (Pydantic models, JSON Schema) as the contract for model responses and downstream consumers.
Capture common failure modes and operational mitigations so operators and runtime code can map errors to corrective actions and observability signals.
Centralize guidance so that runtime implementations, CI tests, and runbooks remain consistent with intended inference behavior.

insights.title: Backend trade-offs are a functional constraint on runtime behavior
insights.problem: Different inference backends (Transformers, llama.cpp, vLLM, OpenAI) impose incompatible constraints (latency, batch sizes, memory, API semantics) that affect deployment choices and runtime routing.
insights.root_cause: Each backend exposes different performance and API characteristics; the same prompt or architecture may require different handling or fallbacks per backend.
insights.solution: Treat backend guidance as normative constraints: any change that affects runtime adapters, model routing, or resource allocation must preserve or explicitly update the documented trade-offs and ensure downstream code and deployment manifests are aligned.

insights.title: Examples serve as canonical contracts for structured outputs
insights.problem: Production consumers rely on example-driven contracts; mismatches between generated outputs and the documented examples lead to parsing failures and downstream breaks.
insights.root_cause: The outlines define expected JSON shapes and behavior patterns which runtime generation and post-processing must meet.
insights.solution: Consider the outlined examples as authoritative specifications: edits to examples or formats require concurrent verification of runtime generation logic, validators, and any integration tests that assert these contracts.

insights.title: Schema-driven patterns are the primary validation boundary
insights.problem: Without synchronized schema definitions and runtime validators, model outputs may pass through the system unchecked and cause silent failures.
insights.root_cause: The repository uses Pydantic/JSON Schema patterns to define robust parsing; disruptions occur when schema docs drift from implementation.
insights.solution: When editing JSON/schema guidance, ensure that corresponding Pydantic models, JSON Schema artifacts, and parsing logic elsewhere remain consistent; treat these outlines as the source of truth for validation expectations.

insights.title: Troubleshooting guidance maps to observability and error handling
insights.problem: Operational runbooks and troubleshooting notes identify common failure modes that runtime components and operators rely on to triage incidents.
insights.root_cause: Operational failure modes span model timeouts, memory exhaustion, malformed outputs, and provider-specific errors; these are described in the troubleshooting outline.
insights.solution: Any change that affects error semantics, timeouts, or output formatting must be reconciled with troubleshooting notes and with observability/alerting configurations to avoid mismatches between documented remediation steps and actual runtime behavior.

insights.title: Documentation acts as a contract across teams and CI
insights.problem: Changes to outlines without synchronized updates in code, tests, and deployment documentation can create hidden incompatibilities and increase debugging time.
insights.root_cause: These markdown files are treated as authoritative guidance for implementers and operators; they are often referenced by tests, runbooks, and deployment manifests.
insights.solution: Treat modifications to these files as requiring cross-cutting awareness: verify references, update CI/integration tests that assume the documented behavior, and notify stakeholders of contract changes.

key_files.why: Entry-level summary that frames the skill's scope and sets expectations for how the reference outlines should be used by implementers and operators.
Canonical comparison of inference backend options whose trade-offs directly constrain runtime adapters, routing logic, and deployment resource planning.
Houses the production examples that serve as canonical input/output contracts which downstream code, tests, and clients must implement and validate against.
Defines schema-driven patterns (Pydantic/JSON Schema) and parsing patterns that are the primary validation and typing contract for model outputs.
Operational runbook content mapping failure modes to mitigations; used by operators and by runtime code to align error handling and observability.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references and summarizes the intent of the reference documents and thus acts as the canonical connector between high-level intent and detailed guidance.
backends.md constrains runtime adapters and model-routing logic (e.g., adapter implementations must honor latency/memory/batch guidance documented for each backend).
examples.md defines concrete I/O contracts that json_generation.md then shapes into schema/Pydantic patterns used by parsers and validators.
json_generation.md and examples.md jointly define the validation boundary: parsing logic elsewhere must align with the schemas and examples to avoid runtime parsing errors.

troubleshooting.md maps observed failures (timeouts, malformed output, provider errors) back to backend characteristics and example/validation mismatches, closing the loop between operational signals and documented root causes.
notable_relationships: Downstream runtime code (inference server, model adapters, prompt builders, and tools) depends on these outlines as authoritative documentation; any behavioral or format change should be reconciled with these files.
CI/tests and integration suites may reference examples and schemas from this folder as fixtures or contract checks; changes here can break tests unless synchronized.
Deployment artifacts (container configs, resource allocations, and orchestrator manifests) rely on backend guidance for sizing and capability expectations.
Operator runbooks and monitoring/alerting playbooks should align with troubleshooting.md; otherwise incident response guidance will be inconsistent with observed runtime behavior.
recommendations: When editing any file here, verify which runtime components (inference adapters, parsers, validators) and CI tests depend on the documented formats or trade-offs and coordinate equivalent updates.
Preserve file paths and example identifiers used as canonical contract references unless you also update all code/tests that import or assert against them.
Treat schema and example changes as breaking changes: run unit and integration tests that validate generation/parsing, and update Pydantic/JSON Schema artifacts in code if formats change.
If backend-related guidance is modified, check deployment manifests and model-routing logic for compatibility with the new trade-offs (latency, memory, batching).
Ensure troubleshooting edits remain consistent with actual runtime error semantics and that observability dashboards / alerting rules reflect any change in error types or thresholds.

skills/mlops/models/audiocraft.description: This folder contains user-facing documentation for the AudioCraft model skill under the MLOps skills suite. It explains how to run, fine-tune (including LoRA and multi-GPU), and recover from errors when using AudioCraft; these docs are the authoritative operational guidance consumers and integrators will follow, so their accuracy and safety matter for any code or infra changes that touch AudioCraft workflows.
key_behaviors: Provide an overview and usage pattern for running AudioCraft-based audio generation within the MLOps skill
Document advanced workflows: model fine-tuning, LoRA adapters, and multi-GPU / distributed training configurations
Surface failure modes and step-by-step troubleshooting guidance for environment, dependency, and runtime issues
Act as the canonical reference that implementers and operators will consult before running heavy GPU workloads or modifying model-serving code

insights.title: High GPU / memory demands for training and generation
insights.problem: Instructions enable workflows (fine-tuning, multi-GPU) that require substantial GPU memory, which can cause OOMs or failed runs if resources are insufficient.
insights.root_cause: Advanced AudioCraft operations assume access to CUDA-enabled GPUs, sufficient VRAM, and possibly distributed training utilities; documentation often omits explicit minimums or fallback paths.
insights.solution: State and respect minimum hardware and memory requirements in docs, call out safe low-resource alternatives (small example configs) and warn consumers that running commands without adequate resources will fail or stall.

insights.title: Version and dependency sensitivity
insights.problem: AudioCraft and associated tooling (PyTorch, CUDA, fairscale/accelerate) are sensitive to exact versions; mismatches lead to runtime errors or silent misbehavior.
insights.root_cause: Upstream changes in model code, serialized checkpoint formats, or CUDA/PyTorch ABI cause incompatibilities between local environment and documented commands.
insights.solution: Treat the docs as tied to explicit package and CUDA versions, and ensure commands reference pinned or recommended versions and compatibility notes; troubleshooting guidance should map common errors to likely version problems.

insights.title: Checkpoint format and storage risks
insights.problem: Fine-tuning and multi-GPU workflows produce and consume large model checkpoints; incorrect handling (sharded vs monolithic, LoRA vs full weights) can cause load failures.
insights.root_cause: Multiple serialization formats and distributed checkpoint sharding strategies are used across tools; users may mix formats or place checkpoints in non-backed-up locations.
insights.solution: Explicitly document expected checkpoint formats, required checkpoint-loading flags, recommended storage locations, and backup/restore considerations; include notes on converting or consolidating sharded checkpoints if applicable.

insights.title: Commands may have destructive or expensive side effects
insights.problem: Examples for training or distributed runs may perform long-running operations, overwrite artifacts, or consume cloud/GPU credits without clear warnings.
insights.root_cause: Documentation contains runnable command lines for convenience but lacks runtime cost and side-effect disclaimers.
insights.solution: Ensure docs highlight that commands are potentially expensive/destructive, recommend dry-run or small-scale test runs, and instruct readers to verify paths, environment variables, and resource targets before execution.

insights.title: Troubleshooting as primary recovery path
insights.problem: Users will rely on the troubleshooting doc to recover from environment and runtime failures; incomplete troubleshooting steps increase the risk of misdiagnosis.
insights.root_cause: Troubleshooting guidance may not cover common root causes (driver mismatches, NCCL/distributed runtime issues, sample-rate mismatches) or may assume prior expertise.
insights.solution: Maintain structured troubleshooting entries mapping common error messages to probable causes and concrete checks (CUDA driver, torch.cuda.is_available(), NCCL env vars, sample rate/codec mismatches).

key_files.why: Primary user-facing guide for the AudioCraft skill — likely the first doc consulted for usage and integration patterns.
Contains advanced operational instructions (fine-tuning, LoRA, multi-GPU) that directly affect runtime and infra requirements.
Consolidates known failure modes and recovery steps; changes here impact how operators debug and recover runs.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references and aggregates advanced-usage.md and troubleshooting.md as subordinate operational guidance for users implementing or running AudioCraft workflows
advanced-usage.md codifies runtime invocation patterns (fine-tune / LoRA / multi-GPU) that will depend on environment configuration described in troubleshooting.md when failures occur
troubleshooting.md links back to specific commands or flags documented in advanced-usage.md and SKILL.md to guide corrective action
notable_relationships: This documentation ties into the broader mlops skills and model-serving code paths; code that alters CLI commands, training scripts, or checkpoint formats must keep these docs consistent.

Commands and dependency recommendations here depend on global packaging and extras (pyproject extras for GPU, torch, accelerate, fairscale) — mismatches between docs and installed extras can cause user failures.
Operational guidance assumes underlying infra: CUDA drivers, GPU nodes, storage for checkpoints (HERMES_HOME or configured artifact storage), and possibly orchestration (systemd/Docker/Kubernetes).
CI and unit tests intentionally avoid heavy GPU workloads; examples in these docs are not executed by default in CI, so documentation drift can occur if code is modified without updating docs.
recommendations: When editing any file in this folder, ensure version pins and package names reflect the actual dependencies used by the codebase (torch, CUDA, accelerate, fairscale, audiocraft).
Preserve and clearly label resource-costly commands; avoid introducing commands that assume privileged credentials or modify global system configuration without warning.
Keep checkpoint format descriptions and example load/save flags in sync with model training/serving code paths and with any scripts elsewhere in the repo that create or consume checkpoints.
Verify troubleshooting steps reference concrete checks (commands or environment variables) that are valid for the expected runtime (e.g., torch.cuda.is_available(), nvidia-smi output, NCCL settings).
When changing wording or examples, consider that readers may copy-paste commands — ensure paths, environment variable names, and flags are safe and do not expose secrets or hard-coded absolute paths.

skills/mlops/models/segment-anything.description: This folder contains the skill-level documentation for integrating and operating the Segment Anything Model (SAM) as an mlops model within the Hermes Agent skills subsystem. It functions as the authoritative how-to, advanced usage notes, and troubleshooting reference for implementers who wire SAM variants (SAMv1, SAMv2, Grounded SAM, batching strategies, etc.) into the runtime and CI/CD pipelines.
key_behaviors: Provides operational guidance for selecting and invoking different SAM variants (SAM2, Grounded SAM) within the mlops skill context
Documents integration patterns and runtime considerations (batching, input/output formats, dependency and environment requirements) used by implementers and automation scripts
Contains diagnostic steps and known failure modes to aid debugging and environment stabilization during model deployment and inference

insights.title: Rapid model variant divergence
insights.problem: Multiple SAM variants (SAMv1, SAMv2, Grounded SAM) have differing APIs and I/O expectations which can lead to mismatches between documented examples and runtime code.
insights.root_cause: Upstream model releases change function signatures, input formats (masks, points), or pre/postprocessing semantics without consistent backward compatibility.
insights.solution: State explicit compatibility notes and reference concrete version pins in docs; include clear examples of the expected I/O shape for each variant so integrators can align runtime adapters and tests.

insights.title: Hardware and performance assumptions
insights.problem: SAM is resource intensive and examples may assume GPU availability, certain CUDA/cuDNN versions, or specific batch sizing — absent those, inference will fail or be impractically slow.
insights.root_cause: Large vision models and batching behavior depend on GPU memory, driver versions, and installed binary wheels (e.g., torch+CUDA, Triton) which vary across developer and CI environments.
insights.solution: Document minimum hardware/software prerequisites, example batch sizes for common GPU classes, and fallback behaviors (CPU/low-memory) to set correct expectations and avoid silent failures.

insights.title: Ambiguous input/output formats
insights.problem: Integrators may not know the exact pixel coordinate conventions, expected image encodings, or mask tensor layouts required by SAM adapters, producing incorrect results.
insights.root_cause: High-level examples omit low-level specifics (coordinate system origin, dtype, normalization) that are critical for deterministic inference.
insights.solution: Include precise I/O contracts (image dtype/shape, coordinate units, mask encoding) and small canonical examples showing raw input and expected output shapes/formats.

insights.title: Common runtime failures and remediation
insights.problem: Threads, memory spikes, OOMs, or mismatched dependency binary wheels surface during inference, and without clear remediation steps, operators may spend time guessing fixes.
insights.root_cause: Heavy reliance on native binaries (PyTorch, CUDA) and operational subtleties (worker processes, multiprocessing start methods) create fragile runtime behavior.
insights.solution: Provide concrete troubleshooting steps including environment checks, driver/version commands, reproducible minimal repros, and recommended process settings (e.g., multiprocessing settings) to accelerate debugging.

insights.title: Model weight availability and license constraints
insights.problem: Using or distributing SAM weights may be subject to licensing or hosting constraints not captured in ad-hoc examples, risking non-compliant deployments.
insights.root_cause: Model checkpoints are large and sometimes gated; examples that omit weight sourcing or license references leave integrators unaware of compliance obligations.
insights.solution: Call out where to obtain weights, any applicable license terms, and recommended storage/access patterns (local cache paths, HERMES_HOME conventions) for reproducible deployments.

key_files.why: Primary skill guide: entry-point documentation that implementers will read to understand how SAM is expected to be used within the skills subsystem.
Holds advanced integration patterns (SAM2, Grounded SAM, batching) that define non-trivial runtime behaviors and adapter requirements.
Captures known failure modes and remediation steps; essential for operators and CI maintainers when tests or deployments fail.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md defines the high-level contract and expected usage flows that advanced-usage.md expands into concrete integration patterns (e.g., batching, variant selection).

advanced-usage.md describes runtime behaviors (batching, grounded prompts) that must align with the code path in the mlops model skill implementation and any image preprocessing or postprocessing utilities.
troubleshooting.md maps observed runtime errors back to environment, dependency, or model-version causes described at a higher level in SKILL.md and advanced-usage.md.
notable_relationships: These docs must align with the skills implementation under skills/mlops/models (code not in this folder) and with image/tooling utilities elsewhere (e.g., tools/image preprocessing).

Operational guidance interacts with platform/deploy concerns: Dockerfile, HERMES_HOME data storage conventions, and CI (tests) that may need pinned versions or environment variables referenced in the documentation.
Hardware and binary dependency notes tie into broader repo constraints (pyproject extras, optional dependencies, and Docker build steps that install CUDA/PyTorch/Playwright).
recommendations: When editing these files, preserve explicit version references, example commands, and exact I/O contracts so runtime adapters remain synchronized with documentation.
Verify any example commands, paths, environment variables, and weight locations against the actual skill code and CI configurations; update them atomically with code changes that alter expectations.
Ensure troubleshooting entries include reproducible checks (commands or small snippets) and reference where to find relevant logs; do not assume hidden global state.

Consider cross-references: when you change wording about resource requirements or compatibility, also verify downstream readme, CI, Dockerfile, and any packaging metadata that enforce those constraints.

Keep license and weight-sourcing statements intact and prominent; any change that affects how weights are obtained or stored should account for legal and storage implications (HERMES_HOME paths, cache conventions).

skills/mlops/models/stable-diffusion.description: This folder holds the Stable Diffusion skill documentation used by the mlops models subsystem. It centralizes user-facing instructions, advanced usage patterns (adapters, extensions, hyperparameters), and practical troubleshooting guidance so integrators and operators can invoke and operate Stable Diffusion pipelines reliably within the Hermes ecosystem.
key_behaviors: Documenting skill intent, invocation surface, inputs/outputs and expected artifacts for a Stable Diffusion image-generation skill.
Describing advanced usage patterns: adapters, extensions, and configuration knobs that change model behavior and resource needs.
Capturing operational failure modes and remedial steps (environment, dependencies, GPU/runtime issues) for reproduceable deployments.

insights.title: Single authoritative skill doc
insights.problem: Consumers depend on a central SKILL.md for how to call and configure the Stable Diffusion capability; inconsistencies across docs will cause incorrect usage.
insights.root_cause: Split material across a top-level skill doc and multiple reference pages increases the chance of drift between example code, flags, and operational notes.
insights.solution: Treat SKILL.md as the canonical invocation surface and keep advanced/troubleshooting pages synchronized; ensure examples and parameter names match implementation and CI docs to avoid user confusion.

insights.title: Heavy external dependencies and runtime surface
insights.problem: Advanced adapters and techniques referenced (e.g., LoRA/ControlNet/quantization) likely require additional libraries, model weights, and GPU resources that cause runtime failures when absent.
insights.root_cause: Advanced techniques depend on external artifacts (weights, drivers, native libs) and optional Python extras not present in minimal installs or CI environments.
insights.solution: Document required extras, weight locations, and fallback behaviors clearly; ensure troubleshooting documents enumerate missing-dependency symptoms and non-destructive remediation steps.

insights.title: GPU/driver and resource failure modes
insights.problem: Users will encounter GPU memory, CUDA, or driver mismatches that manifest as cryptic runtime errors when following examples.
insights.root_cause: Model inference examples assume compatible hardware, drivers, and container configuration that may not exist on all hosts or CI runners.
insights.solution: Reference expected hardware/driver matrix and provide CPU-fallback or low-memory configurations in docs; ensure troubleshooting covers how to detect and isolate driver vs model-weight issues.

key_files.why: Canonical skill documentation and primary integration surface for callers and operators.
Contains advanced adapters/techniques and configuration patterns that change runtime dependencies and expected artifacts.
Operational troubleshooting guidance covering environment, dependency, and runtime failure modes for the skill.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references examples and invocation parameters that are expanded with technique-specific notes in references/advanced-usage.md.

Advanced usage content presumes the runtime behaviors and failure modes documented in references/troubleshooting.md, creating a dependency where advanced configurations require corresponding troubleshooting steps.
Troubleshooting entries map observable errors back to configuration or dependency items described in SKILL.md and advanced-usage.md (e.g., missing weights, adapter mismatch, driver errors).
notable_relationships: Upstream: This documentation must remain consistent with mlops/models implementation code (model pipelines, adapter modules, weight-management) elsewhere in the repository; edits here may need to be matched to code or packaging changes.
Upstream: References to optional extras, native libraries, or Docker settings tie into repository-wide packaging (pyproject extras, Dockerfile) and CI behavior (blanked API keys and minimal runners).
Downstream: Callers from CLI, gateway, or other skills will rely on the SKILL.md contract for arguments, outputs, and artifact locations; changing docs without code changes will break integrators.
Operational: Large model weights and GPU requirements affect CI, Docker builds, and developer workflows; these operational constraints are material when editing examples or recommended commands.
recommendations: When editing files here, verify that all parameter names, CLI options, and example commands match the actual model/adapter code and pyproject extras; coordinate edits with implementation changes.
Do not introduce examples that assume heavy artifacts (large weights or GPU-only features) exist unless you also include explicit download/installation steps and note resource requirements.
Update or cross-check Dockerfile, packaging extras, and any weight-storage/paths referenced elsewhere in the repo if documentation changes reference installation or runtime paths.
Ensure troubleshooting steps reference detectable error signatures and non-destructive fixes; changes should not remove or obscure recovery instructions for dependency/driver issues.

Run or validate against the minimal developer workflow (install with dev/all extras and local invocation) and ensure examples are reproducible without privileged CI resources; avoid edits that require unreproducible host setups.

skills/mlops/models/whisper.description: This folder holds the documentation for the Whisper-based ASR/translation skill within the mlops models skills area. It documents expected usage, supported languages and operational guidance that downstream code, packaging, and runtime orchestration rely on to invoke Whisper correctly. These docs are the authoritative human-facing source of how the skill should be configured and what audio inputs/outputs and dependencies are expected.
key_behaviors: Describe how to invoke the Whisper ASR/translation skill (inputs, outputs, modes: transcription vs translation).
Enumerate supported languages and map user-facing names to Whisper language codes and usage caveats.
Document runtime/environment requirements (model size choices, CPU/GPU trade-offs, system deps such as ffmpeg) and operational notes for deploying the skill.

insights.title: Docs are the primary contract for skill usage
insights.problem: Skill consumers (CLI, gateway platform adapters, or automated tests) may rely on text instructions; gaps or inconsistencies between docs and implementation can cause runtime failures or incorrect invocations.
insights.root_cause: The folder contains only human-readable guidance files (SKILL.md and languages.md) but no embedded test vectors or machine-checkable schema, so drift between code and docs is likely.
insights.solution: Treat these files as the authoritative human contract: any code change that affects invocation signature, language mappings, or expected outputs must be accompanied by updates to these docs and any downstream references; ensure examples and language lists remain synchronized.

insights.title: Heavy native/system dependencies required for audio processing
insights.problem: Whisper-based skills typically require native binaries (ffmpeg) and large ML frameworks (torch, CUDA) which are often not obvious to users and can cause runtime errors if missing or mismatched.
insights.root_cause: Documentation-only presence means install/runtime steps may be omitted or treated as implicit by maintainers.
insights.solution: When editing these docs or touching related skill code, ensure the README/skill doc clearly lists required system packages, recommended model sizes for CPU vs GPU, and any packaging extras; coordinate with packaging (pyproject extras, Dockerfile) and CI so the runtime environment described matches what is installed.

insights.title: Language list must match model capability and invocation parameters
insights.problem: If languages.md lists unsupported languages or incorrect language codes, transcriptions/translation may silently fail or produce incorrect results.
insights.root_cause: Whisper uses specific language codes and model behaviour differs across languages; documentation that omits edge cases (e.g., detection vs forced language, mixed-language audio) will mislead integrators.
insights.solution: Keep the languages.md mapping precise (code ↔ human name), call out detection limitations, and highlight any special handling required (forced-language parameter, fallback behaviours). Any code that uses language identifiers must expect only the documented set and gracefully handle unknown/unsupported codes.

key_files.why: Primary skill documentation describing how to use the Whisper ASR/translation skill, its modes, and runtime expectations — serves as the contract for integrators.
Enumerates supported languages and their codes; changes here affect how callers select language parameters and how the skill handles detection/fallback.

construct_relationships: The skill documentation describes the invocation contract that agent skill registration and skill runners elsewhere in the codebase must implement (inputs: audio files/streams, parameters; outputs: text, language metadata).

languages.md provides the canonical mapping that callers (CLI flags, gateway adapters, or skill wrappers) should translate user language choices into model parameters expected by the Whisper invocation.
Operational notes in SKILL.md implicitly relate to packaging and runtime layers (Dockerfile, pyproject extras) that must provide system dependencies and model/runtime libraries.
notable_relationships: This folder is upstream of skill loader/registry code elsewhere in skills/ and the agent runtime; documentation here informs how those components present options to users or set default parameters.

Packaging and deployment artifacts (pyproject.toml extras, Dockerfile, scripts/install.sh) must supply the system-level dependencies referenced by these docs (e.g., ffmpeg, torch/CUDA), otherwise runtime errors will occur.
Gateway/platform adapters and CLI commands that expose speech features will reference the language mapping and invocation patterns documented here and must remain consistent with it.

Tests and CI do not typically run heavy audio-model workloads; any changes to documented behaviours should consider adding or updating lightweight integration checks or mock expectations elsewhere to avoid silent regressions.
recommendations: Any code edits that change the skill's invocation signature, accepted audio formats, language codes, or runtime parameters must be accompanied by corresponding updates to these docs and verification of downstream callers (CLI flags, gateway adapters, skill registry).

When editing documentation content, preserve explicit notes about required system dependencies (ffmpeg, torch/CUDA), recommended model sizes for CPU vs GPU, and any environment variables or HERMES_HOME considerations used for storing outputs.

Respect the canonical language-to-code mapping in references/languages.md; confirm that callers validate or canonicalize user input to these documented codes and that unknown codes are handled gracefully.
Be cautious about changing examples or sample commands — they are used by integrators and operators; ensure sample command lines reflect current packaging and runtime (extras, Dockerfile).

Run or update any related integration tests or add lightweight mocks for audio paths when changing docs that will affect runtime behaviour, and coordinate with packaging to ensure dependencies described in the docs are available in runtime images.

website/docs/developer-guide.description: This directory contains developer-facing reference material that documents Hermes internals, extension points, and on-disk formats used by contributors and integrators. It codifies the contracts between core subsystems (agent loop, providers, gateway/ACP, storage, cron, skills and tools) and describes formats and invariants that must be preserved when changing code. These docs are a single-source-of-truth for implementers adding providers, tools, memory plugins, or integrating with the gateway and RL workflows.
key_behaviors: Describes end-to-end agent runtime flows: how incoming messages are handled by the gateway/ACP, routed into the agent loop, and how model responses and tool invocations are processed and persisted.
Specifies extension points and integration contracts for pluggable components: inference providers, memory provider plugins, tools, and SKILL.md-based skills.
Explains data formats and persistence semantics: session storage schema, trajectory file formats, memory lifecycle and compression/caching interactions used by telemetry and RL.
Documents scheduled automation behavior and environment constraints: cron subsystem internals and developer environments used for Atropos RL workflows.
Captures internal component responsibilities and interoperability expectations: prompt assembly, caching, context compression, model metadata and credential rotation.

insights.title: ACP/gateway session contract must match implementation
insights.problem: Documentation about the ACP adapter, session bridge, and gateway internals can diverge from code as the adapter or session serialization evolves.
insights.root_cause: The ACP protocol and gateway session handling span multiple components and change independently (adapter, bridge, platform adapters).
insights.solution: Treat ACP/gateway docs as tightly coupled to acp_adapter and gateway/platforms implementations; verify examples, field names and lifecycle diagrams against code and update protocol examples whenever the adapter/server signatures change.

insights.title: Prompt assembly, context compression, and cache are interdependent
insights.problem: Mismatch between documented prompt assembly and actual prompt_builder/prompt_cache/context_compressor behavior can cause malformed prompts or unexpected token usage.
insights.root_cause: Responsibilities for composing system + context + tools, compressing older turns, and deciding what is cached are distributed across multiple modules.
insights.solution: When modifying prompt shape, cache keys, or compression heuristics, ensure docs describing prompt layers and token accounting are updated and reflect provider-specific constraints and routing behavior.

insights.title: Adding a provider requires coordination with credential and routing models
insights.problem: Docs that explain how to add providers may omit required hooks like model metadata, credential pool usage, or routing preferences that the runtime depends on.
insights.root_cause: Provider adapters must integrate with ModelMetadata, CredentialPool and smart routing logic which are not centralized in a single file.
insights.solution: Document the exact adapter interface, expected metadata fields, environment variable names and rotation behavior; confirm examples align with the credential pool and model routing logic used by the agent.

insights.title: Session storage and trajectory formats are schema-sensitive
insights.problem: Schema drift between session-storage.md, trajectory-format.md and the actual DB / file writer/reader leads to incompatibilities and failed migrations or broken telemetry ingest.
insights.root_cause: Session storage and telemetry formats are consumed by multiple subsystems and may evolve without coordinated versioning or migration instructions.
insights.solution: Keep schema versions and migrations documented alongside changes; when altering stored shapes, update the session storage docs, trajectory docs, and include migration/compatibility notes to avoid runtime data corruption.

insights.title: Memory provider and tool plugin contracts must be explicit and stable
insights.problem: Plugins that deviate from expected retrieval/persistence semantics or lifecycle hooks cause subtle bugs in memory recall and tool invocation.
insights.root_cause: Plugins interact with the MemoryManager and tool invocation pipeline but may be developed separately without test coverage of integration points.
insights.solution: Ensure the memory-provider-plugin and adding-tools docs enumerate the exact API surface, concurrency expectations, side effects, and error-handling semantics so implementers can conform to runtime expectations.

insights.title: Cron/Atropos environments can produce nondeterministic behavior if env constraints are unclear
insights.problem: Cron internals and RL environment docs may omit environment variable or filesystem assumptions (HERMES_HOME, node/playwright, platform creds) that affect reproducibility.
insights.root_cause: Scheduled jobs and RL workflows depend on runtime environment setup that differs from interactive CLI or gateway setups.
insights.solution: Document required environment variables, default HERMES_HOME locations, and any external prerequisites; treat cron semantics and RL environment docs as operational constraints to validate during changes.

key_files.why: Describes the core runtime flow and modes of the agent — changes here reflect how messages traverse the system and how subsystems interact.
Documents prompt composition, caching and token accounting which must stay aligned with prompt_builder, prompt_caching, and context_compressor implementations.
Defines session persistence schema and DB expectations used by gateway, agent sessions, and telemetry; critical for storage compatibility.
Explains the ACP adapter and session bridge — any protocol or API changes to acp_adapter must be reflected here.
Guides provider adapter authors and must match ModelMetadata, CredentialPool and smart_model_routing behavior in the codebase.

construct_relationships: Prompt assembly (prompt-assembly.md) relies on context compression and prompt caching to limit tokens; it must be coherent with prompt_builder, prompt_caching, and context_compressor implementations.

Agent loop (agent-loop.md) is the central orchestrator: it accepts sessions from gateway/ACP, invokes model provider adapters, executes tools, and delegates persistence to session storage and the MemoryManager.

ACP/gateway bridge (acp-internals.md and gateway-internals.md) converts external platform messages to internal sessions and back — these sessions are persisted according to session-storage.md and consumed by the agent loop.

Trajectory format and telemetry docs (trajectory-format.md) feed RL workflows and Atropos environments; recorded trajectories are produced by the agent loop and stored alongside session and memory data.

Adding-providers and memory-provider-plugin docs describe contracts consumed by credential pool, model metadata, and MemoryManager; providers and memory plugins interact indirectly via the agent's routing/persistence layers.
notable_relationships: This docset must stay consistent with agent/ (prompt_builder, prompt_caching, context_compressor, memory_manager, smart_model_routing), gateway/ (platform adapters, api server), and acp_adapter/ code — changes in those folders should trigger doc updates.

Session storage and trajectory schemas intersect with persistence code, migration scripts, and HERMES_HOME configuration used by Docker and systemd deployments; ops and packaging must be considered when schemas change.

Provider and plugin docs reference environment variables and credential naming conventions (API keys, credential pools) that are enforced by CI and runtime; CI blanking of API keys may hide issues during doc/code sync.

Cron/Atropos environment docs touch runtime prerequisites (node/playwright, external bridges) that are also defined in packaging/Dockerfile and install scripts; mismatches can break scheduled jobs or e2e tests.
recommendations: When editing docs that describe APIs, formats, or protocols, cross-check against the corresponding source files (acp_adapter, gateway/platforms, agent/*, tools/*) to keep names, field shapes, and examples accurate.

If a change affects persistence shapes (session storage, trajectories, memories), ensure the schema version/migration implications are reflected in docs and that storage consumers are updated in lockstep.
For provider, plugin, or skill integration docs, verify environment variable names, credential usage and routing expectations match CredentialPool/ModelMetadata and smart routing implementations.
Ensure examples and CLI commands documented here are runnable against the current project layout (entry points in pyproject.toml) and adjust if packaging/entrypoints change.

Consider running the unit test suite and relevant integration/e2e tests (including gateway/ACP flows) after changes that touch protocols, storage formats, or prompt assembly semantics to validate end-to-end compatibility.
Preserve operational notes (HERMES_HOME defaults, external deps like Playwright/Node, and cron assumptions) when editing scheduling or environment docs so deployments and CI remain reproducible.

website/docs/guides.description: This folder contains user-facing guides and tutorials that explain common workflows, integrations, and advanced features of Hermes (CLI, gateway, automation, plugins, skills, and platform-specific setups). These markdown files and a category metadata file define site navigation and are intended to stay in sync with runtime APIs, platform adapters, and configuration artifacts referenced by the rest of the codebase.
key_behaviors: Provide step‑by‑step tutorials and conceptual explanations for integrating Hermes with platforms (Telegram, voice, MCP) and running automations (cron, daily bots).
Document developer-facing APIs and extension points (Python AIAgent API, plugin/skill lifecycle) that must reflect code-level contracts.
Control site navigation and grouping via a category metadata file that determines ordering and collapsibility of the Guides & Tutorials section.
Link to external configuration artifacts and runtime components (SOUL.md, local LLM tooling, MCP server integration), creating dependencies between docs and code/config.

insights.title: Multiple guides appear duplicated
insights.problem: Several files are marked as duplicate entries (e.g., plugin, daily briefing, delegation, migrate-from-openclaw), which can cause confusing navigation and stale or conflicting content.
insights.root_cause: Parallel authoring or copies retained in the folder without canonicalization; the site builder will treat each file as a separate page unless collapsed by metadata.
insights.solution: Treat duplicates as non‑canonical until reconciled: avoid programmatic link changes that assume a single canonical path, and preserve frontmatter/filenames so the site builder and external links remain valid until duplicates are intentionally consolidated.

insights.title: _category_.json controls Guides nav and ordering
insights.problem: Changing files without updating the category metadata can break expected ordering, label, or collapse state in the site sidebar.
insights.root_cause: Site navigation is driven by the _category_.json properties (label, position, collapsible, collapsed) rather than inferred from filenames alone.
insights.solution: When renaming, adding, or removing guide files, ensure the category metadata is considered and left consistent with intended navigation behavior; respect position and collapsible flags to avoid unexpected sidebar changes.

insights.title: Guides reference runtime APIs and constructs (AIAgent, skills, tools)
insights.problem: Tutorials that document code-level usage (python-library, work-with-skills) can become incorrect if code APIs change, leading to broken examples or misleading instructions.
insights.root_cause: Docs live separately from implementation and rely on named constructs (AIAgent, chat, run_conversation, skill_list, skill_view) that may evolve.
insights.solution: Assume these pages are consumers of code contracts: any edit to examples or references should be cross-checked against the corresponding runtime APIs and examples in agent/ and tools/ to keep them valid.

insights.title: Guides depend on configuration files and external services
insights.problem: Guides that instruct configuring SOUL.md, MCP servers, local LLMs, or voice platforms implicitly assume specific file paths, environment variables, and runtime capabilities that may differ in deployments.
insights.root_cause: These tutorials document setup steps that cross repository boundaries (config files, platform adapters, system packages like Playwright or native LLM toolchains).
insights.solution: Treat referenced files and external services as fragile integration points: edits must preserve or clearly annotate file paths, required env vars, and platform prerequisites so instructions remain actionable.

key_files.why: Defines the Guides & Tutorials sidebar grouping, order, and collapsed state — changes here affect site navigation for all contained guides.
Documents the AIAgent API and code usage patterns (AIAgent, chat, run_conversation) that should align with the agent/ code; high risk for API drift.
Explains discovery, installation, and UI elements (skills, skill_view, skill_list) tied to the skills subsystem; edits can impact user expectations about skill behavior.
Platform integration tutorial for Telegram that depends on gateway/platform adapters and credential management; changes may imply alterations to runtime configuration steps.
Voice mode guide references cross-platform features and optional binary dependencies (TTS/STT); editing should preserve platform-specific prerequisites and compatibility notes.

construct_relationships: The _category_.json (label/position/collapsible) governs how individual guide pages are presented together in the site sidebar; filenames and frontmatter map to those entries.

python-library.md references AIAgent, chat, and run_conversation which map conceptually to agent-level constructs (agent/AIAgent, prompt builder, model adapters) and must reflect their public API surface.
work-with-skills.md documents 'skills', 'skill_view', and 'skill_list' which correspond to the skills subsystem and UI representations served by the gateway/CLI components.

Platform-specific guides (team-telegram-assistant.md, use-voice-mode-with-hermes.md) reference gateway/platform adapter behavior and configuration files (credentials, env vars), implying a dependency on gateway/platforms/* implementations.

Integration guides (use-mcp-with-hermes.md, use-soul-with-hermes.md) point to external configuration artifacts (MCP servers, SOUL.md) that are consumed by runtime tooling and tooling in other folders (tools/*, gateway/*).
notable_relationships: Docs are coupled to code modules: edits to API examples require verification against agent/, gateway/, and tools/ to avoid documentation inconsistency.
Site navigation (_category_.json) affects how users discover these guides and can break inbound links from marketing or README references if paths or labels change.

Guides instruct installing and configuring optional system-level dependencies (Playwright, local LLM toolchains, native voice tooling); these have cross-folder build/deploy implications and may be documented elsewhere (Dockerfile, scripts/install.sh).

Environmental assumptions (HERMES_HOME, API keys, credential files like SOUL.md) are referenced in guides and are governed by repository-level defaults and deployment scripts; changing docs without aligning those defaults can create mismatch for end users.
recommendations: When renaming, adding, or removing guide files, update or verify website/docs/guides/_category_.json to preserve sidebar ordering and collapse behavior.
Preserve existing file paths and frontmatter (if present) until external links and site references are audited; changing slugs can break inbound links and CI doc references.
Any edits that change API examples (AIAgent, run_conversation, skills interfaces) must be cross-checked against the corresponding implementation files in agent/ and gateway/ to prevent drift.
Do not remove or canonicalize duplicated files without ensuring all internal and external links are updated; maintain duplicates as-is if other parts of the repo or documentation reference them.
After edits, run the static site build or local docs server and link-checker to validate rendering, relative links, anchors, and sidebar behavior across the Guides & Tutorials section.

plugins/memory/honcho.description: This folder implements the Honcho-backed cross-session memory plugin for Hermes. It provides configuration and CLI setup, a client resolver/singleton, a session manager that caches and flushes conversation turns to Honcho (sync or async), and a MemoryProvider that exposes Honcho-backed tools and injects prefetched context into prompts. It matters because it controls persistent, cross-session memory behavior (scoping, syncing cadence, and lifecycle) which directly affects privacy, consistency, and agent context completeness.
key_behaviors: Configuration resolution and client lifecycle: determine Honcho settings from HERMES_HOME/legacy files/env, normalize modes, and lazily create a singleton Honcho client.
CLI-driven enable/disable/setup: interactive and scripted commands that create peers, persist honcho.json under HERMES_HOME, and clone/sync host blocks across profiles.
Session lifecycle and caching: create/retrieve conversation sessions, maintain a local cache of messages/prefetch context, and keep per-session synced/unsynced state.
Flush and sync strategies: flush writes to Honcho either synchronously or via an async writer with a drain-on-shutdown mechanism; support retries and graceful shutdown semantics.

Tool exposure and prompt injection: expose Honcho-backed tool schemas (profile, search, context, conclude) for agent tool invocation and prefetch/inject context into system prompts based on configured cadence and scoping.

insights.title: Peer/session scoping must avoid cross-user memory leakage
insights.problem: A static peer_name in configuration can cause multiple gateway users to share the same Honcho peer and thus shared memory.
insights.root_cause: Default/static peer naming without considering gateway user_id or profile context.
insights.solution: Scope Honcho peer/session names using gateway user_id when present (or profile-specific names). Any edits must preserve the per-user scoping behavior to avoid leaking memories across users.

insights.title: Configuration precedence and normalization
insights.problem: Multiple sources (HERMES_HOME honcho.json, legacy files, env vars) and mode normalization must be resolved deterministically.
insights.root_cause: Honcho config can be provided from different locations/formats and recall/observation modes need canonicalization.
insights.solution: Maintain the established precedence (HERMES_HOME -> default/legacy -> env), keep normalization of modes, and ensure client construction follows the same resolution order so behavior is predictable across environments.

insights.title: Async flushing introduces shutdown and consistency risks
insights.problem: Asynchronous writers may lose in-flight messages if not drained correctly, and synchronous flushes block agent progress.
insights.root_cause: Use of async queueing for performance without guaranteed shutdown/drain semantics.
insights.solution: Preserve and test graceful shutdown/draining logic; keep explicit modes for async vs sync flush behavior and ensure retry/backoff semantics are intact to avoid lost or duplicate writes.

insights.title: CLI writes configuration into HERMES_HOME with profile awareness
insights.problem: Improper read/write of honcho.json can lead to inconsistent plugin enablement across profiles or corrupted config states.
insights.root_cause: CLI routines create/update honcho.json and manage per-profile cloning/enabling; concurrency or path resolution errors can cause incorrect persistence.
insights.solution: Respect HERMES_HOME path resolution and profile keys when reading/writing honcho.json; preserve atomic write semantics and error handling used by existing setup/enable/disable commands.

insights.title: Honcho SDK dependency may be optional/missing
insights.problem: Runtime or CLI operations assume the Honcho SDK is available; absent SDK can break setup or memory provider activation.
insights.root_cause: Optional plugin-level dependency and runtime checks done by CLI/ensure helpers.
insights.solution: Retain checks/guards that verify SDK availability and the helper flows that guide installation or fail gracefully so runtime code paths do not raise unexpected import errors.

key_files.why: Defines HonchoMemoryProvider, tool schemas, and the integration points used by the agent to access Honcho-backed memory and inject context.
Resolves configuration precedence and constructs the Honcho client singleton; other modules call get_honcho_client and depend on its normalization behavior.
Manages per-conversation sessions, local caching, flush strategies (sync/async), retry and shutdown semantics — core to data consistency with Honcho.
Provides interactive and programmatic setup, persisting honcho.json under HERMES_HOME, enabling/disabling the plugin per profile, and creating peers — affects runtime configuration and onboarding.
Declares the plugin to the host system (metadata/registration) and may be referenced by plugin discovery or packaging tooling.

construct_relationships: HonchoMemoryProvider (plugins/memory/honcho/__init__.py) calls get_honcho_client() from client.py to obtain a singleton Honcho client for session creation and memory operations.
HonchoMemoryProvider uses HonchoSessionManager (session.py) to create/retrieve HonchoSession instances, manage local caches, and orchestrate flush semantics.
CLI handlers (cli.py) read and write honcho.json under HERMES_HOME; client.from_global_config (client.py) consumes that file to build HonchoClientConfig used by get_honcho_client().

HonchoSession._flush/_flush_session (session.py) invokes methods on the Honcho client (provided by client.py) to persist messages; the async writer and _ASYNC_SHUTDOWN coordinate graceful draining on provider shutdown.
Tool schemas (ALL_TOOL_SCHEMAS, PROFILE_SCHEMA) defined in __init__.py are exposed to the agent/tool-dispatch system; the provider relies on these schemas to surface Honcho-backed capabilities.
notable_relationships: Environment and filesystem: HERMES_HOME and honcho.json are central — changes affect persisted configuration and must respect Docker/volume mounts and backup strategies used by the host.
Gateway/agent integration: provider behavior uses gateway user_id for peer scoping — changes can impact multi-user deployments (gateway platforms in gateway/platforms/*).
Optional dependency: Honcho SDK is an external dependency checked/ensured by CLI; missing SDK affects both interactive setup and runtime. Edits must consider import guards and graceful failure.
Tooling/agent core: the plugin exposes tool schemas consumed by the core agent tool invocation machinery; renaming or removing schemas will break agent-tool routing unless synchronized.
Testing/CI expectations: CI blanks API keys; integration/e2e tests are separated — changes relying on live Honcho interactions need appropriate test isolation or mocks.
recommendations: Preserve configuration precedence and normalization: retain HERMES_HOME -> legacy -> env ordering and canonicalization of recall/observation modes when editing client resolution code.

Respect per-user and per-profile scoping: do not alter peer/session naming semantics without ensuring gateway user_id and profile names continue to scope Honcho peers to avoid cross-user memory exposure.

Maintain async flush and shutdown semantics: any edits to session flushing must keep the async drain-on-shutdown behavior, retry/backoff handling, and the option for synchronous flush to avoid data loss.
Keep client as a lazy singleton: other modules rely on get_honcho_client() creating/returning a single configured client; preserving lazy creation avoids lifecycle and import-order issues.

When changing CLI persistence, ensure atomic read/write to honcho.json under HERMES_HOME and preserve per-profile keys; test enable/disable/setup flows and file permissions in environments with mounted volumes.
Guard against missing Honcho SDK: retain installation checks or import guards used by CLI/setup flows so runtime does not fail on import in environments without the SDK.

Run targeted tests after edits: unit tests covering config resolution, CLI read/write, session creation, flush (sync and async), and graceful shutdown; integration tests against Honcho should remain optional and isolated (CI uses blanked keys).

website/docs/reference.description:
{
  "description": "This folder contains the canonical reference documentation used by the Hermes website: CLI commands, environment variables, configuration references (MCP), skill catalogs, tools reference, and command subsets (profile/slash). It provides the stable, human-facing contract between the codebase (runtime flags, env vars, skills, tools) and operators/users, and is consumed by the site generator (category file and Markdown pages) to build the reference section of the documentation site.",
  "key_behaviors": [
    "Expose authoritative, human-readable descriptions of runtime interfaces (CLI commands, slash/profile commands) and operational controls (environment variables, MCP config).",
    "Document the inventory of runtime extensions and capabilities (core and optional skills, built-in tools) and caveats related to their use.",
    "Provide examples and configuration snippets intended to be directly copy-pasted into deployments (MCP examples, environment usage).",
    "S

plugins/memory/holographic.description: This subtree implements a holographic structured-memory plugin: a single-user SQLite-backed fact store augmented with HRR (holographic reduced representation) phase-vector utilities, a hybrid retrieval pipeline, entity resolution, trust scoring, and session-integrated auto-extraction. It matters because it supplies persistent, reproducible, and compositional memory representations (text + algebraic vectors) that the agent prefetches, queries, and updates — so changes here can materially alter retrieval relevance, correctness, and persistence compatibility across agent runs.
key_behaviors: Deterministic phase-vector encoding and algebra (encode atoms, bind/unbind, bundle) to produce reproducible HRR vectors for tokens, entities, and facts; serialization/deserialization for storage.
Persistence of structured facts, entities, and metadata into a SQLite store with FTS5 full-text indexing, HRR vector columns, trust scores, timestamps, and entity links.

Hybrid retrieval pipeline that produces candidate facts (FTS5), reranks by Jaccard and optional HRR similarity, applies trust-weighting and temporal decay, and supports compositional HRR probes and contradiction detection.

Session lifecycle integration: hooks to prefetch relevant facts, optionally auto-extract facts when sessions end, expose tools/schemas for fact storage and feedback, and record user feedback to adjust trust.

insights.title: Schema and serialization are persistent contract surfaces
insights.problem: Stored facts, entities and HRR vectors are persisted in SQLite using explicit schema and serialized byte formats; changing schema or vector serialization will invalidate existing data or break cross-process reproducibility.
insights.root_cause: Binary HRR vector serialization and table schema are consumed across runs and by retrieval code; no implicit migration path is described in the plugin metadata.
insights.solution: Treat the SQLite schema and HRR serialization format as stable public contracts: preserve backward-compatible serialization, add explicit versioning/migration steps if formats change, and ensure retrieval/deserialize code remains compatible.

insights.title: HRR encoding must remain deterministic
insights.problem: Holographic encoding produces phase vectors that other processes expect to be identical; nondeterministic encoding would lead to mismatched similarity scores and broken compositional probes.
insights.root_cause: HRR algorithms rely on deterministic token/atom encodings and operations; changes to hashing, RNG seeds, vector dimensionality, or ordering would break repeatability.
insights.solution: Preserve encoding algorithm parameters and any seeding/hashing strategy; if changes are unavoidable, provide a migration strategy or differentiate encodings by version tag stored with vectors.

insights.title: Hybrid ranking combines orthogonal signals (FTS, Jaccard, HRR, trust, decay)
insights.problem: Multiple signals (FTS candidates, Jaccard reranking, optional HRR scores, trust weighting, and decay) interact nonlinearly; inadvertent modifications to weighting or ordering can meaningfully alter retrieval outputs and downstream agent behavior.
insights.root_cause: FactRetriever composes several heuristics and numeric weights; trust feedback and temporal decay create closed-loop effects on future ranking.
insights.solution: Maintain explicit, test-covered contracts for score composition and the ordering of retrieval stages; when changing any component, validate end-to-end ranking on representative cases and ensure trust/decay semantics remain consistent.

insights.title: SQLite single-user semantics and heavy maintenance tasks
