insights.problem: Operations like rebuilding category banks, writing HRR vectors, or schema migrations can be expensive and conflict with concurrent access; uncoordinated writes or long transactions risk locking or data corruption in single-file SQLite.
insights.root_cause: MemoryStore is a single-user SQLite store (with FTS5) that supports writes for extraction, feedback, and rebuilds; these operations may run in session hooks or background tasks.
insights.solution: Respect SQLite transactional semantics and exclusive locks: keep transactions bounded, serialize rebuilds/maintenance tasks, and ensure callers handle transient locking errors. Tests should exercise concurrent read/write patterns.

insights.title: Entity extraction/linking is coupled to fact identity
insights.problem: Entity resolution heuristics and link tables determine how facts are associated with entities; modifying extraction heuristics or entity key formats can fragment or duplicate entity links and impair multi-entity probes.
insights.root_cause: Heuristics and normalization logic create and link entity records; changes to normalization, ID generation, or linking rules change which facts are considered related.
insights.solution: Preserve entity normalization and link-table contracts; if changing entity IDs or extraction heuristics, provide a deterministic remapping or rebuilding path and verify compositional probe behavior across entity-linked facts.

key_files.why: Plugin entry that exposes HolographicMemoryProvider, the plugin registration hook, FACT_STORE_SCHEMA and integrates the provider with session hooks and tool schemas — editing here affects plugin discovery and lifecycle integration.
Core HRR utilities (encoding, bind/unbind, serialize) that produce the vectors stored and compared across processes; changes here affect all vector computation and compatibility.
SQLite MemoryStore implementation that persists facts, entities, HRR vectors and handles schema; edits impact persistence, migrations, FTS usage, and feedback handling.
FactRetriever composes the hybrid retrieval pipeline (FTS candidates, reranking, HRR scoring, trust/decay) and contradiction detection; changes alter retrieval semantics and agent-visible relevance.
Plugin manifest used by plugin loading/metadata; modifications change how the plugin is discovered and configured.

construct_relationships: HolographicMemoryProvider (exported in __init__) orchestrates MemoryStore for persistence and FactRetriever for queries, and registers session hooks and tool schemas.
MemoryStore persists facts, entities, metadata and serialized HRR vectors; it uses holographic.encode_fact/serialize for vector creation and stores vectors in the SQLite schema.

FactRetriever asks MemoryStore for FTS5 candidates, applies Jaccard reranking, optionally calls holographic HRR similarity computations (encode/bind/measure) to refine scoring, and factors trust scores and temporal decay from stored metadata.

Entity extraction/linking logic in MemoryStore produces normalized entity records that are referenced by facts; FactRetriever uses these links when performing multi-entity or compositional HRR probes.
Serialization/deserialization functions in holographic.py are used both at write time (store) and read time (retrieval/probing), so their formats must be compatible across both flows.
notable_relationships: Depends on SQLite with FTS5 enabled; environment lacking FTS5 or using a different SQLite build may break full-text candidate retrieval.

Provides a MemoryProvider implementation expected by the broader agent memory subsystem (agent.memory_manager or plugin registry); changes here affect prefetch, session-end auto-extraction, and tool-driven fact writes.
Trust/feedback signals recorded here feed back into agent behavior (via retrieval weights), so modifications can ripple into conversation quality and downstream skill/tool invocation.
Persistence lives under the agent's data directory (HERMES_HOME); schema changes or heavy rebuilds have operational implications for backups, user data migration, and Docker volume management.
plugin.yaml/README are part of plugin discovery/documentation and may be consumed by external tooling that loads available memory providers.
recommendations: Preserve HRR encoding algorithm parameters and vector serialization format or introduce explicit versioning with migration paths before changing them.
Treat the SQLite schema as a stable contract: add migrations and document schema version changes; test data round-trips (write -> read -> decode) across versions.

When modifying retrieval scoring or ordering, include end-to-end tests exercising FTS candidate selection, Jaccard reranking, HRR scoring, trust-weight changes, and temporal decay to detect behavioral regressions.
Guard long-running operations (bank rebuilds, full re-encodes) to avoid blocking writes/reads; ensure code handles SQLite locking errors and keeps transactions short.
Retain compatibility of tool schemas and the register() interface exported by __init__.py so plugin loading, prefetch hooks, and session lifecycle integrations remain functional.

Add/maintain unit tests for encode/decode determinism (cross-process signoffs), entity linking stability, contradiction detection, and feedback-to-trust adjustments; run tests with a real FTS5-enabled SQLite build.

website/docs/getting-started.description: This folder contains the Getting Started documentation for the website: short, task-oriented pages that onboard new users (installation, quickstart, Nix install, updating/rollback, and learning path) plus the category index used by the static site generator. These pages are the primary first-touch documentation for users and must remain accurate with respect to CLI commands, installer scripts, platform-specific notes, and navigation metadata so site builds and user flows continue to work.
key_behaviors: Provide a fast end-to-end path to run Hermes locally (quickstart) and link into fuller installation options.
Offer platform/packaging-specific install instructions and deployment choices (notably a Nix-flavored path).
Explain update/rollback procedures and tie those to installer artifacts (scripts, Docker image tags, HERMES_HOME).
Act as navigation targets driven by the category index (_category_.json) so pages appear/ordering in the docs sidebar.
Cross-link to deeper learning resources (learning path) for progressive onboarding after quickstart.

insights.title: Category index controls sidebar presence and ordering
insights.problem: The static site generator (category JSON), not file presence alone, determines whether these pages appear in the Getting Started section and in what order.
insights.root_cause: Site generator relies on _category_.json metadata to present a cohesive sidebar and ordering for these markdown files.
insights.solution: When editing filenames, titles, or expected order, update _category_.json and validate a local site build to confirm navigation and slugs are preserved.

insights.title: Duplicate content entries present risk of divergence
insights.problem: installation.md and learning-path.md are flagged as duplicate entries in the metadata, which can lead to inconsistent instructions if both are modified independently.
insights.root_cause: Multiple docs covering overlapping onboarding topics were created without a canonical single source of truth or redirects between them.
insights.solution: Treat duplicates as linked artifacts: preserve consistent headings, cross-links, and add explicit mention/redirects if one file is meant to supersede another. Verify site search and sidebar behavior after changes.

insights.title: Nix and other platform-specific instructions carry environment assumptions
insights.problem: Nix-based guidance depends on different tooling and environment semantics (e.g., environment isolation, package availability), which can produce different runtime layouts or paths (HERMES_HOME, node/playwright installs).
insights.root_cause: Installer scripts and Dockerfile assume non-Nix environments; Nix steps must reconcile those differences explicitly.
insights.solution: When updating Nix docs, ensure references to scripts, required system dependencies (node, npm), and persistent data paths reflect Nix behavior, and indicate divergence from the standard installer steps.

insights.title: Update and rollback guidance must map to actual artifacts
insights.problem: Instructions for updating/rolling back need to reference concrete artifacts (release tags, Docker images, installer scripts) or they will become stale and unsafe.
insights.root_cause: Docs are decoupled from release process and build artifacts; user commands change over time (image tags, entrypoints, installer script options).
insights.solution: When editing these docs, cross-check referenced commands, script names, Docker tags, and environment variables against repository files (scripts/install.sh, Dockerfile, run_agent entry points) and ensure the doc specifies exact commands or points to the authoritative script.

key_files.why: Controls site navigation, ordering, and whether pages are visible in the Getting Started sidebar; any rename/reorder must keep this in sync.
Primary fast path for new users to run Hermes and validate the main CLI/runtime; many readers will follow this step-by-step, so its accuracy is critical.
Provides installation options and is flagged as duplicate—edits here affect the canonical install flow presented to users.
Contains alternative Nix-based installation/deployment instructions which have environment-specific assumptions and dependencies distinct from standard install docs.
Describes update and rollback procedures and must remain consistent with repository release artifacts, installer scripts, and Docker images.

construct_relationships: Category metadata (_category_.json) defines ordering and visibility for the markdown pages (quickstart, installation, learning path, nix-setup, updating) and therefore affects site navigation and user onboarding flow.
Quickstart.md is expected to reference installation.md and nix-setup.md for extended steps; edits to quickstart must consider link targets and anchors in those files.

Updating.md must align with installer artifacts and runtime entrypoints referenced elsewhere in the repo (scripts/install.sh, Dockerfile, run_agent entry points) because its commands map to concrete files and tags.

Nix-setup.md contains instructions that differ from standard installer steps and therefore needs to reference or explicitly call out divergences from the common installation artifacts referenced by installation.md.
notable_relationships: These docs are upstream of the website build system (likely Docusaurus): changes to frontmatter, filenames, or _category_.json affect the generated site and sidebar.

Content references external repository artifacts: installer scripts (scripts/install.sh), Dockerfile, entrypoints (hermes_cli.main, run_agent), and HERMES_HOME data directory conventions—these must be consistent with the docs.
Removing or consolidating duplicate docs has downstream effects on search, redirects, and external links; consumers (README, release notes, external blog posts) may link to these specific slugs.
Platform-specific docs (Nix) may depend on external tooling (node, playwright, system packages) described in other repo files; maintainers will need to ensure cross-references remain correct.
recommendations: Preserve or update _category_.json when renaming, reordering, or changing titles so the site sidebar and permalinks remain stable; validate with a local docs build.

When editing installation/updating content, cross-check commands and artifact names against repository scripts (scripts/install.sh), Dockerfile, and runtime entrypoints (run_agent, hermes CLI) to avoid stale commands.
Treat duplicated pages as linked: coordinate edits across installation.md and learning-path.md to avoid conflicting guidance and ensure redirects or canonical references exist if one page is removed.

For Nix-specific content, ensure environment notes explicitly call out differences (paths, package availability, node/playwright) and confirm referenced package names/commands in the Nix instructions match current repo/setup scripts.

After edits, run the static site build and link-checker (local Docusaurus build or equivalent) to catch broken anchors, missing images, or navigation regressions; also verify search/indexing behavior if applicable.

plugins/memory/mem0.description: This directory contains the Mem0 memory provider plugin that integrates Hermes with the Mem0 Platform API. It implements a MemoryProvider surface used by the agent to prefetch and search semantic memories, synchronize conversation turns to Mem0 asynchronously, and expose profile/search/conclude tooling and schemas; reliability and API compatibility (Mem0 SDK v2) are explicit concerns.
key_behaviors: Load plugin configuration and determine per-session scoping (user_id) for Mem0 operations.
Prefetch relevant memories for prompt assembly to reduce latency and token usage.
Perform non-blocking (background) synchronization of conversation turns to Mem0 with circuit-breaker logic to avoid failures cascading into the agent loop.
Expose tool schemas and handlers for profile, search, and conclude operations used by the agent/tool runtime.
Call Mem0 Platform API (via SDK) for semantic search and optionally perform reranking locally; marshal API request/response shapes.

insights.title: Avoid accidental global/shared memory when running as a gateway
insights.problem: By default Mem0 config.user_id was set to a constant ('hermes-user'), which causes memories to be shared across different gateway users/sessions.
insights.root_cause: Default user_id fallback in plugin configuration vs. per-gateway-session identity.
insights.solution: Prefer and propagate the gateway session user_id for Mem0 scoping; ensure config loading allows overriding the default and that tools and sync operations use the resolved session-scoped user_id.

insights.title: Mem0 SDK v2 changed call signatures and return shapes
insights.problem: Existing code assumed membrane calls accepted user_id= and returned lists; Mem0 SDK v2 expects filters={} and returns dict responses ({'results': [...]}) leading to broken call sites and runtime type errors.
insights.root_cause: Upstream SDK breaking changes (parameters and wrapped responses) not accounted for.
insights.solution: Adapt all call sites to use filters= where required, unwrap dict responses (extract results), and add defensive parsing around the returned structure so the plugin handles both legacy and v2 shapes when necessary.

insights.title: Non-blocking sync must not disrupt the main agent loop
insights.problem: Sync/prefetch operations that call external Mem0 may introduce latency or exceptions that affect agent responsiveness.
insights.root_cause: Synchronous or unguarded network calls against an external API in the agent request path.
insights.solution: Maintain non-blocking behavior for sync/prefetch (run in background tasks), and preserve circuit-breaker/timeout/retry behavior so Mem0 failures are isolated; ensure exceptions are caught and logged without propagating to caller flows.

insights.title: Tool schemas (PROFILE_SCHEMA, SEARCH_SCHEMA) are the contract with the tools system
insights.problem: Mismatch between schema definitions and handler expectations will break tool invocation and validation.
insights.root_cause: Schemas and handlers are implemented in the same plugin; changes to one without updating the other (and plugin.yaml) lead to runtime validation or invocation errors.
insights.solution: Keep PROFILE_SCHEMA and SEARCH_SCHEMA stable and ensure plugin.yaml and README document any schema changes; validate inputs/outputs and align handler code to the declared schema.

key_files.why: Primary implementation of the Mem0MemoryProvider: configuration loading, API calls, prefetch/sync logic, circuit-breaker, and tool handlers (PROFILE_SCHEMA, SEARCH_SCHEMA).
Plugin manifest that registers the Mem0 semantic memory plugin and declares metadata/schemas used by the plugin loader and tooling registry.
Documentation for setup, expected behavior, and operational notes (including user scoping and compatibility caveats) that must remain accurate when code or configuration changes.

construct_relationships: Mem0MemoryProvider calls _load_config to resolve runtime configuration (including user_id) used for all Mem0 operations.
Mem0MemoryProvider exposes PROFILE_SCHEMA and SEARCH_SCHEMA which are used by tool registration and the plugin manifest (plugin.yaml) to validate tool inputs and outputs.
Prefetch logic in Mem0MemoryProvider is invoked during prompt assembly to fetch relevant memory snippets and is implemented as a non-blocking background activity to avoid delaying the request flow.
Sync logic writes conversation turns to Mem0 asynchronously and is guarded by circuit-breaker/timeouts so Mem0 API failures do not interrupt the main agent loop.
Search and optionally reranking flow: Mem0MemoryProvider calls Mem0 semantic search API and may perform local reranking of returned results before returning to the caller.
notable_relationships: Gateway / session layer: the plugin expects a session-scoped user identifier (e.g., gateway user_id) to scope Mem0 data; changes to gateway session identity propagation affect memory isolation.
Agent prompt assembly / memory manager: prefetches from this provider will be consumed when building prompts; timing and returned shapes must match prompt builder expectations.
Mem0 SDK/API: the plugin directly depends on Mem0 SDK call signatures and response shapes (notably v2 changes to filters and dict-wrapped results); breaking SDK upgrades will require plugin changes.

Tools system / plugin registry: plugin.yaml and tool schemas are part of the broader plugin registration; any schema or handler changes must remain consistent with the platform tool invocation/validation system.
Async execution model: background sync/prefetch operations assume the agent runtime provides an event loop/task scheduling; changes to concurrency model can break background behavior.
recommendations: When editing, preserve or intentionally update how user_id is resolved and propagated; test multi-user gateway scenarios to avoid inadvertent cross-user memory sharing.

Ensure all Mem0 API call sites accept the SDK v2 parameter shapes (filters=) and unwrap dict responses (extract 'results'); add defensive handling for both legacy and v2 shapes if backward compatibility is required.
Keep non-blocking sync and prefetch semantics (background task scheduling, proper awaits, and exception-suppression) intact; ensure circuit-breaker timeouts/retries remain enforced and logged.
Any change to PROFILE_SCHEMA or SEARCH_SCHEMA must be reflected in plugin.yaml and README; maintain input/output validation to keep the tool contract stable.

Do not execute real Mem0 network calls in unit tests unless credentials are explicitly provided; mock Mem0 SDK responses and include integration tests that exercise the v2 response shape. Run integration/e2e tests only in environments configured for external API access.

plugins/memory/retaindb.description: This folder implements a RetainDB-backed MemoryProvider plugin that supplies durable, crash-safe memory ingestion, semantic search/context queries, file upload/ingest tooling, and overlay formatting for synthesized context. It matters because it replaces an in-process/in-memory memory backend with an externally-hosted RetainDB service while preserving reliability (durable write-behind queue) and the agent-facing tool schemas the core system expects.
key_behaviors: Durable write-behind ingestion: agent events and sessions are enqueued locally to a SQLite-backed queue and asynchronously uploaded to RetainDB to avoid blocking the agent hot path.
HTTP client interactions: a _Client performs REST calls against a RetainDB endpoint for writes, reads, file uploads, and semantic search.
Prefetch/async processing: background worker threads (prefetch/write) drain the SQLite queue and perform ingestion, with thread-local SQLite connections and lifecycle/join handling to avoid leaks.
Semantic search and overlay composition: provider executes context/semantic queries against RetainDB and builds formatted overlays for prompts.
Tool schema exposure: plugin exposes multiple agent-facing tool schemas (profile/search/context/remember/forget and file tools) used by the agent/tool invocation system.

insights.title: Thread-local SQLite connections and proper worker shutdown
insights.problem: Prefetch/worker threads previously created/closed sqlite3 connections per operation, causing many short-lived connections and thread accumulation; shutdown did not join threads, producing resource leaks and race conditions.
insights.root_cause: Improper per-operation connection handling and missing coordination on thread lifecycle and shutdown.
insights.solution: Use thread-local SQLite connections and ensure background prefetch/write threads are joined at shutdown; coordinate connection reuse per thread to avoid leaks and racing closures.

insights.title: Project name should be optional and have a safe default
insights.problem: Code assumed a RETAINDB_PROJECT default of 'hermes' or required it be set, causing errors when unset or when different defaults are expected.
insights.root_cause: Hard-coded assumptions about project configuration and lack of optional handling for project name.
insights.solution: Treat RETAINDB project as optional and provide a sensible default (e.g., 'default') while honoring explicit environment/config values.

insights.title: Correct RetainDB endpoints and non-blocking ingestion
insights.problem: Calls used incorrect RetainDB routes (404s) and synchronous uploads that blocked the agent's hot path.
insights.root_cause: Mismatched/old endpoint definitions and synchronous implementation for ingestion.
insights.solution: Align HTTP client routes to the RetainDB API surface, implement asynchronous/durable write-behind queueing, and add file upload and file tool integrations to match agent expectations.

key_files.why: Core implementation: defines _Client, _WriteQueue, RetainDBMemoryProvider, overlay builder, HTTP interactions, durable queue and background thread logic, and exposes tool schemas used by the agent.
Integration guide documenting configuration, expected RetainDB endpoints/credentials, and operational notes that must remain consistent with runtime behavior.
Plugin manifest used by the plugin registration/discovery system; describes exposed tools and plugin metadata the host uses.

construct_relationships: RetainDBMemoryProvider composes _Client for all HTTP interactions and _WriteQueue for durable ingestion.
_WriteQueue persists pending writes to a local SQLite DB and spawns background worker threads which use thread-local SQLite connections to drain the queue and invoke _Client to upload data.
_Client performs file uploads, semantic search, and context queries against RetainDB endpoints; results are passed back to RetainDBMemoryProvider which formats overlays via _build_overlay.
The plugin exposes tool schemas (profile/search/context/remember/forget, file tools) declared in plugin.yaml and implemented by RetainDBMemoryProvider methods.
notable_relationships: The agent core (agent.MemoryManager or plugin loader) will instantiate this MemoryProvider and expects the provider to implement the MemoryProvider interface and tool schemas; any signature changes will affect agent-memory integration.
Tools and other subsystems (tools/*, gateway/*) rely on the tool schemas produced here for file upload and semantic search workflows; plugin.yaml must remain consistent with those callers.
External dependency on a running RetainDB service (endpoint, project, credentials): network/API compatibility and error handling affect agent reliability.
Local persistence (SQLite queue) interacts with the host filesystem and HERMES_HOME/data; permission/volume mounts and crash recovery behavior are relevant for deployment (Docker/systemd).
recommendations: Preserve thread-safety and lifecycle semantics: any edits must maintain thread-local SQLite connections and ensure background worker threads are joined during shutdown to avoid leaks and races.
Do not change public method signatures or tool schema names exposed in plugin.yaml without coordinating with the agent's plugin loader and tooling callers.
Respect configuration defaults: treat RETAINDB project as optional and honor explicit config/env values; avoid hard-coded project names.
When modifying HTTP endpoints or client behavior, validate against the deployed RetainDB API version and update tests/mocks accordingly to avoid 404s or breaking ingestion.

Run unit tests and integration checks that exercise: durable queue enqueue/drain, worker shutdown/join behavior, file upload flows, and semantic search/overlay formatting; if possible, run an integration test against a local RetainDB test instance or a mocked HTTP server.
Be mindful of file system locations for the SQLite queue (permissions, relative vs absolute paths, HERMES_HOME) to preserve crash-recovery guarantees and container deployments.
Keep README.md and plugin.yaml consistent with implementation changes so configuration instructions and plugin discovery metadata remain accurate.

tests/plugins/memory.description: This folder contains unit tests that validate memory-provider integrations (notably Supermemory and Mem0 v2) via mocked clients and helper utilities. The tests exercise provider configuration persistence, prefetch/ingest flows, query/filter semantics, response shapes, and attribution behavior — they serve as a behavioral contract for memory-provider implementations and any code that interacts with them.
key_behaviors: Simulating remote memory-provider APIs with FakeClient/mocks to verify local provider logic without network calls.
Validating provider configuration load/save semantics and that config values survive persistence operations.
Exercising prefetch and ingest flows: formatting prefetch context, sanitizing text for capture, and invoking provider ingestion APIs.
Verifying query/filter behavior and response shapes (list vs dict) returned by provider clients and how the agent code consumes them.
Asserting write attribution metadata is applied correctly on ingested items and preserved in subsequent reads.

insights.title: Tests enforce a comprehensive provider contract
insights.problem: Provider implementations can diverge in config shapes, prefetch formatting, ingest parameters, filtering semantics, and response structures which breaks consumer code.
insights.root_cause: Different memory backends expose different API surfaces and JSON shapes; lack of strict interface guarantees leads to subtle mismatches.
insights.solution: Treat the tests here as the canonical behavioral contract: when changing provider code or introducing new providers, ensure mocks and tests cover config persistence, prefetch formatting, ingestion, filters, and response shape cases.

insights.title: Tests rely on mock clients to avoid network
insights.problem: If tests accidentally exercise real network calls or rely on live credentials, CI or local runs will fail or leak secrets.
insights.root_cause: Provider code may fall back to real HTTP clients when mocks are not used or mock interfaces don't fully replicate runtime behavior.
insights.solution: Ensure test fixtures (FakeClient) fully emulate the external API surface used by provider code and that any refactor keeps the points where mocks are injected intact; maintain isolation from environment API keys.

insights.title: Consumers expect specific list/dict shapes and attribution fields
insights.problem: Variations in whether query responses are lists or dicts, or presence/absence of attribution/write metadata, cause parsing or downstream-tool failures in tests.
insights.root_cause: Provider adapters may transform or wrap provider responses differently; tests assert particular shapes and metadata keys.
insights.solution: When changing provider adapters or transformations, preserve declared response shapes and include required attribution metadata, or update tests and mocks together to reflect intentional changes.

insights.title: Config load/save behavior is verified by tests
insights.problem: Renaming or relocating config helpers will break tests that depend on _load_supermemory_config/_save_supermemory_config semantics.
insights.root_cause: Tests call helper functions that encapsulate file/serialize semantics; changes to storage location, format, or defaults will alter observed behavior.
insights.solution: Any edits touching provider config APIs, file locations, or serialization formats must be mirrored in these tests/mocks to maintain expected behavior.

key_files.why: Primary test suite asserting Supermemory provider behavior: config load/save, prefetch formatting, sanitization helpers, ingestion and client interactions via a FakeClient.
Tests for Mem0 API v2 provider semantics: filters= parameter behavior, write attribution, and expected dict/list response shapes.
Package initializer for the test module; may contain or enable test fixtures or imports relied upon by the sibling test modules.

construct_relationships: FakeClient in test_supermemory_provider simulates remote API calls and is used by SupermemoryMemoryProvider tests to validate ingestion and query interactions.

_clean_text_for_capture and _format_prefetch_context in test_supermemory_provider are used together to verify that prefetch payloads are sanitized and formatted before being sent to the provider client.
_load_supermemory_config and _save_supermemory_config in test_supermemory_provider are exercised to assert config persistence semantics expected by provider code.
test_mem0_v2 exercises Mem0 provider query paths that must honor filters= arguments and preserve write attribution fields in provider responses consumed by caller code.
notable_relationships: Tests assume provider adapter implementations (SupermemoryMemoryProvider, Mem0 provider) are present in the main codebase and that those adapters accept injectable clients/mocks rather than always instantiating real HTTP clients.

Config persistence behavior tested here likely depends on the same HERMES_HOME/data location or other shared config utilities used elsewhere in the project; changes to global config resolution will affect these tests.
CI conventions (blanking API key env vars) mean these tests must remain isolated from real external APIs; any test that reaches out will conflict with CI environment settings.
recommendations: When editing provider adapters or shared memory utilities, co-change the corresponding tests/mocks here to preserve the behavioral contract (config shapes, prefetch formatting, ingestion parameters, filters, response shapes, and attribution fields).
Do not introduce code paths that instantiate real network clients in the test execution path; ensure tests continue to inject FakeClient/mocks or mock the network layer completely.

If renaming or moving helper functions used by tests (_clean_text_for_capture, _format_prefetch_context, _load_supermemory_config, _save_supermemory_config), update test imports/fixtures to match to avoid import errors.
Preserve expected response shapes (list vs dict) and metadata keys in provider adapters or update tests and mocks simultaneously; failing to do so will break parsing assumptions elsewhere.
Be mindful of global config/file locations used by providers; changes require updating tests that assert persistence, and consider CI environment (API key blanking) to keep tests hermetic.

plugins/memory/openviking.description: OpenViking memory plugin that implements a MemoryProvider backed by an OpenViking REST service. It provides tiered contextual retrieval (semantic search, browsing), explicit memory writes, a thin HTTP client, background prefetch/sync workers, and tool schemas so the core agent and tools can call search/read/browse/remember/add_resource functionality. This plugin matters because it supplies external, server-backed long-term memory and context injection used by the agent runtime and gateway flows.
key_behaviors: Translate agent session/turn events into OpenViking REST calls via a thin HTTP client
Buffer and commit session messages to the server, including a session-commit flow that triggers server-side extraction
Background prefetch and sync threads to asynchronously retrieve search results and keep local state in sync
Expose tool schemas (search/read/browse/remember/add_resource) used by the agent/tool subsystem to perform memory operations
Integrate with process lifecycle (init/register plugin, shutdown/commit sessions) to avoid message loss

insights.title: Pending session messages can be lost on abnormal shutdown
insights.problem: If shutdown_memory_provider is not invoked (gateway crash, process killed, or async flush exceptions), pending in-memory session messages may never be committed to OpenViking and the final turn may be skipped due to turn_count checks.
insights.root_cause: Commit/shutdown logic depends on an explicit shutdown path and uses turn_count checks before waiting for pending sync threads; background sync/flush exceptions or missing exit hooks allow messages to be left uncommitted.
insights.solution: Ensure commit semantics are idempotent and robust: register/handle process exit and signals, join/wait for prefetch/sync workers reliably, surface and handle exceptions from async flush, and make commits tolerant of repeated calls so double-commit is safe.

insights.title: Background prefetch/sync threads create race and exception risks
insights.problem: Prefetch and sync threads can race with main-thread session updates and can fail silently if their exceptions are not handled, resulting in stale or missing context injection.
insights.root_cause: Concurrent access to provider state and reliance on background threads without comprehensive exception handling, timeouts, or retry logic in the thin HTTP client.
insights.solution: Treat prefetch/sync threads as first-class concurrent components: guard shared state with thread-safe patterns, catch and log thread exceptions, implement timeouts and retries for network calls, and clearly define join/stop semantics during shutdown.

insights.title: Tool schema and provider API stability is critical
insights.problem: Tool consumers (agent skills, tools subsystem) rely on exposed SEARCH_SCHEMA and other tool schemas; changes can break tool calls and agent flows.
insights.root_cause: Tight coupling between the provider's declared schemas and the rest of the system; plugin.yaml/README serve as contract surfaces.
insights.solution: Treat tool schemas and plugin registration metadata as public contract: maintain backward compatibility, validate inputs/outputs against schemas, and update plugin.yaml/README alongside any schema/API changes.

key_files.why: Primary implementation: defines _VikingClient, OpenVikingMemoryProvider, background prefetch/sync behavior, tool schemas, and session commit/shutdown semantics.
Plugin registration and metadata that other subsystems use to discover and load the provider; governs plugin contract and capability exposure.
Documentation for setup, usage, and the tool schemas exposed by the provider; important for maintainers and integrators to understand expected behavior.

construct_relationships: OpenVikingMemoryProvider composes and uses _VikingClient to make REST calls to the OpenViking server
OpenVikingMemoryProvider manages background prefetch/sync threads which call into _VikingClient and update provider-local state used for context injection

SEARCH_SCHEMA (and other tool schemas declared in the module) are exposed by the provider and consumed by agent tools/skills and the tools subsystem to perform memory search/read/browse/remember/add_resource operations
Session commit flow in OpenVikingMemoryProvider triggers a server-side extraction via _VikingClient and is expected to be called on provider shutdown or at end-of-session
notable_relationships: Depends on the external OpenViking REST API (network reliability, authentication/credentials) — network failures and latency must be tolerated by the plugin
Integrates with the agent MemoryProvider interface consumed by the core agent and gateway; lifecycle hooks (init, per-turn writes, shutdown) must align with global agent lifecycle
Tool schemas are part of the broader tools/skills subsystem contract; other folders (tools/*, agent/*) will call these schemas and expect stable behavior
plugin.yaml participates in plugin discovery/registration and therefore must remain consistent with any system that loads memory plugins
recommendations: Preserve idempotent commit semantics: changes must not create unsafe double-commit or irreversible partial-commit states; ensure commit operations can be retried safely.

Respect and maintain shutdown semantics: edits must ensure background workers are stopped/joined and pending sessions are flushed on normal exits and signal-driven shutdowns; treat missing shutdown calls as a failure mode to mitigate.
Keep tool schemas stable: any change to SEARCH_SCHEMA or other exposed schemas requires updating plugin.yaml and README and careful coordination with tools/skills that consume them.

Handle concurrency explicitly: changes touching prefetch/sync logic, shared in-memory session state, or the thin HTTP client must preserve thread-safety, add exception handling/logging, and not rely on silent thread failures.

Consider network failure modes: maintain sensible timeouts, retries, and error propagation so agent flows using this provider can degrade gracefully; tests that touch network behavior should be mocked in CI.

When modifying behavior that affects persistence or external calls, run unit/integration tests that exercise commit-on-shutdown, prefetch flows, and tool schema validation; ensure CI/local tests mock external OpenViking endpoints to avoid live API calls.

plugins/memory/supermemory.description: This folder implements a Supermemory memory provider plugin that integrates the Hermes MemoryProvider interface with the Supermemory semantic memory service. It contains a lightweight client wrapper, the provider implementation (semantic recall, profile prefetch, explicit store/forget, conversation ingestion), config load/save helpers, and schema definitions for the explicit memory tools, and is registered as a discoverable plugin via plugin.yaml.
key_behaviors: Perform semantic recall and profile-prefetch of memories to supply context for prompts.
Store explicit memories and forget requests into the Supermemory service using a validated store schema.
Ingest full conversations (trajectories) into Supermemory for long-term storage and later retrieval.
Load and persist plugin configuration locally (credentials, config flags) and initialize the client wrapper for network calls to the external Supermemory API.

insights.title: Local storage path hardcoded to user home breaks multi-profile isolation
insights.problem: The provider was observed to use a hardcoded ~/.hermes path for local config/persistence, which can mix data across user profiles and contradicts project expectations for profile-scoped data.
insights.root_cause: Direct use of a fixed filesystem location instead of using the project's HERMES_HOME/get_hermes_home abstraction.
insights.solution: Respect the project's HERMES_HOME/profile-scoped helper for all local config and persisted files; ensure any new or existing files are placed under that location to preserve profile isolation and predictable backups.

insights.title: Networked semantic provider requires robust error handling
insights.problem: The plugin relies on the external Supermemory service for core flows (search, store, ingest). Network errors, rate limits, and transient failures will affect agent behavior.
insights.root_cause: Direct runtime dependency on a remote API without documented retry/backoff/timeout semantics in provider metadata.
insights.solution: When editing client/provider logic, maintain/observe explicit timeouts, retry/backoff strategies, and clear error propagation (so callers can fall back or surface errors). Ensure secrets/credentials are loaded from secure config sources.

insights.title: Explicit store/forget tools rely on stable schemas
insights.problem: The STORE_SCHEMA (and related schemas) define the contract for explicit memory storage and are consumed by tool invocation paths; schema changes can break callers or tooling that validates requests.
insights.root_cause: Schema definitions are centralized in the plugin and used by both the provider and any tool invocation code expecting specific fields/validation.
insights.solution: Treat schemas as part of the public contract; any change requires coordinated updates to callers and tests. Preserve field names, types and validation semantics unless all consumers are updated in lockstep.

insights.title: Prefetch/context formatting must match prompt-builder expectations
insights.problem: Prefetched memories are formatted and injected into prompts; mismatches in ordering, delimiting, or token estimation could cause prompt quality regressions or token overflows.
insights.root_cause: Formatting logic (_format_prefetch_context) is responsible for creating prompt-ready context and is tightly coupled to how prompt composition expects context to look and to token budget assumptions.
insights.solution: Maintain compatibility with the PromptBuilder contract: ensure formatted context respects delimiting, ordering, and size/truncation semantics used by the rest of the agent when editing formatting logic.

key_files.why: Contains the provider implementation, lightweight Supermemory client wrapper, config helpers, prefetch formatting, and STORE_SCHEMA — this is the functional core of the plugin.
Plugin manifest used for registration/discovery of the Supermemory provider by the Hermes plugin system; changes affect plugin availability.
Documentation for integrators/operators about configuration, expected behavior, and any operational constraints (credentials, HERMES_HOME usage, network expectations).

construct_relationships: SupermemoryMemoryProvider uses _SupermemoryClient to perform network operations (search, store, ingest, forget).
SupermemoryMemoryProvider calls _load_supermemory_config to locate credentials/config and initialize the client wrapper before service calls.
_format_prefetch_context is invoked by the provider when constructing prompt-context payloads returned to the prompt composition layer.
STORE_SCHEMA is used by provider methods and any explicit-memory tools to validate incoming store requests before sending to Supermemory.
notable_relationships: Agent core (MemoryManager / PromptBuilder) will call this MemoryProvider via the project's MemoryProvider interface — any API or behavioral changes must remain compatible with that interface.
The plugin reads/writes local configuration and persisted files; these must align with the project's HERMES_HOME (or get_hermes_home) conventions used elsewhere in the codebase.
Network interactions to Supermemory depend on credentials and environment secrets (API keys); CI/tests should avoid calling the real service and may need mocks.
The plugin's plugin.yaml is part of the global plugin discovery/registration flow — changes affect downstream plugin loading.
recommendations: Preserve the MemoryProvider interface contract: ensure method signatures, return types, and error behaviors remain compatible with callers (MemoryManager, PromptBuilder, tools).
Do not hardcode filesystem paths; use the project's get_hermes_home/HERMES_HOME abstraction for config and persisted files to maintain profile isolation.

When changing client/network logic, respect timeouts, retries, and explicit error propagation so calling code can handle failures predictably; add or preserve hooks for mocking network calls in tests.
Treat STORE_SCHEMA and any exported schemas as stable public contracts; coordinated updates to consumers are required if schemas change.
After edits, run unit test suite with network calls mocked; verify plugin discovery (plugin.yaml) and that prompt prefetch formatting still fits into prompt composition/token budget expectations.

website/docs/user-guide.description: This folder contains end-user documentation for the Hermes Agent: how to run and operate the CLI, gateway, Docker deployment, and feature-level guides (API server, credential pools, memory providers, context files, cron, delegation, etc.). It is the canonical user-facing mapping between runtime behavior, configuration knobs (env vars and config.yaml), and operational/security guidance, so accuracy and alignment with code-level constructs matter for correct deployments and safe operation.
key_behaviors: Map user actions to runtime controls: documents translate features into concrete environment variables, config keys, file names and commands users must set or run.
Describe operational flows and lifecycle concerns: startup (CLI/gateway/docker), long-running tasks (cron/batch), delegation/subagent lifecycles and rollback safeguards.
Surface security and credential handling guidance: credential pools, API server authentication, context file sensitivity and rules for safe injection of files/URLs.
Tie feature documentation to implementation artifacts: each feature doc references specific runtime modules, endpoints, or files (e.g., batch_runner.py, AGENTS.md, API endpoints).
Note optional/conditional features and their platform/back-end dependencies (browser backends, memory providers, Honcho integration).

insights.title: Docs must stay synchronized with runtime symbols and file names
insights.problem: Many pages reference concrete runtime constructs (env vars, config keys, filenames, scripts and endpoints). If code-level names change, docs diverge and users will be given incorrect commands or configuration.
insights.root_cause: Documentation embeds implementation identifiers (e.g., API_SERVER_ENABLED, API_SERVER_KEY, credential_pool, AGENTS.md) rather than being generated from source.
insights.solution: When editing docs, verify referenced env vars, config keys, filenames and endpoint paths against the corresponding code modules and configuration files; treat each named symbol as a linkage point that must be validated.

insights.title: Credential and API guidance is security-sensitive
insights.problem: Pages describe credential pools, API server keys and context file injection; inaccurate or overly permissive instructions could cause accidental exposure of secrets or misconfigured public endpoints.
insights.root_cause: User guide includes concrete setup steps for features that expose network endpoints or rotate keys and therefore directly affect attack surface.
insights.solution: Any edits that touch credential/credential-pool/API server/ context-file docs must preserve warnings, example redaction, and reference to secure defaults; validate that examples do not include real keys and that recommended env vars and flags match secure values in code and Docker/CI.

insights.title: Category metadata controls site organization
insights.problem: _category_.json files govern how pages are grouped and surfaced. Incorrect metadata will change navigation and hide or mis-order important feature docs.
insights.root_cause: Site navigation is driven by these JSON files, separate from page content.
insights.solution: When adding/removing/moving pages, update the _category_.json files and validate the rendered site navigation; ensure links and cross-references remain valid.

insights.title: Docs describe optional, platform-dependent features with external prerequisites
insights.problem: Several feature pages document optional backends (browser engines, Honcho, MCP servers, memory providers). Users may follow docs but lack required platform binaries or extras and fail silently.
insights.root_cause: Features depend on optional extras, native binaries, third-party services or environment (Playwright, Honcho, external memory services).
insights.solution: Verify that pages clearly map features to required extras/packages and that references to platform-specific variables (e.g., CAMOFOX_URL, honcho.json) are accurate and reflect any conditional behavior in code; ensure any platform caveats reflect current build/deployment constraints.

key_files.why: Controls top-level grouping and navigation for the User Guide; changing it affects how the entire section is presented.

Documents the OpenAI-compatible API surface and auth environment variables (GET /v1/chat/completions, GET /v1/models, API_SERVER_ENABLED, API_SERVER_KEY) that operators rely on to expose Hermes programmatically.
Explains credential pool concepts and rotation strategies tied to runtime credential_pool logic and config.yaml; security- and operation-critical.
Describes AGENTS.md, SOUL.md and loading/security rules for injected context files — directly maps to how the agent reads and trusts file-based context.
Provides Docker setup/runtime guidance and interacts with container concerns (HERMES_HOME, system deps, Playwright) that impact deployment and persisted data.

construct_relationships: api-server.md references API_SERVER_ENABLED and API_SERVER_KEY which must match runtime flags and gateway/api_server implementation in the codebase.
credential-pools.md maps to credential_pool configuration and rotation_strategies used by agent credential management components.
context-files.md ties AGENTS.md and SOUL.md filenames to build_context_files_prompt and SubdirectoryHintTracker behavior in the prompt/context pipeline.
batch-processing.md refers to batch_runner.py and Trajectory format, which must align with the agent trajectory storage/compression schema.
features/acp.md references acp_registry and acp_adapter which correspond to the ACP adapter implementation and its registration entrypoints.
notable_relationships: Docs must remain aligned with agent/ and gateway/ code: many pages reference specific source-level constructs (modules, endpoints, config keys) that are implemented outside the docs subtree.
Docker guidance relates to Dockerfile and container entrypoints — changes in the Dockerfile, Node/Playwright setup, or HERMES_HOME defaults will affect documented instructions.
Security guidance must reflect CI and runtime environment behavior (CI blanks API keys; Docker and deployment scripts set defaults) to avoid conflicting operational advice.

Feature pages describing optional integrations (Honcho, MCP, memory providers, browser backends) are dependent on optional extras and native binaries defined elsewhere (pyproject.toml extras, build scripts).
recommendations: When editing any page that names code symbols (env vars, config keys, filenames, endpoints, module names), verify those names against the corresponding code modules, configuration files (config.yaml), Dockerfile, or CI settings before committing.

For changes touching credential, API server or context-file guidance, ensure examples have secrets redacted and validate instructions against the current runtime defaults (e.g., API_SERVER_* env vars, HERMES_HOME).
If pages are moved/renamed, update the nearest _category_.json and confirm site navigation and cross-page links; run the site build/link-checker to catch broken references.
Edits that document optional features should be cross-checked against pyproject.toml extras and installation notes to confirm stated prerequisites and platform caveats remain accurate.

After doc edits that change commands, endpoints, or sample config, run the local documentation build and link validation (the website generator’s dev server and link checker) and, where possible, smoke-test the described CLI/docker commands against a local environment to ensure commands still behave as documented.

plugins/memory/byterover.description: This folder implements a ByteRover memory plugin that integrates the external brv CLI as a MemoryProvider for the agent. It matters because it delegates long‑term memory queries and curation to an external binary, exposing query/curate/status tools and mirroring agent memory writes into ByteRover so the agent can rely on an external knowledge store.
key_behaviors: Resolve and validate the brv CLI binary path before use.
Execute synchronous brv queries for prefetching relevant long‑term memories.
Schedule and run background curation jobs for conversation turns and memory writes.
Mirror memory write operations to the brv backend to keep external store consistent.
Expose tool schemas and entry points (brv_query, brv_curate, status) so the agent can invoke ByteRover functionality as tools.

insights.title: Runtime dependency on brv binary
insights.problem: The plugin requires a host brv CLI binary; if missing or incorrect the provider cannot operate.
insights.root_cause: Integration relies on invoking an external process rather than an internal library.
insights.solution: Any edits must preserve or extend the binary resolution and robust error reporting behavior; callers and operator docs/packaging must account for the external binary being present and reachable.

insights.title: Synchronous prefetch can block agent flows
insights.problem: Prefetch queries are executed synchronously, which can introduce blocking latency during agent startup or request handling.
insights.root_cause: Prefetch uses synchronous subprocess calls to brv without offloading or explicit timeouts in the visible behavior.
insights.solution: When modifying prefetch logic, respect existing synchronous semantics and ensure timeout and failure behaviors remain well-defined and tested so agent responsiveness and startup behavior are not unintentionally impacted.

insights.title: Background curation and write mirroring introduce concurrency/ordering concerns
insights.problem: Background curation tasks and mirrored writes may run concurrently with the main agent loop and with each other, risking race conditions or inconsistent external state.
insights.root_cause: Curation and mirroring are executed asynchronously relative to synchronous agent operations and rely on correct sequencing/idempotency.
insights.solution: Edits must preserve or clearly manage task lifecycle, cancellation on shutdown, idempotency of mirrored operations, and ordering guarantees expected by the rest of the agent.

insights.title: Tool schema and provider interface compatibility
insights.problem: Tool schemas (e.g., QUERY_SCHEMA) and the MemoryProvider contract must match the agent's tool invocation expectations; mismatch causes runtime failures.
insights.root_cause: The plugin exposes tool schemas and implements provider behaviors that other subsystems call directly.
insights.solution: Keep schema definitions and exported tool names consistent with the agent's tool and MemoryProvider interfaces; update plugin metadata (plugin.yaml) in lockstep with behavioral changes.

key_files.why: Contains the ByteRoverMemoryProvider implementation: binary resolution, brv invocation helpers, synchronous prefetch, background curation, write mirroring, and tool schemas — core runtime logic to preserve when editing.
Plugin descriptor consumed by the plugin/loader system; must remain consistent with the provider implementation and any exposed tool names/schemas.

construct_relationships: ByteRoverMemoryProvider uses _resolve_brv_path to find the brv binary before any operation.
ByteRoverMemoryProvider calls _run_brv to execute brv CLI commands for queries and curation.
ByteRoverMemoryProvider exposes QUERY_SCHEMA and tool entry points (brv_query, brv_curate, status) that map agent tool invocations to brv CLI actions.
Prefetch logic invokes brv via _run_brv synchronously to return initial memory contexts to the agent.
Background curation tasks are scheduled by ByteRoverMemoryProvider and perform turn curation and mirroring of writes to brv asynchronous to the main request path.
notable_relationships: Runtime depends on an external brv CLI binary being installed and accessible on the host environment (system-level dependency).
This plugin implements the MemoryProvider surface that the agent's memory manager and tool invocation system will call into — changes here affect memory read/write flows across the agent.
plugin.yaml ties this implementation into the agent's plugin registry; inconsistencies between metadata and runtime behavior will impact plugin discovery and tooling.
Mirrored writes affect downstream persistence and any services that rely on ByteRover as the source of truth for long‑term memories.
recommendations: When editing, preserve or explicitly handle binary resolution semantics and error messages so failures are diagnosable and consistent with existing behavior.
Respect existing synchronous prefetch behavior: if changing it, verify agent startup and request latency implications and maintain clear timeout/failure handling.
Ensure background curation tasks remain cancelable and idempotent; verify proper cleanup on shutdown and that ordering with respect to memory writes is respected.
Keep QUERY_SCHEMA and exported tool names in sync with plugin.yaml and the agent's tool invocation expectations to avoid runtime schema mismatches.

Run unit tests with the brv invocation mocked and, if applicable, integration tests against a real or controlled brv binary to validate subprocess behavior and external‑dependency handling after edits.

plugins/memory/hindsight.description: This folder implements the Hindsight long‑term memory plugin: a MemoryProvider that can operate against a cloud Hindsight service or an embedded local daemon. It encapsulates async client lifecycle and event loop management, background retention/prefetching of conversation turns, and exposes RPC-style tool schemas (retain/recall/reflect) for the agent/tooling layer. The module is a bridge between the core memory subsystem and external/persistent storage with lifecycle and concurrency implications.
key_behaviors: Initialize and resolve configuration for either cloud or embedded (local daemon) mode at startup.
Manage an asynchronous client and its dedicated event loop for background operations (retain, recall, reflect).
Prefetch relevant long‑term memories on session start via recall/reflect to seed the agent context.
Retain conversation turns to long‑term storage asynchronously in the background to avoid blocking the agent loop.
Expose RPC‑style tool endpoints and JSON schemas (RETAIN_SCHEMA, etc.) used by the agent/tool invocation system.

insights.title: Dedicated event loop and cross‑thread async calls
insights.problem: The provider creates and uses its own event loop and helper wrappers to run async code from sync contexts.
insights.root_cause: Background I/O and daemon interactions require async semantics but the host agent or tooling may call provider APIs synchronously.
insights.solution: Edits must preserve the _get_loop/_run_sync semantics and ensure any changes maintain safe cross‑thread event loop creation, proper exception propagation, and clean shutdown to avoid hanging threads or un-awaited tasks.

insights.title: Local embedded daemon lifecycle is managed by the plugin
insights.problem: Embedded mode requires starting/stopping a local daemon and coordinating background sync while ensuring no resource leakage.
insights.root_cause: The provider owns daemon startup and background retain/sync tasks which run beyond instantaneous request handling.
insights.solution: Changes must respect daemon startup ordering, check for idempotent start/stop, and preserve any existing mechanisms to wait for daemon readiness and to shut it down cleanly on provider teardown.

insights.title: Asynchronous background retain and prefetch flows
insights.problem: Retain and prefetch operations run asynchronously and can impact ordering, consistency, and perceived latency if mismanaged.
insights.root_cause: Background tasks are decoupled from immediate agent responses to avoid blocking, but they still affect long‑term state and subsequent recalls/reflects.
insights.solution: When modifying retention/prefetch logic, preserve the asynchronous semantics, ensure idempotency/retry behavior, and be aware of eventual consistency implications for immediate recall operations.

insights.title: Tool handlers and JSON schemas are part of the public plugin API
insights.problem: Tool schemas (e.g., RETAIN_SCHEMA) and RPC handlers are used by the agent tooling layer; schema changes affect tool invocation compatibility.
insights.root_cause: Other subsystems rely on stable tool signatures to call retain/recall/reflect functionality.
insights.solution: Any edits that alter tool payloads or handler contracts must be coordinated with the agent/tool registry (including plugin.yaml metadata) to avoid breaking callers; maintain backward‑compatible schema changes or update consumers accordingly.

key_files.why: Contains the HindsightMemoryProvider, event loop helpers, background retain/prefetch logic, and tool schema definitions — the core implementation in this plugin.
Plugin metadata used by the host plugin/registry system; changes here affect discovery and declared capabilities of the memory provider.

construct_relationships: HindsightMemoryProvider exposes tool handlers (retain/recall/reflect) that are bound to RETAIN_SCHEMA and related JSON schemas defined in the module.
HindsightMemoryProvider uses internal helpers _get_loop and _run_sync to execute async client operations from synchronous entry points and background tasks.
In embedded/local mode the provider starts and manages a daemon process and schedules background retain/sync tasks that interact with the async client.
notable_relationships: This plugin implements the MemoryProvider interface expected by the core agent memory manager; any signature or behavioral changes can affect agent memory lifecycle, prefetching, and retrieval flows across the system.
Tool schemas and RPC handlers are part of the global tool invocation layer — updates will impact any component that calls memory tools (skills, tool runner, UI/gateway).

Network/IO and daemon startup make this plugin sensitive to environment (API keys, local sockets, ports, file system paths like HERMES_HOME), so edits may affect CI/integration tests that expect no external calls.
Event loop and background tasks interact with process lifecycle and test harnesses; improper cleanup can cause test flakiness or hung processes in the larger application.
recommendations: Preserve the event loop creation and cross‑thread helpers (_get_loop/_run_sync) semantics; ensure any refactor maintains safe cross‑thread async invocation and graceful shutdown.
When modifying daemon startup/teardown logic, keep startup idempotency and explicit readiness checks; ensure clean termination to avoid orphaned processes or background tasks.
Do not change tool JSON schemas or handler signatures without coordinating updates to plugin.yaml and any consumers (tools/skill invocations) to maintain compatibility.
Treat retain/recall/reflect flows as eventually consistent and non‑blocking; respect existing background scheduling, retry/idempotency behavior, and ordering assumptions.
Run unit tests with external calls mocked and CI test profiles (integration/e2e) isolated; verify no real network/daemon calls occur during unit test runs to avoid CI exposure and flakiness.

website/docs/integrations.description: This folder contains the user-facing documentation for Hermes integrations: an index/overview page and a detailed providers page describing AI provider choices, endpoints, routing, and fallback behavior. These docs are the canonical place for users and operators to understand how the agent selects models, supplies credentials, and integrates with external messaging platforms and memory, and therefore must stay aligned with the runtime code and configuration.
key_behaviors: Provide a human-readable overview of supported integrations, how they connect to Hermes, and where to configure them.
Describe provider-specific details: endpoints, credential names, routing/fallback strategies, and expected behaviors when a provider fails or is unavailable.
Link and anchor to other technical docs (memory, gateway/platform adapters, config / env vars) so users can find operational and setup instructions.
Act as the authoritative documentation for external-facing configuration names and examples that operators will copy into environment files or deployments.

insights.title: Docs must mirror runtime configuration and env var names
insights.problem: If documentation uses different provider names, endpoints, or environment variable names than the running code, users will misconfigure the system and integration will fail.
insights.root_cause: Documentation drift: prose is edited independently of the implementation (agent adapters, credential pool, gateway adapters) and becomes outdated.
insights.solution: When editing these documents, verify and preserve exact identifiers and examples used by the codebase (credential keys, provider identifiers, endpoints) and confirm against the corresponding modules that implement provider routing and credential management.

insights.title: Index must reliably link to provider and memory docs
insights.problem: Broken or ambiguous cross-doc links reduce discoverability and can leave users unaware of critical setup steps (e.g., memory, gateway credentials).
insights.root_cause: Manual link maintenance and duplicated or moved files without updates to references.
insights.solution: Ensure index.md and providers.md maintain correct relative links to other docs (memory, gateway/platforms, CLI setup), and validate links as part of edits (site build/linkcheck).

insights.title: Duplicate or placeholder files exist
insights.problem: There is a duplicate providers.md path noted for resilience; editing one copy without awareness of duplicates can cause inconsistent content to appear in the published site.
insights.root_cause: Multiple files or placeholder/duplicate entries were kept to ensure tooling tolerance or legacy paths.
insights.solution: Be aware of duplicate file paths and update all relevant copies or the canonical source so published content remains consistent across builds and routes.

insights.title: Provider fallback and routing descriptions must match implementation
insights.problem: If the documented fallback ordering, retry semantics, or route selection differs from the code (smart_model_routing, credential_pool), operators will get unexpected runtime behavior under error conditions.
insights.root_cause: High-level documentation abstracts behavior and can omit edge cases or specific ordering used by the agent.
insights.solution: When changing language about routing/fallbacks, check the agent's routing and credential code paths to ensure statements about fallback order, soft/hard failures, and retry limits are accurate.

key_files.why: Landing page for integrations that orients users to provider choices, memory integration, and links to detailed provider docs; changes here affect discoverability and navigation.
Authoritative documentation of AI providers, endpoints, routing, and fallbacks; directly referenced by users when configuring credentials and selecting providers.
Duplicate/placeholder path noted for resilience; may be an alternate route used by tooling or legacy links and must be kept coherent with the main providers.md.

construct_relationships: index.md references providers.md and other operational docs (memory/gateway/config) to form a navigation graph for integration-related information.

providers.md describes provider identifiers, endpoints, and fallback semantics that correspond to runtime constructs: credential names in CredentialPool, routing in smart_model_routing, and adapters in agent/*_adapter.py.
Duplicate providers.md file(s) represent the same documentation node and must remain synchronized so the site's routing and any tooling that tolerates duplicate paths produce consistent output.
notable_relationships: These docs are upstream of runtime modules: agent/credential_pool.py, agent/smart_model_routing.py, and individual model adapter modules (e.g., agent/anthropic_adapter.py). Any mismatch between text and code can cause operator misconfiguration.

Configuration examples and environment variable names in these docs are consumed by deploy scripts, Dockerfile setups, and developer setup docs (.env.example, scripts/install.sh); edits must respect those downstream consumers.

Gateway/platform adapter docs (gateway/platforms/*) and memory docs are likely linked from index.md; changes here affect navigation to multi-component setup instructions used by operators of the gateway or messaging integrations.
recommendations: When editing, verify all provider names, endpoints, credential keys, and routing/fallback descriptions against the corresponding code (agent adapters, credential pool, routing) to avoid doc/code drift.
If you modify or consolidate the duplicate providers.md files, update every copy or the canonical source so published site output and any legacy paths remain consistent.
Run the documentation site build and a link checker after edits to confirm relative links (to memory, gateway, CLI setup) resolve and no broken anchors are introduced.
Preserve exact syntax in any copyable config snippets or environment examples; changes to examples should be cross-checked with deployment scripts and .env.example to prevent operational breakage.
Maintain the navigation structure in index.md: ensure edits do not orphan other integration docs (memory, gateway/platform adapters) referenced by the integration overview.

optional-skills/mcp/fastmcp.description: This subtree provides a small FastMCP starter-kit: a scaffold CLI plus three ready-to-use server templates (HTTP API wrapper, read‑only SQLite server, and file processor) and accompanying documentation. It exists to let developers quickly bootstrap FastMCP servers that expose tool functions via an expected mcp runtime interface; the scaffold and templates encode conventions about file/DB/network access, authentication via environment variables, and safe defaults.
key_behaviors: Template discovery and rendering: the scaffold CLI enumerates templates, substitutes a placeholder server name, and writes a scaffolded Python file to disk with overwrite protection.
Tool surface patterns: each template constructs an mcp object and exposes named tool functions (e.g., health_check, get_resource, list_tables, summarize_text_file) that the FastMCP runtime will call.

External I/O interactions: templates perform outbound HTTP calls, read local files, or open a SQLite DB; they expect tokens or configuration via environment variables and include basic validation/safety checks.
Safety/validation flows: templates include guards (path validation, UTF‑8 assumptions, SQL mutation rejection, query limits) intended to bound what a hosted MCP server can execute.

insights.title: Template rendering and file write safety
insights.problem: The scaffold CLI performs string substitution and writes generated files to disk, so naive input or path handling can lead to overwrites or invalid output.
insights.root_cause: Simple placeholder substitution combined with filesystem writes and only basic overwrite protection.
insights.solution: When modifying scaffold behavior, preserve explicit overwrite safeguards; ensure rendered filenames and server name inputs are validated/escaped and the write flow respects encoding and atomic write semantics to avoid partial files.

insights.title: Templates rely on environment tokens
insights.problem: Templates assume tokens are provided via environment variables and pass them to upstream services; this can accidentally expose credentials if logging or output is not careful.
insights.root_cause: Example code demonstrates header injection and token usage for HTTP requests without a centralized secret-management contract.
insights.solution: Treat template environment-credential usage as sensitive: avoid logging raw tokens, clearly document expected env var names, and maintain the convention when adjusting template code so callers and deploy scripts know where to supply credentials.

insights.title: File path and encoding assumptions
insights.problem: File-processing template assumes UTF-8, validates paths, and returns previews; incorrect assumptions or relaxed validation may allow path traversal or decoding errors.
insights.root_cause: Templates intentionally keep examples simple, using naive path checks and single-encoding handling.
insights.solution: Any edits must respect path validation and explicit encoding expectations. Preserve or strengthen checks that prevent reading outside intended directories and handle decoding errors robustly so the agent using these skills receives predictable results.

insights.title: Read-only enforcement for SQLite template
insights.problem: The database template enforces rejection of mutation queries but relies on simple validation; incomplete validation could permit destructive SQL.
insights.root_cause: Template implements a validation layer (e.g., rejecting SQL containing certain keywords) rather than using a separate read-only connection mode or database permissions.
insights.solution: Maintain explicit read-only behavior when editing; keep or enhance validation and consider the template's expectations (limits, result wrapping) so callers assume safe, read-only access.

insights.title: Consistent mcp tool signatures and runtime expectations
insights.problem: Templates follow a convention (an mcp object and named tool functions) that the rest of the system will expect; breaking those signatures would reduce interoperability with the FastMCP runtime or skill loader.
insights.root_cause: Templates are example implementations that implicitly define the contract between a FastMCP server and the agent runtime.
insights.solution: When editing templates or scaffold logic, preserve the external-facing names and return shapes (tool names, result wrappers, health_check semantics) documented in SKILL.md and fastmcp-cli.md so other parts of the codebase or external tooling can load and call these skills predictably.

key_files.why: CLI entry that discovers and renders templates; edits here change how templates are discovered, validated, and written to disk.
Example HTTP-backed FastMCP server demonstrating token usage, upstream API calls, and typical tool function patterns consumed by the FastMCP runtime.
Read-only SQLite template that encodes SQL validation and result shaping conventions for DB-backed skills.
File-processing template that demonstrates path validation, UTF-8 encoding expectations, previews, and search/summarization tool behavior.
Documentation that defines expected conventions and usage for FastMCP servers in this subtree; authoritative reference for the scaffold and templates.

construct_relationships: scripts/scaffold_fastmcp.py reads files from templates/ and performs placeholder substitution (server name) to produce a runnable Python file.
Each template file defines an mcp object and exposes multiple named tool functions (health_check, get_resource, list_tables, summarize_text_file, etc.) that the FastMCP runtime expects to call.

Templates depend on external resources or libraries (httpx or environment tokens for api_wrapper, sqlite3 for database_server, local filesystem for file_processor) and include local validation helpers (_request, _connect, _read_text, _reject_mutation) that mediate those interactions.
SKILL.md and references/fastmcp-cli.md describe the CLI usage and conventions that scaffold_fastmcp.py and the templates intend to follow; scaffold behavior should align with those docs.
notable_relationships: These optional skills are likely loaded by a higher-level skill loader or MCP client elsewhere in the codebase; changes to function names, return shapes, or expected env var names can break discovery and invocation by upstream components.

Templates will interact with external systems at runtime (HTTP APIs, local databases, file system). Deployers and the broader system must supply credentials, set appropriate file paths, and control network access.
scaffold_fastmcp.py relies on the templates directory layout; moving or renaming templates affects CLI discovery and any automation that bootstraps FastMCP servers.
recommendations: Preserve scaffold overwrite protection and validate the server name and output path when changing scaffold_fastmcp.py; ensure writes use explicit encoding and are atomic where possible.
Maintain the external-facing tool names, argument/return shapes, and the presence of an mcp object in templates so existing skill-loading and invocation logic remains compatible.
When touching templates that access secrets or external networks, avoid emitting secrets to logs and document env var names used; assume deployers will supply tokens via environment.
Retain or strengthen file path validation and UTF-8 handling in the file processor template to prevent path traversal and decoding errors; maintain clear behavior for previews and search results.
Keep read-only enforcement semantics in the database template; respect query limits and mutation rejection logic and be aware that simple keyword-based validation is brittle.

Run any unit tests that exercise skill discovery/scaffolding and smoke-test generated templates against the documented FastMCP runtime contract after edits; verify that documentation (SKILL.md / fastmcp-cli.md) remains in sync with code.

optional-skills/mlops/accelerate.description: This folder contains an optional MLops skill pack focused on HuggingFace Accelerate: a set of user-facing documentation and reference notes that explain how to scale training (including a Megatron-LM integration), how to interpret metrics, and how to tune performance. It functions as a self-contained knowledge module that other parts of the repository (or end users) rely on to configure distributed training runs and understand operational trade-offs.
key_behaviors: Provide an entrypoint overview and usage guidance for HuggingFace Accelerate (SKILL.md) to onboard users to the skill.
Offer focused reference documents that cover configuration/index types, a special-case Megatron-LM integration, performance tuning knobs, and metric definitions/interpretation.
Surface environment- and version-sensitive advice (hardware, distributed setup, and provider-specific integrations) rather than executable code.
Act as canonical documentation that should remain consistent with any code examples or integrations elsewhere in the repo.

insights.title: Duplicate content for Megatron-LM integration
insights.problem: There is at least one duplicate file (megatron-integration.md flagged as duplicate), which risks divergence when edits are made in only one place.
insights.root_cause: Multiple copies or mirrors of highly specific integration notes were created rather than a single canonical reference.
insights.solution: Treat the duplicated Megatron integration file as content that must be synchronized; when editing, ensure all copies (or canonical location) are updated so users don't read conflicting instructions.

insights.title: Advice depends on external tools, versions and runtime environments
insights.problem: Performance tuning and integration instructions are inherently tied to Accelerate, Megatron-LM, CUDA, and distributed runtime versions/configurations; stale or version-mismatched guidance can produce failed runs or misconfigured clusters.
