insights.root_cause: Docs describe runtime behavior and knobs that change across library releases and hardware setups.
insights.solution: When modifying guidance, preserve or clearly mark version-specific recommendations and ensure any examples reference explicit Accelerate/Megatron/runtime versions and environment assumptions.

insights.title: Performance and metrics guidance can be misapplied across environments
insights.problem: Performance tuning suggestions and metric interpretations may not generalize (e.g., single-node vs multi-node, NVLink vs Ethernet) and could lead users to misconfigure resource allocations or misinterpret health signals.
insights.root_cause: The reference material provides prescriptive tuning without embedded environment qualifiers.
insights.solution: Keep changes mindful of environment qualifiers — if editing performance or metrics content, retain or add explicit statements on scope (hardware topology, trainer/distributed launcher) so recommendations aren’t mistakenly applied out of context.

insights.title: Index/types and examples may be referenced by other artifacts
insights.problem: The index/types reference likely maps to configuration keys or interfaces used by scripts/examples elsewhere; changing terminology or expected types without coordination can break consumers.
insights.root_cause: Documentation serves as a de-facto spec for configuration shapes and expected values used by tooling.
insights.solution: Respect the documented names/types; when editing, verify that any renamed keys or altered examples remain compatible with other repo components that parse or present those configurations.

key_files.why: Primary entrypoint that frames the Accelerate skill, links to the reference documents, and guides users on when/how to use the materials.
Defines configuration/index types and typical usage patterns that are likely relied upon by example runs or other repo tooling.
Contains specialized integration notes for Megatron-LM; flagged as duplicate so it is an important integration surface with higher risk of divergence.
Defines metrics and interpretation guidance used to evaluate training and performance; consumers will rely on precise definitions.
Contains tuning and performance guidance (knobs and trade-offs) that influence runtime configurations and expectations for resource use.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references and links to the detailed reference documents (index_types.md, metrics.md, performance.md, megatron-integration.md) and functions as the high-level guide tying those pieces together.
index_types.md provides the configuration shape and terminology that performance.md and megatron-integration.md assume when giving examples and tuning instructions.
metrics.md supplies metric definitions that performance.md uses to explain tuning outcomes and trade-offs (i.e., performance tuning advice is justified by specific metrics).
megatron-integration.md is a special-case extension of Accelerate guidance and reuses the same index/types and performance considerations but introduces provider-specific integration steps.
notable_relationships: This documentation depends on external projects (HuggingFace Accelerate, Megatron-LM, CUDA drivers, distributed launchers) — changes must consider external API and runtime changes.
Other optional-skills or top-level mlops artifacts in the repository may reference these docs as canonical guidance; edits here can affect downstream examples, CI scripts, or user workflows.
Because SKILL.md is the entrypoint for users, edits can change user expectations across the gateway/CLI flows that present or link to optional skills.
recommendations: Preserve SKILL.md as the canonical entrypoint; if moving or renaming reference files, update links within SKILL.md and any other references elsewhere in the repo.

When editing content that references configuration keys or types, verify compatibility with any tooling or examples that parse those configurations (check other folders that implement mlops scripts or examples).
Treat the megatron-integration document as duplicated content: ensure all copies are synchronized simultaneously to avoid conflicting instructions.
Keep version and environment qualifiers intact: ensure any version numbers, hardware topology assumptions, or launcher commands remain explicit and accurate.

Avoid changing metric definitions or semantics without confirming downstream consumers (dashboards, monitoring scripts) that depend on those definitions; validate wording to prevent misinterpretation.

When adjusting performance guidance, do not remove environment-scoped qualifiers (single-node vs multi-node, network vs intra-node bandwidth) and ensure recommended knobs map to actual Accelerate/Megatron options.

optional-skills/mlops/chroma.description: This folder contains documentation for the optional Chroma vector-database skill used by Hermes. It explains how to configure and integrate Chroma with Hermes and how to use it via downstream retrieval libraries (LangChain, LlamaIndex). The content is purely integration/docs-facing and informs optional mlops workflows rather than providing runtime code.
key_behaviors: Describes how Hermes users can wire Chroma as a persistent vector store for embeddings and retrieval.
Explains integration patterns with common retrieval toolkits (LangChain, LlamaIndex) so the agent/tooling stack can perform similarity search.
Specifies configuration and operational considerations (persistence path, host/port, environment or extras) required to enable the optional skill.
Acts as the authoritative reference for examples and compatibility notes when enabling the optional Chroma skill.

insights.title: Docs may become inconsistent with code or dependency changes
insights.problem: The textual guides (SKILL.md and integration.md) are the only artifacts in this subtree; if code or external libraries change, the docs can quickly become outdated.
insights.root_cause: These are static markdown files separated from the code that actually implements or uses Chroma; no automated linkage to package dependency versions or runtime tests is implied.
insights.solution: Treat the files as the single source of user-facing instructions and ensure edits check compatibility with package extras, examples in tools/ or optional-skills, and any referenced code paths (embedding, persistence locations, client config). When modifying, verify examples against the current runtime environment and dependency matrix.

insights.title: External library compatibility (Chroma, LangChain, LlamaIndex)
insights.problem: Integration examples reference third-party packages and APIs whose versions/behavior change independently of this repo.
insights.root_cause: The docs assume specific client APIs and behaviors for Chroma and downstream libraries; Hermes installation extras may or may not include matching versions.
insights.solution: When editing docs, make explicit which package versions or pyproject extras are required and validate commands/examples with those versions. Ensure text aligns with the project's extras and packaging policy so users can install required dependencies.

insights.title: Persistence location, runtime config, and environment expectations
insights.problem: Chroma requires configuration (persistence directory, server host/port, embedding model hookup) which, if misdocumented, leads to incorrect runtime setups and data placed outside HERMES_HOME.
insights.root_cause: Documentation might reference default paths or environment variables that differ from the agent's HERMES_HOME or the Dockerfile/entrypoint defaults.
insights.solution: Ensure any referenced paths, env vars, and example commands match project-wide defaults (HERMES_HOME) and container settings. Verify the examples do not assume privileged ports or filesystems and note persistence implications for backups and volumes.

insights.title: Local/remote Chroma instances and potential exposure
insights.problem: Integration examples may direct users to run local servers or networked instances without warning about ports, credentials, or exposure.
insights.root_cause: Docs focus on usage patterns and integration but may omit operational warnings about network binding, authentication, or data leakage.
insights.solution: When updating text, respect and call out the operational model implied by examples (local vs remote), and ensure any commands or config snippets do not inadvertently recommend insecure defaults. Cross-check with repository-wide security policies for optional services.

key_files.why: Primary user-facing documentation describing how the Chroma optional skill integrates with Hermes and how to enable/use it.
Detailed integration guide covering examples with LangChain and LlamaIndex; it contains concrete code snippets and interoperability guidance that must match library APIs and project extras.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references integration patterns and likely points to code-level concepts such as embeddings, persistence path, and retrieval workflows described in references/integration.md.
Integration guide ties Hermes' skill concept to external libraries (LangChain, LlamaIndex) by showing how to wrap Hermes embeddings and storage with those toolkits' vector-store adapters.
Both documents implicitly depend on project-wide configuration constructs (HERMES_HOME, pyproject extras) and on the agent/tooling layers that call vector stores for retrieval.
notable_relationships: This subtree is an optional-skills doc set—changes should be consistent with pyproject.toml extras and the packaging policy for optional dependencies (the 'mlops' or 'chroma' extras).
Integration examples will be used by tool implementations and agent components (embedding providers, memory_manager, tools/*) and therefore must align with those subsystems' expected interfaces.

Operational defaults referenced here (persistence directories, server host/port) must be consistent with Dockerfile, HERMES_HOME, and any gateway/platform run-time configurations documented elsewhere in the repo.
recommendations: Treat these files as documentation-only artifacts: when editing, verify all examples against actual library versions and the project extras declared in pyproject.toml.
Confirm any referenced paths, env vars, or defaults align with HERMES_HOME and Docker/container settings to avoid mismatches in where persistent data is stored.
When editing examples that show networked Chroma instances, ensure any host/port or auth notes match the repo's security expectations and do not encourage insecure defaults.

If the edits change required external packages or versions, update packaging (extras) and verify installation before merging; run a small local integration smoke test of the steps in the doc to validate them.

Remember CI/test isolation: repository CI blanks provider keys; do not rely on external services in automated tests unless explicitly marked as integration/e2e. If new integration instructions require running a server, document how to run it locally but do not add tests that depend on external network services.

optional-skills/mlops/faiss.description: This folder contains the optional MLOps skill documentation for integrating FAISS-based vector similarity search into the agent. It explains FAISS index types, tradeoffs, and guidance needed by developers who enable a FAISS-backed retrieval layer or who reproduce examples from the optional-skills subsystem.
key_behaviors: Documentation-focused: provides human-facing guidance and examples for choosing and using FAISS index types (creation, persistence, and search).
Integration guidance: describes how FAISS indices should be configured relative to vector dimensionality, metric choice, and memory/storage constraints.
Operational considerations: highlights platform and dependency concerns (CPU vs GPU builds, serialization portability) that affect deployment and testing of the FAISS-backed skill.

insights.title: FAISS build variants (cpu/gpu) matter at runtime
insights.problem: Code and examples may assume a FAISS build that isn’t present in the runtime environment, causing import or runtime failures.
insights.root_cause: FAISS has multiple binary distributions (cpu vs gpu) and native dependencies that are not pure-Python; CI/dev environments may not install the required variant.
insights.solution: Documentation and examples must clearly state which FAISS variant is required, ensure examples avoid GPU-only APIs unless GPU is present, and callers must verify availability before invoking FAISS-specific flows.

insights.title: Index type must match data properties and retrieval semantics
insights.problem: Using an inappropriate FAISS index (e.g., an index that requires inner-product vectors without normalization) yields incorrect similarity results or runtime errors.
insights.root_cause: FAISS index variants differ by supported metric, vector dimensionality assumptions, and index-building parameters; examples may omit explicit guidance on normalization or metric selection.
insights.solution: Examples and skill docs should pair index types with metric and preprocessing steps (e.g., L2 vs inner-product, when to normalize), and callers must ensure input embeddings conform to those expectations.

insights.title: Saved FAISS indexes are binary and version-sensitive
insights.problem: Serialized FAISS index files may be incompatible between FAISS versions or between CPU/GPU builds, causing load failures when moving indexes between environments.
insights.root_cause: FAISS uses native binary serialization that can change across versions or be tied to build variants; there is no guaranteed cross-version compatibility.
insights.solution: Documentation should warn about version and build mismatches, recommend storing metadata (FAISS version, index type, vector_dim, metric) alongside index files, and advise validating loads in target environments.

insights.title: Index selection impacts memory and latency
insights.problem: Examples that don't call out resource tradeoffs can lead to deployments that exhaust memory or have unacceptable query latency.
insights.root_cause: Different index types (flat, IVF, HNSW, PQ) trade off accuracy, memory, and query speed; defaults may not be suitable for large collections.
insights.solution: Docs should surface tradeoffs and recommend testing with realistic data sizes and hardware; callers must consider shard/flush strategies and monitor resource usage.

insights.title: Embedding dimensionality and schema assumptions
insights.problem: Index inserts or queries fail or return poor results when embedding dimensionality or preprocessing differs between index build and query time.
insights.root_cause: Examples may not emphasize enforcing consistent embedding dimensions, normalization, or tokenization steps across pipeline stages.
insights.solution: Ensure examples include explicit checks and clear instructions to validate embedding vectors (dimension, dtype, normalization) before building or querying an index.

key_files.why: Primary human-facing skill README that defines the FAISS integration, usage patterns, and any top-level operational notes the optional skill consumer must follow.
Detailed reference that enumerates FAISS index types, their tradeoffs, and examples — used as the authoritative mapping between retrieval behavior and index selection.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references and links to index_types.md to provide detailed guidance; index_types.md is the lower-level reference called by the top-level skill documentation.
Documentation describes interactions among embeddings (input vectors), FAISS index construction (index type, parameters), and runtime search/serialization flows.
Guidance assumes upstream producers of vectors (embedding providers in agent/) must produce consistent vectors (dimension/normalization) consumed by FAISS index creation and queries.
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency on embedding providers (agent model adapters and prompt/embedding builders): the FAISS skill expects embeddings compatible in dimension and preprocessing.

Deployment constraints with packaging and extras: FAISS native packages may require adding extras or native dependencies in packaging (pyproject/requirements/Dockerfile) — runtime environments must match the documented FAISS variant.
Persistence and HERMES_HOME: saved index files referenced by the skill will live in agent data paths; migrations or restores must account for FAISS version and build compatibility.
Testing and CI: integration tests that exercise FAISS behavior must ensure the CI environment installs a compatible FAISS binary or mock/skip those tests.
recommendations: When editing files here, preserve and update any FAISS build/variant notes and explicitly include FAISS version/build metadata in examples.
Ensure examples and code snippets consistently state expected embedding dimensionality, dtype, and any normalization steps.
Avoid introducing examples that assume GPU-only APIs unless the documentation and CI indicate GPU availability; document alternatives for CPU-only environments.
If changing serialization examples or filenames, maintain explicit metadata (faiss_version, index_type, vector_dim) alongside persisted index artifacts.
Validate that any example commands or installation instructions align with project packaging (pyproject extras, Dockerfile) and note when additional native dependencies are required.
When making edits that affect retrieval semantics, consider downstream consumers (agent embeddings, tools) and ensure the docs remain consistent with their expectations.

optional-skills/mlops/flash-attention.description: This folder holds optional skill documentation for integrating and benchmarking Flash Attention to accelerate transformer inference/training. It is purely documentation-focused: usage instructions (SKILL.md), practical notes for enabling Flash Attention with HuggingFace Transformers, and benchmarking results/recipes. The content informs optional, hardware- and build-sensitive capabilities that must remain consistent with the rest of the codebase and deployment artifacts.
key_behaviors: Documenting installation and build steps required to enable Flash Attention on supported hardware (CUDA/PyTorch/Triton builds).
Describing integration steps and code flags necessary to enable Flash Attention within HuggingFace Transformers workflows.
Presenting benchmarking methodology, measured results, and reproducible commands for comparing Flash Attention vs baseline implementations.

insights.title: Hardware and runtime constraints
insights.problem: Flash Attention requires specific GPU drivers, CUDA versions, and PyTorch/Triton builds which are not universally available across environments.
insights.root_cause: The acceleration relies on native/binary extensions and tight coupling with CUDA and specific PyTorch ABI/flags.
insights.solution: Document explicit supported CUDA/PyTorch/Triton versions, note OS/build caveats, and identify environments where Flash Attention is optional rather than required; warn against assuming availability in CI or in minimal developer setups.

insights.title: Benchmark fragility and variance
insights.problem: Benchmark numbers can vary greatly depending on hardware, driver versions, model configuration, and build options, making stale benchmarks misleading.
insights.root_cause: Benchmarks in the folder are static snapshots that do not capture the full variability or the exact environment used to generate them.
insights.solution: Include the exact commands, environment variables, dependency pins, and runtime configuration used to produce results; mark results with hardware identifiers and provide reproducible scripts or seed instructions.

insights.title: Transformer version sensitivity
insights.problem: Steps to enable Flash Attention with HuggingFace Transformers can change across Transformers releases and may require code/flag changes.
insights.root_cause: Transformers' internal APIs and integration points evolve, and Flash Attention patches or configuration flags are version-dependent.
insights.solution: Tie documented integration instructions to specific Transformers versions and document fallback behavior or detection logic to avoid silent breakage when users run on incompatible versions.

insights.title: Optional-skill isolation
insights.problem: Documentation that implies or prescribes installation of heavy native dependencies could be construed as required and lead to bloated installs or CI failures.
insights.root_cause: This folder lives under optional-skills but its instructions may reference global project defaults or lack clear isolation guidance.
insights.solution: Clearly label steps as optional, avoid referencing project-wide dependency management as mandatory, and note that enabling Flash Attention should not change core runtime behavior or CI unless intentionally enabled.

insights.title: Binary/build licensing and distribution risks
insights.problem: Native extensions and third-party builds may have licensing or redistribution constraints not obvious to users.
insights.root_cause: Flash Attention and related tooling often involve upstream repos, specialized compilers (Triton), and binaries with their own license/usage terms.
insights.solution: Call out licensing or redistribution considerations in documentation and specify when users must build from source versus installing prebuilt wheels.

key_files.why: Primary user-facing documentation summarizing the skill, when to enable Flash Attention, and top-level usage notes.
Detailed instructions for enabling Flash Attention within HuggingFace Transformers — contains the version-dependent integration steps consumers will follow.
Benchmarks and comparison data that justify enabling the skill and provide reproducibility details for performance claims.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references and summarizes detailed instructions in references/transformers-integration.md to present a high-level enablement workflow.

Benchmarks (references/benchmarks.md) depend on the exact integration steps outlined in references/transformers-integration.md and thus implicitly require the same environment/flags to reproduce results.

All files together form a documentation triad: capability summary (SKILL.md) → integration recipe (transformers-integration.md) → empirical validation (benchmarks.md); changes in any integration details should be reflected across the other two documents.
notable_relationships: This folder's instructions have upstream dependencies on the runtime environment (CUDA, GPU drivers), PyTorch/Triton builds, and HuggingFace Transformers versions maintained elsewhere in the project.

Any changes that introduce installation steps or dependency pins here could affect packaging, Docker images, or CI if they are propagated to top-level dependency manifests (pyproject.toml, Dockerfile, installer scripts).

The folder is optional and should not impose changes on core agents, model adapters, or gateway code; consumers of model adapters (agent/anthropic_adapter.py, etc.) may benefit from Flash Attention but do not depend on it being present.
recommendations: Preserve clear statements of optionality: edits must not make Flash Attention appear mandatory for core functionality.
When updating integration or benchmark instructions, ensure exact version numbers, build commands, environment variables, and hardware identifiers are included so results are reproducible.
Avoid introducing implicit global dependency changes; if documentation calls for installing additional packages, verify whether packaging manifests or Dockerfiles need coordinated changes elsewhere.
Assume CI and many developer environments lack CUDA/GPUs; do not rely on CI to validate Flash Attention instructions unless a separate GPU-enabled CI job is provisioned.
If renaming or moving files, update any cross-references in SKILL.md and other docs; maintain relative paths and link integrity.

optional-skills/mlops/hermes-atropos-env.description: This folder contains documentation and reference specifications for building Hermes "Atropos" reinforcement-learning environments used by the optional MLOps skillset. It defines the expected BaseEnv interface, the shape and extraction of AgentResult objects, and common usage/evaluation patterns so environment implementations interoperate with Hermes training/evaluation tooling and downstream pipelines.
key_behaviors: Environment implementations conform to a shared BaseEnv interface (lifecycle methods, state/observation/reward semantics).
Environments produce AgentResult dataclass instances with a defined set of fields consumed by trainers, evaluators, and extractors.
Standard usage follows a process/evaluate/process loop: step the environment, collect AgentResult(s), evaluate outcomes, persist trajectories/metrics.
Deterministic/evaluable runs require seeding, explicit metric emission, and reproducible state serialization for offline training and analysis.

insights.title: BaseEnv is the canonical contract for environment implementations
insights.problem: If environment implementations diverge from the BaseEnv interface, downstream trainers and evaluators will fail or behave inconsistently.
insights.root_cause: The interface prescribes lifecycle, observation, and return semantics but is only a documented reference; implementations may omit fields or methods.
insights.solution: Treat the BaseEnv reference as the authoritative contract — ensure implementations provide the documented methods, lifecycle behaviors, and documented data shapes before changing signatures or behavior.

insights.title: AgentResult field stability is required across tools
insights.problem: Changes to AgentResult fields or their meanings break extractors, metric collectors, and trajectory storage.
insights.root_cause: Multiple consumers (trainers, evaluators, analysis tools) assume particular field names/types without runtime validation.
insights.solution: Preserve AgentResult field names/types or provide explicit migration/versioning; validate AgentResult instances at environment boundaries and update dependent consumers if the schema changes.

insights.title: Usage patterns standardize evaluation loops and expectations
insights.problem: Inconsistent handling of evaluation runs (e.g., mixed training vs. evaluation modes, missing deterministic seeding) produces non-reproducible metrics and invalid comparisons.
insights.root_cause: Usage patterns document intended flows but code may not enforce mode separation, seeding, or metric collection hooks.
insights.solution: Respect the documented process/evaluate/process patterns: ensure evaluation mode, seeding, and metric emission are explicitly set and observed by environment and orchestration code.

insights.title: Serialization and metrics integration are cross-cutting concerns
insights.problem: Trajectory serialization or metric formats that differ from downstream expectations prevent offline training and analysis, and can silently drop important fields.
insights.root_cause: References document structures but do not enforce serialization contract; different components may use different persistence formats or omit metadata.
insights.solution: Maintain consistent serialization formats and include required metadata (timestamps, seeds, env version); ensure metric hooks align with downstream consumers and that stored trajectories include the full AgentResult payload.

key_files.why: Top-level guide describing goals and how to build/use Atropos environments — primary onboarding reference for contributors.
Defines the BaseEnv interface and lifecycle/data shape that environment implementations must follow — the authoritative contract.
Documents the AgentResult dataclass fields and extraction helpers that downstream trainers/evaluators rely on for metrics and trajectory assembly.
Describes canonical process/evaluate/process flows and evaluation semantics that must be preserved for reproducible experiments and correct metric reporting.

construct_relationships: Implementations in this subtree must implement the BaseEnv interface described in atropos-base-env.md (lifecycle methods, step/reset/serialize semantics).
Environments return AgentResult dataclass instances (agentresult-fields.md); extractors/helpers read those fields to produce training/evaluation artifacts.
Usage patterns (usage-patterns.md) orchestrate how BaseEnv.step/AgentResult emission are sequenced (process → evaluate → process) and how seeding/metrics should be applied.
Trajectory persistence and offline training pipelines consume the AgentResult shape and expect metadata produced by BaseEnv serialization hooks.
notable_relationships: Downstream MLOps/training pipelines expect stable AgentResult fields and serialized trajectories — the docs here are relied on by other ML code outside this folder.

Environment implementations interact with Hermes core components (skills, agent execution) via the AgentResult contract; any change affects skill-based invocations and RL adapters elsewhere in the repository.
Metrics, logging, and storage backends (trajectory store, HERMES_HOME persistence) must be supplied the metadata and formats implied by the references; changing those formats has system-wide impact.
Evaluation semantics interact with experiment orchestration (schedulers, reproducibility tooling) and with any external evaluators that consume exported trajectories or metrics.
recommendations: Treat atropos-base-env.md and agentresult-fields.md as authoritative contracts: do not change method signatures, field names, or types without coordinating updates to all consumers and adding migration/versioning.
If modifying any AgentResult fields or BaseEnv semantics, update SKILL.md and usage-patterns.md simultaneously and ensure downstream trainers/evaluators are adapted.
When editing, validate serialization formats and include/backfill required metadata (seed, env version, timestamps) so stored trajectories remain usable by offline pipelines.
Preserve evaluation-mode semantics and deterministic seeding; add or run tests that exercise the process/evaluate/process loop, metric emission, and reproducibility.
Verify integration points with trainers, trajectory storage, and metrics/logging systems after changes — ensure consumers still parse AgentResult payloads and environment serialization.

optional-skills/mlops/huggingface-tokeni.description: This folder contains documentation for an optional MLOps skill that focuses on Hugging Face tokenizers: algorithm theory (BPE, WordPiece, Unigram), practical tokenizer training, and integration with the Transformers ecosystem. It is a purely documentation/artifact-guidance subtree that informs how to create, train, persist, and plug custom tokenizers into the larger Hermes Agent pipeline and deployment workflows.
key_behaviors: Explain and compare tokenizer algorithms (BPE, WordPiece, Unigram) to guide selection for a corpus and downstream model.
Document step-by-step training workflows for producing a custom tokenizer (data preparation, training commands, artifact outputs).
Show integration patterns for using Hugging Face tokenizers with Transformers and downstream components (serialization formats, API usage, loading in runtime).
Provide a skill-level overview (SKILL.md) that ties algorithm knowledge and operational guidance into the optional MLOps skill flag used by the rest of the project.

insights.title: Tokenizers rely on non-core Python packages and version constraints
insights.problem: Documentation describes usage of Hugging Face tokenizers and Transformers which require additional Python packages and possibly specific versions or extras.
insights.root_cause: Tokenizer training and runtime integration depend on optional third-party libraries not installed by the core package and may break with incompatible versions.
insights.solution: When working with or referencing these docs, assume consumers must enable/ install optional extras, pin compatible versions, and validate environment; ensure serialized artifacts use stable tokenizer formats supported by the target Transformers/runtime versions.

insights.title: Trained tokenizers produce serialized artifacts that must be persisted and discovered
insights.problem: Training guidance implies generated tokenizer files (vocab, merges, tokenizer.json) but does not manage storage or naming conventions.
insights.root_cause: Docs are instructional and do not enforce a repo-level artifact layout or runtime discovery mechanism.
insights.solution: Treat the documentation as authoritative for expected artifact file names/locations; any runtime code that loads custom tokenizers must be made aware of those conventions and HERMES_HOME / skill storage paths.

insights.title: Tokenization choices affect prompt construction and token accounting
insights.problem: Different tokenizers change token counts and thus cost/length behavior for prompt builders and context compressors.
insights.root_cause: PromptBuilder, prompt caching, and context compression rely on tokenization when estimating tokens; documentation covers tokenizer behavior but runtime components may assume different tokenization.
insights.solution: Assume tokenizer docs are a reference that must align with runtime tokenization used by model adapters; any edits must preserve clear mapping between tokenizer selection and downstream token estimation semantics.

insights.title: Tokenizer training depends on corpus preprocessing and hyperparameters
insights.problem: Training.md and algorithms.md note parameter choices and preprocessing steps; small changes can materially alter resulting tokenization.
insights.root_cause: Tokenization algorithms and training hyperparameters (vocab size, special tokens, normalization) are sensitive and not reversible from model artifacts alone.
insights.solution: Ensure documentation records exact commands, seed, and preprocessing steps; consumers should treat training outputs as tied to that documented configuration and preserve metadata alongside artifacts.

insights.title: This subtree is an optional skill and must be discoverable by higher-level orchestration
insights.problem: SKILL.md provides a human-facing entry but there may be no machine-readable metadata linking this skill into the optional-skills registry.
insights.root_cause: Optional skills directory contains documentation rather than programmatic registration; orchestration/installer may not enumerate or install skill-specific dependencies automatically.
insights.solution: When making edits, assume SKILL.md is the canonical human guide; operational tooling or installers must explicitly check for and handle optional skill dependencies and artifacts referenced here.

key_files.why: Top-level skill manifest / overview that ties algorithm, training, and integration docs into the optional MLOps skill; primary entrypoint for users and installers.
Operational guide for training custom tokenizers; defines commands, expected artifact outputs, and reproducibility considerations that downstream code will assume.
Practical integration instructions for loading and using trained tokenizers within Transformers and runtime code paths; informs runtime compatibility and loading expectations.
Algorithmic reference comparing BPE/WordPiece/Unigram; influences selection decisions and parameterization described in training/integration documents.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references and summarizes the deeper guides in references/* to present a single skill-level view linking algorithm selection, training steps, and integration patterns.
training.md prescribes artifact filenames and formats that integration.md expects for runtime loading into Transformers and tokenizers APIs.
algorithms.md provides conceptual rationale that training.md maps to concrete hyperparameter choices (vocab size, normalization), creating a chain from theory -> training -> runtime behavior.
integration.md must align with runtime token estimation behavior relied upon by PromptBuilder and context compression elsewhere in the codebase (token counts, special tokens).
notable_relationships: This documentation affects optional-skills/ mlops workflows and therefore has downstream impact on agent components that rely on tokenizer behavior (agent/prompt_builder.py, agent/prompt_caching.py, agent/context_compressor.py).

The artifact conventions and storage hints in training.md interact with repository/global persistence settings (HERMES_HOME and packaging/deploy scripts, Docker image volumes) and any skill installer logic.

Dependency requirements implied by these docs (tokenizers, transformers) intersect with pyproject extras and Docker build steps; CI and packaging must account for optional heavy deps if automated tests or builds exercise these workflows.

Because this is an optional skill, installers or orchestration code that enumerate and enable optional-skills must treat dependencies and runtime loading as opt-in; there may be no automatic activation otherwise.
recommendations: Preserve SKILL.md as the canonical human entry; maintain relative links in references/ so tooling or readers can navigate without modifying higher-level registries.

When editing documentation, ensure any described artifact filenames, serialization formats, and command-line examples remain consistent with runtime loader conventions used elsewhere (prompt builders, model adapters).

Assume consumers may not have optional dependencies installed; include or keep notes about required pip extras / package versions and where trained artifacts should be placed (HERMES_HOME or skill-specific path).
Be mindful of reproducibility: retain exact training commands, seed, and preprocessing steps stated in training.md because runtime behavior, token counts, and downstream costs depend on them.

Do not introduce implicit assumptions that installers will auto-install heavy dependencies; any references to runtime code that imports tokenizers/transformers must be accompanied by explicit dependency notes so CI and packaging can opt-in safely.

optional-skills/mlops/instructor.description: This folder houses the optional "Instructor" MLOps skill documentation: the SKILL manifest and three reference guides (examples, provider configuration, and advanced validation). It serves as the authoritative human-readable specification for how the skill expects model outputs, how to configure different model providers, and how to validate and parse structured outputs in downstream code paths.
key_behaviors: Provide a canonical skill manifest and usage guidance that drive how the Instructor skill is registered and invoked.
Document provider-specific configuration and expectations (Claude, OpenAI, local models) that map to runtime adapters and credentials.
Describe and demonstrate structured-output extraction workflows and validation strategies (Pydantic-based patterns).
Offer concrete examples that illustrate end-to-end prompt -> model -> parse -> validated-structure flows used by integrators and tests.

insights.title: SKILL.md functions as the skill's contract
insights.problem: Consumers (humans or loader code) expect SKILL.md and the reference docs to accurately reflect the skill's runtime schema, invocation pattern, and metadata.
insights.root_cause: The skill manifest and auxiliary docs are separate from implementation and are not automatically synchronized with code or runtime schemas.
insights.solution: Treat these files as the authoritative contract: when editing the skill's runtime schema, invocation API, or metadata, verify and update SKILL.md and the reference docs to avoid mismatches; beware that stale documentation can cause integration and runtime failures.

insights.title: Provider-specific guidance impacts runtime behavior
insights.problem: Different model providers and local runtimes produce different output formats, token limits, and capabilities, which can break extraction or increase validation failure rates.
insights.root_cause: Provider docs describe behavior (system messages, streaming, tokenization) that affect prompt design and parsing but providers are external and evolve independently.
insights.solution: Ensure any code modifications that change prompt shape, expected output formatting, or timeouts take into account the provider-specific notes in providers.md; verify environment variable and credential names referenced in provider docs align with runtime credential management.

insights.title: Pydantic-based validation patterns are sensitive to model output variance
insights.problem: Strict validation examples may reject otherwise acceptable model outputs or produce brittle parsing logic under small format differences.
insights.root_cause: Validation examples assume the model adheres to the structured schema exactly; natural language model output can deviate.
insights.solution: When editing code that changes expected schema or parsing logic, cross-check with validation.md patterns and test against noisy/edge-case outputs; ensure Pydantic version expectations and import paths used in examples remain compatible with project dependencies.

insights.title: Examples document real usage and implicitly encode assumptions
insights.problem: Examples.md may become stale when prompts, schema, or provider configs change, reducing their usefulness for implementers and tests.
insights.root_cause: Examples are static markdown separated from code and tests.
insights.solution: When changing formats, prompts, or validation behavior, update examples to reflect new flows and consider that downstream editors or automated checks might rely on the patterns expressed in examples.md.

key_files.why: Primary skill manifest and high-level usage contract for the Instructor skill; likely consumed by skill loader and by humans configuring the skill.
Documents provider-specific configuration and constraints; maps to runtime adapters and credential names used elsewhere in the codebase.
Contains advanced Pydantic validation patterns that callers or skill code may follow; changing validation expectations has downstream parsing implications.
Illustrates end-to-end examples of extracting structured data; used by integrators and for sanity-checking changes to prompt/parse logic.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references and summarizes the provider guidance and validation approaches contained in references/providers.md and references/validation.md.
Examples in references/examples.md demonstrate the combined flow of provider configuration (providers.md) -> prompt/response shape -> validation (validation.md).
Validation patterns (validation.md) presuppose schema contracts described in SKILL.md and are intended to be applied to model outputs produced under provider configurations (providers.md).
notable_relationships: Provider guidance in providers.md ties to core model adapter code (e.g., OpenAI/Anthropic adapters) and credential management (credential pool/environment variables) in the broader agent — any mismatch in provider names, env var names, or capability notes can cause misconfiguration at runtime.

Validation patterns assume Pydantic and project dependency versions; compatibility with the project's pyproject.toml (Pydantic version) matters when editors change validation examples or recommended code.

Examples and SKILL.md may be consumed by skill registration or loader tooling in other directories; structural changes here can affect skill discovery or runtime invocation if that tooling parses these files.
recommendations: When editing these files or related code, verify that SKILL.md remains consistent with any code-level schema or manifest the runtime expects (the loader or registration system may parse SKILL.md).
Confirm provider names, environment variable names, and capability notes in providers.md align with the actual model adapter implementations and the project's credential management conventions.
Check Pydantic version compatibility and import paths before changing validation.md content; run tests that exercise parsing/validation logic against representative noisy model outputs.
Update examples.md whenever prompt shapes, expected output formats, or validation behavior change; consider executing example flows against a local or mocked provider to validate assumptions.
Run unit tests and the relevant integration/e2e scenarios that cover model output parsing and skill invocation after any change that affects schemas, prompts, or provider configuration.

optional-skills/mlops/lambda-labs.description: This directory contains the optional MLOps 'Lambda Labs' skill documentation for provisioning GPUs, running training and inference, and resolving issues when using Lambda Labs cloud infrastructure. It is a docs-first subtree that encodes operational procedures, distributed-training patterns (DDP/FSDP/DeepSpeed), API automation examples, and troubleshooting guidance which other code, automation scripts, or user-facing skill manifests may rely on.
key_behaviors: Onboarding and environment preparation for using Lambda Labs (credentials, images, and instance selection).
Launching and orchestrating model training and inference workloads on Lambda Labs, including single-node and distributed setups.
Applying advanced distributed-training strategies (DDP, FSDP, DeepSpeed) and corresponding environment/configuration patterns.
Automating infrastructure and job lifecycle via Lambda Labs APIs (scripted job creation, monitoring, and teardown).
Diagnosing and resolving common infrastructure and training failures (network, GPU memory, driver/container mismatches).

insights.title: Docs act as the single source of truth for Lambda Labs usage in this skill
insights.problem: Operational and automation code may drift from documented examples (config flags, CLI/API parameters, environment variables).
insights.root_cause: Documentation is stored separately from implementation and examples can become stale as APIs, tooling, or library versions change.
insights.solution: When making changes that affect training or API automation behavior, ensure docs are updated in lockstep and examples remain runnable; validate examples against the current SDK/API and version pins.

insights.title: Distributed training patterns require matching runtime assumptions
insights.problem: DDP/FSDP/DeepSpeed examples assume network topology, NCCL configuration, and specific container/runtime setups that, if mismatched, lead to hangs or OOMs.
insights.root_cause: Distributed strategies depend on consistent environment variables, synchronization endpoints, and matching library versions across nodes/containers.
insights.solution: Any code edits that change launch semantics, container images, or environment propagation must preserve the exact environment configuration described in the advanced-usage guidance; automated launch scripts must set and expose the same variables, ports, and mounts that the docs expect.

insights.title: API-driven automation must safeguard credentials and error handling
insights.problem: Automation examples may show direct API usage without explicit guidance on credential management, retries, or idempotency, creating risk of leaked keys or brittle automation.
insights.root_cause: Documentation-level examples prioritize clarity over production-grade patterns for credentials, rate limiting, and exponential backoff.
insights.solution: Treat any code that implements API automation as requiring secure credential injection, robust retry/backoff and idempotency design; ensure docs and code avoid hardcoding keys and document required env vars/permissions.

insights.title: Troubleshooting guidance ties to concrete logs and runtime artifacts
insights.problem: Troubleshooting steps reference specific log locations, CLI exits, or error signatures that may not match modified runtime locations or new orchestrator behavior.
insights.root_cause: Operational fixes assume where logs and artifacts are produced; code or configuration changes (log paths, container drivers, storage mounts) can invalidate these references.
insights.solution: When changing logging, runtime directories, or orchestration pathways, ensure the troubleshooting docs are updated to point to the current log locations and error traces so operators can follow them reliably.

key_files.why: Primary user-facing skill README that summarizes how to use Lambda Labs from the skill; likely referenced by the skill registry or onboarding flows.
Contains advanced distributed-training recipes (DDP/FSDP/DeepSpeed) and API automation patterns that implementation code or scripts will need to match.
Captures operational failure modes and resolution steps; important when changing runtime, logging, or orchestration behavior.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md aggregates and references the more specialized reference documents (advanced-usage.md, troubleshooting.md) to form a cohesive skill guide.
advanced-usage.md prescribes runtime and orchestration assumptions (DDP/FSDP/DeepSpeed configs and API automation flows) that any automation scripts or training launchers must implement consistently.
troubleshooting.md maps errors and logs back to the operational commands and deployment assumptions described in SKILL.md and advanced-usage.md, forming a feedback loop for operators.
notable_relationships: This documentation subtree is likely consumed by the optional-skills/mlops skill registration and by any tooling or scripts in the codebase that implement training job launches or cloud automation (e.g., tools or cron jobs). Changes in code paths that perform provisioning, job submission, logging, or credential loading may need doc updates here.

Operational content depends on external Lambda Labs services and APIs; runtime behavior in other parts of the repository that interact with cloud providers (credential pools, API wrappers, automation scripts) are upstream/downstream concerns.

Troubleshooting guidance references runtime artifacts (logs, container images, mounts) that can be affected by CI, Dockerfile changes, or packaging; maintainers editing those layers must verify alignment with these docs.
recommendations: When editing code that implements Lambda Labs automation or training orchestration, verify and update the examples and commands in SKILL.md and the reference docs to keep them runnable and accurate.
Do not hardcode or commit credentials in examples; ensure environment variable names and expected credential loading behavior match across docs and implementation.

Preserve distributed-training environment assumptions (network ports, NCCL settings, hostnames, container images, and library versions) described in advanced-usage when altering launch scripts or container definitions; run a representative distributed job to validate changes.
If you change logging locations, error messages, or artifact paths in runtime code, update troubleshooting.md to reflect new locations and failure signatures so operators can diagnose issues.

Run any code examples from the docs (or unit/integration tests that exercise API automation flows) after changes to confirm commands and API calls still work against a staging/test Lambda Labs account; ensure retry and backoff behaviors are present where the docs imply automated usage.

optional-skills/mlops/pinecone.description: This folder contains the optional Pinecone vector-database skill for Hermes and its operational guidance. It is documentation-only in this subtree: user-facing integration instructions (SKILL.md) and production deployment patterns (references/deployment.md) that dictate how Hermes components should be configured to use Pinecone in production.
key_behaviors: Describes how Hermes can connect to and use Pinecone as a managed vector store for embeddings and retrieval.
Specifies operational and deployment patterns (index provisioning, region/endpoint selection, scaling/cost trade-offs) for running Pinecone-backed functionality in production.
Defines configuration/credential expectations and runtime constraints that other Hermes subsystems must satisfy to enable the skill.

insights.title: External API keys and environment configuration are required and sensitive
insights.problem: Pinecone requires API keys and environment/region settings that must be provided at runtime and must not be leaked or hard-coded.
insights.root_cause: The skill relies on a hosted external service and thus needs secret credentials and runtime endpoint configuration outside the codebase.
insights.solution: Treat Pinecone credentials as external configuration: reference environment variable names consistently, avoid hard-coding values in docs or code, and ensure CI, Docker, and deployment docs show how to inject keys securely (and how CI blanks keys to avoid accidental calls).

insights.title: Deployment guidance must align with Hermes runtime and data persistence
insights.problem: Production deployment patterns (index provisioning, regional placement, network egress, and backups) can conflict with Hermes container/volume and networking expectations if not coordinated.
insights.root_cause: Pinecone is a managed service with region-specific endpoints, cost/latency tradeoffs, and backup/retention semantics that intersect with Hermes' HERMES_HOME, containerization, and runtime choices.
insights.solution: Documentation should reference and remain consistent with Hermes runtime defaults (HERMES_HOME, Dockerfile, entrypoints) and highlight region/endpoint choices, network egress, and backup considerations so deployers can align infrastructure (VPCs, NAT, volumes) accordingly.

insights.title: Integration tests can accidentally call external providers
insights.problem: CI or local tests may attempt to contact Pinecone if documentation or examples cause devs to run integration flows without credentials or mocks.
insights.root_cause: Examples and deployment docs may demonstrate live usage without emphasizing that CI blanks keys or that tests should mock the service.
insights.solution: Ensure the documented usage clearly signals that CI/test environments should mock or stub Pinecone and that API keys are omitted in CI; when adding examples, ensure they are marked as integration-only and align with the repository's CI practices.

key_files.why: Primary user-facing integration guide that describes how to enable and use the Pinecone skill with Hermes; authoritative source for expected configuration and runtime behavior.
Operational guidance for production deployments (index provisioning, region, scaling, backup) that impacts infrastructure and deployment templates across the project.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md documents how Hermes components (MemoryManager, tools that use vector stores, and any skill invokers) should connect to Pinecone, implying a runtime dependency between those components and the external Pinecone service.

deployment.md maps Pinecone index and network considerations to Hermes deployment artifacts (Dockerfile, entrypoints, and HERMES_HOME persistence), so deployment decisions in Hermes must remain consistent with patterns described here.

Both docs imply the use of environment variables / credential names that must align with the project's credential management (credential_pool, CI env blanks, and .env usage) to avoid mismatches at runtime.
notable_relationships: External dependency on Pinecone service: this subtree depends on externally managed infrastructure, network connectivity, and billing — edits must respect operational/credential boundaries.
CI and test workflows: the repository blanks provider keys in CI; documentation here must align so tests do not attempt real Pinecone calls.
Packaging and extras: enabling Pinecone usage may require optional dependencies or extras in pyproject.toml and Docker images; deployment docs should be consistent with those packaging choices.
recommendations: When editing files here, preserve the exact environment variable names and credential instructions or verify they match the rest of the codebase's credential handling (credential_pool, .env.example, CI blanks).

Keep deployment guidance consistent with global deployment artifacts (Dockerfile, scripts/install.sh, HERMES_HOME defaults) and note any network/region implications so container and infra configurations remain compatible.
Mark any runnable examples as integration-only and ensure they align with CI/test policies (mocking/stubbing Pinecone); do not introduce examples that cause CI to require real API keys.
Avoid embedding secrets or concrete account identifiers in the docs; assume operators will supply keys via environment or secret management.

If adding references to runtime behavior (timeouts, batch sizes, index names), check for alignment with memory/tool code that will interact with Pinecone to avoid mismatches in schema, index naming, or expected semantics.

skills/github/github-auth.description: This directory provides a small GitHub authentication helper and accompanying documentation for a GitHub-related skill. The shell helper (gh-env.sh) is intended to be sourced by other tooling or CI to detect available GitHub credentials, repo context, and export a standardized set of environment variables that downstream code relies on. The SKILL.md documents expected auth flows and how the script is intended to be used.
key_behaviors: Detect available GitHub authentication methods (gh CLI credentials, environment token, git credential helper) without performing API calls.
Extract repository context (owner, repo, username) when executed inside a Git repository with a GitHub remote and export that context as environment variables.
Expose a concise, human-readable summary of detected auth method and repo context to stdout while avoiding leaking secrets.
Be sourced by other scripts/tools so that exported variables (GH_AUTH_METHOD, GITHUB_TOKEN, GH_USER, GH_OWNER, GH_REPO) become available in the caller environment.

insights.title: Token exposure risk in output
insights.problem: The script prints a concise summary and exports GITHUB_TOKEN; careless printing or debugging could leak secrets to logs or CI output.
insights.root_cause: Summary output and environment export occur in potentially noisy contexts (CI, terminal, logs) and there is no explicit redaction policy enforced by the helper.
insights.solution: When modifying or invoking the script, preserve redaction/no-printing of secret values; avoid adding debug prints that include token content and ensure summary strings omit or mask tokens. Assume CI may capture stdout/stderr.

insights.title: Sourcing vs. executing semantics must be preserved
insights.problem: Changes that introduce exit calls, error traps, or reliance on non-existent shell features can terminate the caller shell or behave differently when sourced versus executed.
insights.root_cause: The script is intended to be sourced to set environment variables; using constructs that call exit or depend on bash-only features can break callers.
insights.solution: Keep the script safe to source: avoid exit/eval that terminates the parent shell, avoid set -e/-u changes without restoring prior state, and maintain POSIX-compatible constructs if callers may use different shells.

insights.title: Dependency on external CLI and git config
insights.problem: Detection logic queries gh CLI, git config, and credential helpers which may be absent or vary across environments (CI, containers, developer machines).
insights.root_cause: The script attempts to detect multiple auth sources but must operate gracefully when tools are missing or git remotes are absent.
insights.solution: Ensure detection remains non-failing: preserve graceful fallbacks, treat missing binaries as a valid non-authenticated state, and do not assume network access. Tests/editors must validate behavior across scenarios with and without gh/git present.

insights.title: Concise summary is used for diagnostics
insights.problem: Downstream users rely on the printed summary to understand which auth method and repo context were detected; inconsistent formatting or missing fields will hinder debugging.
insights.root_cause: The helper both exports environment and prints a compact summary; changes may alter the exact variables or summary wording that tools or humans expect.
insights.solution: Preserve stable variable names and the concise summary format. If changing wording or exported names, update documentation (SKILL.md) and any callers that parse the output.

insights.title: Environment variable contract
insights.problem: Other skills/tools expect specific environment variables to be set by this script; altering names or export behavior breaks callers.
insights.root_cause: The script defines a small contract (GH_AUTH_METHOD, GITHUB_TOKEN, GH_USER, GH_OWNER, GH_REPO) used by other code in the repository.
insights.solution: Any edits must keep the environment variable contract intact or coordinate updates with all callers and documentation. Assume callers will source the script and read the exported variables immediately after.

key_files.why: Primary implementation: a sourcing-friendly shell helper that detects auth and repo context and exports the environment variables other skills rely on.
Documentation for how the GitHub auth flows are intended to work and how to use the gh-env.sh script; authoritative consumer-facing guidance that must remain consistent with script behavior.

construct_relationships: gh-env.sh exports GH_AUTH_METHOD, GITHUB_TOKEN, GH_USER, GH_OWNER, GH_REPO for consumption by other skills/tools which expect these env vars to be present after sourcing.
gh-env.sh detects auth methods by invoking external tools (gh CLI, git, credential helpers) and maps detection results into the GH_AUTH_METHOD value documented in SKILL.md.
SKILL.md documents the expected auth flows and the semantics of the variables exported by gh-env.sh, forming the documentation-contract pair with the script.
notable_relationships: Downstream skills, tools, or CI pipelines that perform GitHub operations will rely on the exported environment variables; modifications here affect all such consumers across the repository.
CI workflows intentionally blank API keys; the helper must behave correctly in CI (report no auth) without causing failures or exposing blanks.

Runtime environments vary (local developer shells, containers, GitHub Actions, Docker images). The script depends on availability of git and possibly gh, so callers in minimal containers may need to provision these tools or expect a no-auth state.
Any logging or printing from this helper flows into terminal/CI logs and could influence audit/secret-scanning; changes may have security-compliance implications.
recommendations: Preserve the exported environment variable names and semantics: GH_AUTH_METHOD, GITHUB_TOKEN, GH_USER, GH_OWNER, GH_REPO.
Ensure the script remains safe to source: do not introduce exit calls, traps, or shell options that alter the caller's shell state unexpectedly.
Avoid printing secret values; summary output must not contain raw tokens. If summary format changes, update SKILL.md and any callers that parse output.
Maintain graceful fallbacks when required tooling (gh, git) or remotes are absent; tests editing this script should cover scenarios with/without gh, with empty GITHUB_TOKEN, and outside a git repo.
When editing, verify behavior in CI-like environments (API keys blanked) to ensure the script reports a non-authenticated state but does not fail or leak information.

skills/github/github-code-review.description: This folder implements a reusable "GitHub code review" skill for the agent: a workflow that fetches diffs from a repository or pull request, runs a review process, formats findings, and posts or returns review results. It supports multiple access methods (git/gh CLI primary, curl-based GitHub API fallback) and includes a canonical output template for human-readable and machine-parsable review results. This matters because changes here influence how the agent gathers code context, composes review comments, and interacts with GitHub (or a fallback API) — operations that involve credentials, network IO, and potential side effects on remote repositories.
key_behaviors: Obtain repository and diff context using local git or the GitHub CLI (gh); if unavailable, use HTTP requests (curl) to the GitHub API as a fallback.
Run an automated code review over the provided diffs/patches and produce structured review output comprising summary, inline comments, and suggested fixes.
Format review results according to a maintained template and either return them to the caller or post them back to GitHub as a review/inline comments using gh or API calls.
Handle credentialed operations (GitHub token / PAT) and surface API or CLI errors when posting reviews; operate idempotently to avoid duplicate comments where possible.

insights.title: Token management and exposure risk
insights.problem: The skill needs a GitHub token (or relies on gh-auth) to post reviews and make API calls; improper handling could leak credentials or fail in locked-down environments.
insights.root_cause: Credential access is required for remote operations and the fallback (curl) path expects an environment-provided token or header; posting actions are side-effectful.
insights.solution: Treat credential usage as an explicit dependency: preserve token-less read-only flows where possible, ensure credentials are consumed from environment or gh auth, and avoid embedding secrets in code or output templates. When editing files, maintain clear boundaries where tokens are read and never printed into logs or returned in responses.

insights.title: Multiple access methods with differing semantics
insights.problem: The skill supports both local git/gh CLI and curl-based API fallback; the two approaches produce different data shapes and error modes (e.g., gh vs REST pagination, rate limits).
insights.root_cause: Different clients (CLI vs HTTP) expose varying fields, metadata, and error codes; code that consumes these results is sensitive to format differences.
insights.solution: Keep the selection order and normalization points intact: preserve the primary CLI path and the normalized data format the rest of the skill expects. Any edits must respect the normalized schema between retrieval and review formatting so downstream formatting/posting logic continues to work.

insights.title: Posting reviews may create duplicate comments
insights.problem: Posting a review to GitHub is a side-effect that can create duplicate comments or thread noise if not guarded.
insights.root_cause: Lack of idempotency checks or deduplication when issuing review API/CLI commands.
insights.solution: Ensure edits preserve any existing checks that determine whether a comment/review was already posted (timestamps, in-review markers, or message fingerprinting). When making changes, do not remove or alter the mechanisms used to avoid duplicate postings without providing a replacement.

insights.title: Structured output template used by callers
insights.problem: A canonical review-output template exists and likely is consumed by other subsystems or returned to users; changing it breaks downstream parsing or user expectations.
insights.root_cause: The template defines both human and machine-readable structure (summary, inline comments, suggested patches).
insights.solution: Preserve the template shape or provide a documented adapter layer. Any edits must not rename or remove required fields referenced by callers or external integrations and must maintain the template in references/review-output-template.md.

key_files.why: Primary skill manifest describing the workflow, expected inputs/outputs, and execution paths (git/gh primary, curl fallback) — edits here change how the skill is understood and used by the agent.
Defines the canonical review output and inline comment format that downstream consumers (posting logic, UI, or other skills) rely on; it is the contract for produced reviews.

construct_relationships: The skill orchestration (documented in SKILL.md) invokes a retrieval construct (git/gh CLI or curl) to obtain diffs/PR context.
Retrieved diffs are normalized into a common internal representation which is then fed into the review formatter that follows the review-output-template.
The formatted review is either returned to the caller or handed to a posting construct that uses gh CLI or the GitHub REST API (curl) to create a review and inline comments.
Credential consumption (environment variables or gh auth) is a cross-cutting construct used by both the retrieval and posting paths.
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency on the runtime environment having git and/or gh CLI installed and authenticated; if not present the skill falls back to making HTTP calls (curl) to the GitHub API — the selector between these paths must remain consistent.
Downstream consumers (agent UI, gateway responses, or automated posting) depend on the stable review-output template for parsing comments and applying suggestions.
External dependency on GitHub API behavior and rate limits; changes to posting behavior affect remote repositories and CI workflows.

Potential interaction with global agent tools that perform shell execution, HTTP requests, and logging — edits should respect the agent-wide conventions for invoking external processes and handling secrets.
recommendations: Preserve the primary CLI-first retrieval/posting order (git/gh → curl fallback) and any normalization step that makes CLI and API results interchangeable.
Do not hardcode or expose secrets; continue using environment/gh auth for tokens and avoid emitting tokens into logs or outputs tied to the template.
Maintain the review-output-template.md contract; any changes to format must be coordinated with code that parses or posts reviews.
When modifying posting logic, ensure existing idempotency/deduplication checks remain functional so edits do not cause duplicate public comments.
Test edits in both environments: with local git/gh authenticated and with the curl/API fallback mocked to validate error handling, rate limit behavior, and formatting consistency.

skills/github/github-issues.description: This subtree implements a GitHub Issues skill: documentation and reusable Markdown templates used by the agent to create bug reports and feature requests. It is lightweight and primarily provides the content (issue templates) and usage notes (SKILL.md) that upstream skill-invocation code will rely on when composing and sending issues via the gh CLI or an HTTP fallback.
key_behaviors: Provide canonical markdown bodies for new GitHub issues (bug and feature reports) that the agent will populate and submit.
Document preferred invocation and fallback methods for creating issues (gh CLI preferred; curl/REST as fallback).
Serve as a stable content source that other parts of the agent load, render, and submit to GitHub using credentials and network calls.

insights.title: Dual submission paths (gh CLI preferred, curl fallback)
insights.problem: The skill documentation indicates two different submission mechanisms; edits may unintentionally break one path or assume capabilities only present in the other.
insights.root_cause: Different runtime environments will have different tooling available and different authentication flows (gh CLI handles auth/2FA differently than raw REST calls).
insights.solution: When changing documentation or templates, keep both submission approaches in mind; avoid embedding assumptions about auth, CLI availability, or response formats into templates or docs, and ensure placeholders and required metadata are compatible with either method.

insights.title: Issue submissions require valid GitHub credentials
insights.problem: Creating issues via curl/REST requires exposing or using an API token; accidental edits or tests might expose tokens or make it easier to commit secrets.
insights.root_cause: Templates and docs can encourage copy-paste usage; repository and CI environments may have different secret-masking practices.
insights.solution: Treat SKILL.md and templates as public content; avoid hard-coding example tokens or commands with embedded secrets. Edits should preserve placeholder conventions and not introduce credentials.

insights.title: Template formatting and placeholders are relied upon by callers
insights.problem: Downstream code likely fills specific placeholders or expects particular sections (steps to reproduce, expected behavior, environment). Altering headings or placeholder syntax can break automated population and parsing.
insights.root_cause: Templates are simple markdown files; the agent or skill invocation code depends on stable structure to insert content and may parse sections programmatically.
insights.solution: Maintain markdown structure, headings, and placeholder tokens when editing templates. If structural changes are necessary, ensure all callers that render or populate these templates are updated in lockstep.

key_files.why: Primary documentation for the GitHub Issues skill; explains preferred submission method (gh) and fallback behavior (curl/REST) that callers and operators will rely on.
Canonical Markdown template for bug reports; downstream code will populate its sections and submit as issue bodies.
Canonical Markdown template for feature requests; must keep stable structure for automated population and user-facing clarity.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md documents the operational behavior used by callers that perform submissions (preference for gh CLI with curl/REST fallback).
Templates (bug-report.md, feature-report.md) are inputs to the issue-creation flow: they are rendered/populated by skill-invocation code before calling gh or the REST API.
The skill's templates and docs form the content-contract that credential-handling and network-call components must honor when creating issues.
notable_relationships: Runtime environment must provide either the gh CLI (preferred) or network access plus a GitHub token for the REST fallback — edits should not assume both are present.

This skill will be used by the agent's skill dispatch subsystem (agent/skill_commands or equivalent) which is responsible for loading templates and submitting issues; changes here have downstream effects on those callers.

CI and repository policies that blank or omit GitHub API keys (similar to other provider keys) mean automated tests will not be able to exercise real submissions; manual testing or mocks are required to validate end-to-end behavior.
recommendations: Preserve markdown headings and placeholder tokens in template files; callers likely depend on stable section names and structure.
Do not add or commit real tokens or authentication samples with secrets into SKILL.md or templates; use placeholders and document where credentials are sourced at runtime.
When editing SKILL.md, keep both submission paths (gh CLI and REST/curl) consistent — ensure any example command syntax remains valid for its targeted environment.

If changing template semantics or placeholders, audit upstream callers that render/populate these files (skill invocation code, credential managers, and REST/CLI invocation code) to ensure compatibility.

Validate changes in an environment that simulates both modes: one with gh CLI available (authenticated) and one relying on REST with token-based auth; unit tests alone may not cover external submission behavior.

skills/github/github-pr-workflow.description: This folder packages an end-to-end GitHub pull request workflow skill: human-facing documentation (SKILL.md), reusable PR body templates for feature and bugfix PRs, and short reference guides (conventional commits and CI troubleshooting). It matters because other automation or agent skills will use these templates and guidance when composing PRs, selecting whether to invoke the gh CLI or fall back to direct HTTP (curl), and when instructing contributors about commit message and CI expectations.
key_behaviors: Compose PR bodies using provided markdown templates (feature vs bugfix) and populate placeholders.
Invoke a PR creation mechanism with two execution modes: preferred gh CLI, fallback to curl/HTTP if CLI not available.
Provide inline guidance to users and automation about commit message format (Conventional Commits) and quick CI troubleshooting steps.
Serve as a canonical source for textual artifacts used by agent-driven or scripted PR creation and contributor instructions.

insights.title: Template placeholders and markdown must remain stable
insights.problem: Automations populating PR templates can break if placeholder names, formatting, or required sections change unexpectedly.
insights.root_cause: Inconsistent or undocumented placeholder conventions and fragile assumptions about template structure.
insights.solution: Treat templates as stable contract artifacts: keep placeholder names consistent, document expected variables in SKILL.md, and preserve section headings/markdown that downstream automation parses.

insights.title: Dual-mode PR creation (gh CLI vs curl) creates environment-dependent behavior
insights.problem: Behavior can differ across environments: scripts that assume gh is present may fail; curl fallback requires correct API usage and auth headers.
insights.root_cause: Implicit dependency on gh CLI and varying availability of network/credentials across runtime environments.
insights.solution: Document the two modes and their preconditions (binary availability, required token scopes, network access) so calling code can detect and choose the correct path, and ensure templates remain compatible with both modes.

insights.title: Conventional commit guidance is relied on by downstream tooling
insights.problem: If commit messages deviate from Conventional Commits, changelog generation, release automation, or PR categorization may fail.
insights.root_cause: Human-entered messages are not always validated; automation expects a conventional format.
insights.solution: Reference and preserve the Conventional Commits quick reference; if templates or guidance are changed, ensure any parsers/linters consuming commit messages are updated or have clear expectations communicated.

insights.title: Troubleshooting tips are lightweight and may become stale relative to GitHub Actions
insights.problem: Outdated CI troubleshooting steps can mislead users, causing wasted debugging time or incorrect fixes.
insights.root_cause: External CI behavior evolves and short reference docs require maintenance.
insights.solution: Keep the troubleshooting reference focused on stable diagnostics (interpreting logs, common failure causes, how to re-run workflows) and ensure SKILL.md links or notes reflect where to update guidance if CI workflows change.

insights.title: PR creation requires appropriate GitHub token scopes and safe handling
insights.problem: Using fallback curl or automation without correct auth can produce permission errors or leak tokens if not handled safely.
insights.root_cause: Token scope and confidentiality expectations are environment-specific and not enforced by templates alone.
insights.solution: Document required token scopes and handling expectations in SKILL.md; ensure templates do not embed or imply secret values and that calling code passes tokens securely.

key_files.why: Primary documentation and orchestration for the skill: describes usage, environment assumptions (gh vs curl), and how templates/references are intended to be used.
Canonical PR body template for feature work; used by automation and humans to compose consistent PR descriptions consumed by reviewers and tooling.
