Canonical PR body template for bugfixes; separates communication patterns and expected metadata from feature PRs.
Defines the commit message convention relied on by release/changelog tooling and by this skill's guidance.
Provides quick diagnostics and guidance for CI failures referenced by users and possibly by automated post-failure messaging.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references and documents the templates (pr-body-feature.md, pr-body-bugfix.md) and the reference guides (conventional-commits.md, ci-troubleshooting.md).
PR templates act as inputs to the PR creation flow (the skill's documented flow) and are expected to contain stable placeholders that calling automation will populate.
Conventional-commits.md provides the semantic contract for commit message formats that templates and downstream tooling (changelog/release) depend on.
CI troubleshooting reference is a support artifact referenced from SKILL.md and expected to remain aligned with the repository's CI configuration and common failure modes.
notable_relationships: This skill will be consumed by agent skills or scripts elsewhere in the repo that perform PR creation (they will depend on the templates and SKILL.md to detect placeholders and execution mode).
Cross-cutting dependency on environment/tooling: callers must have access to either gh CLI (binary) or network+auth for GitHub API (curl).
Upstream/downstream ties to repository CI configuration (.github/workflows) and release tooling: changes here can affect changelog generation, release automation, and CI troubleshooting procedures.
Authentication and secrets handling link this folder to any credential/secret management subsystem used by the project (environment variables, credential helpers, or a credential pool).
recommendations: Preserve template placeholder names, markdown headings, and section structure unless you also update all consumers and document the change in SKILL.md.
When editing SKILL.md or templates, update the documented preconditions for gh CLI vs curl fallback (binary presence, required GitHub token scopes, and network requirements).
Do not add sensitive data to templates or docs; ensure any examples of tokens are clearly labeled as placeholders and not real credentials.
If changing Conventional Commits guidance or template expectations, verify downstream tooling (changelog/release scripts or commit linters) remain compatible.

Validate edits by exercising both execution modes in a safe dry-run: a) gh CLI present environment and b) no-gh environment using curl with a test token; also sanity-check parsing of populated templates as rendered PR bodies.
When updating CI troubleshooting text, cross-check against the repository's current .github/workflows to ensure recommendations map to actual job names and artifacts.

skills/github/github-repo-management.description: This folder contains a documented skill for managing GitHub repositories, with an authoritative SKILL.md describing the skill's behavior and a GitHub REST API cheatsheet used as a reference for curl-based fallbacks. It exists to let the agent perform repository tasks via the gh CLI when available and fall back to direct REST calls when the CLI is unavailable, centralizing repository management guidance and HTTP endpoint details for reliable operation.
key_behaviors: Attempt to perform repository management operations using the gh CLI where available (preferred path).
When gh is unavailable, construct and execute equivalent GitHub REST API calls (curl fallback) using endpoints and request formats from the cheatsheet.
Handle authentication and authorization via environment-provided credentials/tokens and ensure correct scopes are used for operations.
Document and surface required API endpoints, request payloads, and expected responses so the agent can map high-level repo intents to concrete CLI or HTTP actions.
Account for and handle GitHub REST API behaviors such as pagination, rate limits, and error responses when using the curl fallback.

insights.title: Dual-path execution (gh CLI vs curl fallback)
insights.problem: Behavior varies depending on the presence of the gh CLI; curl fallback must reproduce functionality and side effects.
insights.root_cause: Two different execution paths (CLI and raw HTTP) with different surface areas, defaults, and error modes.
insights.solution: When editing, ensure documentation and examples align for both paths, and preserve equivalent payloads, flags, and postconditions between gh commands and curl requests; maintain parity in permission handling and side-effect semantics.

insights.title: Credentials and token scope sensitivity
insights.problem: Operations touching repositories (create, delete, change permissions) require appropriate tokens and scopes; mishandling can expose secrets or perform unintended destructive actions.
insights.root_cause: SKILL.md and cheatsheet trigger network calls that must include tokens (GITHUB_TOKEN or similar) and can perform destructive mutations.
insights.solution: Edits must avoid embedding secrets, clearly indicate required env vars and scopes, and preserve any safeguards or explicit confirmation language for destructive operations; callers must be expected to supply tokens via environment or agent credential management.

insights.title: API drift and endpoint correctness
insights.problem: The REST API cheatsheet can become outdated as GitHub evolves endpoints and request/response formats.
insights.root_cause: Static reference material (cheatsheet) that may diverge from actual API behavior over time.
insights.solution: Treat the cheatsheet as canonical for curl fallbacks but note that changes may require synchronizing examples; changes to this folder should preserve explicit endpoint URI, verb, header, and payload formats or clearly mark them as versioned and source-referenced.

insights.title: Network/runtime assumptions
insights.problem: Operations assume network access, appropriate CLI tooling (curl/gh), and agent runtime configuration; failures surface differently across paths.
insights.root_cause: Skill implements external system interactions dependent on runtime environment and connectivity.
insights.solution: When modifying files, preserve notes about required runtime capabilities (gh/curl availability, outbound network, proxy settings) and document failure modes (rate limiting, 4xx/5xx responses) so callers can handle them appropriately.

key_files.why: Primary skill specification describing repo-management behaviors, preferred gh CLI usage, and operational guidance the agent will follow.
Authoritative reference of GitHub REST endpoints and request formats used to construct curl fallback calls when gh is unavailable.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md references and relies on the github-api-cheatsheet.md to construct equivalent curl requests for operations that are normally executed with the gh CLI.
The skill documentation encodes decision logic: prefer gh CLI commands; if unavailable, build REST calls using endpoints, headers, and payloads from the cheatsheet.
Both documents together form the contract the agent uses to map high-level repo-management intents to concrete commands or HTTP requests (authentication, endpoint selection, request body).
notable_relationships: Upstream: depends on agent-wide credential management (e.g., GITHUB_TOKEN env var or agent credential pool) for authenticated API/CLI calls; changes here affect how credentials must be supplied.

Downstream: callers (agent tool/skill invocation layer) will execute gh or curl commands derived from this documentation; edits can change expected command shapes and therefore must align with the executor implementation.

Cross-cutting: interactions with network, rate-limiting, and error-handling subsystems in the agent; destructive operations also interact with auditing/logging and persistence (e.g., marking state changes).
CI/Testing: any example commands or expectations may need to respect CI practices that blank API keys; tests invoking this skill should provision tokens or mock API calls.
recommendations: When editing, do not embed secrets or API tokens; preserve or clearly state required environment variables and token scopes.
Keep gh CLI and curl examples aligned; ensure any change to one path is reflected in the other so behavior and side effects remain equivalent.
Document expected failure modes (rate limits, 401/403, 404) and note whether destructive operations require explicit confirmation; retain any warnings.
Assume runtime variance: callers may run without gh installed, behind proxies, or with limited network; maintain clear preconditions for both execution paths.
Remember that the cheatsheet is a static source of truth for curl fallbacks—updating it may be necessary when GitHub API changes and will affect any automated fallback logic.

skills/leisure/find-nearby.description: This folder implements a standalone skill for finding nearby places using OpenStreetMap services. It provides a CLI entry (find_nearby.py) that accepts an address or coordinates, resolves geocoding via Nominatim, queries Overpass for amenities within a radius, computes distances and link metadata, and emits either structured JSON or human-readable output. This skill is intended to be callable by the broader skills/agent system and depends on live external HTTP APIs.
key_behaviors: Resolve input location: accept an address or lat/lon and, when needed, call a geocoding service (Nominatim) to obtain coordinates.
Search nearby amenities: construct and run an Overpass query using the resolved coordinates and radius to retrieve place nodes/ways/relations.
Post-process results: compute distances from the query point, extract optional metadata (cuisine, opening hours, phone), and synthesize navigation/map links.
Output formatting: present results either as structured JSON (for programmatic consumers) or as human-readable text for CLI users.
CLI interaction & validation: parse command-line options, validate input (address vs coordinates), and surface errors/timeouts related to external API calls.

insights.title: Reliance on Nominatim and Overpass endpoints
insights.problem: The skill directly depends on external OpenStreetMap services which can be rate-limited, temporarily unavailable, or return partial data.
insights.root_cause: Runtime HTTP calls to public OSM endpoints without implicit retry/backoff or centralized credential/rate-limit management.
insights.solution: When modifying behavior, assume network unreliability and rate limits; any calling code or tests must account for timed-out or partial responses and avoid hard assumptions about completeness of returned records.

insights.title: Dual input modes (address vs coordinates)
insights.problem: Different input modes produce different flows (geocoding + search vs direct search) and consumers may rely on consistent output schemas.
insights.root_cause: Code branches based on whether an address was supplied or coordinates were provided.
insights.solution: Maintain consistent output structure and document which fields may be missing depending on input mode; changes must preserve stable JSON keys and CLI flags expected by callers.

insights.title: Structured JSON & human-readable outputs both supported
insights.problem: Downstream tools or automated tests may depend on field names, link formats, distance units, and ordering.
insights.root_cause: The script emits two kinds of outputs; programmatic consumers require stable JSON keys while CLI users expect readable text.
insights.solution: Treat the current JSON schema and link generation conventions as an API contract. Any edits must keep keys, units, and link formats stable or update documentation (SKILL.md) and dependent callers accordingly.

insights.title: Handles user-provided locations and generates shareable links
insights.problem: Coordinates, addresses, and derived links are sensitive data that may be logged, stored, or transmitted externally.
insights.root_cause: Skill communicates with third-party services and prints/outputs location data.
insights.solution: Be mindful of logging and persistent storage when changing output or error behavior; ensure edits do not inadvertently leak location details in debug logs or persistent caches.

insights.title: External service dependency complicates tests
insights.problem: Unit/integration tests that exercise real Overpass/Nominatim calls will be flaky and slow.
insights.root_cause: Direct network calls are performed during normal execution.
insights.solution: When editing, plan to mock or stub geocoding and Overpass responses for automated tests; preserve clear seams where responses can be injected for deterministic testing.

key_files.why: Primary executable implementing the geocoding, Overpass queries, distance computation, metadata extraction, and CLI/formatting behavior.
Human-facing documentation and metadata for the skill; likely consumed by maintainers or skill registries to explain capability and expected behavior.

construct_relationships: CLI input parsing in find_nearby.py determines whether to call the geocoder (Nominatim) or use provided coordinates.
Geocoding component produces a lat/lon that is fed into Overpass query construction to search for nearby amenities.
Overpass query results are parsed into place records; distance calculation logic enriches each record with a numeric distance and ordering.
Result formatting logic serializes enriched records either into JSON (stable schema) or into a human-readable textual summary and link generation for mapping/directions.
notable_relationships: This skill depends on external network services (Nominatim and Overpass) which are upstream providers outside the repository.
The skill's JSON output acts as a downstream API for any caller in the skills/agent subsystem or CLI wrappers; changes here can affect other skills/tools that invoke it programmatically.
SKILL.md serves as the catalog/metadata that other parts of the skills system or documentation generators may read; keep it consistent with executable behavior.
Because this is a CLI script, it may be invoked directly by developers or by agent tool runners; runtime environment (network, timeouts, locale) affects runtime behavior.
recommendations: Preserve the JSON output schema (field names, distance unit, link format) or ensure callers and SKILL.md are updated in lockstep if changes are necessary.
Treat all external HTTP interactions as unreliable: respect rate limits, propagate clear timeout/error messages, and avoid long blocking operations in synchronous flows.
When adding or changing logic, include test coverage that uses mocked Nominatim and Overpass responses to validate parsing, distance computation, and formatting deterministically.
Avoid introducing persistent logging of raw user addresses/coordinates; verify existing logging behavior and consider privacy implications of any changes.
If altering CLI flags or behavior, update SKILL.md and any code paths that programmatically invoke this script so invocations remain compatible.

skills/media/youtube-content.description: This folder provides a small Hermes skill that extracts YouTube video transcripts and emits them in machine-friendly or human-readable formats. It centers on a command-line utility that parses input URLs/IDs, calls an upstream transcript provider (youtube-transcript-api), and produces structured JSON or plain text following the documented output-format guidelines. This skill matters because downstream tools (summarizers, TTS, ingestion pipelines) depend on stable input shapes and reliable transcript retrieval.
key_behaviors: Parse a provided YouTube URL or raw video ID and normalize it to a canonical video identifier.
Fetch transcript segments from the external youtube-transcript-api for a given language (or default) and handle absence of transcripts.
Assemble fetched segments into multiple output shapes: full text, segment list, and optional timestamped human-readable text.
Emit output either as structured JSON (with segments and full_text keys) or as plain text, controlled via CLI flags (language selection, timestamps toggle).
Follow the repository's documented output format contract (references/output-formats.md and SKILL.md) so downstream components can consume results.

insights.title: Transcript source availability and transient failures
insights.problem: Fetching transcripts relies on an external library/service and may fail due to network errors, provider rate limits, or missing transcripts for a video.
insights.root_cause: Direct dependency on youtube-transcript-api and the availability/permissions of YouTube transcripts for the given video/language.
insights.solution: When modifying behavior, preserve explicit error detection and propagation for network failures and missing transcripts; ensure calling code can distinguish transient errors from permanently unavailable transcripts and preserves informative error messages.

insights.title: Robust video-id extraction required
insights.problem: URL-to-ID extraction can fail for nonstandard or edge-case YouTube URL formats, causing downstream fetch attempts to use invalid IDs.
insights.root_cause: Variability in YouTube URL formats and potential user-supplied inputs (short links, embedded URLs, extra query params).
insights.solution: Keep or improve strict, well-tested parsing logic that normalizes many URL forms to a single video ID and surface clear errors when extraction fails. Changes must not silently accept invalid IDs.

insights.title: Consumers depend on documented JSON shape and text variants
insights.problem: Downstream tools assume a stable schema (segments array, full_text, optional timestamped_text) and specific timestamp formatting; changes may break those integrations.
insights.root_cause: Tight coupling between emitted output shapes and downstream processors without schema contracts enforced in code.
insights.solution: Any edits must preserve the documented output structure and timestamp formatting conventions in references/output-formats.md and SKILL.md, or include explicit versioning/announcements if the schema is changed.

insights.title: Language selection affects transcript availability
insights.problem: Requesting a non-existent language or relying on automatic language fallback can return no transcript or unexpected results.
insights.root_cause: Transcript data availability varies by video and language; the fetch library may raise different exceptions or return different shapes for fallbacks.
insights.solution: Respect CLI language flags and ensure code clearly reports when the requested language is unavailable; preserve behavior for how fallbacks are handled so callers know whether they received requested-language content.

insights.title: Timestamps must be consistent and human-readable when requested
insights.problem: Multiple representations (seconds, hh:mm:ss, millis) could be introduced, confusing downstream consumers and humans reading the timestamps.
insights.root_cause: Formatting transformation applied at output layer with potential inconsistencies across code paths.
insights.solution: Maintain the current timestamp format conventions for the timestamps flag as documented; retain consistent rounding/precision rules and timezone-naive epoch offsets so consumers interpret timestamps predictably.

insights.title: Script interface used both interactively and programmatically
insights.problem: Modifying CLI flags, exit codes, or stdout structure can break automated invocations or wrappers that spawn the script.
insights.root_cause: The script is a command-line utility invoked directly by users and potentially by other Hermes components or CI scripts.
insights.solution: Preserve existing CLI flags, return codes, and stdout/stderr usage patterns documented in SKILL.md unless coordinated; when adding behavior, ensure backward-compatible defaults and clear error codes.

key_files.why: Primary executable: parses inputs, calls youtube-transcript-api, formats and emits transcript output; core behavior and CLI contract live here.
Authoritative description of expected JSON and text outputs; downstream consumers rely on this schema and formatting guidance.
High-level skill descriptor describing purpose, usage, and expectations that external callers and skill registry may reference.

construct_relationships: fetch_transcript.py parses incoming URL/ID and normalizes it to a video ID before invoking the youtube-transcript-api client.
fetch_transcript.py maps fetched transcript segments into the documented output schema described in references/output-formats.md (segments array, full_text, optional timestamped text).

SKILL.md describes usage and expectations that must align with the CLI behavior implemented by fetch_transcript.py; references/output-formats.md defines the data contract consumed by downstream skills/tools.
notable_relationships: This skill depends on network access and the third-party youtube-transcript-api package; changes may require updating dependency manifests or dev extras if the package is added/removed.
Outputs are intended to be consumed by other agent components (summaries, TTS, ingestion pipelines) that expect the documented JSON/text shapes — breaking changes will cascade to those modules.
CLI interface may be invoked from external automation, CI, or other Hermes scripts; maintain backward compatibility of flags and exit codes to avoid breaking wrappers.
recommendations: When editing, preserve the documented JSON schema and timestamp formatting in references/output-formats.md and SKILL.md unless you also update all consumers and documentation.
Do not change CLI flags, expected stdout structure, or exit codes without considering automated callers; if adding flags, keep defaults backward-compatible.
Account for network failures, missing transcripts, and provider rate-limiting: ensure error conditions are surfaced distinctly (non-zero exit codes and clear stderr messages) so callers can react.
Keep the URL-to-ID extraction logic robust—validate inputs and surface precise errors for malformed URLs to avoid accidental calls with invalid IDs.

If modifying dependencies (adding/removing youtube-transcript-api), update packaging metadata (extras/pyproject) and ensure CI/dev environments can install the required package; prefer mocking the external client in tests.
Run or add unit tests that assert output shapes and timestamp formatting (preferably with the fetch call mocked) so downstream consumers remain compatible after changes.

skills/mlops/cloud.description: This subtree contains the Modal cloud provider documentation used by the skills/mlops subsystem to guide serverless GPU deployments. It provides a canonical entrypoint deployment guide and focused reference material (advanced usage and troubleshooting) that operationalize how MLOps skills target Modal for GPU workloads. These docs are consumable by humans and by other parts of the skills system that map provider-specific operational steps to runtime actions.
key_behaviors: Present an authoritative, step-by-step deployment playbook for running GPU workloads on Modal (entrypoint guide)
Describe advanced runtime configuration patterns (container images, persistent volumes, web endpoints) required by production workloads
Capture operational failure modes and recovery workflows for diagnosing and troubleshooting Modal-hosted jobs
Serve as linked, referential material where the primary SKILL.md delegates to deeper reference documents for edge cases and platform-specific details

insights.title: Modal CLI/API and GPU runtime version drift risk
insights.problem: Deployment commands and examples in the docs can become invalid as Modal changes CLI flags, API surfaces, or GPU instance offerings.
insights.root_cause: Documentation contains concrete CLI/API examples and resource names that assume a specific Modal release and GPU availability.
insights.solution: Treat snippets as versioned artifacts: record the Modal CLI/API version referenced, and ensure any edits preserve or annotate versioned commands so automated or human consumers know which platform revisions the instructions target.

insights.title: Separation between entrypoint guidance and advanced references can lead to missing cross-references
insights.problem: Readers following the SKILL.md entrypoint may miss required advanced configuration (e.g., volumes or endpoint exposure) if links or prerequisites are not explicit.
insights.root_cause: The main skill guide delegates complex topics to reference files; implicit assumptions about required steps or prerequisites are not centralized.
insights.solution: Ensure the entrypoint remains a complete minimal path: maintain explicit calls-to-action or prerequisite notes that point to specific sections in advanced-usage and troubleshooting so consumers do not omit critical configuration.

insights.title: Troubleshooting instructions rely on observable platform outputs and error patterns
insights.problem: Recovery steps and diagnostic guidance assume particular log formats, error messages, and resource signals which may vary across Modal environments.
insights.root_cause: Operational remediation steps are written against specific observed failures and may not generalize across Modal zones, image variants, or account quotas.
insights.solution: Keep troubleshooting entries tied to the explicit log/error text they reference and avoid sweeping generalizations; when editing, preserve quoted error examples and the exact commands to fetch diagnostics so automated tooling or readers can match them.

insights.title: Inter-file references must remain correct and discoverable
insights.problem: Changing file names, moving content, or reformatting headings can break references from SKILL.md to the advanced or troubleshooting docs and impair discoverability.
insights.root_cause: Relative links and implicit navigational structure are used to connect the entry guide to references.
insights.solution: When renaming or reorganizing, update all in-tree links and any mention of paths; preserve stable paths or include clear versioning to avoid stale references for downstream consumers.

key_files.why: High-level summary of cloud provider options that contextualizes why Modal-specific docs exist and how Modal compares to other providers.
Primary entrypoint guide used by the skills subsystem and operators to deploy serverless GPU workloads on Modal; acts as the canonical how-to.
Contains deeper operational patterns (containers, volumes, web endpoints) that production deployments depend on and that the entry guide references.
Captures failure modes, diagnostic commands, and remediation steps relied upon during incident response and by automated tooling that parses documented error patterns.

construct_relationships: DESCRIPTION.md frames provider choices and points consumers to modal/SKILL.md as the Modal-specific deployment playbook
modal/SKILL.md links to and relies on modal/references/advanced-usage.md for non-trivial configuration like volumes, container images, and exposing web endpoints

modal/SKILL.md and modal/references/troubleshooting.md together form the happy-path + remediation pair: the entry guide describes deployment steps while troubleshooting provides post-failure diagnostics and recovery commands
Advanced usage examples (volumes, containers, endpoints) are expected to align with debugging steps in troubleshooting.md so that configuration details and log locations map cleanly to error messages
notable_relationships: Skills subsystem and any automation that instantiates Modal deployments will consume SKILL.md as procedural guidance—breaks or misalignments can directly affect runtime deployment scripts and CI/integration tests

CLI/SDK compatibility: the docs implicitly depend on a specific Modal CLI/SDK version and available GPU instance types; changes in platform offerings upstream (Modal) are an external dependency that can invalidate examples

Operational tooling (log collectors, test harnesses, or agent components that parse documented error strings) may rely on exact wording or commands from troubleshooting.md; edits can impact downstream parsers and incident automation
recommendations: Preserve explicit commands, quoted error strings, and any version annotations present in examples—downstream tooling and human operators rely on exact text to reproduce or parse failures.
If moving or renaming files, update all relative links and any references in the skills subsystem that import or display SKILL.md content to avoid broken navigational paths.

When editing deployment snippets, note the Modal CLI/SDK version implicitly targeted and consider adding an explicit version note in-place so readers and automation know the expected platform release.

Validate edits by running the consumer flows: check the skills subsystem documentation rendering and any integration tests that reference these docs; if possible, verify CLI snippets against the Modal CLI in a sandboxed environment.

Avoid removing or rewriting troubleshooting steps that contain exact diagnostics or remediation commands without preserving the original examples somewhere accessible for incident response and automated log matching.

skills/mlops/evaluation.description: This subtree contains the MLOps evaluation skill documentation used by the project to run, track, and reproduce model evaluations. It codifies operational contracts (CLI flags, environment/credentials, custom task schemas), usage patterns for an external lm-evaluation-harness, and experiment tracking best-practices with Weights & Biases (W&B), all of which are treated as authoritative by automation, CI, and human operators.
key_behaviors: Specifying and documenting how to invoke the lm-evaluation-harness (CLI flags, environment variables, credentials) for automated and manual runs.
Defining and documenting the schema/contract for custom evaluation tasks so pipelines and discovery logic can load them reliably.
Describing distributed evaluation patterns (node orchestration, environment propagation) and their operational prerequisites.
Recording benchmarking and metrics reporting practices so downstream tools and CI can collect and interpret results reproducibly.
Documenting experiment tracking flows with W&B: run lifecycle, artifact management, framework integrations, and hyperparameter sweep patterns.

insights.title: Documentation is the authoritative contract for automation and CI
insights.problem: Automation, scheduled jobs and CI tooling rely on the CLI flags, env var names, and task schemas described here. Mismatches will cause failed runs or silent misconfigurations.
insights.root_cause: Guidance in these markdown files is used as the source of truth by humans and scripts; there is no separate machine-enforced schema in this folder.
insights.solution: When modifying these files, preserve exact names/flags/env-vars and clearly signal any breaking changes and required co-updates to automation/CI consumers; treat text as contract for downstream tooling.

insights.title: Credentials and environment propagation are security and reproducibility concerns
insights.problem: Descriptions include expectations for API keys and environment variables (lm-eval harness and W&B). Incorrect guidance could leak secrets or create inconsistent CI behavior.
insights.root_cause: Operational guides include example commands and credential propagation patterns without enforcement.
insights.solution: Ensure guidance aligns with CI practices (e.g., blanked keys in CI) and Docker/HERMES_HOME usage; any edits must maintain secure examples and explicit notes about where keys must/should not be present.

insights.title: Custom task contract must remain discoverable and stable
insights.problem: Pipelines and harness invocations expect a stable custom task schema; changes can break task loading and evaluation result mapping.
insights.root_cause: Custom tasks are authored per the documented schema; there is implicit coupling between schema text and code that loads tasks elsewhere.
insights.solution: Preserve schema fields and examples or include explicit migration instructions. Assume automated loaders parse these conventions and that breaking changes will require co-updates in code that discovers/loads tasks.

insights.title: Distributed execution assumptions are fragile
insights.problem: Distributed-eval guidance includes environment, networking and orchestration assumptions that, if changed, will affect reproducibility and job orchestration.
insights.root_cause: Distributed eval relies on external infrastructure and specific environment propagation patterns described in docs.
insights.solution: Treat distributed-eval docs as operational preconditions; edits must keep requirements clear (SSH keys, node roles, mounting of HERMES_HOME, scheduler expectations) because automation will depend on them.

insights.title: W&B usage patterns affect artifact reproducibility and downstream tooling
insights.problem: Artifact naming/versioning, integrations and sweep configurations described here are relied upon by artifact ingestion and result dashboards; inconsistent docs lead to hard-to-reproduce runs.
insights.root_cause: W&B guidance defines canonical artifact workflows that other scripts and pipelines assume when saving/loading evaluation outputs.
insights.solution: Maintain artifact naming conventions, example commands, and integration notes. Any edits should note backward compatibility and where consumers (CI, downstream analysis) must be updated.

key_files.why: High-level description of the evaluation/experiment tooling; frames the purpose and scope for automation and humans.
Primary operational guide for invoking the lm-evaluation-harness, including CLI flags, env expectations and custom task schema referenced by pipelines.
Benchmarking and metrics reporting guidance consumed by CI and downstream result collectors.
Authoritative W&B experiment tracking workflow (initialization, logging, run lifecycle) that other skills and scripts rely on for reproducible experiments.
Detailed hyperparameter sweep guidance which is referenced by automation/sweep orchestrators and experiment reproducibility workflows.

construct_relationships: lm-evaluation-harness SKILL.md defines CLI flags and env var names that are referenced by the 'distributed-eval' and 'api-evaluation' reference docs for specialized invocation patterns.
Custom task schema described in lm-evaluation-harness/references/custom-tasks.md is the contract expected by benchmark-guide.md and by CI/automation that discovers and runs tasks.
weights-and-biases SKILL.md prescribes artifact naming/versioning and run lifecycle that benchmark-guide.md references when describing how to record and archive evaluation results.
References across the lm-evaluation-harness and W&B subfolders are cross-dependent: harness outputs are expected to be logged/archived via W&B artifact patterns described in the W&B references.
notable_relationships: CI and automation: The repository's CI and scheduled jobs (cron, pipeline scripts) consume these docs as the operational contract — CI blanks API keys by policy; any doc changes must be consistent with that.

Persistence and artifact storage: Documents assume a persistent HERMES_HOME/data location (Dockerfile and deployment scripts) for storing results/artifacts; artifact guidance must align with deployment volumes.

Upstream tools: The lm-evaluation-harness and W&B SDK versions and breaking changes are external dependencies; docs function as a compatibility layer and must be considered alongside package versions in pyproject/Dockerfile.

Runtime code: Higher-level MLOps skill code (not in this folder) will invoke harnesses and W&B according to the documented contracts — edits can affect runtime invocations and automation parsing of examples.
recommendations: Treat the markdown content as an operational contract: preserve exact CLI flags, environment variable names, custom task field names, and artifact naming conventions unless coordinated with all consumers.
When editing examples that include credentials or CI behavior, align examples with CI policy (blanked keys) and Docker/HERMES_HOME conventions to avoid suggesting insecure practices.
If altering schema or invocation patterns, identify and coordinate co-changes in automation, CI, and any runtime code that parses or relies on these docs (assume there is no automatic enforcement).
Keep distributed-eval and W&B guidance explicit about preconditions (networking, mounts, SSH keys, SDK versions); edits must not remove operational prerequisites that automation assumes.

After edits, validate downstream effects by checking CI job definitions, scheduled job scripts, artifact storage expectations (HERMES_HOME), and any pipeline code that references these docs or their conventions.

skills/mlops/inference.description: This subtree is a documentation-first skill pack for model serving and inference workflows used by the MLOps part of the project. It contains operational runbooks (GGUF, llama.cpp), structured guidance for generation (Outlines, Guidance), and experiment templates and methods (OBLITERATUS) that downstream runtime code, experiment runners, and automations treat as authoritative contracts for model artifacts, configuration, and experiment reproducibility.
key_behaviors: Define and prescribe artifact and metadata conventions for model binaries, embeddings, and derived outputs that downstream pipelines and loaders will rely on.
Capture runtime inference patterns and parameter trade-offs (quantization, batching, speculative decoding, CPU/GPU optimizations) for llama.cpp and compatible loaders.
Provide machine-consumable experiment configurations and templates (YAML) plus methodological guidance for ablation/analysis studies used by orchestration tooling.
Specify structured generation contracts (JSON, Pydantic, JSON Schema) and backend selection trade-offs that guide how model responses are validated and consumed by the system.

insights.title: Docs serve as authoritative behavioral contracts
insights.problem: Operational markdown and templates are used as the expected specification for downstream code and automation, but they are not intrinsically enforced.
insights.root_cause: Separation between human-readable guidance/templates and runtime code/schema validation leads to possible drift between docs and implementations.
insights.solution: Treat these files as the source of truth for expected artifact names, config shape, and runtime parameter semantics; any code edits that change behavior, config keys, or artifact layout must account for and keep these documents consistent.

insights.title: GGUF / model artifact conventions are relied upon by loaders
insights.problem: Downstream loaders and pipelines expect specific GGUF packaging, metadata, and artifact outputs (embeddings, metadata files) to be present and named predictably.
insights.root_cause: The docs describe packaging conventions and expected outputs; runtime components assume those conventions when locating and loading models/artifacts.
insights.solution: Preserve artifact metadata formats, filenames, and packaging semantics; any change to artifact structure requires updating all references across deploy/run tooling and templates that consume them.

insights.title: Inference parameter trade-offs are documented and prescriptive
insights.problem: Guidance documents prescribe batching, quantization, and speculative decoding parameters which downstream runtimes assume for performance/accuracy trade-offs.
insights.root_cause: Performance and correctness depend on model format and loader configurations; the documentation encapsulates those operational decisions.
insights.solution: When changing runtime behaviors, ensure the documented parameters remain accurate for the targeted loader/backends and that callers and orchestration tools are compatible with those settings.

insights.title: OBLITERATUS experiment templates encode sensitive experiment contracts
insights.problem: YAML templates and methods describe safety-relevant ablation studies and analysis flows that orchestration tooling will execute; inconsistencies can produce invalid or unsafe experiments.
insights.root_cause: Experiment configs are machine-consumable and control potentially sensitive model manipulations; they rely on exact schema, keys, and expected inputs.
insights.solution: Maintain the shape and semantics of YAML templates and method descriptions; validate edits against any orchestration/runner expectations and preserve fields that downstream experiment tooling consumes.

key_files.why: Top-level explanation of the folder's purpose and scope — used to orient editors and automation about the role of these docs in runtime contracts.
Authoritative guide for GGUF quantization and llama.cpp compatibility — defines conversion and runtime expectations relied on by model loading/serving code.
Documents runtime behaviors (speculative decoding, batching, quantization) that directly influence inference settings and performance tuning in production.
Machine-consumable experiment config used by runners/orchestrators to create ablation experiments — changes here affect reproducibility and safety controls.
Specifies structured generation contracts (Pydantic/JSON Schema) which downstream validators, parsers, and consumers depend on for correct output handling.

construct_relationships: GGUF guides (gguf/SKILL.md and references) define model artifact formats and runtime parameters used by Llama/LlamaCpp loaders referenced across inference runtime code.
Advanced usage docs (gguf/references/advanced-usage.md) map performance controls (batching, speculative decoding) to loader configurations that orchestration tooling will apply at serving time.
Outlines JSON generation docs establish schema and Pydantic-based contracts that validators/parsers in runtime pipelines must implement and enforce.
OBLITERATUS YAML templates encode parameters and input shapes that experiment runners expect; methods and analysis docs describe the processing modules those runners must invoke.
notable_relationships: Runtime code under agent/ and gateway/ will consume the artifact conventions and inference parameters documented here — edits that alter names/keys/semantics will have downstream impact on model loading and API compatibility.

Experiment orchestration and any automation that launches OBLITERATUS studies depend on the exact YAML schema and method descriptions; those are upstream inputs to orchestration tooling outside this subtree.

Backend selection guidance (Outlines/Guidance) ties into provider adapters and model routing components elsewhere in the codebase (agent/model adapters, credential pool) and therefore must remain aligned with their capabilities.
Persistence and storage conventions (artifact naming/locations) interact with project-wide HERMES_HOME/data paths and deployment tooling (Docker/systemd) that mount or back up model artifacts.
recommendations: Treat these files as specification: when modifying any doc, template, or example, check for and coordinate corresponding runtime/configuration references elsewhere (model loaders, orchestration runners, validators).
Do not change artifact filenames, metadata key names, or YAML schema fields without auditing all consumers (loaders, experiment runners, deployment scripts) that parse them.

When altering any documented inference parameter or backend mapping, validate compatibility with the actual loader implementations (e.g., llama.cpp/LlamaCpp) and consider running the inference integration tests or a focused end-to-end run using a local model.

For edits to experiment templates or methods (OBLITERATUS), verify that downstream orchestration tooling and safety review processes accept the new shape; preserve fields relevant to reproducibility and safety controls.

Run relevant unit and integration tests that exercise model loading, artifact discovery, and structured-output validation after edits; be aware that full e2e tests may require heavy optional extras and real model artifacts.

skills/mlops/models.description: This folder contains the authoritative user- and operator-facing documentation for the mlops model skills (AudioCraft, CLIP, Segment Anything, Stable Diffusion, Whisper). It centralizes invocation surfaces, advanced workflows (fine‑tuning, adapters, multi‑GPU), and troubleshooting guidance that downstream code, packaging, and deployment automation rely on for correct runtime behaviour and operator remediation.
key_behaviors: Documenting model invocation surfaces (inputs, outputs, expected artifacts) that implementers and runtime orchestrators must honor.
Defining advanced training and deployment workflows (fine‑tuning/LoRA, multi‑GPU, adapters) that map to resource and orchestration requirements.
Enumerating platform and dependency requirements (GPU, ffmpeg, CUDA, drivers) and configuration knobs that affect runtime stability and performance.
Capturing failure modes and step‑by‑step troubleshooting patterns used by operators to diagnose and recover model runs.
Mapping user‑facing options (language codes, zero‑shot prompts, batching strategies) to concrete implementation expectations.

insights.title: Docs are the operational source of truth for skills
insights.problem: Code, packaging, or ops automation may diverge from the documented invocation surface and operational steps.
insights.root_cause: These Markdown files are maintained as the user/operator contract; changes in code or deps are not automatically reflected in prose.
insights.solution: Treat the SKILL.md and references as the canonical specification when editing code or ops; any change in runtime behaviour, CLI flags, or required deps must be reconciled with these docs and vice versa to avoid operator surprises.

insights.title: Model workflows depend on fragile system-level dependencies
insights.problem: Instructions reference GPU drivers, CUDA toolkits, ffmpeg, and other native binaries whose mismatch causes common failures.
insights.root_cause: Advanced ML model runtimes and media pipelines require native libraries with specific versions/configurations that are outside Python package management.
insights.solution: When modifying code or CI/packaging, ensure compatibility with the exact native dependencies described (or update docs if the supported set changes). Verify that environment preconditions and recovery steps in troubleshooting remain applicable.

insights.title: Fine‑tuning and multi‑GPU flows impose orchestration requirements
insights.problem: Distributed training and LoRA instructions describe behaviors (batching, device mapping, multi‑node) that, if not respected by orchestration code, can fail silently or degrade reproducibility.
insights.root_cause: Operational patterns require alignment between scripts, scheduler configs, and runtime resource allocation which are documented here but implemented elsewhere.
insights.solution: Any code changes that affect training loops, data loaders, or device placement must be validated against the advanced usage guidance and the documented resource expectations; ensure examples and defaults match actual runtime behavior.

insights.title: Troubleshooting flows depend on expected logs, error signals, and remedies
insights.problem: Operators rely on the troubleshooting docs to interpret errors and take remedial action; divergence between logs produced and documented symptoms reduces debuggability.
insights.root_cause: Docs list known failure modes and recovery steps; code changes that alter error messages, exit codes or recovery paths make the docs inaccurate.
insights.solution: Preserve or update diagnostic output, log messages, and recommended remediation steps in step with any code or packaging changes so operators can follow the documented workflows.

insights.title: Language and modality mappings are normative
insights.problem: Whisper language support and CLIP/SAM invocation patterns are used as exact mappings by callers; mismatches lead to incorrect model selection or poor results.
insights.root_cause: The docs map user‑facing names to model codes, modes (transcription vs translation), and pre/post processing expectations that downstream code assumes.
insights.solution: When editing code that selects models, formats inputs, or interprets outputs, ensure those mappings remain consistent with the documented tables and caveats; update docs if behavior or supported sets change.

key_files.why: Top‑level overview that frames the scope and expectations for model skills; used as the first reference for integrators and maintainers.
Authoritative operational guide for AudioCraft workflows (generation, fine‑tuning, multi‑GPU) that implementers and CI rely on.
Defines the SAM skill's invocation surface and integration notes (variants, batching) required by runtime adapters and orchestration.
Describes Stable Diffusion inputs/outputs, adapters and expected artifacts; a primary reference for image generation skill implementation and deployment.
Documents Whisper modes, supported languages, and runtime preconditions that downstream audio pipelines and transcriber code depend on.

construct_relationships: SKILL.md files define the invocation surface (inputs, outputs, flags) that must match the skill implementation and any orchestration code that calls these models.
references/advanced-usage.md documents fine‑tuning and multi‑GPU patterns that map to training scripts, device allocation, and scheduler config used elsewhere.
references/troubleshooting.md entries map observed errors and remediation steps to expected logs, exit codes, and environment checks produced by runtime components.
Language/support docs (e.g., whisper/references/languages.md) provide mapping tables used by input preprocessor and model selection logic in the skill runtime.
notable_relationships: These docs are consumed by the skills subsystem and skill loaders elsewhere in the repo; changes here can affect how operator tooling, CI, and packaging present and validate model skills.

Operational advice references system-level dependencies (CUDA, ffmpeg, device drivers) and Dockerfile/packaging instructions; maintainers of build and deployment artifacts need to keep parity with these docs.
Advanced training notes affect scheduler, CI job definitions, and any automation that spins up distributed training — cross‑impacting cron, CI jobs, and deployment scripts.
Language and modality mappings tie into agent prompt construction and tool invocation flow; mismatches can surface across gateway and CLI integrations that route user requests to these skills.
recommendations: Preserve the docs as the canonical contract: when editing code that changes invocation or runtime behaviour, ensure corresponding documentation is updated (and vice versa).
Verify native dependency compatibility (CUDA, ffmpeg, drivers) and reflect exact version or installation steps in docs when changing packaging, CI, or runtime code paths.

When changing training or inference code paths (device placement, batching, adapters), run representative workloads that exercise the advanced usage scenarios documented here (multi‑GPU, LoRA, large batches).
Keep troubleshooting text aligned with actual logs and error messages emitted by the runtime so operator remediation steps remain actionable.
Check language/model mapping tables (Whisper, CLIP modes) for consistency with any code that selects or formats inputs; ensure no silent remapping or aliasing is introduced.

skills/mlops/training.description: This subtree contains MLOps training templates, skill documentation, and references for fine-tuning and RL training workflows (notably a GRPO + TRL + PEFT template). It codifies dataset/prompt shape, reward function composition, model/tokenizer + adapter configuration (LoRA/PEFT/FSDP), and operational notes that downstream model artifacts and deployment workflows will consume. The folder matters because it is the canonical source of training recipes, expectations about input/output formats, and operational constraints for reproducible model fine-tuning within the project.
key_behaviors: Prepare and stream datasets into prompt/label shapes expected by TRL trainers (dataset loading, prompt assembly, tokenization assumptions).
Define, compose, and evaluate reward signals (correctness/format/incremental) that produce scalar rewards used by GRPO-style RL trainers.
Configure and wrap model + tokenizer with PEFT/LoRA adapters (and optionally integrate with FSDP/quantization) before training.
Launch training runs (GRPOTrainer) and persist model checkpoints/artifacts in a format expected by downstream consumers.
Provide documentation, troubleshooting guidance, and reference formats to ensure reproducible runs and interoperability across distributed setups.

insights.title: Dataset shape / streaming is a brittle integration point
insights.problem: Training templates depend on a specific dataset schema and streaming behavior; mismatches cause prompt assembly, tokenization, or trainer failures.
insights.root_cause: Prompt-builder and reward functions assume particular field names, delimiters and conversational structure (inherited from Axolotl formats).
insights.solution: Treat dataset format and streaming behavior as a strict contract: validate incoming datasets against the expected schema, include small-unit tests or synthetic examples to exercise prompt assembly and tokenization, and surface clear errors when shapes differ.

insights.title: Reward functions are tightly coupled to prompt / label formatting
insights.problem: Composed reward signals (correctness/format/incremental) can give misleading scores if model outputs or references deviate in tokenization, delimiters, or whitespace.
insights.root_cause: Reward logic assumes canonical output formatting and exact-match or structured comparisons without exhaustive normalization.
insights.solution: Ensure reward functions are validated against representative model outputs (including edge cases), and unit-test reward computations with varied formatting to detect fragility early.

insights.title: PEFT/LoRA/quantization integration is fragile across versions and backends
insights.problem: Applying LoRA/PEFT wrappers, mixing quantized weights, or combining with distributed sharding can produce incompatible parameter shapes, device placement issues, or checkpoint incompatibility.
insights.root_cause: Multiple moving parts (transformers version, peft, trl, FSDP, quantization libs) each change runtime expectations for model state, param names, and save/load semantics.
insights.solution: Treat PEFT/adapter configuration and model setup as a compatibility boundary: assert library versions, test save/load of a small LoRA-wrapped model, and include smoke tests for parameter freezing/unfreezing and device placement.

insights.title: Checkpoint and distributed training semantics differ across runtimes
insights.problem: Sharded/FSDP checkpoints and single-node checkpoints are not interchangeable without explicit conversion; trainer wrappers may expect particular serialization formats.
insights.root_cause: Different libraries (TRL, PyTorch FSDP, PEFT) implement distinct checkpointing strategies and naming for parameters/states.
insights.solution: Document and test checkpoint save/load paths for single-node and sharded runs, and maintain clear expectations in docs for how artifacts are consumed downstream (including any required conversion steps).

insights.title: Documentation and runnable examples must remain synchronized with code and extras
insights.problem: Multiple SKILL.md/README and reference files can become out of sync with templates or required extras, causing confusion during reproduction of training runs.
insights.root_cause: Operational instructions depend on optional extras, environment variables, GPU availability, and heavy third-party installs which vary by developer/CI environment.
insights.solution: When editing code, ensure docs reflect required extras, expected environment variables, and minimal reproducible commands; validate that README/SKILL.md align with the behavior of the templates.

key_files.why: Primary runnable GRPO training template that encodes dataset shaping, reward definitions, model/tokenizer setup, and the GRPOTrainer invocation — the canonical implementation to be preserved for reproducibility.
Top-level overview and structural notes for GRPO training that explain how the template is intended to be used and integrated into workflows.
User-facing documentation describing the GRPO/TRL training workflow and operational expectations; must be consistent with template behavior.
Defines dataset formats and streaming guidance that the training templates rely on; changes here affect how get_dataset and prompt assembly must behave.
Holds known issues and fixes for PEFT/LoRA interactions which are directly relevant to model setup and checkpointing in the training templates.

construct_relationships: basic_grpo_training.get_dataset -> prepares prompts and labels that feed into reward functions (correctness/format/incremental).
Reward functions -> produce scalar rewards consumed by GRPOTrainer to compute policy updates.

basic_grpo_training.setup_model_and_tokenizer -> constructs a model wrapped with PEFT/LoRA adapters which is passed into GRPOTrainer; adapter configuration affects checkpoint format and device placement.
axolotl dataset format references -> define the expected input schema for get_dataset and prompt assembly in the training templates.
PEFT troubleshooting docs -> document incompatibilities and operational steps relevant to the model setup performed in the training template.
notable_relationships: External library dependencies: templates assume TRL, PEFT, transformers, and optionally FSDP/quantization libraries are available and compatible; editing templates may require coordinating with package versions declared in project-level configuration.

Downstream consumption: produced model checkpoints/artifacts are expected to be consumed by runtime/deployment workflows or evaluation pipelines; their serialization and compatibility are upstream concerns for serving components.

Operational constraints: training recipes reference heavy optional extras and hardware (GPUs, sharding) not present in CI; edits should respect the separation between unit-level smoke tests and full-scale training runs.
Documentation coupling: SKILL.md/README and references are authoritative for users reproducing runs — code and docs must remain aligned to avoid operational drift.
recommendations: When editing any training template or reward logic, do not change dataset field names, prompt delimiters, or expected schema without updating and validating axolotl dataset-format references and all SKILL/README docs.
Validate reward functions with unit tests that exercise edge-case outputs and tokenization differences before running full training; include small synthetic examples as smoke tests.

Any change to model, PEFT/LoRA, or checkpointing behavior must be validated with save/load round-trip tests (single-node and, if applicable, sharded/FSDP) to ensure downstream consumers can load artifacts.

Respect library compatibility constraints: check and pin TRL/PEFT/transformers/FSDP versions where applicable, and run a minimal training smoke test on a CPU/GPU light mode to detect integration regressions.

Avoid running full-scale distributed training in CI; instead add focused unit/smoke tests for prompt assembly, reward computation, and adapter wrapping. Update SKILL.md/README and troubleshooting references to reflect any behavioral changes that affect reproducibility.

skills/productivity/powerpoint.description: This subtree implements CLI utilities and XML-processing helpers for manipulating unpacked Office package directories (primarily PPTX and DOCX). It provides slide-level operations (create/duplicate), integrity-preserving cleanup, tracked-change and run-canonicalization helpers, and a packer that validates and zips the directory back into a .pptx/.docx/.xlsx. These tools are used to produce Office-compatible archives and thus must preserve cross-file relationships, namespaces, and manifest invariants.
key_behaviors: Create or duplicate slide XML and integrate it into the presentation manifest (presentation.xml), updating relationship (.rels) files and [Content_Types].xml so the new slide is recognized by Office.

Traverse .rels relationship graphs to compute transitive reachability from the presentation entrypoint and iteratively prune orphaned slides, media, and resource .rels while updating manifests accordingly.
Canonicalize document XML to reduce fragmentation and tracked-change noise (merge adjacent runs with identical formatting; collapse adjacent w:ins/w:del for the same author) before packing.
Pack an unpacked Office directory into a zipped Office file, optionally running validators/auto-repair and XML condensation steps, and output byte-compatible archives meant for Office consumption.
Operate as CLI utilities that make in-place changes to an unpacked Office tree and print small machine-readable outputs (e.g., <p:sldId> elements) that callers may parse.

insights.title: Merging and condensation operations depend on exact XML node adjacency and xml:space preservation
insights.problem: Helpers that merge runs or collapse tracked-change wrappers rely on adjacency semantics and must preserve xml:space and mixed text node boundaries to avoid altering visible text or whitespace.
insights.root_cause: Office XML varies between editors and saves; whitespace and text-node fragmentation are common and merging without paying attention to xml:space or adjacent text nodes can change rendering or semantics.
insights.solution: When modifying XML, preserve xml:space attributes, consolidate adjacent text nodes carefully, and ensure operations are whitespace- and namespace-aware; tests should include documents with varied whitespace and tracked-change patterns to detect regressions.

insights.title: Slide creation and deletion require coordinated updates across multiple manifests
insights.problem: Adding, duplicating, or removing slides touches presentation.xml, slide .rels, package .rels, and [Content_Types].xml. Missing any update leaves the package inconsistent or Office may ignore the slide or fail to open the file.
insights.root_cause: Office packages are multi-file ZIPs whose correctness depends on coherent IDs, relationship targets, and content-type overrides in several places.
insights.solution: Any change that adds or removes items must atomically update all related .rels entries, the presentation slideId list, and [Content_Types].xml. CLI behavior and printed outputs (e.g., <p:sldId>) are part of the contract and must remain stable so callers can integrate changes.

insights.title: Orphan detection requires iterative transitive traversal and is potentially destructive
insights.problem: Pruning logic that naively deletes files not referenced in a single-pass can remove resources referenced indirectly or by custom relationships, causing data loss.
insights.root_cause: Relationship graphs across .rels can be nested and non-linear; custom/third-party parts or unusual relationship types might be missed by a single-pass algorithm.
insights.solution: Pruning must compute transitive closure of references, iterate until fixed point, and be conservative about unknown/unsupported relationship types. Operations are inherently destructive so callers should expect backups or dry-run modes and tests should include nested/edge-case relationship topologies.

insights.title: Packing may rely on optional validators/auto-repair utilities that are not always installed
insights.problem: Pack CLI supports running validators and auto-repair steps that can be absent in minimal installs; invoking them without checking can fail the pack step.
insights.root_cause: Validator/auto-repair functionality is treated as optional and may require extras or external binaries.
insights.solution: Pack operations must detect the presence of validators and gracefully skip or provide clear error messages if they are unavailable. Tests should run both with and without optional validators to ensure graceful behavior.

insights.title: Scripts are used programmatically and print machine-parsable fragments
insights.problem: Downstream callers expect stable CLI outputs (for example, printed <p:sldId> snippets) and deterministic side effects; changing output format or side-effect ordering breaks integrators.
insights.root_cause: Scripts serve as both user-facing CLIs and programmatic primitives for other skills/tools; output and behavior are part of an implicit API.
insights.solution: Preserve the exact printed formats and documented side-effects. If behavior must change, coordinate change across callers and ensure backwards compatibility or version the CLI behavior.

key_files.why: Primary entry that creates or duplicates slide XML and orchestrates updates to presentation manifests and content types; its output (<p:sldId>) is consumed by callers.
Responsible for computing transitive reachability and removing orphaned slides/media/.rels; performs iterative pruning and manifest updates—high-risk destructive operations.
Zips an unpacked Office directory back into a .pptx/.docx/.xlsx, optionally running validators/auto-repair and XML condensation steps; produces final artifacts consumed by Office.
XML canonicalization helper that merges adjacent word runs with identical formatting; used to reduce fragmentation and simplify downstream processing prior to packing.
Helper that collapses adjacent tracked-change wrappers by author and infers tracked-change authorship; affects redline semantics and final document readability when packing.

construct_relationships: add_slide.py modifies presentation.xml slide list, writes new slide XML files, and updates slide-level .rels and package [Content_Types].xml so the presentation recognizes the new slide.

clean.py reads presentation.xml and related .rels, computes transitive reachability, removes unreachable slide files/media/rels, and updates presentation relationships and [Content_Types].xml accordingly.
pack.py consumes an unpacked directory, optionally invokes XML cleaners (merge_runs and simplify_redlines) and validators/auto-repair, then zips the directory into an Office archive.
merge_runs.py and simplify_redlines.py operate on document XML nodes (e.g., word/document.xml) and are used by pack.py to condense XML and reduce noise introduced by editing/tracked changes.
CLI scripts and helper modules share the same unpacked-directory layout and must agree on path conventions, relationship parsing, and content-type manipulation logic.
notable_relationships: Produced .pptx/.docx artifacts are consumed by external Office applications; any incompatibility will surface as file open errors or lost content downstream.
These scripts are likely invoked by higher-level skills/tools in the agent runtime; CLI output and side effects form an implicit API other components rely on.
Pack and helper steps may depend on optional external validators or binaries (installed via extras); local dev environments and CI may differ in available features.
Filesystem semantics (in-place edits, backups, atomicity) matter because these tools operate on user worktrees; callers may expect durable or reversible behavior (e.g., backups or dry-run).
recommendations: Respect Office package invariants: when adding/removing parts always update presentation.xml, all affected .rels, and [Content_Types].xml in a coordinated manner; preserve namespaces and xml:space.
Treat cleanup as destructive: ensure backups/dry-run behavior or explicit confirmations are preserved; tests should include nested/edge-case relationship graphs to avoid accidental deletion.
Maintain CLI output formats and side-effect ordering (e.g., printed <p:sldId>) because other code may parse these outputs programmatically.

When editing helpers, preserve text-node boundaries and xml:space when merging runs or collapsing tracked changes; validate results against varied real-world documents to avoid rendering/regression issues.

Account for optional validators and external binaries: detect availability at runtime and handle missing dependencies gracefully; CI/dev differences mean code should not assume validators are present.
Run the pack + validate flow and open the resulting archive in an Office validator or application where possible after changes; include both validator-present and validator-absent test scenarios.

skills/red-teaming/godmode.description: This folder contains an offline red‑teaming toolkit ("godmode") that automates jailbreak discovery, races multiple models for best responses, and generates obfuscated input variants. Its scripts interact directly with model provider clients, scoring heuristics, and on‑disk persistence (config/prefill templates), so changes can affect querying behavior, persistence of jailbreak artifacts, and external API usage.
key_behaviors: Discover and lock working jailbreak prompts by testing candidate strategies against canary queries, scoring responses, and persisting chosen prompt/prefill artifacts.
Race multiple models in parallel (tiered lists) by issuing concurrent requests, scoring returned outputs for refusal/hedging/detail, and selecting the best non‑refusal response.
Generate many obfuscated variants of an input (33 transformation techniques) and detect trigger words to escalate encodings for retries when attempting to bypass input‑side classifiers.
Dynamically load and expose script functions/constants into an interactive namespace via a loader that exec()s helper scripts and adjusts process state (sys.argv, imported symbols).

insights.title: On‑disk persistence of chosen jailbreak/prefill
insights.problem: auto_jailbreak can write config.yaml and prefill.json to lock in a working jailbreak, which persists potentially sensitive or policy‑violating prompts and may overwrite existing files.
insights.root_cause: Scripts deliberately persist artifacts to the repo/runtime file system without centralized guardrails or explicit backup/versioning.
insights.solution: Treat writes as high‑impact operations: ensure callers/agents check intended paths, permissions, and backups before overwrite; consider visibility of persisted artifacts (secrets, policy exposure) and where files are stored relative to HERMES_HOME. Validate file I/O paths and fail safely if storage is not available.

insights.title: Parallel multi‑model racing consumes credentials, rate limits and costs
insights.problem: race_godmode and auto_jailbreak issue concurrent model requests across provider clients, which can trigger rate limits, cost spikes, or key exhaustion if not coordinated with credential/quotas management.
insights.root_cause: Scripts manage parallel requests locally (tiered model lists) and do not appear to integrate with centralized credential pools or quota management in the main agent.
insights.solution: When editing, respect provider quotas and credential rotation policies: ensure concurrency controls, backoff handling, and explicit dependency points for supplying API keys. Tests and local runs should account for CI behavior that blanks API keys.

insights.title: Refusal/hedge scoring is heuristic and brittle
insights.problem: Scoring functions (is_refusal, score_response, refusal detection rules) drive selection and acceptance; small changes to heuristics can dramatically change outputs and false positive/negative rates.
insights.root_cause: Rule‑based detection relies on token/phrase checks and handcrafted scoring thresholds rather than a single authoritative signal.
insights.solution: Treat these heuristics as high‑impact decision points: preserve signature behavior or provide explicit tests that capture expected refusal/non‑refusal classification for known examples. When altering thresholds or rules, update accompanying reference docs/templates and tests.

insights.title: Obfuscation generates combinatorial variants
insights.problem: Parseltongue's many transform techniques can generate a very large candidate set causing CPU, memory, and request volume issues when combined with parallel model queries.
insights.root_cause: A registry of 33 transforms plus escalation encodings can be applied in many combinations without built‑in limiting logic.
insights.solution: Be mindful of generation limits and runtime cost: ensure caller code bounds the number of returned variants, avoid unbounded cartesian product expansion, and surface generated counts in logs/tests to catch runaway behavior.

insights.title: Loader uses exec() and mutates process state
insights.problem: load_godmode exec()s scripts into the current process, adjusting sys.argv and exposing symbols; this is brittle and can cause unexpected state leakage or name collisions in the runtime environment.
insights.root_cause: Dynamic loading provides interactive convenience at the cost of isolation and determinism.
insights.solution: When modifying the loader, preserve the expected symbol export behavior and process state adjustments; ensure any changes maintain clear boundaries for what symbols are imported and clean up temporary state to avoid side effects when used interactively or in automated runs.

key_files.why: Primary pipeline for discovering, testing, scoring, and optionally persisting jailbreak prompts and prefill artifacts — touching persistence and multi‑model querying.
Parallel multi‑model racing and response scoring engine that orchestrates concurrent requests and refusal/hedge detection.
Obfuscation engine that generates trigger‑evading input variants; influences request volume and effectiveness of red‑teaming pipelines.
Dynamic loader that exec()s scripts into the interactive namespace and controls imported symbol exposure and temporary state handling.
Canonical prefill template used when persisting or loading godmode prompts — changes affect what is written by auto_jailbreak and used during replay.

construct_relationships: auto_jailbreak.py invokes model querying and scoring logic similar to godmode_race.py and relies on the scoring/refusal detection heuristics (is_refusal, score_response) to decide which candidate to persist.
auto_jailbreak.py may consume obfuscated variants generated by parseltongue.py to probe input‑side classifiers or to retry with encoded escalations.

godmode_race.py issues parallel requests to model provider clients and returns the top non‑refusal response; its refusal/hedging detectors align with rules documented in references/refusal-detection.md.

load_godmode.py exec()s the helper scripts (auto_jailbreak, godmode_race, parseltongue) into the current process and exports callable symbols and UPPERCASE constants for interactive use, creating tight coupling between loader behavior and those scripts' module globals.
Templates (prefill.json/prefill-subtle.json) are read/written by auto_jailbreak.py and shape the persisted prompt/prefill artifacts used by other tools that may rehydrate or dispatch them.
notable_relationships: These scripts make outbound calls to model provider clients (OpenRouter/OpenAI or other provider clients used elsewhere in the project); changes affect API usage, quotas, and credential handling that may be centrally managed under agent/credential_pool or provider adapter code.

Persisted artifacts are stored on disk (templates/config/prefill) and interact with project persistence conventions (HERMES_HOME/data or other configured storage); altering paths or formats affects backup, restore, and gateway/skill replay behavior.

Refusal detection and scoring rules reference documentation in references/refusal-detection.md and templates; changes should maintain alignment with the documented rules to avoid test and behavioral drift.
CI and test environments blank API keys; local edits that invoke provider clients must consider CI guards and avoid running network calls in unit tests unless explicitly intended (integration/e2e).
recommendations: When editing any file here, preserve or explicitly document places that persist files (config.yaml, prefill.json): check file paths, backups, permissions, and whether writes should be gated.

Respect external API constraints: maintain or add explicit concurrency/rate limit controls for parallel model requests and ensure code integrates or can be wired into centralized credential/quota management.
Do not modify scoring/refusal heuristics without adding or updating unit tests that assert expected classifications on representative examples; treat heuristics as behavioral contracts.
When changing parseltongue, ensure generation limits are enforced (max variants) and measure performance/memory impact in tests to avoid unbounded variant explosion.
Any change to load_godmode must preserve its exported symbol list and restore mutated process state (sys.argv, temporary variables) to avoid unexpected interactive or automated side effects.

Run unit tests and relevant integration/e2e tests when edits affect external calls or persistence; remember CI blanks API keys so local runs that use providers require configured credentials or mocks.

optional-skills/mlops/nemo-curator.description: This folder contains reference documentation for the Nemo Curator dataset curation optional skill within the MLOps tooling. It codifies strategies for identifying and removing duplicate examples (exact, fuzzy, semantic) and for applying quality-filtering stages so curated datasets are consistent, high-quality, and cost-effective to use in downstream model training and evaluation.
key_behaviors: Define and document multiple deduplication strategies (exact match, fuzzy/token-based, and semantic/embedding-based) and their tradeoffs.
Specify a staged quality-filtering pipeline that scores, normalizes, and removes low-quality or malformed examples before or during curation.
Coordinate ordering and interaction between filtering and deduplication to balance compute cost and dataset quality.
Surface operational concerns (compute cost, latency, false positives/negatives) and provide parameters/thresholds for tuning.
Provide guidance that downstream training/data-storage systems must adhere to (e.g., canonical normalization, hashing, embedding computation).

insights.title: Filtering must gate expensive deduplication
insights.problem: Running costly semantic deduplication across large noisy corpora wastes compute and can amplify false positives.
insights.root_cause: Unfiltered ingestion pipelines let malformed or low-quality records reach deduplication, where expensive embedding or neighbor-search steps are performed on unlikely-to-be-kept data.
insights.solution: Ensure the documented pipeline emphasizes early, deterministic quality checks and normalization (tokenization, truncation, language detection, bad-character removal) so that downstream deduplication operates on a smaller, higher-signal set; document expected ordering and thresholds to make tradeoffs explicit.

insights.title: Exact, fuzzy, and semantic approaches have distinct failure modes
insights.problem: A single deduplication strategy will miss some duplicate classes or will incorrectly collapse distinct but similar examples.
insights.root_cause: Exact matching is brittle to minor edits; fuzzy/token-based approaches can be confused by paraphrases or reordered tokens; semantic methods depend on embeddings and thresholds that can produce false merges or miss duplicates due to domain drift.
