insights.solution: Document the complementary nature of the methods, the contexts where each is appropriate, and the evaluation signals (precision/recall, sample inspection) teams should collect when choosing thresholds. Highlight risks of semantic-similarity thresholds and the need for domain-specific calibration.

insights.title: Semantic deduplication imposes compute and consistency requirements
insights.problem: Embedding-based deduplication can be expensive and non-deterministic if embedding models or preprocessing change over time, causing non-reproducible curation results.
insights.root_cause: Dependence on external models, different tokenization/normalization, or unstated seeding/nearest-neighbor indexing configuration.
insights.solution: Document the expected embedding model specifications, hashing/normalization steps, index/versioning practices, and indicate that reproducibility requires pinning model and preprocessing versions; warn about cost implications and recommend logging of parameters and artifacts.

insights.title: Aggressive deduplication can remove valuable diversity
insights.problem: Over-aggressive thresholds or poorly chosen fuzzy/semantic heuristics can collapse distinct examples, reducing dataset diversity and harming downstream model generalization.
insights.root_cause: Applying similarity thresholds without empirical calibration or without preserving provenance and human review for edge cases.
insights.solution: Advise conservative defaults in documentation, require sampling-based evaluation of removals, and recommend preserving originals or provenance metadata so removals can be audited or reversed.

key_files.why: Primary reference describing the deduplication strategies, tradeoffs, and operational considerations that downstream curation code will follow or implement.
Defines the quality-filtering pipeline and normalization steps that are intended to precede or integrate with deduplication and which materially affect deduplication behavior and costs.

construct_relationships: The filtering pipeline produces normalized, quality-scored records that feed into deduplication; filtering decisions (e.g., language, length, tokenization) directly affect deduplication matching results.
Exact deduplication operates on canonicalized identifiers or hashes produced by the filtering/normalization steps, while fuzzy deduplication uses token-based similarity computed on filtered text.

Semantic deduplication depends on embeddings derived from records post-filtering; therefore, embedding model choice and preprocessing described in filtering.md are prerequisites for consistent semantic matching.
Thresholds and parameters documented for deduplication reference the scoring produced by the filtering stage; changes in one side (e.g., normalization) change effective thresholds for the other.
notable_relationships: Upstream: The curation directives here assume integration with ingestion pipelines that supply raw records and with embedding services or model adapters elsewhere in the codebase; changes to model adapters or ingestion normalization will affect outcomes.

Downstream: Results of filtering and deduplication feed training datasets, storage systems, and data-versioning/lineage tooling; incorrect or non-reproducible curation will impact model training, evaluation, and auditing.

Cross-cutting: Operational concerns documented (compute cost, reproducibility, thresholds) imply dependencies on resource configuration (GPU/CPU for embeddings), index/storage for neighbor search (vector DBs or FAISS), and logging/metadata systems for provenance.
recommendations: When editing these documents, preserve and clearly state any normalization, tokenization, embedding-model, and threshold parameters since they are normative and consumed by code and pipelines elsewhere.
Keep ordering dependencies explicit: note whether filtering is expected to run before each deduplication stage and any invariants (e.g., canonical hashing) relied upon by exact/fuzzy matching.

Ensure any described embedding/model names, versions, or external services match the code/config in other subsystems (model adapters, vector DB connectors); if changing names or parameters, coordinate with those components.

Maintain guidance about operational cost, reproducibility, and sampling/evaluation practices; downstream training or CI that depends on deterministic curation must be considered when altering heuristics.

Update or run dataset-level sanity checks, sampling-based validation, and provenance audits after changes: verify deduplication recall/precision characteristics and that removals are reversible or logged for audit.

skills/creative/p5js.description: This subtree provides a small toolchain to develop, serve, deterministically capture, and encode p5.js sketches into frame sequences and MP4 movies. It matters because the exporter relies on specific sketch-side conventions and on external system tools (Node/Puppeteer/Chromium, ffmpeg, Python/serve) so changes must preserve coordination between browser automation, local serving, and media encoding.
key_behaviors: Serve a local sketch directory over HTTP so browser-loaded p5.js sketches can access assets during development and automated runs.
Launch headless Chromium via a Node/Puppeteer script to drive a p5.js sketch, trigger redraws, and capture deterministic, frame-accurate PNGs based on a sketch readiness contract.
Fall back to timed capture if the sketch does not signal readiness, with configurable resolution, FPS, duration, and output directory.
Encode a numbered sequence of PNG frames into an MP4 using ffmpeg, with options for quality, frame-only export, and temporary directory management.
Check and report presence of required optional tools (Node, npm, Puppeteer, ffmpeg, Python) to help ensure the host environment supports the capture/render pipeline.

insights.title: Exporter requires sketch-side readiness and noLoop() contract
insights.problem: The Puppeteer frame exporter assumes sketches call noLoop() and set window._p5Ready so redraw() yields deterministic frames; sketches that do not follow this contract will break deterministic capture.
insights.root_cause: The capture script can't introspect arbitrary sketch state and depends on an explicit signal and redraw control to synchronize frame capture.
insights.solution: Treat the readiness / noLoop contract as an invariant when modifying exporter/serve scripts; any edits must preserve the mechanism and output behavior because downstream encoding expects ordered, frame-accurate PNGs.

insights.title: Strong coupling to system tools (Puppeteer/Chromium, ffmpeg, Python/serve)
insights.problem: Scripts orchestrate external binaries whose absence, version differences, or PATH/location differences will break the pipeline.
insights.root_cause: Design delegates rendering and encoding to third-party programs rather than embedding equivalents, relying on environment tooling and conventions.
insights.solution: Maintain explicit environment checks and clear error behavior; any change that alters invocation must preserve argument semantics and exit codes so callers and CI can detect failures.

insights.title: Timed fallback capture increases fragility and potential nondeterminism
insights.problem: If the readiness signal isn't set, the timed fallback may produce non-deterministic frames or duplicate/missed frames relative to intended FPS/duration.
insights.root_cause: Network/load variability and asynchronous sketch initialization can prevent the deterministic redraw pathway from being used.
insights.solution: Treat the fallback as best-effort; preserve its behavior and configuration knobs (timeout, fallback FPS/duration) and ensure callers can opt to fail fast or use fallback-only modes.

insights.title: Temporary frame directories and disk usage risks
insights.problem: Frame export and ffmpeg encoding produce large sequences of PNGs; improper cleanup or naming collisions can consume disk or confuse subsequent runs.
insights.root_cause: Shell pipeline creates and manages temporary directories and relies on naming conventions for ffmpeg inputs.
insights.solution: Preserve deterministic, zero-padded naming and cleanup semantics; maintain clear signals/options for frames-only mode and temporary-dir handling so automation can manage artifacts safely.

insights.title: Shell scripts assume Unix-like environment and availability of utilities
insights.problem: Scripts use bash/sh and rely on Python 3 or npx, which may not behave the same on Windows without WSL/Unix compatibility.
insights.root_cause: Lightweight dev tooling chosen for Unix-like systems; Windows native support isn't guaranteed.
insights.solution: When changing scripts, preserve shebangs, executable flags, and fallbacks between python3 and Node-based servers so the existing developer workflow (including WSL) remains functional.

key_files.why: Core frame exporter: headless Puppeteer script that loads a sketch, relies on window._p5Ready/noLoop(), triggers redraw() and writes numbered PNG frames; edits here affect capture determinism and frame naming.

Orchestrates full pipeline: invokes export-frames.js, manages temporary frame directories, and runs ffmpeg to produce MP4; changes here affect encoding parameters, temp handling, and end-to-end result.
Starts a local HTTP server for serving sketches and assets; used during development and by export processes that require HTTP access to sketches.
Environment verifier: checks for Node, npm, Puppeteer, ffmpeg, and Python and reports installation guidance; impacts developer setup and CI prechecks.

construct_relationships: render.sh invokes export-frames.js to produce a sequence of numbered PNG frames and then invokes ffmpeg with those images to produce an MP4.

export-frames.js expects to load the sketch over HTTP (so serve.sh or another HTTP server must make the sketch assets available) and depends on the sketch to signal readiness via window._p5Ready and to use noLoop() so redraw() advances frames deterministically.
serve.sh is an alternative local server provider used by developers and by automation pipelines to satisfy export-frames.js's need for HTTP-hosted sketch files.
setup.sh is orthogonal but should reflect the runtime requirements used by the other scripts (Node/Puppeteer/ffmpeg/Python) so that the environment checks align with actual invocations.
notable_relationships: Runtime depends on system-level toolchain: Node/npm (Puppeteer), a Chromium install managed by Puppeteer, ffmpeg for encoding, and Python or npx serve for HTTP hosting. CI or container images must provide these tools for successful runs.

Sketch-side contract (noLoop() and window._p5Ready) is an implicit API between user sketches and the exporter; changes here impact any sketches in other parts of the repo or user content consumed by the exporter.

Artifacts (PNG frame sequences, temporary directories, generated MP4s) are external to repository and may interact with broader storage/CI cleanup policies—disk usage and naming conventions matter for downstream consumers of the media.
recommendations: Preserve the sketch readiness contract: maintain reliance on window._p5Ready and noLoop()/redraw() semantics; do not change export timing/naming without considering caller expectations.
Keep CLI flags, env var interfaces, and output naming (zero-padded numbered PNGs) stable because render.sh and external tooling rely on them.
Retain or update comprehensive environment checks (Node, Puppeteer/Chromium, ffmpeg, Python) whenever changing invocations so failures remain detectable and actionable.
Ensure temporary directory creation, naming, and cleanup behavior are respected; avoid introducing race conditions or ambiguous paths that could collide across concurrent runs.

When editing shell scripts, preserve executable shebangs and fallbacks (python3 -m http.server vs npx serve) to maintain cross-developer compatibility; test end-to-end: serve -> export -> ffmpeg encode in an environment matching CI or intended deployment.

website/src/pages.description: This folder implements the Skills Hub page for the Docusaurus website: a client-side React page that presents a searchable, filterable, paginated catalog of skills with per-skill install hints, category/source faceting, and statistics. It combines a single page React component tree (index.tsx) with a CSS module (styles.module.css) and is intended to be built statically by Docusaurus and hydrated in the browser to enable interactive behavior.
key_behaviors: Client-side loading and rendering of a skills catalog into a dashboard view (cards, stats, sidebar).
Incremental, client-side filtering and keyword search with result highlighting and ranked presentation.
Category and source faceted filtering, pagination, and UI-driven install-hint presentation per skill.
Presentation-layer styling via a CSS module that scopes layout and visual components for the page.

insights.title: Client-only interactivity can produce SSR/hydration mismatches
insights.problem: Interactive behavior (filtering, pagination, DOM-dependent code) runs after static build/hydration and may cause differences between server-rendered HTML and client DOM.
insights.root_cause: Docusaurus performs static render / server-side render of pages; the page relies on client-side state and possibly browser-only APIs.
insights.solution: When making edits, preserve guards around window/DOM usage, avoid executing browser-only code at module-evaluation time, and validate changes with docusaurus start / build + client hydration checks.

insights.title: Tight coupling between CSS module class names and JSX
insights.problem: Renaming or removing CSS module classes can break layout, responsive behavior, or visual affordances used by the page.
insights.root_cause: index.tsx imports styles.module.css and directly references class names; styling is not global but referenced in markup.
insights.solution: Any change to class names or CSS structure must be coordinated with the component markup and validated in the built site across breakpoints.

insights.title: Client-side filtering/pagination operates on full catalog in-memory
insights.problem: Large skill catalogs can make filtering, highlighting, and pagination slow or memory intensive in the browser.
insights.root_cause: All filtering and search ranking appear to be implemented in-client against the loaded dataset.
insights.solution: When modifying filtering, state, or data-loading logic, consider performance characteristics and validate with large datasets during local site testing (dev server / built site).

insights.title: Install hints and skill metadata are presented directly in UI
insights.problem: Install instructions or metadata formatting changes may expose incorrect commands, broken links, or unintentionally expose sensitive tokens if underlying metadata changes.
insights.root_cause: Skill cards format and display raw metadata and install hints drawn from the catalog.
insights.solution: When altering rendering or formatting for install hints or metadata, ensure output is escaped/formatted consistently and verify that presented content remains accurate and safe in the built site.

insights.title: Interactive controls and highlighting must remain accessible and responsive
insights.problem: Custom interactive widgets (search input, sidebar filters, pills, cards) can break keyboard navigation, focus management, or mobile layout if changed without consideration.
insights.root_cause: The page implements bespoke interactive components and styles rather than relying solely on global theme components.
insights.solution: When editing component structure or styles, validate keyboard navigation, ARIA roles where applicable, and mobile/responsive behavior in the site preview.

key_files.why: Primary React page component implementing the Skills Dashboard: filtering, search/highlight, pagination, skill card rendering and install-hint interactions.
CSS module that provides scoped visual and layout styles used throughout the Skills page; changes directly affect layout and responsive behavior.

construct_relationships: SkillsDashboard (index.tsx) composes SkillCard and StatCard components to render the catalog and statistics UI.
SkillCard uses highlightMatch utility to apply search-term highlighting within rendered skill fields.
The page imports styles.module.css as a CSS module and applies scoped class names to dashboard, cards, search input and sidebar elements.
Filtering, faceting (categories/sources), and pagination are implemented as client-side stateful logic inside the page component and drive which SkillCard instances are rendered.
SkillCard emits or triggers install-hint presentation (tooltip/modal/inline content) that is managed by the page-level UI state.
notable_relationships: This page is a Docusaurus route and participates in the static-site build + client hydration lifecycle; changes affect both server-rendered HTML and client behavior.
The skills catalog data is consumed by this page (likely from a static JSON or site data source elsewhere in the repo) — component edits should remain compatible with that data shape.
Styling interacts with the site theme and global CSS; CSS module changes may conflict or interact with global theme styles.
Any modifications that change public markup (links, anchors, skill slugs) can affect external linking, bookmarks, and site search/indexing.
recommendations: Preserve server-render safe module initialization: avoid unconditional window/DOM use during module evaluation; validate with Docusaurus build + serve to detect hydration issues.
When renaming CSS classes or restructuring styles.module.css, update all JSX references in index.tsx and verify layout/responsive behavior in the site preview.
Validate performance and behavior with large skill datasets when changing filtering, search, or pagination logic—test on the dev server and a built site.
Confirm that install-hint and metadata rendering remains correctly escaped/formatted and does not surface secrets; test rendered output for accuracy.
Run TypeScript checks, linting, and a local Docusaurus start/build to verify SSR/hydration, accessibility (keyboard focus), and visual regression across breakpoints after edits.

tests/hermes_cli.description: This folder contains the unit tests that validate the hermes_cli command surface, tooling/config persistence, service management helpers, and related CLI UX pieces. These tests codify invariants about command registration, platform/tool mappings, HERMES_HOME persistence, and OS service integration (systemd/launchd) and are relied upon to prevent regressions when modifying CLI logic or platform/tool definitions.
key_behaviors: Validate command registry invariants (unique canonical names, categories, alias resolution, cli_only/gateway_only exclusivity) and drive prompt_toolkit integrations (completer and autosuggest).
Verify platform tool selection persistence, toolset/skill mapping consistency, and provider visibility gating (e.g., Nous-managed defaults).
Test webhook CLI flows including subscribe/list/remove, file persistence under HERMES_HOME, corrupted-file robustness, and gating when webhook support is disabled.

Simulate OS service management operations (generate systemd unit/plist, install/start/restart, detect running services and virtualenvs) and ensure HERMES_HOME and VIRTUAL_ENV are captured properly for services.
Ensure update/restart flows detect and restart gateway services correctly across systemd and launchd, and report outcomes with appropriate user hints.

insights.title: Registry invariants and cross-platform command collection
insights.problem: Tests assert strict invariants (no duplicate canonical names, valid categories, mutual exclusivity of cli_only/gateway_only) but some cross-platform aggregation behaviors (shared skill-to-command collection, Discord registration constraints) were not covered originally.
insights.root_cause: Command registration and aliasing logic is centralized; adding platforms/skills or back-compat aliases can silently violate invariants or platform-specific constraints if not validated.
insights.solution: When changing command registration/alias logic or adding platforms/skills, preserve canonical name uniqueness, category constraints, and platform gating semantics; ensure any cross-platform collection logic is exercised by tests.

insights.title: Telegram command name sanitization and platform menu behavior
insights.problem: Telegram-specific name constraints and sanitization must be enforced wherever menus or bot command lists are generated; prior gaps led to inconsistent sanitization across code paths.
insights.root_cause: Multiple code paths generate Telegram menu/command lists; sanitization lived in some but not all of them.
insights.solution: Treat Telegram name constraints as a cross-cutting requirement: changes that affect command names or menu generation must preserve sanitization in every generation path used by the CLI and gateway integrations.

insights.title: Toolset definitions must match platform mappings
insights.problem: Adding a platform or updating platform defaults without corresponding toolset or skills mapping can break installer flows and tests silently.
insights.root_cause: Tests and installer logic assume a 1:1 consistency between platform entries and toolset definitions; missing mappings manifest only at install-time or when tests verify toolset consistency.
insights.solution: Any change that adds/removes platforms or modifies default platform tool lists must also update the corresponding toolset definitions and skill mappings; tests expect platform/toolset consistency to be preserved.

insights.title: Environment capture in generated systemd/launchd units
insights.problem: Generated systemd units and launchd plists must include the resolved HOME path, PATH entries like ~/.local/bin, and virtualenv bin directory; omissions break service runs in user/system contexts.
insights.root_cause: Service generation logic must reconcile user vs system scope and local PATH differences; tests assert presence of specific PATH/VIRTUAL_ENV/HERMES_HOME entries.
insights.solution: When modifying service generation or environment detection, ensure the unit/plist generation continues to include resolved home paths, local bin entries, VIRTUAL_ENV, and HERMES_HOME with the same ordering/deduplication semantics the tests expect.

insights.title: Protect service-managed processes during manual sweeps
insights.problem: Update/restart logic risked killing service-managed PIDs during manual sweeps if those PIDs weren't excluded.
insights.root_cause: Manual process-sweep heuristics lacked checks to exclude PIDs known to be managed by systemd/launchd.
insights.solution: Restart/update flows and their tests assume service-managed PIDs are excluded from manual kill sweeps; changes to restart logic must maintain or reintroduce those exclusion checks and keep test coverage for the behavior.

insights.title: HERMES_HOME file persistence and corrupted-file handling
insights.problem: CLI features that persist state (webhook subscriptions, saved tool selections, config) must handle corrupted files gracefully and allow isolation for tests.
insights.root_cause: Persistent data under HERMES_HOME can be corrupted or inconsistent; tests expect graceful fallback and isolation via tmp_path fixtures.
insights.solution: When changing persistence formats or file locations, preserve robust load/fallback semantics and ensure tests can isolate HERMES_HOME via environment fixtures.

key_files.why: Central tests asserting command registry invariants, alias/canonical name resolution, and prompt_toolkit completer/autosuggest behavior — changes to commands or CLI completion must preserve these tests' expectations.
Validates platform tool selection persistence and platform/toolset consistency, exercising private helpers that installer flows and CLI use to persist tool choices.
Covers systemd/launchd unit/plist generation and service install/manage flows including PATH and VIRTUAL_ENV handling — important for OS integration logic.
Exercises auto-restart and update flows for gateway services and checks process exclusion and restart messaging — tied closely to service management behavior.
Tests webhook CLI persistence, corrupted-file handling, and gating behavior tied to HERMES_HOME isolation and feature flags.

construct_relationships: Command registry (hermes_cli.commands) -> prompt_toolkit completer/autosuggest: completer and autosuggest tests depend on canonical/alias resolution and command metadata.
Tools configuration helpers -> persistence under HERMES_HOME: tools_config tests verify that platform tool selections are saved/loaded while preserving MCP server names and defaults.

Gateway service helpers (systemd/launchd unit generation) <-> update/restart logic: both unit generation tests and update/restart tests depend on consistent environment capture (PATH, VIRTUAL_ENV, HERMES_HOME) and service detection heuristics.
Webhook CLI persistence <-> HERMES_HOME isolation: webhook subscribe/list/remove tests rely on tmp_path isolation of HERMES_HOME and corrupted-file fallback semantics.
Multiple tests use monkeypatch/fixtures to fake subprocess, env, and filesystem: tests expect code to be amenable to injection/mocking of subprocess.run, env vars, and file reads/writes.
notable_relationships: These tests assume hermes_cli code will not perform real network calls or mutate host services; they mock subprocess and rely on environment blanking in CI — changes that introduce external calls or tighter coupling to real services will break CI expectations.

HERMES_HOME is a shared persistence surface across CLI subsystems; modifications to file formats or locations affect multiple tests (webhooks, config, tools_config, gateway service HERMES_HOME remapping).

Gateway/service management code interacts with OS-specific behavior and virtualenv detection; changes may have downstream impact on packaging, Docker entrypoints, and systemd/launchd integration tested here.
Platform additions (Telegram/Slack/Discord) have downstream effects on command registration, telegram name sanitization, toolset definitions, and installer flows referenced by these tests.
recommendations: Run the tests in this folder (pytest tests/hermes_cli/) after edits; many tests rely on monkeypatching and tmp_path isolation — ensure test fixtures remain satisfied.
Preserve command registry invariants (unique canonical names, valid categories, cli_only/gateway_only mutual exclusivity) and the alias resolution semantics expected by completer/auto-suggest tests.

When changing persistent formats or file locations, maintain backward-compatible load/fallback behavior for HERMES_HOME persisted files and ensure tests can continue to isolate HERMES_HOME via tmp_path.

Any change to service unit/plist generation or restart logic must continue to include resolved HOME, ~/.local/bin PATH entries, VIRTUAL_ENV, and HERMES_HOME in the generated outputs, and must keep logic that excludes service-managed PIDs from manual kill sweeps.

Avoid introducing uncaptured external side effects (real subprocess/systemd interactions or network requests) in code paths exercised by these tests; the tests expect mocking and CI blanks API keys to prevent real calls.

tests/gateway.description: This folder contains unit tests that validate the gateway subsystem: configuration loading and overrides, per-platform adapters (Matrix, DingTalk, Discord, etc.), GatewayRunner lifecycle and error semantics, session lifecycle and transcript persistence, API server endpoints/security, and auxiliary gateway features (approvals, background tasks, delivery routing, channel directory). These tests codify cross-cutting expectations (environment-driven overrides, atomic persistence, E2EE and platform edge cases, and runner exit/reconnect semantics) that are critical for safely evolving gateway behavior and integrations.
key_behaviors: Configuration load and serialization round-trips, including YAML bridging and environment-variable overrides that can inject per-platform home_channel entries.

Platform adapter behaviors: message formatting, mxc://→http conversions, DM/thread detection and caching, reply-fallback stripping, E2EE/device identity handling, and platform-specific requirement checks.

GatewayRunner lifecycle and fault semantics: distinguishing non-retryable startup failures (triggering clean exit and user-friendly exit_reason) from retryable runtime failures (queued for reconnection).
Session lifecycle, context/prompt construction, and transcript persistence with resilience to corrupt JSONL lines and preference rules when multiple transcript sources exist.
Command-driven flows and synchronization: blocking /approve and /deny mechanisms with FIFO resolution, registration/unregistration cleanup, and end-to-end blocking/unblocking behavior.
API server endpoint semantics and security: health/models/chat endpoints, response stores, auth, and CORS/security header expectations.

insights.title: Env overrides must correctly set per-platform home_channel
insights.problem: Environment-derived overrides need to be applied into existing PlatformConfig objects and should be reflected in dataclass round-trips.
insights.root_cause: Config YAML keys and legacy aliases vary; environment variables are applied after load and can change nested config fields.
insights.solution: Preserve dataclass serialization semantics and ensure _apply_env_overrides writes into the same PlatformConfig structures so tests asserting round-trip and env-derived home_channel entries continue to pass.

insights.title: Matrix adapter has many E2EE and identity edge cases
insights.problem: Matrix-specific features (end-to-end encryption, device_id, encrypted send retries) have fragile behavior and require targeted mocking in tests.
insights.root_cause: Matrix integrations depend on optional third-party libraries and nuanced client account/device state that must be simulated; missing mocks can break tests.
insights.solution: When changing Matrix adapter code or test harnesses, preserve or update mocks for matrix-nio and sys.modules injection; ensure mxc→http conversion uses configured homeserver consistently and E2EE retry paths remain testable.

insights.title: Transcript and channel directory persistence must be resilient and atomic
insights.problem: Corrupt JSONL lines or failed writes can lead to data loss or inconsistent state if not handled atomically.
insights.root_cause: I/O operations span multiple formats (JSONL and SQLite) and partial writes can occur; JSONL readers must skip bad lines and writers must not clobber existing caches on failure.
insights.solution: Maintain corrupt-line tolerance for JSONL loaders, prefer longer/more complete transcript sources when comparing, and keep atomic write semantics for directory caches so failed writes preserve prior state.

insights.title: GatewayRunner distinguishes non-retryable vs retryable adapter failures
insights.problem: Startup-time non-retryable adapter errors must cause a clean shutdown while runtime retryable errors should schedule reconnection attempts.
insights.root_cause: Different adapter error types represent different operational decisions; the runner's flags and failed_platforms bookkeeping guide shutdown/retry behavior.
insights.solution: Respect and preserve the runner flags (_exit_with_failure, should_exit_cleanly, exit_reason, _failed_platforms) and the semantics tests assert when modifying runner or adapter startup/connect code.

insights.title: Approval blocking flow requires FIFO semantics and cleanup
insights.problem: Blocking approval queues must unblock only via explicit /approve or /deny commands (and support resolve_all), and must be cleaned on session unregister.
insights.root_cause: Blocking operations are queued per-session and rely on registration callbacks and well-defined resolution semantics.
insights.solution: When editing approval or session cleanup code, ensure registration, FIFO resolution order, resolve_all behavior, and unregister cleanup remain intact so tests and blocking flows are predictable.

insights.title: API responses must include security/CORS expectations
insights.problem: Clients and browser integrations expect specific security headers and Access-Control-Max-Age behavior for preflight; breaking these can cause runtime failures or security regressions.
insights.root_cause: API server middleware and endpoint handlers must set headers consistently across endpoints.
insights.solution: Preserve security header injection and CORS max-age semantics tested (including /v1/health alias and CORS Idempotency-Key handling) when modifying API server routing or middleware.

key_files.why: Validates config dataclass round-trips, YAML bridging, and environment-based overrides that other gateway components rely on.
Exercises MatrixAdapter edge cases (E2EE, mxc conversions, thread/DM detection) and documents required mocking for optional matrix-nio dependencies.
Asserts GatewayRunner behavior for fatal adapter errors and the runner flags/exit_reason semantics that control shutdown vs reconnect.
Covers session keying, context/prompt generation, and transcript persistence/resilience — core for session continuity across platforms.
Validates API server endpoints, security headers, and response behavior used by external clients and gateway tooling.

construct_relationships: GatewayRunner:create_adapter/_create_adapter => BasePlatformAdapter implementations: GatewayRunner relies on adapter connect semantics and interprets adapter fatal_error types to set runner flags and _failed_platforms.

Config loading (_apply_env_overrides) => PlatformConfig instances: environment values mutate PlatformConfig.home_channel and extras after YAML load, and code consuming PlatformConfig must read the applied values.

SessionStore/SessionSource => build_session_context_prompt: session serialization/deserialization influences prompt construction (platform notes, channel topic, thread semantics) used by the agent when responding.

MatrixAdapter helpers (mxc->http, reply stripping, DM detection) => homeserver and client mocks: conversion and formatting functions use configured homeserver and client account data; DM/thread caches are refreshed from client account data.

API server endpoints => ResponseStore/Auth/Middleware: chat and health endpoints depend on ResponseStore and must include consistent security/CORS headers; adapters are initialized for API operations and auth affects access to responses.
notable_relationships: Tests rely on optional third-party libraries (e.g., matrix-nio) being mocked; changes that remove or change import points may require test harness updates that inject into sys.modules.
Gateway tests interact with persistence layers (JSONL, SQLite) and expect HERMES_HOME-like data paths and atomic write semantics; changes to storage paths or formats affect multiple tests.
GatewayRunner, adapters, and API server behavior are upstream for external integrations (messaging platforms, web clients); regressions in these tests indicate user-visible runtime failures.
CI configuration blanks external API keys to avoid provider calls; introducing code that attempts real provider access during tests will be blocked unless tests/mocks are updated.
Channel directory and transcript tests imply backward-compatible file formats and safe failure modes; external tooling that reads these files depends on stable shape and atomicity.
recommendations: When editing gateway code, ensure dataclass to_dict/from_dict and YAML->GatewayConfig mappings remain backward-compatible or update tests that assert round-trips and old aliases.
Preserve _apply_env_overrides behavior: environment-injected home_channel and platform extras must be written into PlatformConfig instances before other components consume them.

Maintain GatewayRunner flags and error semantics: non-retryable startup failures should still trigger clean-exit semantics and user-facing exit_reason; retryable runtime failures should queue reconnection attempts.

Respect session persistence expectations: JSONL loaders must continue to skip corrupt lines and prefer the longer/more complete transcript source; atomic write semantics for directory caches must be preserved to avoid data loss on write failures.

If modifying Matrix integration points, keep or update test-side mocks for matrix-nio and device/E2EE flows; ensure mxc->http conversion uses configured homeserver consistently and encrypted send/retry behavior remains testable.
Any changes to API server middleware or endpoints must preserve security headers and CORS max-age behavior validated by tests (including /v1/health alias and Idempotency-Key handling).
Run the gateway unit test suite (tests/gateway) after edits, with external integrations mocked/disabled as CI does (empty API keys), to verify behavioral contracts before wider integration tests.

tests/tools.description: This folder contains unit and regression tests that validate the tools subsystem and its security/operational guards. Tests cover command-safety/approval flows, delegation/subagent behavior, credential file mounting/sanitization, multiple browser backends and their persistence/security, sandboxed code execution, and various small utilities (ANSI stripping, clipboard, config guards). These tests encode behavioral contracts and security constraints that implementations must preserve.
key_behaviors: Validation of dangerous command detection and per-session approval flows (contextvar-bound session keys, pending-approval queue semantics).
Delegation/subagent orchestration: creation/inheritance of child agents, credential/credential-pool sharing and leasing, tool filtering, observability tracing, and depth/limit enforcement.
Credential file handling and mount generation with path containment, symlink sanitization, and HERMES_HOME-based mapping for secure container mounts.
Browser backend workflows: session lifecycle, persistence toggles, snapshot/cleanup, SSRF and secret-exfiltration guards, CDP/WS URL resolution, and platform-specific path discovery.
Sandboxed code execution and related schema/tooling validation ensuring safe execution, output handling, and compatibility of helper utilities.
Small utilities and guards (ANSI stripping, clipboard integration, config null guards) that normalize outputs and provider/backend configuration.

insights.title: Dangerous command pattern coverage is broad and sensitive to false positives
insights.problem: The approval tests assert detection across many shell constructs (multiline, process substitution, find -exec, SQL patterns); gaps lead to missed dangerous commands or false positives that break workflow.
insights.root_cause: Pattern-based detection is brittle: many shell and SQL syntaxes, aliases, and edge cases can bypass or trigger patterns incorrectly unless patterns and key-generation are carefully managed.
insights.solution: Any code changes touching command detection, pattern keys, or session approval logic must preserve the broad pattern matching surface, per-session uniqueness of pattern keys, and multithreaded/contextvar behavior; extend unit tests when introducing new dangerous constructs (e.g., pkill/killall).

insights.title: Credential file mounting enforces path containment and symlink sanitization
insights.problem: Mount registration must reject absolute/traversal host paths and prevent symlink escapes that could expose host secrets.
insights.root_cause: Container mount mapping and skills/cache packaging assume files reside under HERMES_HOME and that symlinks are sanitized or copied to a safe path.
insights.solution: Code touching credential registration, mount generation, or skills/cache discovery must respect containment checks, sanitization/copying semantics, and mapping to container-side paths used in tests.

insights.title: Subagent creation enforces tool stripping, credential inheritance, and observability pairing
insights.problem: Delegate tool tests assert that blocked tools are removed, runtime credentials and callbacks are inherited, tool names persist, and trace pairing between calls/results exists; incorrect inheritance or missing trace metadata breaks observability and auditing.
insights.root_cause: Subagent launch is a combination of copying runtime state, enforcing policies, and emitting rich metadata; subtle ordering or shallow copies can lose critical fields.
insights.solution: Changes to delegate/subagent creation or credential pool handling must preserve deep/shallow inheritance semantics required by tests and maintain the emitted observability schema and tool name propagation.

insights.title: Browser tests validate SSRF, snapshot redaction, persistence modes and cleanup
insights.problem: Browser backends require careful handling of local-vs-cloud SSRF checks, snapshot redaction to avoid secret leaks, discovery/normalization of CDP endpoints, and toggles for ephemeral vs managed persistence.
insights.root_cause: Browser tooling interacts with external endpoints and filesystem state; misnormalization of devtools URLs or careless persistence handling can open SSRF or secret-exfiltration vectors and break session recovery.
insights.solution: When editing browser-related code or URL normalization/persistence logic, ensure SSRF/local checks, redaction mechanisms, and persistence toggles remain aligned with test expectations and that devtools/CDP resolution fallbacks are preserved.

insights.title: Code execution and utility tests assert strict sandbox behavior and normalized outputs
insights.problem: Sandboxed execution must meet schema expectations, not leak host context, and produce consistent outputs (e.g., ANSI-stripped, clipboard image handling) that tests assert.
insights.root_cause: Test suites rely on deterministic sandbox setup and utilities that clean or normalize outputs and file artifacts.
insights.solution: Modifications to the sandbox, utilities (ANSI stripping, clipboard handling), or their interfaces must preserve deterministic behaviors and the contracts asserted by tests.

key_files.why: Central regression suite for dangerous-command detection and session approval semantics; encodes multithreaded contextvar expectations and pattern-key uniqueness guarantees.
Validates delegate/subagent creation, credential/credential-pool inheritance, tool filtering, and observability metadata—critical for subagent/child-run contracts.
Asserts secure credential file registration, mount mapping, symlink sanitization, and containment under HERMES_HOME—defines host↔container file safety requirements.
Representative browser-backend tests covering navigation, snapshots, interactions, and cleanup; exercises persistence and security behaviors specific to the Camofox backend.
Exercises sandboxed code execution, schema validation, and utilities used by execution tooling; important for safety and deterministic behavior.

construct_relationships: Approval/session logic (tests in test_approval.py) constrains command-detection patterns used by the code-execution tool (tested in test_code_execution.py) to decide when to request approvals.

Delegate/subagent tests (test_delegate.py) expect credential_pool behavior and credential inheritance consistent with credential file mounting and registration (assertions in test_credential_files.py).

Browser backend tests (test_browser_camofox*, test_browser_camofox_persistence.py, test_browser_ssrf_local.py) share expectations about URL/CDP resolution and persistence toggles; snapshot redaction behavior is asserted alongside secret-exfiltration tests.

ANSI stripping and output normalization (test_ansi_strip.py) are relied upon by other tooling tests to guarantee deterministic log/output comparisons (e.g., sandbox outputs and browser console outputs).

Checkpoint and sandbox tests (test_checkpoint_manager.py, test_code_execution.py) interact with repository isolation and filesystem-based fixtures that also feed into credential/skills mount behavior tested in test_credential_files.py.
notable_relationships: Tests implicitly depend on HERMES_HOME and contained files; implementations that change HERMES_HOME mapping or container mount paths will affect credential-file and skills-mount tests.

Browser tests interact with external tooling (CDP endpoints, possible Playwright/Node binaries); CI and test harnesses likely mock or stub network/devtools endpoints—real network calls are guarded against in tests.
Delegate tests mock AIAgent to avoid real LLM/API calls; changes that remove or alter mocking hooks will cause external provider/API calls unless tests are updated.

Multithreaded/contextvar expectations in approval tests mean execution behavior across threads must preserve session-context binding; altering context propagation or asynchronous/thread semantics across the codebase can break these tests.
recommendations: When modifying code targeted by these tests, run the tests in this folder (and related integration tests) to validate behavior: tests assume mocks for external APIs and no real provider keys in CI.
Preserve containment and sanitization semantics for credential files and skills/cache mounts; do not assume absolute or symlink-resolved host paths without explicit checks.
Respect per-session context propagation (contextvars) and pending-approval queue semantics—multithreaded scenarios are asserted by tests and can fail silently if context leaks or is lost.

Maintain delegate/subagent contracts: credential/credential-pool inheritance, tool stripping, tool-name preservation, and observability/tracing metadata; these are validated by unit tests that mock AIAgent and inspect emitted traces.
For browser/backends, preserve SSRF/local checks, snapshot redaction, CDP/WS URL resolution fallbacks, and persistence toggle semantics. Tests rely on deterministic discovery and cleanup behavior.
Avoid introducing real network/LLM calls in tests: ensure existing mocks/stubs remain usable or are updated in lockstep with implementation changes.
When changing utilities that normalize outputs (ANSI stripping, clipboard image handling), ensure normalized outputs remain stable because many tests compare strings or paths deterministically.

tests/agent.description: This folder contains the unit and small-scale integration tests that validate the agent subsystem (prompt building, model/provider resolution, memory handling, skill discovery, routing, and related utilities). The tests codify expected behaviors around safety, truncation, caching, provider resolution, memory scoping and tooling interfaces — they serve as behavioral contracts the code must keep to remain interoperable with gateways, tooling, and CI constraints.
key_behaviors: Prompt construction and sanitation flows are validated end-to-end: injection scanning, truncation strategy (head/tail preservation), skills prompt assembly, and provider-specific subscription prompts.
Memory lifecycle and scoping flows are exercised: provider interface conformance, per-user memory scoping, prefetch/fencing semantics, and persistence interaction.
Model/provider resolution and usage metadata: resolve forced/auto providers, auxiliary client selection, usage/pricing estimation and context-length probing.
Caching and performance controls: prompt caching markers, Anthropic cache control behavior, and prompt cache invalidation across environments.
Skill discovery and invocation: parsing SKILL.md frontmatter, sanitizing generated command keys, building preloaded skills prompts and platform-filtered scans.
Compressor/summary behavior and rate limiting: context compression summary cooldowns and omission of model temperature in summaries to avoid accidental configuration leakage.

insights.title: Prompt injection and invisible characters must be sanitized
insights.problem: Prompts containing common injection patterns or invisible/unicode trickery can evade naive string checks and lead to unsafe model input.
insights.root_cause: PromptBuilder and consumers accept raw context files and skill text; tests demonstrate multiple injection patterns and invisible characters that need explicit detection.
insights.solution: Treat prompt scanning as a required pre-step when composing prompt context; preserve head/tail truncation semantics while ensuring scanning runs on both truncated and full contexts to avoid escaped payloads.

insights.title: Prefetched memory must be fenced to avoid being interpreted as user input
insights.problem: Model could treat prefetch/prefilled memory snapshots as part of the conversation if not clearly demarcated, leading to hallucinations or policy violations.
insights.root_cause: MemoryManager returns prefetched entries into the prompt context; previously tests exposed cases where fencing markers were absent or insufficient.
insights.solution: Memory integrations must insert explicit fenced separators or metadata wrappers around prefetched content; maintain tests that assert fencing remains intact across providers and that prefetching does not alter conversational semantics.

insights.title: SKILL.md frontmatter parsing must degrade gracefully
insights.problem: Skill files may be malformed, missing, or include unexpected frontmatter; code must not crash when scanning skill directories.
insights.root_cause: Skill discovery scanning touches working dir and HERMES_HOME content; production skill files can vary in frontmatter style and presence.
insights.solution: Skill parsing routines should have deterministic fallback behavior and be compatible with tests that simulate missing/corrupt frontmatter; consumers expect a sanitized command key and robust error handling.

insights.title: Cache markers for Anthropic style responses must be injected/recognized consistently
insights.problem: Cached prompt responses need explicit markers to avoid serving stale or inappropriate content; Anthropic-specific caching control behaviors can alter prompt composition.
insights.root_cause: PromptCache injects provider-specific cache markers; tests assert both application and removal behaviors.
insights.solution: Maintain provider-specific cache marker insertion/removal behavior and ensure tests that simulate cache-control remain reliable across provider mock setups.

insights.title: Compressor summaries should not leak model temperature and should respect cooldowns
insights.problem: Summary-generation calls could accidentally include temperature or be performed too frequently, causing nondeterminism or extra LLM cost.
insights.root_cause: ContextCompressor/test indicates temperature was being set or summary logic lacked cooldown/backoff.
insights.solution: Summary generation must omit mutable generation parameters like temperature and enforce a failure cooldown to prevent repeated LLM invocations; tests verify both behaviors.

insights.title: Generated skill-derived command keys must be sanitized
insights.problem: Skill scanning previously allowed invalid or unsafe characters into generated command keys, breaking downstream routing or CLI parsing.
insights.root_cause: Skill name/title extraction logic did not normalize/sanitize characters used to build command keys.
insights.solution: Enforce deterministic sanitization when building command keys and keep tests that verify invalid chars are removed or replaced to avoid regressions.

insights.title: Per-user memory scoping is required for multi-user gateway flows
insights.problem: MemoryManager behavior needs to route reads/writes through a user_id so memories don't leak across users or sessions.
insights.root_cause: Gateway adapters supply user_id; memory provider interfaces and MemoryManager must propagate and honor it consistently.
insights.solution: Memory provider APIs and MemoryManager must accept and pass user_id to providers and plugins; tests assert scoping and should continue to be used as guardrails for changes.

key_files.why: Comprehensive validations for prompt scanning, truncation, skill prompt assembly and environment-driven prompt composition — changes to prompt code must preserve these behaviors.
Exposes MemoryProvider/MemoryManager contracts including fencing and prefetch semantics; critical for any edits touching memory persistence or prompt composition.
Asserts provider-specific cache marker injection and cache-control behavior that affect model call reduction and correctness.
Validates skill discovery, platform filtering, and sanitization of generated command keys used by CLI and gateway routing.
Checks summary cooldowns and omission of generation temperature for compression summaries; informs safe use of LLM calls in background tasks.

construct_relationships: PromptBuilder composes system + context + skills prompts and relies on context scanning/truncation and prompt caching behaviors validated in prompt_builder and prompt_caching tests.
MemoryManager coordinates with MemoryProvider implementations; tests ensure MemoryManager propagates user_id and fences prefetched memories so PromptBuilder receives prefetches wrapped correctly.
PromptCache and provider adapters (Anthropic behaviors) interact: cache marker injection/removal is applied before model invocation and must be recognized by adapter mocks in tests.
Skill scanning/parsing produces command keys used by CLI/gateway routing and by prompt assembly; tests verify sanitized keys are used by build_preloaded_skills_prompt and resolve_skill_command_key.

ContextCompressor/summary generation is invoked as a pre-processing step for large contexts; tests ensure compressor omits mutable generation parameters (temperature) and respects cooldowns to avoid extra LLM calls.
notable_relationships: Tests manipulate HERMES_HOME and working directory contents (tmp_path) — code that reads files or environment-specified paths must behave deterministically when HERMES_HOME is changed.

Many tests monkeypatch environment variables and imports to avoid external network/provider calls; CI policy blanking of API keys is mirrored in tests and must be respected when running locally or in CI.
Provider resolution and auxiliary client selection tests simulate multiple provider behaviors — model/provider adapter APIs and credential rotation code are upstream dependencies of these tests.
Skill discovery tests depend on platform filtering semantics (gateway/platforms) indirectly — changes to platform-specific behavior may require test alignment.
Prompt caching and model metadata tests depend on token estimation and context-length probing utilities which influence routing and cost estimation downstream (smart_model_routing, usage_pricing).
recommendations: When editing any code referenced by these tests, ensure you run the tests in tests/agent (or full test suite) because they encode contract-level expectations for prompt composition, memory scoping, caching, and skill parsing.
Do not introduce external network calls or require live provider keys in tests — tests rely on monkeypatching and CI blanked envs; preserve ability to inject mocks for providers and clients.

Preserve API signatures and behaviours that tests assert: MemoryManager/user_id propagation, fence markers around prefetched memory, prompt cache marker insertion/removal, deterministic skill key sanitization, and compressor cooldown/parameter omission.

Respect filesystem/environment interactions: HERMES_HOME, working directory scanning, and tmp_path usage in tests — changes to path handling must remain testable under overridden env vars and temporary directories.

If modifying provider-specific behaviors (Anthropic/OpenAI/etc.), ensure parallel test coverage or update tests in this folder to reflect new semantics; maintain provider-specific cache control hooks recognized by prompt cache tests.

tools/environments.description:
{
  "description": "This folder implements pluggable execution environments used by Hermes tools to run commands and sandboxes locally or in various remote/cloud runtimes. It defines a common BaseEnvironment API and multiple concrete backends (local, docker, singularity, ssh, Modal, Daytona, managed Modal) plus shared mixins/helpers for persistence, polling, stdin wrapping, file syncing, and sanitization. These backends are responsible for safe command execution, resource and credential isolation, interrupt/timeout semantics, and lifecycle/cleanup of ephemeral resources, so changes here directly affect task execution security and reliability.",
  "key_behaviors": [
    "Standardized execute/oneshot/cleanup lifecycle: callers use BaseEnvironment API to start commands, handle timeouts/interrupts, and clean up resources.",
    "Credential and skill file syncing: backends sync Hermes-managed skills/credentials into sandboxes or mount them read-only for execution.",
    "Persistent vs onesh

tests/acp.description: This folder contains unit and end-to-end tests that validate the Hermes ACP (agent-client-protocol) adapter: startup wiring, provider detection, server session lifecycle, event callback factories, permission bridges, tool reporting and MCP registration. These tests codify the expected public API shapes, callback signatures, and runtime behaviors of the ACP surface so that changes in the adapter, server, or tool serialization are detectable by CI/local test runs.
key_behaviors: Startup and bootstrapping: entrypoint wiring must enable the required protocol flags and initialize the ACP server.
Authentication and provider detection: utilities detect provider availability and the server authenticates/authorizes incoming requests accordingly.
Session lifecycle management: sessions are created, listed, forked, configured, and used to route prompts and slash commands through the server.
Event emission and callbacks: factories produce callbacks that create ACP-compliant events (message/step/thinking/tool progress) consumed by clients and server code.
Tool & MCP reporting: tools are classified, serialized, and reported to MCP; MCP registration and sanitization of tool metadata are validated end-to-end.
Permission flow bridging: permission request/approval callbacks return well-formed responses (allowed/denied) and are integrated with the server flow.

insights.title: Stable shapes of ACP events and permission responses are relied upon across tests
insights.problem: Tests assert specific structures and return types for event payloads and permission responses; changing shapes silently breaks multiple tests.
insights.root_cause: Centralized expectations in test suites that mirror public protocol payloads and server API surface.
insights.solution: Preserve event and permission response signatures and fields; if protocol changes are unavoidable, update corresponding tests and any code that serializes/deserializes these payloads in lockstep.

insights.title: Entrypoint must toggle protocol/runtime flags
insights.problem: Tests validate that the entry startup sets an unstable protocol flag and initializes the server correctly; incorrect startup wiring will fail those assertions.
insights.root_cause: Entrypoint controls runtime configuration that downstream server behavior and tests assume.
insights.solution: When editing startup logic, ensure any flags or initialization side effects expected by tests remain present or that tests are updated to reflect new startup semantics.

insights.title: Event callback factories are deterministic and form the canonical event stream
insights.problem: Event factories produce ACP events consumed by server and MCP registration tests; differing event content/order will break consumption logic.
insights.root_cause: Event factories centralize how in-process actions are represented as protocol events.
insights.solution: Maintain callback signatures, produced event fields, and ordering guarantees; any change requires adjusting all consumers and tests that validate the produced events.

insights.title: Tool kind mapping and call ID generation are contract points for MCP and server reporting
insights.problem: Tests exercise TOOL_KIND_MAP, tool title/start/complete builders, and call ID extraction; instability here affects MCP registration and client tool ingestion.
insights.root_cause: Tool metadata is the bridge between internal tool implementations and external MCP/clients, so its exact shape is important.
insights.solution: Keep TOOL_KIND_MAP keys and tool content builders stable; if renaming or reshaping fields, update MCP registration, server reporting code, and tests concurrently.

insights.title: Session lifecycle and authentication flows are end-to-end validated
insights.problem: E2E tests validate registration, sanitization, and session lifecycle; breaking session operations or auth detection will cause cascading failures.
insights.root_cause: Session operations, authentication detection utilities, and server endpoints are tightly coupled in tests that exercise full flows.
insights.solution: Ensure changes to session APIs, authentication helpers, or server endpoints preserve expected behaviors (create/list/fork/configure/prompt); rerun E2E tests when making any such modifications.

key_files.why: Comprehensive coverage of ACP server behaviors and session lifecycle; edits affecting server API or session semantics must satisfy assertions here.
Validates startup wiring and that the entrypoint toggles the unstable protocol flag — a gating concern for server initialization tests.
Defines expectations for event callback factories that produce ACP event payloads consumed across the protocol surface.
Exercises tool metadata builders, kind mapping and call-id logic used by MCP registration and tool reporting flows.
End-to-end tests for MCP registration and tool reporting; sensitive to cross-component integration and sanitization logic.

construct_relationships: Entry startup (tested in test_entry.py) initializes server runtime flags that the ACP server tests (test_server.py) and MCP e2e tests (test_mcp_e2e.py) rely on.

Event factory functions (make_message_cb, make_step_cb, make_thinking_cb, make_tool_progress_cb) produce events consumed by server session handlers and MCP registration flows (test_events.py ⇄ test_server.py ⇄ test_mcp_e2e.py).
Tool builders and TOOL_KIND_MAP (test_tools.py) feed into MCP registration and sanitization checks (test_mcp_e2e.py) and are validated by server-side tool reporting tests.

Permission callback factories and response types (test_permissions.py) are invoked by server endpoints during flows that require approvals; tests assert AllowedOutcome/DeniedOutcome shapes expected by the server.
Provider detection utilities (has_provider, detect_provider in test_auth.py) are used to gate behavior in startup and authentication tests, linking auth utilities with server authentication tests.
notable_relationships: These tests assert protocol-level contracts between acp_adapter components and the rest of the agent stack; changes in agent prompt or tool interfaces elsewhere (tools/, agent/) can ripple into these tests.

MCP registration and sanitization E2E tests imply network/server lifecycle concerns — edits that change how servers are started, port bindings, or sanitization routines may require updating E2E fixtures or test expectations.

Authentication and provider-detection tests are sensitive to external configuration (environment variables, credential pools); CI blanks keys to avoid external calls, so local edits may require setting test fixtures or env vars.
Permission bridge tests indicate integrations with higher-level permission models; upstream permission model changes will impact the expected Allowed/Denied outcomes here.
recommendations: When modifying any ACP adapter, server, events, permissions, or tool-serialization code, run the tests in tests/acp (unit + E2E where relevant) to detect contract regressions.
Preserve public payload shapes (event objects, permission responses, TOOL_KIND_MAP keys, tool call ID formats) or update all dependent tests and consumers together.
Be mindful of startup side effects (flags set by entrypoint) — changes to entry initialization may require adjusting how the server is started in tests or CI fixtures.
If editing code that affects session operations, authentication, or MCP registration, include the MCP E2E tests in validation runs because they exercise cross-component integration and sanitization.

Account for CI environment constraints: tests assume no real external provider keys are present; locally set equivalent test credentials or mocks if changes cause tests to attempt real provider calls.

tests/integration.description: This folder contains end-to-end integration tests that exercise real subsystems and external integrations (batch runner, terminal backends, gateway/tool integrations, web tools, and voice/crypto flows). These tests validate runtime behaviors, stateful persistence (checkpoints/files), networked protocols (WebSocket/REST/voice), and platform-specific features, and therefore are sensitive to environment, credentials, and optional heavy dependencies.
key_behaviors: Orchestration and verification of multi-step batch runs including dataset creation, run execution, output verification, and cleanup.
Checkpointing and resume semantics: create monitoring points during long-running runs, simulate interruptions/crashes, and verify resumed state and outputs.
Terminal backend validation (Daytona and Modal): execute commands, validate stdout/stderr/exit codes, test filesystem persistence and environment isolation, and perform runtime installs.
Gateway/tool integration flows: exercise WebSocket/REST interactions with Home Assistant and notify/send workflows, including auth/error handling.

Networked web tooling and voice flows: run web search/extract/crawl flows against external network resources and exercise voice channel flows using NaCl crypto and Discord-style voice/session behaviors.

insights.title: Tests rely on real external services and credentials
insights.problem: Several integration tests interact with remote services (web endpoints, Home Assistant, Discord voice) or crypto libraries that require real keys/certs, making runs environment-sensitive and likely to fail in CI unless keys are provided or tests are explicitly mocked/skipped.
insights.root_cause: Tests are written to exercise end-to-end production flows rather than purely mocked unit behavior and assume access to networked services and credentials.
insights.solution: Treat these tests as environment-gated: they should be run only when the required credentials and services are available. When editing code that affects these integrations, ensure test guards/skips, mocking hooks, or documentation of required env vars are preserved so CI and local developers can opt in/out predictably.

insights.title: Tests create and verify on-disk artifacts and checkpoints
insights.problem: Batch and checkpoint tests create datasets, checkpoint files, and expect consistent filesystem state across run/resume cycles; improper cleanup or incompatible format changes break re-runs and other tests.
insights.root_cause: Tests assume stable on-disk formats and deterministic cleanup routines that are coupled to implementation details (checkpoint layouts, artifact paths).
insights.solution: When modifying code that affects persistence formats, paths, or cleanup semantics, maintain backward-compatible formats or update tests and fixtures together; always respect the tests' cleanup and verification expectations.

insights.title: Timing, ordering, and external network cause flakiness
insights.problem: Integration tests simulate interruptions, run shell commands with nonzero exits, and interact with network resources, introducing timing sensitivity and transient failures.
insights.root_cause: Reliance on live services, shared ports/resources, and OS-level behavior (process exit codes, network latency) creates non-deterministic outcomes.
insights.solution: Edits that change asynchronous behavior, timeouts, retry logic, or resource contention must preserve test-time assumptions; tests may need to be run serially or with explicit synchronization, and CI should keep these tests separated from unit tests.

insights.title: Terminal and voice tests depend on OS, binaries and optional heavy deps
insights.problem: Daytona/Modal terminal and voice-channel tests expect specific runtime tools (shell, pip, NaCl libs) and may fail on unsupported platforms or when optional extras are not installed.
insights.root_cause: Integration tests execute real commands and use native libraries not covered by minimal installs.
insights.solution: Respect platform boundaries and optional dependency gating: preserve test markers/requirements and document required dev extras so local runs reproduce CI intent; changes that alter platform interactions or package requirements must account for these test dependencies.

insights.title: Gateway and Home Assistant tests exercise protocol contracts
insights.problem: Home Assistant gateway and tool tests depend on WebSocket/REST message formats, auth patterns, and error handling behavior; changing those contracts will invalidate tests.
insights.root_cause: Tests validate black-box integrations against expected external protocol messages and server behavior rather than internal mocks.
insights.solution: When modifying gateway/tool interfaces or message shapes, update tests or maintain backwards-compatible behavior; preserve any explicit auth/error response semantics that tests assert.

insights.title: Voice/crypto tests use real NaCl flows that verify cryptographic behavior
insights.problem: Tests perform crypto operations and validate voice reconnection, auth filtering and speaking hooks—broken crypto or changed key handling will cause failures and may hide security regressions.
insights.root_cause: These tests exercise low-level security and real-time session behavior that is sensitive to key formats and timing.
insights.solution: Any edits touching cryptography, key handling, or voice session lifecycle must preserve public interfaces and serialization formats used by tests, or tests must be adapted in lockstep.

key_files.why: Validates end-to-end batch run lifecycle (dataset creation, execution, verification, cleanup) and underpins checkpoint/resume semantics used by other tests.
Exercises interruption/resume and simulated-crash scenarios; tightly coupled to checkpoint file formats and resume logic.
Exercises the Home Assistant gateway and tool REST/WebSocket interactions, including auth and error handling; represents gateway-to-external-service contract tests.
Covers real NaCl crypto flows and voice channel lifecycle (auth, reconnect, speaking hooks) and therefore is sensitive to crypto and realtime session code.
Validates web search/extract/crawl flows and integration with external web resources and LLM-extraction hooks; represents networked tool behavior.

construct_relationships: Batch runner tests create test datasets and then invoke the batch-run orchestration code to produce outputs that are verified by verify_output routines.

Checkpoint resumption tests monitor checkpoint files produced during a run, trigger interruptions or simulated crashes, then resume the same runner to assert that persisted state yields identical or expected final outputs.

Terminal backend tests (Daytona/Modal) execute commands via terminal abstractions, then inspect stdout/stderr, exit codes, and filesystem artifacts to validate environment isolation and persistence semantics.

Home Assistant integration tests instantiate WebSocket and REST client scenarios (TestGatewayWebSocket, TestToolRest) and assert authentication flows, error handling, and notification propagation across gateway/tool code paths.
Web tools and voice-channel tests exercise networked modules (web search/extract/crawl, voice crypto/session layers), relying on the same network and protocol interfaces used by production adapters.
notable_relationships: These tests exercise code in gateway/platforms, tools/*, and batch-runner modules; edits in those folders will directly impact integration tests in this directory.
Persistent artifacts and checkpoints produced by tests map to the same persistence conventions (HERMES_HOME/data etc.) used by runtime code; changes to storage layout affect test expectations.

CI practice blanks API keys and separates integration/e2e tests from the default unit suite; locally, running these tests typically requires setting environment variables or running supporting services.

Optional and heavy dependencies (native crypto libs, voice libs, terminal backends, Modal/Daytona runtimes) are required by some tests; removing or changing optional extras can make these tests fail or be un-runnable without alternate mocks.
recommendations: Do not change public test fixture names, checkpoint file formats, on-disk artifact paths, or protocol message shapes without updating these integration tests in the same change set.
Preserve test cleanup and teardown behaviors; ensure changes do not leave behind artifacts or ports that will cause subsequent tests to fail.

When modifying gateway, tool, or persistence code, validate integration tests with the required environment (credentials, services) enabled locally; CI will likely continue to run unit tests without external keys.
Expect non-determinism: run integration tests serially and with realistic timeouts; verify any changes to retries/timeouts do not mask transient failures relied upon by tests.
Respect platform and optional dependency gating: maintain test markers/skips and document required extras so developers can opt into running these heavier tests.

tests/cron.description: This folder contains the unit tests that validate the cron/scheduler subsystem: scheduling/parsing/storage of jobs, the runtime behavior of run_job (session lifecycle, environment markers, delivery wrapping), script-injection within job prompts, and inactivity/timeout diagnostics. These tests encode expected behaviors and edge-cases that any changes to the cron scheduler, delivery wrapping, media handling, session management, or job persistence must preserve.
key_behaviors: Parsing and scheduling: parsing human-friendly durations/schedules and computing next run times for jobs
Job lifecycle: creating, saving, loading, updating, listing, pausing jobs and their persistence semantics
Run-time execution: run_job sets up a session (SessionDB injection), sets/cleans environment markers (HERMES_CRON_AUTO_DELIVER), invokes the agent, and tears down state

Delivery resolution and wrapping: resolve origin and delivery targets (IDs, topic/thread parsing, human-friendly names via gateway.channel_directory), optionally wrap messages with headers/footers, and support disabling wrapping via config
Media/attachments handling: extract MEDIA: tags into attachments rather than sending raw media tokens, matching gateway extraction behavior
Script injection and validation: inject job scripts into prompts with containment/validation rules and include script execution context in built prompts
Inactivity and timeout diagnostics: detect slow/unresponsive agents, produce diagnostics, and handle timeouts deterministically in tests

insights.title: Fallbacks and human-friendly delivery targets
insights.problem: Delivery target parsing requires multiple fallback paths: explicit IDs, platform-specific topic/thread parsing, and user-facing name lookups via gateway.channel_directory.
insights.root_cause: Delivery targets are tolerant and user-facing (accepting various syntaxes and names), so logic must implement ordered resolution and fallbacks.
insights.solution: Any change to delivery parsing/lookup must preserve the resolution order and ensure gateway.channel_directory remains consulted as a fallback; tests depend on that exact behavior.

insights.title: Attachments must be separated from message text
insights.problem: Cron delivery must strip MEDIA: tags from body text and pass attachments separately instead of sending media tokens inline.
insights.root_cause: Gateway and cron must share the same extraction semantics; otherwise recipients may receive raw MEDIA: lines or attachments duplicated/missing.
insights.solution: Maintain or mirror the gateway media extraction logic when altering cron delivery/wrapping so tests continue to see attachments separated and MEDIA: tags removed from text.

insights.title: Global [SILENT] marker handling
insights.problem: A [SILENT] marker anywhere in a response should suppress delivery entirely, but tests currently note a missing assertion for trailing markers.
insights.root_cause: Marker handling is position-agnostic but subtle; if detection logic changes (e.g., only prefix/suffix checks), deliveries might leak.
insights.solution: Ensure [SILENT] detection is applied against full response content (not only head/tail) and that delivery is suppressed when present anywhere.

insights.title: Run_job must set and clean runtime state
insights.problem: run_job injects SessionDB and sets environment variables (HERMES_CRON_AUTO_DELIVER) and tests assert both setup and teardown; leaks would flake tests.
insights.root_cause: Persistent process state or env variables left after run_job would affect subsequent tests or runtime behavior.
insights.solution: Preserve deterministic setup/teardown: SessionDB injection and environment variable modification must be reversible even on error paths; tests assume cleanup occurs.

insights.title: Malformed config must be logged predictably
insights.problem: Tests check that malformed job configs produce specific logging output; changing log messages or validation behavior can break tests.
insights.root_cause: Tests assert on presence/format of logged diagnostics; subtle message or level changes cause failures.
insights.solution: If adjusting config parsing or validation, keep logging semantics (contents and levels) stable or update tests accordingly.

insights.title: Script path containment & prompt injection rules
insights.problem: Job scripts are injected into prompts, but there are containment and validation rules to prevent unsafe script paths and ensure proper prompt building.
insights.root_cause: Script injection touches filesystem and prompt construction; insecure or incorrect path handling could allow directory traversal or malformed prompts.
insights.solution: Maintain script path containment checks and validation logic; tests expect these constraints and specific prompt composition when scripts are present.

key_files.why: Comprehensive test suite for cron scheduler runtime: origin/delivery resolution, wrapping, media extraction, and run_job lifecycle; encodes many behavioral invariants the runtime must keep.
Unit tests for job parsing, scheduling, and persistence APIs (create/load/save/get/list/update/pause); changes to job storage or schedule computation must satisfy these tests.
Tests script-injection, prompt-building with scripts, path containment and validation rules; modifications affecting script handling must remain compatible.
Verifies inactivity timeout and diagnostic behavior using fake agents and time-sensitive scenarios; important for changes to timeout or diagnostic code paths.

construct_relationships: run_job (tested in test_scheduler.py) injects SessionDB and toggles HERMES_CRON_AUTO_DELIVER env var; tests assert both setup and teardown.
Delivery wrapping logic (scheduler) calls or reuses gateway media extraction semantics so MEDIA: tags are removed from message text and attachments are delivered alongside wrapped content.
Delivery target resolution uses an ordered strategy: explicit ID parsing -> platform-specific topic/thread parsing -> gateway.channel_directory human-friendly name lookup.
