Job persistence helpers (create_job, save_jobs, load_jobs, get_job, list_jobs, update_job, pause_job) are interdependent: parsing/compute_next_run is used by scheduling and listing behaviours.
Script injection into job prompts interacts with prompt-building logic: scripts must be validated (path containment) and their content incorporated into the built prompt used by run_job.
notable_relationships: Tests rely on gateway-related utilities (gateway.channel_directory and media extraction) — changes in gateway/platform adapters can affect cron tests.
SessionDB is an external persistence/injection mechanism used by run_job; modifications to SessionDB API or lifecycle handling elsewhere will impact cron tests.
Environment variable HERMES_CRON_AUTO_DELIVER is used as a runtime marker by run_job; other subsystems that inspect environment variables may be implicitly coupled.
Script path containment touches filesystem semantics; repository layout, HERMES_HOME, or path resolution utilities elsewhere can affect the tests' expectations.
Timeout and diagnostic behavior assumes deterministic timing; changes to concurrent scheduling infrastructure or event loops (gateway/agent) can change test timing characteristics.
recommendations: When editing code that affects delivery parsing/lookup, preserve resolution ordering and keep gateway.channel_directory fallback behavior intact (or update tests that assert it).
Any change to message wrapping or media handling must maintain MEDIA: extraction semantics and the separation of attachments from body text to satisfy wrapping tests.
Ensure run_job always performs deterministic setup and teardown: SessionDB injection must be reversible and HERMES_CRON_AUTO_DELIVER must be unset on exit paths (including exceptions).
If modifying config validation or logging text/levels, update or re-run these tests since they assert on logged diagnostics and malformed-config behavior.
Changes to script-injection or script path handling must preserve containment/validation rules and prompt composition expected by the tests.
Run the tests in this folder (pytest tests/cron) after edits; many tests are timing-sensitive (inactivity/timeout), so maintain deterministic sleeps/mocks and avoid introducing flakiness.

tests/e2e.description: This folder contains end-to-end tests that validate the Telegram gateway adapter and the full async message pipeline (adapter → gateway runner → command handlers → adapter.send) without requiring real external Telegram services. These tests rely on fixtures and a lightweight telegram module mock to exercise slash-command behavior, session lifecycle, authorization/pairing flows, and resilience to send failures, making them a high-confidence guard against regressions in messaging integration.
key_behaviors: Bootstrapping a gateway runner and a Telegram adapter instance via shared fixtures (make_runner, make_adapter) that assemble the async pipeline used by the gateway.
Installing a local telegram module mock at import-time when the real library is not available to permit importing and instantiating the TelegramAdapter in test environments.
Dispatching inbound updates/commands through the adapter into the runner and command handlers, then capturing outbound adapter.send calls to assert responses and pipeline side-effects.
Exercising session lifecycle and slash-command semantics (/help, /status, /new, /commands), including authorization/pairing prompts for unauthorized users.
Simulating adapter send failures and asserting the runner/pipeline handles exceptions without crashing or leaking background tasks.

insights.title: telegram module import fallback
insights.problem: The tests install a mock telegram module when the real library is absent; changes to the adapter import-time expectations can break test imports silently.
insights.root_cause: The TelegramAdapter (and tests) import symbols from the third-party telegram library at module import time; CI/test environments may not have that package installed.
insights.solution: Any edits that change import names, required classes, or attributes from the telegram package must be mirrored in the test mock or the import surface; keep mock shape aligned with adapter import-time usage to avoid import errors during test collection.

insights.title: Runner/Adapter fixture signatures are relied upon by tests
insights.problem: Tests assume specific behaviors, lifecycles, and return shapes from make_runner, make_adapter, and send_and_capture fixtures/factories.
insights.root_cause: Tests use these fixtures to construct the pipeline and to synchronously capture outbound sends after background processing; any change to fixture contracts breaks many tests.
insights.solution: Preserve fixture API, lifetime semantics and the way send captures are returned; if modifying the runner/adapter lifecycle or APIs, update the fixtures and all tests that depend on them in lockstep.

insights.title: Deterministic capture of adapter.send in async background dispatch
insights.problem: Capturing adapter.send calls after the background dispatcher runs assumes deterministic scheduling and may become flaky if task timing or await behavior changes.
insights.root_cause: send_and_capture depends on awaiting background work to complete and on adapter.send being called in a predictable order.
insights.solution: When changing async control flow, ensure adapter.send call sites and background dispatcher await points remain compatible with the capture helper, or adapt the capture helper to robustly await and collect sends to avoid race conditions.

insights.title: Tests encode specific slash-command and session reset semantics
insights.problem: The tests assert exact outputs and lifecycle effects for commands such as /new, /status and for unauthorized pairing prompts; changing session management or command output will break assertions.
insights.root_cause: Tests serve as behavioral contracts for session state initialization, status reporting, pairing prompts and authorization responses.
insights.solution: Respect current session lifecycle semantics and command output shapes when changing session, authorization, or command handler logic; if those behaviors change, update tests accordingly and ensure pairing/authorization flows continue to be representable by the fixtures.

insights.title: Resilience to adapter send failures is asserted
insights.problem: Tests expect that send failures from the adapter are handled gracefully by the pipeline and do not crash background tasks.
insights.root_cause: The gateway pipeline must catch and isolate platform-level send exceptions to preserve overall runner stability.
insights.solution: Any modification to adapter.send error semantics, exception types, or exception propagation must preserve the contract that failures do not terminate the pipeline; update tests/mocks to reflect new error modes if behavior intentionally changes.

key_files.why: Provides the shared fixtures, factories, and the telegram module mock used by all e2e tests; changing it affects test discovery, import-time mocking, and how the adapter/runner are constructed and observed.
Houses the end-to-end assertions for slash commands, session lifecycle, authorization pairing, and resilience to send failures — it defines the behavioral contracts the gateway adapter must meet.
Package initializer for the e2e tests; relevant if tests are reorganized or import paths are changed.

construct_relationships: conftest.make_adapter constructs a Telegram adapter instance; tests call adapter.dispatch/update builders to inject inbound messages.
conftest.make_runner creates a background gateway runner that registers the adapter and routes inbound adapter dispatches to command handlers; tests rely on this to exercise the full pipeline.
send_and_capture in conftest intercepts and collects adapter.send calls emitted by the pipeline after background dispatch; tests assert against that captured output.

conftest._ensure_telegram_mock installs a fake telegram module at import-time so TelegramAdapter can be imported in environments without the real telegram library; test modules depend on this to import adapter-related symbols during collection.

test_telegram_commands invokes slash commands which traverse adapter → runner → command handler → adapter.send, asserting session state changes and authorization prompts emitted by the command handlers.
notable_relationships: These e2e tests are tightly coupled to gateway/platforms/telegram.py, gateway/run.py, and the command handler/session/authorization subsystems — changes in those modules will likely require test updates in this directory.

The folder intentionally avoids real external dependencies — CI/e2e job separation and test setup likely expect these tests to be run in a controlled environment with mocks rather than real networked Telegram credentials.

Test behavior is sensitive to asynchronous scheduling and exception propagation in the gateway runner; upstream changes to async patterns or error propagation in the runner or adapter will surface here.
Because the tests rely on import-time mocking of the telegram module, renaming or moving adapter classes or changing import paths can break test collection even before any test runs.
recommendations: Preserve fixture names, signatures and lifecycles in conftest.py; if changing them, update dependent tests and any helper send-capture logic in the same commit.

If you change any imports, class names, or attributes referenced from the telegram third-party library (used by the adapter), update the telegram mock in conftest.py so tests continue to import cleanly.

When changing adapter.send semantics, error types, or the background dispatch/waiting behavior, run these e2e tests (they exercise end-to-end async flows) to validate send capture and pipeline resilience; be mindful of potential flakiness due to async timing.

Respect the asserted command output shapes and session lifecycle behaviors in tests; if those behaviors must change, adjust the tests to reflect the new contract and ensure pairing/authorization flows remain testable with the existing fixtures.

Ensure test isolation and cleanup for background tasks started by the runner so edits do not introduce leaked tasks or flakiness; run the e2e job or local equivalents rather than only unit tests when modifying gateway/adapter code.

tools/browser_providers.description: This folder contains cloud browser provider implementations and their abstract interface used by the browser tool subsystem. It standardizes how different third-party cloud browser services create, return connection metadata (CDP/connect URLs, feature flags), and tear down sessions, including emergency cleanup and provider-specific error/fallback handling. These providers are critical because they encapsulate service-specific behavior (managed vs direct modes, idempotency, billing fallbacks) that other tools consume to open/close remote browser sessions.
key_behaviors: Abstract session lifecycle management: create_session -> use session (returns CDP/connect URL and metadata) -> close_session -> emergency_cleanup.
Provider-specific negotiation: feature flag toggles, optional proxy/stealth settings, keep-alive options and fallback behaviors when features are unavailable or billed.
Managed-gateway idempotency and short-lived session semantics for providers that support a managed mode (idempotency tokens, preserved pending create keys, managed timeouts).
HTTP API orchestration and error handling: building requests, parsing provider responses, interpreting HTTP status codes (e.g., 402 for feature/billing fallbacks), and mapping to session behavior.
Central interface export and conformance enforcement: a single CloudBrowserProvider ABC defines required methods and is re-exported for implementers to import against.

insights.title: Idempotency and short-managed session semantics for managed gateways
insights.problem: Managed gateway flows require idempotency keys to avoid duplicate session creation and need short lifecycle/timeouts that differ from direct API usage.
insights.root_cause: Switches from one managed backend (e.g., Browserbase/Nous) to another (Browser Use) introduced different API expectations (headers, idempotency behavior, ephemeral timeouts).
insights.solution: Ensure provider implementations maintain idempotency-key creation/preservation and explicit managed-timeout handling; consumers rely on these semantics for safe retries and cleanup.

insights.title: Provider feature toggles and 402 fallbacks
insights.problem: Some providers return HTTP 402 (or similar) when requested features require payment, leading to failed session creates unless fallback behavior is implemented.
insights.root_cause: Client requested optional features (proxies, advanced stealth, keep-alive) that are not available on the current account / plan.
insights.solution: Providers should detect billing/feature errors and implement deterministic fallbacks (disable optional features) or surface clear error metadata; callers expect a predictable session or a clear failure reason.

insights.title: Single ABC and package-level re-export
insights.problem: Multiple implementations must adhere to the same interface for the rest of the system to consume them interchangeably.
insights.root_cause: Providers are used polymorphically by browser tools and gateway code; any divergence in method signatures or returned metadata breaks callers.
insights.solution: Maintain CloudBrowserProvider ABC method signatures (create_session, close_session, emergency_cleanup, provider_name, is_configured) and stable session metadata fields (CDP URL, feature flags, timeouts).

insights.title: Short-lived sessions and emergency cleanup importance
insights.problem: Cloud sessions are ephemeral and may need forced release or emergency cleanup when normal close fails (network errors, provider-side truncation).
insights.root_cause: Cloud browsers often impose short lifetimes and provider APIs can return partial states or fail to release resources on close.
insights.solution: Implement robust emergency_cleanup primitives and ensure callers invoke them when session lifecycle errors occur; document the expected behavior and side effects.

key_files.why: Defines the CloudBrowserProvider ABC that all provider implementations must implement; the canonical interface for session lifecycle and metadata.
Re-exports the CloudBrowserProvider for consumers and importers; changing exports or names here affects all imports across the codebase.
Implements managed-gateway-aware provider with idempotency handling and is the intended backend for managed Nous gateway flows; contains managed-specific behaviors.
Implements the direct-only Browserbase provider with feature toggles and 402 fallback logic; historically mixed managed/direct behavior and is now direct-only.
Adds Firecrawl provider implementation for short-lived sessions and wraps Firecrawl API calls; introduces another provider with its own API semantics.

construct_relationships: CloudBrowserProvider (base.py) defines the interface consumed by BrowserUseProvider (browser_use.py), BrowserbaseProvider (browserbase.py), and FirecrawlProvider (firecrawl.py).
__init__.py re-exports CloudBrowserProvider so external callers import this single symbol rather than module-local names.
BrowserUseProvider implements idempotency helpers (_get_or_create_pending_create_key, _should_preserve_pending_create_key) that augment create_session behavior for managed gateway flows.
BrowserbaseProvider contains feature negotiation logic that may toggle requested session features based on provider responses (e.g., fallback on HTTP 402) before returning session metadata.

FirecrawlProvider translates Firecrawl API responses into the same session metadata shape (CDP/connect URL, session id) expected by callers, enabling polymorphic use of sessions across provider implementations.
notable_relationships: These providers are consumed by the browser tool(s) in tools/ (e.g., a browser tool that opens a CDP session) and may be invoked by higher-level agent flows; edits here affect any code that obtains CDP/connect URLs and expects specific session metadata fields.
Browser Use provider is used by managed gateway (Nous-managed) flows; Browserbase is direct-only — changing mode semantics can break gateway-managed session orchestration.

Provider implementations depend on configuration/credentials (API keys, gateway-managed flags) supplied from global config/credential management (outside this folder). Edits should remain compatible with the way credentials and mode flags are read elsewhere.
Emergency cleanup semantics affect global resource usage and billing; failures to properly release sessions may leave orphaned provider-side resources that other systems (billing, quotas) care about.
HTTP status handling (402, 4xx/5xx) and error propagation tie into broader retry/agent error-handling policies; changing interpretation here can alter higher-level retry or fallback behavior.
recommendations: Preserve CloudBrowserProvider method signatures and the shape of returned session metadata (e.g., CDP/connect URL, session id, feature flags, timeouts); changing these requires coordinated updates across callers and __init__.py exports.

When altering BrowserUseProvider, keep managed-mode idempotency token logic and managed timeout semantics intact because gateway-managed flows depend on them; do not remove idempotency helpers without auditing gateway callers.

When modifying BrowserbaseProvider, retain the 402 detection and fallback behavior for optional features (proxies, stealth, keep-alive) or ensure callers are aware of changed billing/fallback semantics.

Maintain robust emergency_cleanup implementations and ensure callers still invoke them on abnormal session termination; test cleanup behavior against provider APIs or mocks to avoid orphaned sessions.

Run unit tests for provider implementations and any browser tool integration tests after edits; also test flows that exercise managed vs direct modes, idempotent session creation, and billing fallback paths.

plugins/memory.description: This subtree contains Hermes memory provider plugins implementing the MemoryProvider interface and a lightweight discovery/loader. Providers cover a range of backends (local SQLite holographic store, cloud services like Honcho/Mem0/Supermemory/Hindsight/OpenViking/RetainDB, and an external CLI-backed ByteRover). These plugins matter because they supply long‑term context (prefetch/inject), durable persistence, tool schemas, and lifecycle/async behavior that directly affect agent context, privacy scoping, and shutdown correctness.
key_behaviors: Discovery and dynamic loading: scan plugins/memory, perform light availability checks, and instantiate a chosen MemoryProvider while allowing CLI command registration without fully loading provider code.
Prefetch and context injection: providers fetch relevant memories (synchronously or asynchronously) to seed system prompts and session context before model calls.

Durable ingestion and background sync: plugins persist conversation turns and metadata using non‑blocking patterns (background threads, async loops, write‑behind queues) to avoid blocking the agent hot path.
Tool schema exposure and RPC handlers: each provider exposes tool schemas (search/recall/retain/profile/store/curate/etc.) that the agent and gateway call to execute memory operations.

Provider lifecycle and shutdown: providers manage resources (event loops, background threads, DB connections, external binary handles, pending session commits) and must flush/commit pending data on shutdown.

insights.title: Gateway user scoping must be respected
insights.problem: Several providers (Honcho, Mem0, earlier Supermemory) defaulted to a single static user/peer or hardcoded home path, causing memory to be shared across distinct gateway users/profiles.
insights.root_cause: Default configuration or hardcoded values (peer_name, user_id, ~/.hermes) were used instead of per-session or per-profile scoping derived from gateway user_id or HERMES_HOME.
insights.solution: Ensure provider client/session naming and storage paths are derived from profile or gateway user identifiers (use get_hermes_home()/gateway user_id) so memories are isolated per user/peer.

insights.title: Background workers, event loops and pending syncs must be drained
insights.problem: Pending messages or pending session commits could be lost (OpenViking), threads leaked (RetainDB), or async writers left unflushed (Honcho/Hindsight) during process exit or plugin shutdown.
insights.root_cause: Background workers and event loops were started without robust join/drain semantics or relied on turn_count checks that could skip final flush; thread-local DB connections and non-joined threads produced leaks.
insights.solution: Providers must expose and be invoked with deterministic shutdown hooks that join background threads, drain queues, and await async flushes; final pending commits must be guarded to avoid double-commit while guaranteeing persistence.

insights.title: External API/SDK breaking changes surfaced as integration bugs
insights.problem: Mem0 plugin assumed older API signatures/return shapes (e.g., user_id parameter and list returns), resulting in runtime errors after SDK v2 changes.
insights.root_cause: Tight coupling to older SDK call signatures and return formats without adapter-layer normalization.
insights.solution: Normalize external client calls and response shapes at the plugin client layer; implement compatibility handling for filters/response unwrapping to isolate callers from SDK changes.

insights.title: Write queue DB usage and thread-local connections can leak under load
insights.problem: RetainDB write-behind previously opened/closed a new sqlite3 connection per operation and did not join worker threads, causing resource leaks and race conditions under high load.
insights.root_cause: Shared SQLite access across threads without thread-local connections and missing lifecycle joins for worker threads.
insights.solution: Use thread-local SQLite connections for worker threads, keep a durable DB connection lifecycle per thread, and ensure shutdown joins worker threads to avoid leaks and partial writes.

insights.title: HRR vector encoding must be reproducible and portable
insights.problem: Holographic HRR vectors are used cross-process and persisted in SQLite; any change in encoding/serialization would break retrieval compatibility.
insights.root_cause: HRR relies on deterministic atom-to-phase encoding and specific serialization bytes; changes to algorithm or byte layout can break stored vectors and retrieval.
insights.solution: Treat encode/serialize formats as a compatibility contract between encode/have store and retrieval components; preserve deterministic atom encoding, binding algebra, and serialization format across upgrades.

insights.title: External CLI integration requires robust binary discovery
insights.problem: ByteRover integration depends on resolving and invoking the brv binary; absence or incorrect path resolution causes plugin failures.
insights.root_cause: Runtime reliance on external CLI without robust resolution/fallback and availability checks.
insights.solution: Resolve and validate external binary path at load time; providers should surface clear availability checks and avoid blocking agent flows when binary is missing.

key_files.why: Discovery and dynamic loader for memory provider plugins — central entry used by runtime to locate/instantiate providers and expose CLI hooks without fully loading plugin code.
Persistent SQLite fact store and entity resolution — authoritative persistence layer for the holographic memory plugin and source of HRR vector storage and FTS indices.
Hybrid retrieval pipeline combining FTS, Jaccard and HRR — shapes what facts are returned for prefetch and probe operations used by the agent.
Honcho session manager handling local caching, async flush, and shutdown semantics — critical for ensuring Honcho-backed writes are not lost and for per-session caching behavior.
RetainDB provider implementing durable write-behind queue and HTTP client — contains queuing, thread lifecycle, and upload semantics that must be preserved for crash-safe ingestion.

construct_relationships: discover_memory_providers (plugins/memory/__init__.py) enumerates plugin.yaml entries and either calls register(ctx) or constructs MemoryProvider subclasses exported by plugin modules.

Each MemoryProvider implementation exposes tool schemas consumed by the agent/tool call dispatcher; these schemas form the contract between core agent flows and provider RPC handlers (e.g., search/recall/retain/profile/curate).

HolographicMemoryProvider uses holographic.holographic encode/bind/bundle utilities to compute HRR vectors which MemoryStore persists; FactRetriever reads both FTS candidates from the SQLite store and HRR vectors for reranking.

Honcho client resolution (client.py) and session manager (session.py) provide the singleton client and per-conversation sessions used by the HonchoMemoryProvider for prefetch and background flush; session manager lifecycle ties into provider shutdown.

RetainDB write-behind queue (_WriteQueue) enqueues ingestion events that background worker threads drain and call the HTTP client; thread-local DB connections and join/flush semantics must be coordinated with provider shutdown.
notable_relationships: Agent core and gateway expect memory providers to supply prefetch context and tool schemas; changes here alter agent prompt composition and tool invocation behavior downstream.
HERMES_HOME and profile isolation are an external contract used by multiple providers for config and on-disk state; changing path semantics will impact multi-profile and gateway user isolation.

Provider shutdown semantics interact with process lifecycle (run_agent, gateway.run); failing to flush or double‑commit pending sessions can lead to lost or duplicated memories and inconsistent downstream behavior.

Several providers depend on external binaries or remote SDKs (ByteRover brv, Honcho SDK, Mem0/RetainDB/OpenViking/Supermemory APIs) — CI and tests blank API keys and may mock these connections; local dev runs require configured credentials or mocks.
Holographic vector serialization and SQLite schemas are backward-compatibility boundaries — other components (retriever, store) depend on stable formats for cross-process persistence.
recommendations: When editing provider discovery or registration, preserve support for both register(ctx) plugin pattern and MemoryProvider subclass instantiation and maintain the lightweight CLI command exposure helper.

Any edits that add/remove background threads, event loops, or queues must preserve deterministic shutdown hooks: join worker threads, drain write-behind queues, close thread-local SQLite connections, and await/flush async writers to avoid data loss or leaks.
Respect per-profile and gateway user scoping: use get_hermes_home() and gateway user_id (when present) for client/session naming and on-disk config to avoid cross-user memory leaks.
Treat HRR encoding/serialization and SQLite schema definitions as stable compatibility contracts; avoid changing byte formats or field encodings without a migration path for persisted vectors/facts.

When modifying external client integrations (Mem0, RetainDB, OpenViking, Honcho, ByteRover), ensure client adapters normalize different SDK/API versions and response shapes so provider code downstream remains stable; also validate binary resolution and availability checks at load time.

Run the test suite that covers plugins (unit and integration as appropriate) and simulate shutdown paths: test joining/draining of worker threads and final pending-commit behavior to ensure no messages are dropped or double‑committed.

skills/github.description: This subtree packages GitHub-focused agent skills: auth detection helpers, procedural guides (SKILL.md), and reusable Markdown templates for PRs, issues, and reviews. It centralizes how the agent detects credentials and repo context, composes human- and machine-facing GitHub content, and executes remote actions via the gh CLI with curl/REST as a documented fallback. These assets are consumed by higher-level agent flows that perform network IO and can create persistent side effects on GitHub.
key_behaviors: Detect and export GitHub authentication and repository context so downstream skills can operate without direct secret handling.
Compose templated Markdown bodies for issues, PRs, and review output that the agent populates and submits or returns.
Perform read/write interactions with GitHub using a preferred gh CLI path and a curl/REST fallback when the CLI is unavailable.
Generate and format structured code-review output (summary, inline comments, suggested fixes) for posting or returning to callers.
Provide human-facing guidance (SKILL.md) describing invocation patterns, expected inputs, and fallback behavior for automated workflows.

insights.title: Centralized auth detection is sensitive to output/logging
insights.problem: Scripts exporting tokens or auth method may accidentally print secrets or place them into logs consumed by callers.
insights.root_cause: gh-env.sh prints a human-readable summary and exports environment variables; downstream tooling may capture stdout/stderr.
insights.solution: Treat gh-env.sh output and exported env var names as sensitive contract — avoid adding verbose logging or printing tokens; preserve concise summary behavior and ensure any helpers consuming it do not echo secrets.

insights.title: Dual-mode execution: gh CLI preferred, curl REST fallback
insights.problem: Skills assume either a CLI or HTTP fallback; changes that alter the detection order, expected arguments, or output formats can break consumers.
insights.root_cause: Multiple skill documents and templates rely on consistent detection and invocation semantics across gh and curl paths.
insights.solution: Maintain the existing preference order and invocation contracts (CLI first, curl fallback) and keep template placeholders and parameter names stable so both execution paths can populate and parse content reliably.

insights.title: Templates are treated as canonical content and sometimes machine-parsable
insights.problem: Altering template structure or placeholder names may break automated composition, parsing, or posting logic elsewhere.
insights.root_cause: Agent code composes and parses templated markdown bodies and review outputs expecting specific headings/fields.
insights.solution: Keep template structure, placeholders, and explicit markers (e.g., headings used by parsers) stable; if changing, coordinate updates with any code that reads or fills these templates.

insights.title: Skills can perform remote, persistent changes
insights.problem: Operations (creating issues, posting reviews, opening PRs) have immediate effects on remote repositories and require appropriate scopes and user intent.
insights.root_cause: Skills are designed to post via gh or API once provided credentials; tests or automation may inadvertently perform live actions if credentials are present.
insights.solution: When modifying execution paths or templates, ensure test and CI conventions (empty API keys in CI) remain honored; preserve guard rails in docs and scripts that indicate when actions are dry-run vs live.

insights.title: Auth helper must remain source-able and POSIX-compatible
insights.problem: Non-portable shell constructs or side-effectful behavior in gh-env.sh can break scripts that source it or CI that expects simple exports and a short summary.
insights.root_cause: gh-env.sh is intended to be sourced by other tooling and must not exit the parent shell or emit unexpected output/side effects.
insights.solution: Keep gh-env.sh safe to source: do not call exit, avoid unguarded commands that fail loudly, and limit stdout to the concise summary that consumers expect.

key_files.why: Entry point for credential detection and repo-context exports; downstream skills depend on the exported env vars and the script's safe-to-source contract.
Describes the code-review workflow, input/outputs, and execution modes (gh vs curl) that implementers and the agent rely on.
Canonical review output template that defines the structure (summary, inline comments, suggested fixes) used for machine-parsing and posting.
Standardized issue body template consumed when the agent files bug reports; formatting and placeholders are part of the content contract.
PR body template used by automated PR creation flows; agents populate it and may rely on consistent headings and placeholders.

construct_relationships: gh-env.sh exports GH_AUTH_METHOD, GITHUB_TOKEN, GH_USER, GH_OWNER, GH_REPO which are consumed by skill runners that choose between gh CLI and curl/REST code paths.
Code-review logic composes review output using review-output-template.md, then either posts it via gh CLI or translates it into HTTP requests using the REST endpoints documented in the cheatsheet.

Issue and PR workflows load markdown templates (bug-report.md, pr-body-*.md) and populate placeholders before invoking the preferred submission mechanism (gh CLI) or the curl fallback that uses endpoints from github-repo-management/references.
SKILL.md files document invocation semantics (inputs, fallbacks, expected behavior) that must align with the scripting and templating implementations in sibling files.
notable_relationships: This subtree depends on external tools (gh CLI, git, curl) and on environment-provided credentials; changes here can affect any agent flows that invoke network IO or mutate repositories (CLI, gateway, scheduled jobs).

Upstream agent components (skill invocation framework, prompt builder, tools that execute shell commands) will call into these skills and expect stable env var names, template formats, and fallback semantics.

CI and tests rely on blanked API key env vars to avoid hitting real services; locally, presence of valid credentials will enable live operations — edits must preserve the ability to run safely in CI without side effects.
recommendations: Preserve exported environment variable names and the concise summary behavior of gh-env.sh; ensure the script remains safe to source (no exit, minimal stdout).
Keep the gh-first / curl-fallback invocation contract and the template placeholder names stable — any coordinated changes require updating all consumers that parse or populate templates.
Treat template documents and review-output structure as a machine-facing contract; verify any edits against code that composes/parses them.
Avoid printing or logging tokens and other secrets; changes must not increase the risk of leaking credentials through stdout/stderr or template rendering.
When editing, run the unit test suite and the repository's skill-related tests; verify behavior in a CI-like environment where API keys are blank to ensure no accidental live requests.

skills/mlops.description: The skills/mlops subtree is the canonical home for MLOps runbooks, training templates, evaluation and inference operational guidance, and cloud deployment notes used by the project. It codifies the expected dataset/prompt shapes, reward signal composition, model + adapter wiring (TRL/PEFT/LoRA), artifact and metadata conventions, and deployment/reproducibility constraints that downstream training, inference, and orchestration components rely on.
key_behaviors: Provide production-ready training templates that assemble datasets into prompt/label shapes, construct reward signals, wrap models with PEFT/LoRA, and invoke TRL-style RL trainers (GRPO).
Capture operational contracts for evaluation and experiment tracking (lm-evaluation-harness, Weights & Biases) including CLI flags, credentials, and artifact handling.
Document and prescribe inference artifact formats and optimization/quantization workflows (GGUF/llama.cpp) that downstream loaders and serving code consume.
Describe cloud deployment patterns and troubleshooting steps (Modal serverless GPU) used to run training and inference workloads at scale.

insights.title: Prompt/dataset shape and reward expectations
insights.problem: Training templates assume a specific prompt/label shape and reward composition; mismatches will break tokenization, reward computation, or trainer input.
insights.root_cause: Templates (e.g., basic_grpo_training.py) embed dataset loading, prompt assembly, and reward functions coupled to TRL tokenization expectations and GRPO trainer APIs.
insights.solution: Any edits must preserve the dataset -> prompt/label mapping, tokenization offsets, and the scalar reward semantics (format/correctness/incremental) so that the trainer receives consistent inputs and computed rewards.

insights.title: PEFT/LoRA/FSDP wiring is a compatibility surface
insights.problem: Model and tokenizer setup is layered with PEFT/LoRA adapters and may assume particular model/tokenizer APIs and weight-saving formats; incompatible changes will break training or downstream loading.
insights.root_cause: setup_model_and_tokenizer routines are integrated with TRL trainer expectations and use adapter wrappers (PEFT/LoRA) and sometimes FSDP/quantization options.
insights.solution: When modifying model setup, ensure the wrapping/unwrapping semantics, saved adapter checkpoints, and expected HF/TRL checkpoint layouts remain compatible with the trainer and downstream conversion pipelines.

insights.title: Evaluation & tracking contracts (lm-eval, W&B)
insights.problem: Evaluation and experiment tracking documentation define execution flags, artifact schemas, and credential requirements; divergence creates non-reproducible experiments.
insights.root_cause: The lm-evaluation-harness and W&B guides supply the canonical invocation and artifact expectations used by pipelines and operators.
insights.solution: Preserve CLI/environment contracts and artifact metadata conventions; edits that change output artifact formats or required env vars should be coordinated with evaluation and CI/test flows.

insights.title: Cloud deployment assumptions (Modal)
insights.problem: Modal guides encode container, volume, and endpoint patterns required for serverless GPU execution; mismatches between runtime artifacts and container expectations will cause deployment failures.
insights.root_cause: Documentation prescribes runtime images, persistent volume usage, and web endpoint wiring that training jobs expect.
insights.solution: Changes to produced artifacts, entrypoint scripts, or file-system layouts must be reflected against Modal deployment notes so that images/volumes and entrypoints align with runtime expectations.

insights.title: Artifact/metadata conventions for model serving
insights.problem: Inference guides (GGUF, llama.cpp) expect artifacts with particular metadata/quantization formats; training outputs need conversion pipelines that respect those conventions.
insights.root_cause: The inference subtree defines the artifact formats and metadata downstream serving code will load.
insights.solution: Ensure saved model artifacts include the metadata and layout required for quantization/format conversion; preserve naming and checksum/metadata fields that loaders depend on.

key_files.why: Primary runnable training template: assembles dataset/prompts, defines reward functions, configures model+tokenizer and invokes GRPO trainer — it is the canonical example and integration point for TRL/PEFT-based RL fine-tuning.
Top-level description that frames the MLOps skill intent and boundaries; changes here reflect the conceptual contract for the whole subtree.
Authoritative Modal deployment playbook used by operators to run GPU workloads serverlessly; must remain consistent with artifact layout and entrypoints produced by training templates.
Defines how to invoke the lm-evaluation-harness and the custom task schema; test and evaluation runners rely on these invocation contracts.
Describes GGUF quantization and artifact conventions expected by llama.cpp-compatible loaders — downstream serving and conversion tooling depend on these conventions.

construct_relationships: basic_grpo_training.get_dataset -> prompt assembly: dataset loader returns records that are assembled into prompts and labels with expected tokenization offsets.
basic_grpo_training.correctness_reward_func/format_reward_func -> GRPOTrainer: reward functions produce scalar rewards consumed by the trainer's update loop.
basic_grpo_training.setup_model_and_tokenizer -> PEFT/LoRA wrappers: model/tokenizer are wrapped before passing to TRL/GRPO trainer and saved in adapter-compatible formats.
training templates outputs -> inference/gguf conversion: training artifacts (checkpoints, adapter weights, metadata) are the inputs for inference conversion and gguf/llama.cpp serving flows.
evaluation docs (lm-eval, W&B) -> training/eval runs: experiment tracking and evaluation harnesses consume CLI flags, environment variables, and artifact names produced by training templates.
notable_relationships: Training templates produce artifacts and metadata that downstream inference and deployment docs (inference/gguf, models) and loaders rely on for serving and quantization.
Evaluation and tracking guides (lm-eval, W&B) are upstream consumers of training outputs and depend on coordinated CLI/environment contracts; CI/integration tests may rely on those contracts.
Cloud provider guidance (skills/mlops/cloud/modal) describes runtime expectations (images, volumes, entrypoints) that must match produced artifacts and scripts used by training jobs when deployed.

Cross-subtree dependency on heavy optional extras (TRL, PEFT, FSDP, quantization libraries): changes that alter runtime dependencies or invocation patterns can affect packaging, Dockerfile, and CI install steps.
recommendations: Preserve dataset -> prompt/label mapping and tokenization offset semantics used by TRL trainers; validate any change by running the training template end-to-end with a small dataset.
When editing model/tokenizer setup or adapter wiring, ensure saved checkpoints and adapter weight formats remain loadable by both the trainer and downstream conversion/serving tools.
Maintain artifact metadata (filenames, manifest fields, quantization markers) expected by inference conversion (GGUF) and evaluation tooling; update corresponding docs if metadata changes.

Respect external integration contracts: lm-evaluation-harness CLI flags, W&B env/credentials, and Modal deployment entrypoint/volume expectations — coordinate edits that alter these contracts with those documents.

Account for heavy optional dependencies: running or testing changes that touch training/inference flows may require extras (TRL/PEFT/quant libs) and Python >=3.11; CI blanks real API keys, so local credentials are required for integration runs.

Run unit tests plus the relevant integration/e2e training or evaluation scenarios when modifying templates or artifact formats to ensure downstream tooling (inference, evaluation, cloud deployment) continues to operate.

skills/productivity.description: The skills/productivity subtree contains CLI utilities and helpers that implement productivity-oriented skills for Hermes: Google Workspace integration, OCR/document extraction, and Office (PowerPoint/DOCX/XLSX) manipulation. These scripts operate as thin command-line tools that interact with external services and on-disk Office package layouts; they must preserve external invariants (OAuth token formats, Office .rels/[Content_Types] manifests, and model/dependency expectations) because other parts of the agent and external applications parse their outputs and rely on persisted artifacts.
key_behaviors: OAuth-driven Google Workspace flows: provisioning client secrets, exchanging PKCE codes, persisting and refreshing OAuth tokens, and exposing Google API operations via a CLI that returns JSON to the calling skill layer.

Document extraction: two-tiered extraction flows — heavyweight model-backed OCR and layout extraction (marker-pdf) that checks disk/model requirements and outputs structured JSON/markdown, and lightweight local extraction (pymupdf) that provides page text, markdown, tables, and images for quick processing.

Office package manipulation: operations on unpacked Office package directories (PPTX/DOCX/XLSX) including adding/duplicating slides, pruning orphaned resources, canonicalizing XML (merge runs, simplify redlines), validating/repairing, and zipping back into archive files while maintaining relationship manifests and content types.

CLI-first design: tools are designed to be invoked as standalone scripts by the Hermes skill runtime or by developers; outputs are machine-parsable (JSON/markdown) and scripts may import Hermes project constants/config (HERMES_HOME, profile paths).

insights.title: Google OAuth scripts depend on Hermes project imports and persisted token schema
insights.problem: Direct execution of google-workspace scripts failed to import hermes_constants and may break relative imports; token files and client_secret storage need to be located under the Hermes profile/HERMES_HOME.
insights.root_cause: Scripts assume package-level imports and the Hermes project root on sys.path; they also rely on a specific persisted token format and location in the Hermes profile directory.
insights.solution: When modifying Google Workspace scripts or their CLI surface, preserve import compatibility with hermes_constants/HERMES_HOME and do not change token file layout or refresh semantics without coordinating callers; maintain machine-readable JSON outputs used by the skill layer.

insights.title: Office pack/clean/add-slide utilities must preserve Office manifest invariants
insights.problem: Office files are created by operating on unpacked directories; incorrect updates to .rels, presentation.xml (p:sldId), or [Content_Types].xml will produce invalid or unrecognized archives in Office apps.
insights.root_cause: Office packages are collections of inter-file XML manifests and relationships; operations that add/duplicate/prune files must update multiple cross-referenced manifests and maintain namespace/ID consistency and transitive reachability.
insights.solution: Edits to PowerPoint/DOCX utilities must keep updates atomic and consistent across relationship files, content-type overrides, and presentation manifests; retain the current algorithms for transitive reachability, ID allocation, and content-type updates or ensure callers validate output with the same validators used by pack.py.

insights.title: Document and OCR scripts rely on optional heavy dependencies and disk/model resources
insights.problem: Marker-based extraction and some office/document features require large model downloads or native packages (marker-pdf, pymupdf, pandas, node/playwright in other parts), which may not be installed in minimal installs.
insights.root_cause: The subtree offers both lightweight and heavyweight implementations; heavyweight paths perform disk checks and may install or require external resources at runtime.
insights.solution: When changing scripts, preserve explicit guards and explicit disk/model requirement checks; maintain clear CLI behavior and exit codes when optional dependencies or models are missing so callers (skills/runtime) can fall back or report predictable errors.

key_files.why: Primary CLI wrapper exposing Gmail/Calendar/Drive/Docs/Sheets operations and the runtime entrypoint that authenticates with persisted Google credentials — central to Google Workspace skill behavior.

OAuth provisioning and token lifecycle manager for Google Workspace; responsible for creating/storing client secrets, PKCE exchange, token refresh/revoke — changes affect all Google integration flows.
High-quality model-backed OCR and layout extraction path with disk/model checks and structured JSON/markdown output consumed by document skills.
Implements slide creation/duplication and updates to relationships and presentation manifests — a touchpoint where generated XML must integrate correctly into presentation manifests.
Finalizer that validates/repairs, condenses XML, and zips unpacked Office packages back into .pptx/.docx/.xlsx — it encodes packing invariants and is used to produce Office-consumable archives.

construct_relationships: google_api.py and setup.py share credential/token storage semantics: setup.py provisions client_secret.json and tokens stored under the Hermes profile/HERMES_HOME that google_api.py loads and refreshes when executing API operations.

extract_marker.py and extract_pymupdf.py offer parallel extraction flows: marker-based model-backed extraction for high-fidelity structured output vs. pymupdf-based lightweight extraction; both expose CLI outputs (markdown/JSON) intended for downstream skill parsing.

add_slide.py, clean.py, pack.py and office/helpers (merge_runs.py, simplify_redlines.py) collaborate on Office package lifecycle: helpers canonicalize XML -> add/duplicate/clean mutate files and relationships -> pack.py validates and archives, so changes in helpers affect pack/clean correctness.

Office helper functions (merge_runs, simplify_redlines) operate on the same XML structures that pack.py and clean.py rely on for validation and size/quality guarantees, creating a tight coupling between XML normalization and packing outcomes.
notable_relationships: These scripts depend on Hermes core for configuration and constants (hermes_constants, HERMES_HOME). Altering file locations or token schemas will impact the agent runtime and gateway flows that call these skills.

OCR/model-backed scripts may trigger large downloads or require native dependencies; runtime behavior affects environment setup, Docker builds, and developer installs that are handled elsewhere in the repo.

Office packaging tools produce artifacts consumed outside Hermes (PowerPoint/Word/Excel). Any change that breaks Office compatibility will have downstream impact on users who open generated files in Microsoft Office or other consumers.
recommendations: Preserve existing persisted file formats and locations (OAuth token files, client_secret.json, unpacked Office directory layout) or ensure coordinated updates to any callers and documentation if they must change.

When editing Office-related code, ensure updates to .rels, presentation.xml (p:sldId), and [Content_Types].xml remain consistent and atomic; run packing/validation flows to confirm Office compatibility for changed code paths.
When modifying Google scripts, retain import compatibility with hermes_constants/HERMES_HOME and the token refresh/revoke semantics; validate CLI JSON outputs remain stable for skill callers.
Keep guards around optional heavyweight dependencies and preserve the explicit disk/model requirement checks so runtime callers can detect missing resources and decide fallback strategies.

Test CLI behaviors (JSON/markdown outputs and exit codes) and integration points (token loading, model/disk checks, produced Office archives) after edits; changes can have cross-cutting effects on skills, gateway integrations, and external consumers.

website/docs.description: This folder contains the website documentation used as the canonical, human-facing contract for Hermes: developer reference, getting-started tutorials, and navigation/category metadata consumed by the static site generator. These docs codify runtime contracts (APIs, env vars, file formats, extension points) and must remain consistent with the codebase because contributors and operators rely on them for correct integration and operation.
key_behaviors: Document and preserve APIs, on-disk formats, schema, and extension contracts (providers, tools, memory plugins, SKILL.md) so code changes do not break external expectations.
Provide onboarding and operational instructions (installation, CLI commands, Docker/HERMES_HOME notes) that map directly to scripts, entrypoints and environment variables in the repo.
Drive site navigation and grouping via category _category_.json files; file paths and metadata determine ordering, visibility and build behavior.
Act as the single source of truth for internal architecture and flows (agent loop, gateway/ACP flow, cron scheduling) that implementers consult when extending or integrating with Hermes.

insights.title: Risk of documentation drifting from runtime code
insights.problem: Developer and user docs describe precise runtime behaviors (APIs, env vars, DB schema, formats) that can become inaccurate when code evolves.
insights.root_cause: Docs are maintained separately from code; changes to code-level contracts (prompts, schema, command names, env vars) are not automatically propagated to markdown pages.
insights.solution: When making code changes that alter public contracts, update the corresponding doc pages and any category metadata; when editing docs, reference the exact code locations (agent/, gateway/, cron/) and run the site build/link checks to validate consistency.

insights.title: Category JSON controls site structure
insights.problem: Reordering, renaming or moving doc files without updating _category_.json files will break site navigation and may hide pages from the site build.
insights.root_cause: The static site generator relies on per-folder category metadata for ordering and grouping rather than inferred file order.
insights.solution: Any file-level changes that affect path or title must be reflected in the related _category_.json to preserve navigation and grouping.

insights.title: Docs define machine-parseable formats and schemas
insights.problem: Pages (e.g., session-storage.md, trajectory-format.md, SKILL.md guide) describe concrete file/DB schemas and telemetry formats that external systems and plugins depend on.
insights.root_cause: These formats are de facto stable interfaces used by plugins, skills, and external tooling.
insights.solution: Treat these pages as part of the API surface: any schema/format change requires coordinated code and docs updates, and guidance in the doc should remain exact (field names, types, examples).

insights.title: Duplicate/overlapping pages present
insights.problem: There are duplicate entries (installation.md, learning-path.md) which can create confusion about canonical content or lead to inconsistent edits.
insights.root_cause: Multiple copies/variants ended up in the tree (e.g., mirrored getting-started docs).
insights.solution: When updating content under getting-started, check for duplicate pages and ensure any edits maintain parity where duplication is intentional; confirm which file the site uses for navigation.

insights.title: Docs express extension contracts that code relies on
insights.problem: Guides for adding providers, tools, and memory plugins encode expected interfaces and lifecycle behaviors that implementers follow.
insights.root_cause: Extension authors use docs as the source for required method names, file formats (SKILL.md) and registration steps.
insights.solution: Preserve precise wording, examples, and required fields in these guides; when changing underlying extension APIs, update these docs so third-party implementations remain compatible.

key_files.why: Controls ordering and grouping of developer-facing reference pages; changes affect site navigation and visibility of the developer guide section.
Describes the end-to-end agent runtime flow and API modes—this is a primary reference implementers consult when altering core agent control flow.
Defines the contract and steps for adding inference providers and credential handling; edits here must match provider adapter interfaces in agent/.
Documents session storage and DB schema that external tooling and persistence code rely on; schema changes require coordinated updates.
Primary onboarding/installation instructions referenced by users and CI reproducibility notes; must remain consistent with installer scripts and Dockerfile.

construct_relationships: developer-guide/_category_.json dictates navigation and ordering of markdown files in the developer-guide folder used by the site generator.
agent-loop.md documents the message flow that maps to gateway/run.py and agent/ prompt/adapter modules; editors should map references to those code files.
adding-providers.md and memory-provider-plugin.md define interfaces that provider adapters and credential pool code (agent/*.py, credential_pool) must implement.

prompt-assembly.md and context-compression-and-caching.md describe interactions between PromptBuilder, PromptCache and ContextCompressor; these docs must align with agent/prompt_builder.py and related caching/compression code.
session-storage.md and trajectory-format.md establish on-disk formats and DB schemas referenced by MemoryManager, trajectory storage, and cron telemetry pipelines.
notable_relationships: Docs must reflect CLI entrypoints and scripts defined in pyproject.toml and run_agent.py (hermes, run_agent, acp_adapter entry points).
Installation and Docker instructions link to packaging and Dockerfile; mismatches can lead to broken user installs or images that deviate from docs.
Environment variable names and defaults in docs must match code and CI expectations (CI blanks keys to avoid provider calls); mismatches risk accidental external API usage.
Guides for gateway/ACP interop and platform adapters are tightly coupled with gateway/platforms/* implementation; breaking changes in adapters require doc updates to avoid integration regressions.
Trajectory and telemetry docs feed into RL workflows (Atropos) and external training tooling; changes to formats must be coordinated with those downstream consumers.
recommendations: When editing docs, verify and cite the exact code locations (module/file paths) that implement behaviors described — ensure text matches code-level names and examples.
If you rename/move markdown files, update corresponding _category_.json entries and any internal links to prevent broken navigation or hidden pages in the site build.
Treat pages that document formats, schemas, or extension contracts as part of the API: any code change to those contracts must include an update to the relevant doc page(s).
Run the site build and link-checking steps (or local static site generator checks) after edits to detect broken links, missing pages, or navigation issues.
Be aware of duplicate pages in getting-started; ensure edits don’t create inconsistent versions—confirm which file is authoritative for site navigation before changing content.

hermes_cli.description: This folder implements the Hermes command-line surface and user-facing orchestration: bootstrapping and environment setup, interactive TUI callbacks, diagnostics, profile and plugin management, model/provider selection, and a multi-provider authentication/credential-pool management surface. It centralizes user-visible behaviors and persistence boundaries (HERMES_HOME, .env, auth.json, plugins dir, profiles), so changes here directly affect login flows, provider selection, and all CLI and gateway entrypoints.
key_behaviors: CLI bootstrap and environment initialization: apply profile override pre-import, load .env, ensure HERMES_HOME and config defaults, initialize logging and interactive guards.
Credential and provider lifecycle: resolve credentials (API keys, OAuth/OIDC), persist auth state (auth.json) with locking, seed and manage credential pool entries via interactive commands.
Model/provider selection and normalization: parse /model commands and flags, resolve aliases and provider-specific model names, apply provider/gating policies (Nous free-tier, catalog fallbacks).

Plugin and profile lifecycle management: discover/load plugins (entry points, user/project dirs), provide PluginContext for registration, install/update/remove plugins with manifest/environment prompts, and create/manage isolated profiles.
Diagnostics and maintenance: run hermes doctor to inspect config, provider keys, state.db health and optionally fix WAL/config issues; interactive tools and checklists for user input.
Interactive TUI behaviors: prompt callbacks, secret capture, approval dialogs, and fallback UIs for non-TTY environments (curses/text fallback).

insights.title: Credential precedence, stale token import, and provider quirks
insights.problem: Expired or mis-sourced credentials can be imported or preferred (e.g., expired Codex tokens or OAuth access_token used incorrectly for Nous inference), causing runtime failures and blocking auto-detection.
insights.root_cause: Multiple credential seed/import paths and ad-hoc resolution logic did not consistently consult the credential pool first, validate expiry/state, or respect provider-specific credential types for inference vs portal APIs.
insights.solution: When modifying auth or runtime resolution code treat credential_pool as authoritative, validate token expiry/state before importing or using tokens, and preserve provider-specific credential semantics (do not use OAuth portal tokens as inference API keys). Maintain file locking and caching semantics when persisting auth.json.

insights.title: Profile and environment overrides are applied early and must be honored
insights.problem: Profile overrides and HERMES_HOME-affecting env vars (e.g., HERMES_PORTAL_BASE_URL) were bypassed because profile resolution and env checks happened in different code paths or were lost by fallback logic.
insights.root_cause: Bootstrapping order is significant: profile overrides must be applied before importing modules that read HERMES_HOME or portal envs; some login/resolution code used fallback parameters instead of checking env vars.
insights.solution: Any edits must preserve the early pre-import profile override and ensure all modules that read environment/config do so after override is applied. Respect explicit env vars when resolving provider endpoints and portal base URLs.

insights.title: Alias fallback and provider availability can surprise users
insights.problem: Alias resolution fell back to a hardcoded provider list that might not have credentials, yielding surprising model resolutions; error messages lacked config diagnostics.
insights.root_cause: Fallback logic used a static provider list and did not surface why a resolution failed (e.g., malformed config.yaml or missing credentials).
insights.solution: When changing model_switch or catalog logic, preserve the chain that prefers authenticated providers, communicate clear diagnostics for unknown providers or malformed config, and maintain catalog cache/TTL behavior used for Nous gating.

insights.title: Platform-specific sanitization and limits (Telegram, Discord) must be enforced centrally
insights.problem: Duplicate/ad-hoc sanitization for Telegram names and limits led to invalid commands and API rejections in gateway integrations.
insights.root_cause: Multiple code paths performed name sanitization and clamping inconsistently, and undocumented platform limits (description length, count) were not centralized.
insights.solution: Respect the central COMMAND_REGISTRY and sanitization/clamping utilities; preserve validated output shapes used by gateways and keep any platform-specific rules in one place consumed by both CLI completions and gateway setMyCommands calls.

insights.title: Plugin manifests and env prompts must be surfaced and stored profile-aware
insights.problem: Installed plugins declared required env vars that were not prompted/recorded; user messages referenced hardcoded paths instead of profile-aware HERMES_HOME.
insights.root_cause: Plugin install flow did not consistently surface manifest metadata and used hardcoded path strings rather than profile-aware helpers.
insights.solution: When editing plugins_cmd or plugins modules, maintain manifest parsing tolerance (rich vs simple formats), ensure required env vars are prompted during install and written to the active profile's .env, and use display helpers that are profile-aware.

insights.title: Doctor output and automatic fixes interact with evolving config shapes
insights.problem: Diagnostics reported outdated field names (e.g., memory_mode) and lacked structured config-structure diagnostics before.
insights.root_cause: Config schema and runtime objects evolved but doctor and repair logic were not kept in sync.
insights.solution: Keep doctor checks aligned with current config schema and runtime attributes; when editing config structures ensure doctor can surface ConfigIssue entries and that --fix behaviors (WAL checkpointing, directory creation) are preserved.

key_files.why: Top-level CLI entrypoint that applies pre-import profile overrides, loads .env and initializes CLI runtime — changes here affect all CLI/gateway bootstrapping and profile behavior.
Central multi-provider auth resolver and auth.json persistence with provider-specific quirks; critical for credential resolution and security-sensitive I/O.
Resolves runtime inference provider, api_mode, base_url, and credentials with precedence rules used by CLI, gateway and cron — edits change provider selection logic used across runtimes.
Canonical registry of slash commands and sanitization/clamping logic consumed by CLI completions and gateway integrations (Telegram/Discord/Slack).
Plugin discovery/loading and PluginContext surface used by third-party plugins; edits impact plugin registration, hooks, and lifecycle across the system.

construct_relationships: hermes_cli/main.py applies profile override and sets environment before other hermes_cli modules read config/HERMES_HOME, so main influences auth, runtime_provider, plugins, and profiles behaviors.
hermes_cli/auth.py provides ProviderConfig and credential resolution that runtime_provider.py consumes when resolving the runtime inference provider and credentials.

hermes_cli/auth_commands.py wraps auth.py and the credential pool API to seed/persist/remove credentials; its interactive flows mutate auth.json/.env used by runtime_provider.resolve_requested_provider.
hermes_cli/commands.py exposes COMMAND_REGISTRY and sanitization utilities that gateway adapters and CLI UI consumers rely on for completions and platform-specific setMyCommands calls.

hermes_cli/plugins.py and hermes_cli/plugins_cmd.py share manifest parsing and environment prompting logic; plugins_cmd writes plugin-related env vars into the profile's .env which plugins.py will later read when loading the plugin.
notable_relationships: Persistent state under HERMES_HOME (auth.json, .env, config.yaml, plugins/, profiles/) is an upstream dependency for agent runtime and gateway; edits that change file layout, formats, or keys are breaking for runtime and gateway consumers.

Provider integrations (Nous portal, Z.AI, OpenRouter, Codex) and local CLIs (git, codex CLI) are upstream external dependencies surfaced by auth and doctor modules; code assumes certain API semantics and token types for inference vs portal APIs.

Model catalog and gating logic (hermes_cli/models.py) inform user-facing selection flows but rely on cached/fetched provider metadata that other agent subsystems (agent adapters) ultimately use for invocation.

Plugin installation clones Git repositories into the profile's plugins/ dir and may add env vars to .env; this produces downstream effects for plugin load-time behavior and for commands that read plugin state.
recommendations: Preserve pre-import profile override ordering in main.py — any change to startup ordering can alter which HERMES_HOME, .env, and config.yaml are read.

Respect credential precedence: consult credential pools before importing legacy/seeded tokens, validate expiry/state before accepting tokens into runtime resolution, and maintain auth.json file locking semantics when writing.
Do not conflate OAuth portal tokens with inference API keys; keep provider-specific credential semantics intact in runtime_provider and auth resolution.

When editing command/registry sanitization or platform-specific logic, ensure a single canonical sanitization path is used by both CLI completions and gateway setMyCommands to avoid platform API rejections (Telegram limits/description lengths/counts).
Maintain profile-aware path helpers when displaying or writing .env, plugin manifests, or seed files — do not hardcode ~/.hermes paths when profiles may change HERMES_HOME.

When touching plugin install/update flows ensure required env vars declared by plugin manifests are prompted and written into the active profile's .env and that manifest parsing tolerates simple and rich formats.

Run the unit test suite (pytest tests/ excluding integration/e2e) and run hermes doctor against a test profile after changes that touch config/auth/profile/plugin code to catch config-structure and WAL/DB issues.

Be careful with cached state and TTLs (e.g., Nous free-tier cache, Z.AI endpoint probe caching) — edits that change cache keys/ttl or probe behavior can change runtime provider selection in subtle ways.
Preserve non-TTY guards and fallbacks for interactive components (checklist, TUI callbacks, _require_tty) so non-interactive scripting and gateway operation are not broken.

agent.description: The agent package contains the core utilities, adapters and orchestration helpers used by the Hermes runtime to build prompts, select / probe models, manage credentials, provide memory abstractions, compress context, parse explicit context references, and integrate auxiliary providers (Codex/Anthropic/Copilot). It is a dependency-heavy coordination layer: mostly stateless helpers, provider-specific adapters, and a small set of managers that must remain thread-safe and resilient because they are invoked from CLI, gateway, and background scheduler flows.
key_behaviors: Credential lifecycle and selection: persistent pool loading, seeding from env/legacy stores, rotation, exhaustion marking, soft leasing for concurrent callers and OAuth refresh handling.
Model capability discovery and routing: fetch and cache model metadata from remote registries and local endpoints, normalize model IDs, and provide heuristics for cheap-vs-strong routing.

Prompt composition and caching: assemble system prompts (identity, platform hints, skills index, context files), apply provider-specific cache markers (Anthropic), and avoid heavy imports during prompt assembly.

Memory and context management: register builtin and a single external memory provider, prefetch/sanitize memory into system prompts, compress long conversation context via auxiliary models while protecting head/tail.

Auxiliary provider shims and streaming handling: route short/auxiliary tasks to selected backends (Codex, Anthropic, Copilot ACP), convert provider-specific streams into unified completion shapes and handle function/tool call extraction.
Skill discovery and invocation: parse skill frontmatter, normalize and inject declared config values into skill prompts, and produce slash-command metadata for gateways.

insights.title: Seeded credentials used wrong base_url
insights.problem: Credentials seeded from environment or legacy state used an incorrect default base_url which caused first-call failures for keys bound to non-default endpoints.
insights.root_cause: Seeding logic assumed a default base_url rather than resolving provider-specific/explicit URLs present in environment/legacy config.
insights.solution: When seeding from env/legacy state, resolve and persist the provider/base_url associated with each credential so runtime selection uses the correct endpoint.

insights.title: Concurrent usage and exhaustion
insights.problem: Concurrent child agents could all select the same credential leading to rate-limit hits and accelerated exhaustion.
insights.root_cause: No soft-leasing or lightweight coordination meant pool entries appeared available to multiple concurrent callers.
insights.solution: Expose a soft lease API and enforce thread/process-safe leasing semantics to reduce simultaneous selection of the same credential.

insights.title: Stale OAuth entries from external rotation
insights.problem: Codex/Copilot OAuth refresh tokens rotated externally (e.g., Copilot CLI) leaving pool entries stale and causing failures without recovery.
insights.root_cause: Pool lacked logic to re-sync or refresh entries that may be rotated by external tools and relied solely on local stored tokens.
insights.solution: Provide mechanisms to detect stale entries and re-seed or refresh pool entries from known legacy stores when exhaustion is detected.

insights.title: Outdated default context lengths and prefixes
insights.problem: Some provider/model families (e.g., Gemini/Gemma) had incorrect default context-lengths and provider prefix mappings, causing suboptimal prompt chunking or truncation.
insights.root_cause: Static defaults and mapping tables lag behind provider model introductions/updates.
insights.solution: Keep provider prefix mappings and default context lengths up-to-date and ensure metadata can be updated or overridden at runtime.

insights.title: Disk cache corruption risk
insights.problem: Bundle/disk cache writes to models.dev registry could be corrupted if process killed mid-write, leading to silent failures and empty cache loads.
insights.root_cause: Non-atomic write workflow when updating the on-disk JSON snapshot.
insights.solution: Write disk caches atomically (write-temp-rename) and validate JSON on load to avoid returning corrupted or empty registries.

insights.title: Heavy imports on prompt build / cold start cost
insights.problem: Building the skills-system prompt pulled heavy tool registry imports causing slow cold starts.
insights.root_cause: Prompt builder originally imported or computed skill indexes by touching heavy subsystems instead of using a lightweight cached snapshot.
insights.solution: Keep prompt builder stateless and rely on two-layer caching/snapshots so prompt assembly does not import heavy registries at runtime.

insights.title: Model families require tailored tool-use enforcement
insights.problem: Some model families (e.g., 'grok', GPT/Codex) tended to explain rather than invoke tools, or ignored tool-persistence semantics.
insights.root_cause: Generic guidance blocks were insufficient for families with different action/response patterns.
insights.solution: Inject family-specific enforcement guidance blocks (tool-use enforcement, execution discipline) during prompt composition for models known to misbehave.

insights.title: Unsafe compaction retries and hardcoded timeouts
insights.problem: Compaction calls forced a temperature and used a hardcoded 45s timeout, and repeated failures caused continuous retries increasing load and noise.
insights.root_cause: Compaction logic used fixed parameterization and retry behavior not aligned with provider capabilities and time characteristics.
insights.solution: Use config-driven timeouts (no forced temperature), add cooldown on repeated summary failures, and reduce log severity when no summary model is available.

insights.title: Subprocess hang risk when resolving references
insights.problem: Git and ripgrep subprocess calls could hang indefinitely on large repos or slow outputs, blocking the agent.
insights.root_cause: Subprocess.run invocations lacked timeouts and unbounded waits.
insights.solution: Apply bounded timeouts to subprocess calls and handle timeouts gracefully, returning partial results or user-facing warnings.

insights.title: Streamed Codex/Responses shapes and tool-call collapsing
insights.problem: Streamed items from Codex could be dict-shaped rather than SDK objects, and the adapter collapsed function_call events into plain text, losing structured tool-call semantics.
insights.root_cause: Adapter assumed a single canonical stream shape and flattened function-call structured events into synthesized text.
insights.solution: Harden streaming parsing to accept dict-shaped items and preserve/expose function_call events so upstream tool execution can remain structured and unambiguous.

insights.title: Hardcoded output limits
insights.problem: Adapter defaulted to a hardcoded 16K token max output which could starve models that support larger outputs (e.g., Claude Opus).
insights.root_cause: Fallback used a fixed hardcoded value instead of consulting provider/model-native output limits.
insights.solution: Use model-native output limits and fallbacks from model metadata when constructing requests.

insights.title: Log redaction missing new secret prefixes
insights.problem: New providers use API key prefixes not present in redaction patterns, risking accidental leakage in logs/outputs.
insights.root_cause: Static regex patterns lag provider key prefix evolution.
insights.solution: Keep secret-prefix patterns extensible and update redaction patterns when supporting new providers; apply formatter broadly to all logging outputs.

insights.title: Invalid characters in generated gateway command names
insights.problem: Skill-derived Telegram command names could contain characters invalid for platform command registration (e.g., +, /, @, .), causing API failures.
insights.root_cause: Skill name normalization did not sanitize or validate platform-specific constraints before exposing to gateway registration.
insights.solution: Normalize and validate generated command keys against external platform constraints before using them in setMyCommands or equivalent calls.

insights.title: Prefetched memory can enable prompt-injection
insights.problem: Prefetched memory content could include sequences that the model treats as user input or break system fences, enabling prompt-injection style behavior.
insights.root_cause: Memory content was injected without consistent fencing/sanitization to distinguish recalled context from live user/system prompts.
insights.solution: Sanitize and fence prefetched memory blocks and treat them as read-only structured context to avoid being interpreted as user instructions.

insights.title: Cache-control markers are provider-specific
insights.problem: Anthropic-style cache_control markers must be applied correctly (system + last 3 messages) to reduce repeated prompt costs; misapplication changes request semantics.
insights.root_cause: Cache marker logic is provider-specific and must be preserved when composing messages for Anthropic endpoints.
insights.solution: Ensure Anthropic-specific cache_control injection functions are used only for Anthropic-compatible message flows and preserve message ordering contracts.

key_files.why: Central persistent credential manager used by adapter layers and auxiliary clients for provider failover, rotation, leasing and OAuth refresh — changes here affect all provider interactions.
Provider/model capability discovery and local server probing used by routing, prompt-sizing, and model-specific request construction across the agent.
Implements conversation compression and summarization logic that interacts with auxiliary clients, token budgets, and impacts downstream prompt content and memory.
Coordinates builtin and external memory providers, prefetch/sync lifecycle and system-level memory blocks that feed into prompt building and must be kept safe from prompt-injection.
Assembles system prompts (identity, skills index, platform guidance) and integrates cached skill indexes — changes influence how models are instructed and how skills/configs are injected.

construct_relationships: MemoryManager aggregates system memory blocks and calls MemoryProvider implementations (BuiltinMemoryProvider) for prefetch and sync; providers must implement MemoryProvider contract methods.
ContextCompressor uses an auxiliary LLM client (resolved by auxiliary_client) to generate summaries; compression parameters must align with auxiliary client capabilities and model_metadata limits.
