Auxiliary client adapters (_CodexCompletionsAdapter, _AnthropicCompletionsAdapter) call into credential_pool to select credentials and into model_metadata to determine limits/pricing when shaping requests.
PromptBuilder composes skills index using outputs from skill_utils/skill_commands (frontmatter parsing and resolved config values) and must avoid importing heavy tool registries.
Anthropic adapter and prompt_caching functions coordinate: prompt_caching injects Anthropic cache_control markers and the Anthropic adapter expects/accepts those markers when sending messages.
notable_relationships: agent/* utilities are invoked by run_agent, hermes_cli and gateway adapters — changes must preserve interfaces relied upon by CLI/gateway orchestration.
credential_pool reads/writes auth.json and may read legacy provider state (e.g., ~/.codex/auth.json); edits can impact on-disk auth format compatibility and cross-tool integrations.
models.dev and model_metadata fetch remote registries (models.dev, OpenRouter/OpenAI-like endpoints) and cache to disk; network fetches and disk caches influence runtime routing decisions.
Copilot ACP client runs external copilot CLI subprocesses and returns completion-shaped objects to the agent core; modifications affect how tool-calls are detected and executed across gateways.
ContextReference resolution invokes external tools (git, ripgrep, web_extract) and therefore interplays with tools/ directory behavior and network/time constraints.
recommendations: Preserve thread/process safety: when editing any code that selects or mutates credentials, maintain soft-lease semantics and avoid races that could allow concurrent clients to overuse a single credential.

Respect on-disk formats and atomicity: updates to disk-backed caches (auth.json, models.dev disk cache, skills snapshots) must be written atomically and remain backward-compatible or include migration paths.
Avoid heavy imports in prompt-time code paths: keep prompt_builder and prompt-caching utils lightweight and use cached snapshots to prevent cold-start latency regressions.

Honor provider-specific metadata: when modifying routing, adapters or prompt sizing, consult model_metadata and preserve use of model-native limits and provider-specific markers (e.g., Anthropic cache_control).
Sanitize external inputs and subprocesses: any changes to context_references, Copilot client, or tools that invoke subprocesses must enforce timeouts and handle partial/failed outputs gracefully.

Preserve memory fencing and prefetch sanitization: modifications to MemoryManager or BuiltinMemoryProvider must maintain fences/sanitization preventing recalled memory from being treated as user instructions.

Maintain structured tool-call semantics: when changing auxiliary adapters or streaming parsers, preserve function_call/tool-call structure instead of collapsing into text to keep tool execution deterministic.

Run the test suites that cover affected areas: unit tests under tests/ plus integration/e2e where relevant (and CI-like env constraints); when changing caching/auth behavior, validate with tests that simulate concurrent clients and disk interruptions.

gateway.description: The gateway folder implements the messaging gateway that bridges external messaging platforms (Discord, Telegram, WhatsApp, Matrix, Email, Feishu, etc.) into the Hermes agent. It centralizes configuration, session lifecycle, adapter orchestration, delivery routing, persistence of directory/pairing state, and per-platform adapters including an OpenAI-compatible HTTP API adapter. This layer is critical because it enforces runtime ordering, file/atomic state semantics, and platform-specific normalization that affect agent inputs, privacy, and long‑running reliability.
key_behaviors: Load and normalize gateway configuration from layered sources (env, config.yaml, legacy json) and expose per-platform settings to adapters.
Bootstrap gateway runtime (ensure SSL certs, set env overrides), start/monitor BasePlatformAdapter instances, and manage adapter lifecycle, reconnection/backoff and safe shutdown.
Construct and maintain deterministic session keys and dynamic session context prompts (including PII redaction) to route inbound messages into agent sessions.
Resolve delivery targets and route agent/cron outputs to platforms, files or home channels while optionally mirroring delivered messages into session transcripts.
Persist and safely update on-disk caches and stores used by gateway (channel directory, pairing store, session transcripts) with atomic writes and locking to avoid corruption/races.
Expose Hermes via an OpenAI-compatible HTTP API with SSE streaming, idempotency and CORS/security headers so external frontends can drive sessions.

insights.title: Platform environment overrides must surface provider-specific keys
insights.problem: Important platform overrides (e.g., MATRIX_DEVICE_ID) were not exposed from env/config, preventing stable client behavior in adapters.
insights.root_cause: Gateway config loading did not include some provider-specific environment overrides and did not bridge certain config values into environment extras.
insights.solution: Treat provider-specific environment keys as first-class overrides in gateway config loading and propagate them into adapter extras so adapters can use stable IDs and other required env-driven settings.

insights.title: Thread vs per-user session key semantics changed
insights.problem: Session key construction previously isolated threads by participant IDs but was changed to share threads by default, affecting session routing and history exposure.
insights.root_cause: Thread handling semantics and a new thread_sessions_per_user configuration were introduced without consistent propagation across session construction and adapters.
insights.solution: Session key generation must respect thread_sessions_per_user and maintain deterministic keys so adapters and delivery routing share the same session semantics.

insights.title: Partial writes and races corrupt on‑disk gateway state
insights.problem: Channel directory and pairing files could be truncated or corrupted by concurrent writes or failed writes (e.g., disk full), and concurrent adapter access caused races.
insights.root_cause: Non-atomic JSON/file writes and lack of cross-process locking when multiple adapters or threads read/write the same state files.
insights.solution: Use atomic file write semantics and explicit locking around persistent stores accessed by multiple adapters to avoid partial writes and race conditions.

insights.title: Pending sentinel eviction and queued pending commands leaked to agent
insights.problem: Sentinel objects used during async adapter setup were evicted prematurely and pending slash-command text could be fed to the agent as user input.
insights.root_cause: Stale-detection logic removed pending sentinel markers too early and did not isolate queued control commands from regular message queues during setup/reconnect.
insights.solution: Ensure pending sentinel lifecycle is conservative and that control commands are detected and handled with special-case guards to avoid being passed to the agent as normal messages.

insights.title: Control commands must bypass active-session guards and typing indicators
insights.problem: While the agent was running, control commands (/stop, /new, /approve, /deny) were queued or blocked; typing indicators also interfered with approval flows on some platforms.
insights.root_cause: Base adapter queued all inbound messages under active-session guards and kept typing indicators active during approval wait, preventing users from issuing control commands or approving requests.
insights.solution: Adapters and the base dispatch must treat control commands as high-priority and disable or pause typing indicators during approval windows so users can interact with control flows immediately.

insights.title: SSE and API responses missing security/CORS headers
insights.problem: OpenAI-compatible API responses (especially SSE streams) lacked standard security headers and CORS on streaming responses, causing client issues and repeated preflight OPTIONS requests.
insights.root_cause: Middleware-injected headers ran after Response.prepare() for SSE, and general omission of basic security headers on API responses.
insights.solution: Ensure security headers (e.g., nosniff, no-referrer) are set for all API responses and that CORS and preflight caching are applied to SSE streams and preflight responses.

key_files.why: Authoritative loader and in-memory representation of gateway and per-platform configuration; edits here change runtime adapter behavior and env overrides.
Gateway bootstrap and the GatewayRunner orchestrate adapter lifecycle, SSL cert detection ordering, and environment bridging; changing startup ordering or sentinel logic lives here.
Session key construction, PII redaction and dynamic session prompt generation live here and determine how messages map to agent sessions and what context is exposed to models.
Defines BasePlatformAdapter, message normalization, caching and control-command guards shared by all platform adapters; platform adapters depend on this contract.
Exposes Hermes via an OpenAI-compatible REST/SSE API; changes here affect external integrations, security headers, CORS and idempotency semantics.

construct_relationships: GatewayRunner (gateway/run.py) loads GatewayConfig via gateway/config.py and propagates selected config values into environment variables used by adapters.

BasePlatformAdapter (gateway/platforms/base.py) is the interface and utility provider implemented by each platform module (discord.py, matrix.py, email.py, etc.), and GatewayRunner instantiates and monitors these adapters.
Session construction functions and models in gateway/session.py are used by adapters to build deterministic session keys and dynamic system prompts before dispatching messages into the agent loop.

DeliveryRouter/DeliveryTarget (gateway/delivery.py) resolve targets using data from channel_directory (gateway/channel_directory.py) and dispatches via adapters' send methods, while mirror (gateway/mirror.py) appends deliveries back into session transcripts maintained by session logic.

API server (gateway/platforms/api_server.py) maps incoming HTTP/OpenAI-compatible requests into agent sessions using session/session-store semantics and uses ResponseStore/idempotency caches to persist run state.
notable_relationships: HERMES_HOME / per-profile data paths are relied on by multiple gateway components (Matrix E2EE store, channel_directory.json, pairing files, session transcripts); changes to paths or hardcoded home paths must be coordinated across adapters.

SSL certificate detection in gateway/run.py must happen before importing network libraries used by adapters; reordering imports or initializing networked SDKs before cert setup can break secure connections.

Adapters that manage persistent state (pairing, channel directory, session DB/JSONL) must coordinate with atomic write and locking semantics to avoid corruption when multiple adapter threads/processes access the same files.

Platform-specific extras (e.g., MATRIX_DEVICE_ID, thread_sessions_per_user) originate from gateway/config.py and must be consistently surfaced to adapters via extras/env so adapter behavior matches configured expectations.
API server SSE behavior interacts with middleware ordering; ensure SSE prepare/streaming still receives CORS/security headers expected by browsers and frontends.
recommendations: Preserve startup ordering: run SSL cert detection and any env-bridging in gateway/run.py before importing or instantiating network libraries or platform adapters.

When modifying config loading or platform extras, ensure provider-specific env keys (like MATRIX_DEVICE_ID and thread_sessions_per_user) are surfaced from env/config and propagated into adapter extras.
Respect atomic write and locking patterns for on-disk stores (channel_directory, pairing store, session transcripts); coordinate cross-thread/process access to avoid partial writes and races.

Do not change session key semantics without ensuring gateway/session.py, adapters, delivery routing, and any persisted transcripts use the same keying logic (thread_sessions_per_user toggle is global).
Keep control-command shortcuts and guard bypasses intact: control commands must be detected and handled with priority (bypassing normal active-session queuing and typing-indicator behavior).
If changing API server or SSE logic, verify CORS headers, preflight Access-Control-Max-Age, and basic security headers are present on both normal and streaming responses.

Run unit tests as in CI after edits; if you modify adapters or end-to-end integration points (Matrix, Discord, API server), run integration/e2e tests that exercise reconnection, E2EE, and file persistence behaviors.

tools.description: The tools directory implements the agent-facing side-effecting primitives and safety infrastructure (file I/O, browsers, sandboxing helpers, cron management, delegation, and approval gates) that the core agent and gateway invoke as callable tools. It centralizes safety checks, per-session isolation, persistent allowlists/mounts, and cloud/local backend adapters so that model-driven tool calls can be executed with guardrails and reproducible lifecycles. Because these modules interact with external systems, persistent disk state, and concurrent gateway sessions, they are critical for correctness, security, and cross-session isolation.
key_behaviors: Expose safe, schema-backed tool functions to the agent (create/read/write/patch files, schedule cron jobs, browse the web, spawn subagents) with centralized validation and redaction.
Manage per-task and per-session state (browser sessions, approval queues, registered credential files, env passthrough lists) with ContextVar or explicit locks to avoid cross-session leakage.
Coordinate lifecycle and cleanup of external resources (browser/CDP sessions, sandbox mounts, shadow git checkpoints, RPC servers) including emergency/auto-cleanup paths.
Gate dangerous or sensitive operations via approval flows and heuristics (regex detection, manual CLI prompts, persistent allowlists) and block/notify gateway callers until resolved.
Integrate pluggable provider backends (cloud browsers, execution environments) via abstract provider registries so selection and feature negotiation are centralized.

insights.title: Persistent allowlists and other state must be available early
insights.problem: Persistent allowlists and other safety-critical state were not always loaded until explicit initialization, causing early tool calls to bypass checks.
insights.root_cause: State is sometimes lazily loaded after module import or not loaded on import, creating a window where detection/validation uses incomplete data.
insights.solution: Ensure safety-critical persistent state (e.g., approval allowlists) is loaded or lazily guarded so APIs never run unprotected between import and explicit init; treat import-time state as authoritative or require explicit init contracts for callers.

insights.title: Avoid cross-session leakage of credentials and allowlists
insights.problem: Module-level registries (credential files, env passthrough, etc.) could leak between gateway sessions, exposing credentials or permitting env variables across contexts.
insights.root_cause: Use of global module state instead of context-local storage for per-session registries in long-lived multi-session gateway processes.
insights.solution: Use ContextVar-based registries or otherwise scope state to a session/context; when global state exists, clear/validate it on session boundaries and prefer context-local APIs for gateway use.

insights.title: Blocking approval flows interact with gateway threading
insights.problem: Gateway flows block agent threads awaiting user approval, requiring per-request synchronization and notification; incorrect handling can deadlock or drop notifications.
insights.root_cause: Approval queueing implemented with threading.Event per pending request and shared locks; complexity arises when gateway notifications, timeouts, and module-level state interact.
insights.solution: Treat approval queues as explicit concurrency primitives: ensure thread-safe enqueue/dequeue, consistent timeout semantics, and reliable notification paths from gateway notify hooks; document blocking behavior for callers.

insights.title: Hard-coded paths and workspace assumptions break portability and safety checks
insights.problem: Security checks and child-agent workspace assumptions used literal paths (e.g., ~/.hermes, /workspace) causing bypasses or incorrect behavior when HERMES_HOME or working directories differ.
insights.root_cause: Code assumed default HERMES_HOME and container-like workspaces rather than resolving configured or runtime paths.
insights.solution: Normalize and resolve HERMES_HOME and workspace paths via centralized helpers at runtime; avoid literal-path comparisons—use canonicalized paths and symlink-aware checks when enforcing blocked or sensitive directories.

insights.title: Subagents must share credential pools to avoid hot-spotting
insights.problem: Child/delegate agents were pinned to fixed credentials or unaware of the parent's credential pool, causing credential exhaustion and uneven rate limits.
insights.root_cause: Delegation implementation did not lease credentials from a shared pool and sometimes propagated static key references to children.
insights.solution: Lease credentials from a shared CredentialPool abstraction when spawning subagents and pass opaque leases or short-lived tokens rather than static keys; enforce per-child quotas and rotation awareness.

insights.title: New browser providers must be registered and visible to the browser tool
insights.problem: Cloud browser providers added separately were not discoverable by the browser tool until registered, causing runtime failures or missing backends.
insights.root_cause: Provider implementations and provider registry maintenance were decoupled; registration steps were required for discovery but sometimes omitted.
insights.solution: Ensure provider implementations are registered with the central provider registry used by browser_tool on import or through an explicit plugin registration path; document where registration occurs and how tools resolve providers.

key_files.why: Central authority for detecting and gating dangerous shell/SQL/filesystem commands and the bulk of the manual/gateway approval flow; edits affect safety and gateway blocking behavior.
Manages which host files can be mounted/uploaded into remote sandboxes; changes affect credential exposure and sandbox file mounts.
Implements all file read/write/patch operations, sensitive-path blocking, redaction, and read-size limits used by many tools—critical for preventing prompt injection and data leaks.
Implements spawning of isolated child agents with credential leasing and workspace hints; impacts delegation safety, rate-limiting, and workspace correctness.
Central browser abstraction that resolves local vs cloud providers and manages CDP endpoints and session cleanup—changes affect all browser-backed features and provider integration.

construct_relationships: approval.detect_dangerous_command is invoked before shell/terminal tools and file-mutating tools to decide whether to block and request approval.
credential_files.register_credential_file builds mounts consumed by environment backends in tools/environments when creating sandboxes or container mounts.
file_tools and checkpoint_manager interoperate: checkpointing occurs before file-mutating operations implemented by file_tools to enable rollbacks.

delegate_tool leases credentials from a shared CredentialPool and strips blocked toolsets before constructing a child agent system prompt; resulting child sessions rely on env_passthrough and credential_files for permitted mounts and env access.

browser_tool selects a provider from the browser_providers registry (browserbase, Browser Use, Firecrawl) and coordinates session lifecycle; provider implementations must return CDP/connect URLs compatible with browser_tool's normalization and cleanup paths.
notable_relationships: Many tools depend on HERMES_HOME (via get_hermes_home) for logs, checkpoints, persistent state and must respect configured HERMES_HOME across CLI, gateway, and Docker environments.
Gateway and platform adapters rely on approval.register_gateway_notify to wake blocked approval requests; misalignment here can cause blocked requests to stall when running as a service.

tools that expose model-invoked APIs (cronjob, clarify_tool, browser_tool) are invoked from agent core (PromptBuilder/skill system) and must adhere to function-schema contracts and the agent's tool invocation lifecycle.

Environment backends under tools/environments consume mounts and credential registrations here; changes to credential_files or env_passthrough impact remote execution security across local, docker, Modal, and SSH backends.

Provider-specific behaviors (managed vs direct, idempotency tokens) in browser_providers affect billing and session lifetime semantics for the gateway-managed browser modes used by the agent and gateway.
recommendations: Preserve or honor ContextVar-based isolation for per-session registries (credential files, env passthrough) when modifying stateful APIs; clear or rebind context on session boundaries.

Respect import-time loading semantics for persistent safety data (allowlists); if you alter initialization, ensure tools can't run before safety state is available or require explicit init from callers.

When changing code that blocks (approval queues, delegate blocking), validate thread-safety and notification semantics; run gateway integration scenarios to ensure blocked requests are woken by gateway notifications.
Any change that touches path checks must canonicalize HERMES_HOME and workspace paths (resolve symlinks) and consider symlink-free copies used for mounts to prevent bypasses.

If adding or removing browser providers, update the central provider registry used by browser_tool and verify create/close/emergency_cleanup flows; test managed vs direct modes and idempotency behaviors.

Run unit tests plus gateway integration tests (or manual gateway sessions) after edits that affect stateful or cross-session behavior; check for credential leakage, stale global state, and correct cleanup of external resources.

tests.description: This tests/ subtree encodes the behavioral contracts and invariants across the Hermes Agent codebase: agent prompt construction and safety, gateway adapters and session lifecycle, CLI surface and plugin management, credential/authorization flows, cron/job delivery semantics, tooling/toolset definitions, and server/EventBridge behaviors. These tests rely heavily on environment isolation, temporary HERMES_HOME directories, filesystem persistence formats (JSONL, SQLite, auth.json), and mocked external dependencies to define what changes in the implementation must preserve.
key_behaviors: Environment-isolated unit tests that create temporary HERMES_HOME and monkeypatch env vars to assert persistence and config override semantics.
Extensive mocking of external/optional dependencies (Matrix, prompt_toolkit, browser discovery, OAuth/device flows, mcp) so tests express surface contracts without network calls.

Persistence and serialization invariants: dataclass (to_dict/from_dict) round-trips, JSONL transcript resilience to corruption, auth.json/credential pool migration and persistence, and SQLite state.db usage for select E2E flows.

Platform and runtime resolution flows: provider selection precedence (env, explicit overrides, credential pool), gateway PlatformConfig home_channel env overrides, and toolset/provider visibility logic for CLI and gateway.

Lifecycle and robustness contracts: idempotent logging initialization, GatewayRunner fatal/retry semantics, cron run_job session setup/teardown and delivery wrapping (MEDIA extraction, [SILENT] marker), and EventBridge queue/cursor concurrency behavior.

insights.title: Tests depend on an isolated HERMES_HOME and env var monkeypatching
insights.problem: Many tests create and mutate files under HERMES_HOME and rely on specific environment variables; lack of isolation causes cross-test or local-state leakage and flakiness.
insights.root_cause: Shared filesystem state and environment variables are read/written by code under test and by tests themselves without global isolation by default.
insights.solution: When editing code that reads/writes HERMES_HOME or env vars, preserve the behavior that allows tests to override/use a temporary HERMES_HOME; ensure any new persistence paths or env-driven overrides remain configurable and mockable so tests can isolate state. Risk: changing hardcoded paths or unmockable side-effects will break many tests.

insights.title: Optional/third-party libraries are mocked extensively in tests
insights.problem: Code changes that introduce new imports or change how optional libraries are referenced may break tests that inject lightweight module mocks or rely on import-time behavior.
insights.root_cause: Tests install fallbacks into sys.modules and monkeypatch import-time behavior for components such as Matrix, prompt_toolkit, mcp, and browser discovery.
insights.solution: Maintain import-time guards and allow dependency injection; any new optional integration must be import-safe (i.e., compatible with tests' mocking strategy). Risk: moving imports to top-level without guards or altering import names will cause tests to fail or require updating test mocks.

insights.title: Tests assert robustness to corrupt/mixed persistence formats
insights.problem: Code that reads transcripts/auth/state must tolerate corrupt JSONL lines, prefer longer/authoritative sources, and handle migrations.
insights.root_cause: Multiple persistence backends exist (JSONL, SQLite, auth.json) and historical migration paths create mixed states.
insights.solution: Preserve lenient parsing and preference rules (skip corrupt JSONL lines, prefer longer source, safe rewrite semantics) and ensure migration paths continue to run and persist expected fields. Risk: rigid parsing or changed precedence will break transcript/credential tests and restore flows.

insights.title: Tests codify exact precedence for runtime provider resolution and credential seeding
insights.problem: Provider selection depends on environment variables, explicit overrides, credential pool state, and provider-specific base_url/api_mode rules; incorrect precedence leads to subtle behavioral regressions.
insights.root_cause: Multiple overlapping configuration sources (env keys like OPENAI_API_KEY/OPENROUTER_API_KEY, explicit CLI args, credential pool entries, provider config) and special-case provider behaviors (Nous, OpenRouter, Anthropic).
insights.solution: When changing provider resolution or credential handling, preserve the documented precedence and the ability to skip the pool with explicit overrides; maintain seeding/migration behavior and exhaustion/reset semantics. Risk: altering precedence or token-refresh semantics will require updating many tests and will impact runtime behavior.

insights.title: Gateway and adapter behaviors have detailed edge-case tests (Matrix E2EE, thread/DM semantics, runner exit/reconnect)
insights.problem: Adapters and the gateway runner encode platform-specific edge cases (mxc→http conversion, DM/thread keying, reply-fallback stripping, E2EE device behavior) and robust exit/retry semantics; regressions are easy when changing adapter helpers or runner lifecycle.
insights.root_cause: High platform diversity and platform-specific protocols plus nuanced lifecycle handling make small changes impactful across tests.
insights.solution: Preserve existing helper semantics and lifecycle signals (clean exit flag, exit_reason, _failed_platforms); keep matrix-related optional code paths testable via injected mocks. Risk: changing adapter helper signatures, return shapes, or runner flags will cascade into many gateway tests.

insights.title: Cron tests expect delivery wrapping, MEDIA extraction, and [SILENT] handling
insights.problem: Cron run_job must set/clean env markers, strip MEDIA: blocks and deliver attachments separately, and honor [SILENT] markers to suppress delivery; missing any of these behaviors leads to mismatches with tests.
insights.root_cause: Cron delivery reuses gateway delivery logic but applies additional wrapping and env state; tests assert parity with gateway media handling and silence markers.
insights.solution: Maintain media extraction behavior and ensure [SILENT] anywhere in response prevents delivery; keep session lifecycle behavior (SessionDB injection, env cleanup) intact. Risk: changing wrapping or media handling will require extending test coverage and may break scheduled delivery behavior.

insights.title: Logging and other initializers must be idempotent
insights.problem: Tests verify setup_logging creates handlers once and supports forced reinit; non-idempotent initialization causes duplicate handlers, unexpected files, and test flakiness.
insights.root_cause: Global process-level state (logging handlers) persists across multiple setup calls within the same test process.
insights.solution: Preserve idempotent initialization semantics (no duplicate handlers unless forced) and expose force options for reinitialization used by tests. Risk: altering initializer behavior will break tests that rely on repeated setup calls.

insights.title: CLI and platform tool mapping must remain synchronized
insights.problem: Installer and CLI flows expect continuous consistency between platform lists, toolset definitions, and plugin mappings; adding platforms or toolsets without updating mappings breaks installer/test invariants.
insights.root_cause: Multiple data structures (platform list, default toolsets, toolset-to-skill mappings) must be maintained in lockstep; tests assert complete coverage across platforms.
insights.solution: Keep platform/toolset definitions and visibility gates consistent and ensure tests can detect missing mappings. Risk: adding a platform without a toolset or tests will cause failures in tools_config and plugin-related tests.

insights.title: Browser discovery and filesystem path tests are platform-sensitive
insights.problem: Windows-specific path handling and hardcoded path separators caused flaky browser discovery tests.
insights.root_cause: Tests used hardcoded Windows paths or assumptions about os.path joining leading to mismatches on other platforms or CI.
insights.solution: Any code that affects path discovery should preserve cross-platform path composition behavior or remain easily mockable; tests expect discovery to consult PATH then common install dirs and to call Popen with platform-appropriate kwargs. Risk: introducing OS-specific assumptions breaks cross-platform tests.

insights.title: Plugin hooks must collect non-None returns and swallow exceptions
insights.problem: Hook return collection semantics and exception-handling behavior are required by tests; inconsistent handling leads to missing data or raised exceptions in plugin manager tests.
insights.root_cause: Plugin system aggregates hook returns and expects None to be excluded; tests also expect exceptions from plugin hooks to be swallowed (not crash host).
insights.solution: Keep hook invocation contract: collect non-None returns, ignore None, and catch/log plugin exceptions without escaping to caller. Risk: changing this will affect plugin-related tests and runtime plugin robustness.

key_files.why: Specifies the prompt construction, safety scanning, truncation, and skill-prompt invariants that agent/prompt_builder.py must preserve; many agent-level behaviors are asserted here.
Encodes session keying, prompt generation, transcript persistence, and multi-source transcript precedence — these behaviors interact with gateway/session persistence and playback.
Validates command registry invariants, aliasing, platform gating and prompt_toolkit integration — changes to CLI command metadata or discovery must respect these tests.
Defines credential pool selection, exhaustion/reset logic, seeding and migration expectations — core to provider resolution and runtime credential rotation behavior.
Contains unit-to-E2E coverage for the MCP server and EventBridge including concurrency semantics and SQLite-backed E2E flows; edits affecting MCP or EventBridge must preserve these contracts.

construct_relationships: Many tests assert that code reads HERMES_HOME and supports overriding it via environment or fixtures; persistence readers/writers across auth.json, JSONL transcripts, and SQLite state.db must interoperate.
Gateway PlatformConfig and _apply_env_overrides are tested to inject per-platform home_channel values used by platform adapters and session prompt builders.

CredentialPool and auth store interactions are exercised by CLI auth commands, runtime provider resolution tests, and credential_pool tests; CLI auth_* commands mutate auth.json which load_pool then reads.
Cron run_job behavior interacts with SessionDB, gateway delivery wrapping logic (including MEDIA: extraction and [SILENT] handling), and this same delivery logic is shared with gateway adapters.
Plugin discovery/loading and hermes_cli plugins_cmd tests exercise the same plugin manager behaviors used by runtime plugin hook invocation and CLI plugin install/update/remove logic.
notable_relationships: Tests frequently mock optional external packages (matrix-nio, prompt_toolkit, mcp, playwright/browser discovery) — CI blanks API env vars to prevent real network calls and tests rely on that guard.
Gateway and agent tests are tightly coupled via session/transcript shapes and prompt construction: changes in prompt_builder, session keying, or transcript persistence affect gateway session tests.

Credential and provider precedence logic ties together runtime provider resolution, CLI provider flows, and agent model routing — tests across tests/test_runtime_provider_resolution.py and tests/test_credential_pool.py codify cross-module expectations.
EventBridge and MCP end-to-end tests create and rely on an ephemeral SQLite state.db; changes to database schema, field names, or table layouts will impact these tests and require migration handling.
Logging initialization expectations are global: tests reset logging state between runs and expect idempotent behavior; edits to logging setup will affect tests across the suite.
recommendations: When making edits under this subtree's target code, run pytest with the same exclusions used in CI (ignore integration/e2e unless intentionally changing E2E flows); many tests assume no external API keys are present.
Respect and preserve the ability to override HERMES_HOME and key environment variables in tests; ensure new code paths allow injection/mocking of filesystem locations and env-driven overrides.
Keep top-level imports of optional/third-party packages guarded or import-safe so tests can mock modules in sys.modules without import errors.
Do not change persistence formats or dataclass serialization shapes without considering backward-compatibility and updating tests that assert to_dict/from_dict round-trips and migration behaviors.
Preserve logging and other global initializers' idempotency or expose force-reinitialize flags used by tests to avoid duplicate handlers/state.
Maintain provider resolution precedence and credential pool semantics (seeding, exhaustion TTLs, explicit overrides skip pool) because many tests assert exact behavior.
Ensure gateway/adapter helper signatures (e.g., Matrix mxc→http, reply stripping, DM/thread detection) and GatewayRunner exit/retry flags remain stable; these are relied upon by many gateway tests.

When modifying cron delivery or gateway delivery code, ensure MEDIA: extraction and [SILENT] marker handling remain compatible and that run_job still sets/cleans HERMES_CRON_AUTO_DELIVER and SessionDB state as tests expect.
If adding or renaming platforms, validate that platform/toolset definitions and mapping tables are updated in lockstep (tools_config and hermes_cli tests expect comprehensive mappings).
Run tests with parallelism in mind: many tests run under pytest-xdist (-n auto) and rely on isolation; ensure any global state added is test-friendly (resettable or test-scoped).

acp_adapter.description: The acp_adapter package exposes the Hermes core agent over the Agent Client Protocol (ACP), allowing external ACP-capable clients (editors, IDEs, gateways) to create and drive agent sessions remotely. It translates between ACP concepts (sessions, content blocks, tool calls, permission requests, streaming updates) and in-process Hermes constructs, handles session lifecycle and persistence, and mediates concurrency and event streaming so Hermes can be used as a remote agent endpoint.
key_behaviors: Translate ACP session lifecycle requests into in-process Hermes sessions: create, persist, restore, fork, and delete sessions.
Bridge runtime events (agent thinking, messages, tool progress/complete, steps) into ACP session updates and stream them to connected clients safely across threads/async boundaries.
Map Hermes tool invocations and file locations into ACP ToolCallStart/ToolCallProgress/ToolCallComplete payloads and report observable progress.
Forward ACP permission requests into Hermes approval callbacks with timeout-enforced awaiting of operator decisions.
Bootstrap and run an ACP JSON-RPC server process safely (logging to stderr, preserving stdout for ACP transport) and advertise runtime/provider capabilities to clients.

insights.title: Event callbacks cross thread/async boundaries
insights.problem: AIAgent events are produced in the agent runtime but must be delivered to ACP clients on an asyncio event loop; naive callbacks can race or deadlock.
insights.root_cause: Callbacks run on agent threads while ACP transport expects coroutine-based session updates on an asyncio loop.
insights.solution: All event callbacks must forward updates using thread-safe dispatch (e.g., asyncio.run_coroutine_threadsafe) and maintain stable mapping state (tool-call IDs) to avoid lost or out-of-order updates.

insights.title: Stdout is reserved for ACP JSON-RPC; logging must not pollute transport
insights.problem: Terraforming stdout breaks ACP transport and client JSON-RPC framing.
insights.root_cause: Entrypoint may be invoked as a subprocess; ACP JSON-RPC uses stdout/stderr conventions.
insights.solution: Entry initialization must configure logging to stderr only; code paths invoked by the ACP server must not write arbitrary data to stdout and must respect JSON-RPC framing on stdout.

insights.title: Sessions carry working-directory and persisted state
insights.problem: Restoring or forking sessions affects tool execution contexts and file-resolution logic if cwd or persisted state is not applied consistently.
insights.root_cause: Sessions persist AIAgent state and working directory which tools rely on during execution.
insights.solution: When restoring or forking sessions, ensure session working-directory and persisted fields are applied before any tool runs; persist/restore operations must be backward-compatible and atomic to avoid file-system surprises.

insights.title: ACP permission decisions are time-sensitive and can block agent actions
insights.problem: Waiting indefinitely for a remote operator decision can stall agent flows or lead to inconsistent agent state.
insights.root_cause: Permission bridging relies on awaiting an ACP request_permission coroutine with network/UX latency and potential failure.
insights.solution: Enforce a timeout and clear deny-on-timeout semantics; callers must handle permission denial consistently and not assume synchronous approval.

insights.title: Advertised capabilities depend on Hermes runtime/provider configuration
insights.problem: ACP clients may be shown commands or features that the runtime cannot actually execute if provider creds or runtime components are missing.
insights.root_cause: Adapter advertises supported slash commands and MCP servers based on local configuration; detection is separate from runtime availability checks.
insights.solution: Gate advertised features on provider detection (has_provider/detect_provider) and ensure advertised MCP server entries match tools actually registered into the Hermes tool registry.

key_files.why: Central ACP Agent wrapper: orchestrates session creation, maps ACP requests to Hermes AIAgent calls, registers MCP servers as tools, and streams agent events back to clients.
Session manager and persistence: creates, persists, restores, forks, and deletes agent sessions and manages session working-directory and DB durability.
Event translators: constructs callbacks that convert AIAgent events into ACP session updates, handling tool lifecycle and incremental text streaming.
Permission bridge: exposes approval callbacks that await ACP permission decisions with timeout and maps ACP option kinds to Hermes approval strings.
Process entrypoint: prepares environment, configures logging to stderr (preserving stdout for ACP JSON-RPC), loads .env, and starts the ACP agent server loop.

construct_relationships: HermesACPAgent (server.py) uses SessionManager (session.py) to create, load, fork, and persist sessions.
HermesACPAgent wires event callbacks produced by events.py into the in-process AIAgent so agent runtime events are converted to ACP session updates.
SessionState/SessionManager (session.py) set per-session working directories that tools and tool-invocation helpers (tools.py) rely on to extract file locations and context.
permissions.make_approval_callback (permissions.py) is supplied to AIAgent instances created by session._make_agent to forward permission requests to the ACP connection managed by server.py.
tools.build_tool_start / build_tool_complete (tools.py) are called by events callbacks to construct ACP ToolCall payloads that events.py sends via the ACP connection.
notable_relationships: Relies on the Hermes core agent and tool registry: the adapter registers ACP MCP servers into Hermes tools and expects tool metadata and file-location extraction conventions from the core.
Entrypoint must not interfere with ACP JSON-RPC transport: acp_adapter.entry configures logging/env so stdout is preserved for protocol framing used by ACP clients.
Authentication/feature gating depends on runtime credential detection (acp_adapter.auth): advertised commands and features should align with detect_provider/has_provider outcomes.

Session persistence ties into broader Hermes persistence strategy (HERMES_HOME): sessions persisted here must remain compatible with the main data directory and restore semantics used elsewhere in the codebase.
recommendations: Preserve stdout for ACP JSON-RPC framing; log only to stderr from the entrypoint and any long-running server processes.
Respect asyncio event loop boundaries: use thread-safe dispatch (e.g., asyncio.run_coroutine_threadsafe) when forwarding agent events to ACP coroutines and ensure the target loop is running.
When touching session persistence or schema, maintain backward-compatible serialization and ensure atomic persist/restore to avoid partial session state on crash.
Honor permission timeouts and denial semantics: code paths invoking approval callbacks must handle deny and timeout consistently to avoid leaving agent tasks in limbo.
When modifying mappings between Hermes tools and ACP ToolKind/content, keep the tool-call ID and file-location extraction formats stable because clients rely on those payloads for UI/context.
Any change that affects which features or commands are advertised to ACP clients must consult has_provider/detect_provider results so clients are not shown unavailable capabilities.
Run unit tests that cover session create/restore/fork, event streaming ordering, permission timeouts, and the entrypoint behavior (no stdout writes) after edits to ensure protocol correctness.

cron.description: This folder implements Hermes Agent's scheduled-job subsystem: job storage and scheduling (jobs.py) and the tick/run orchestration that executes jobs, runs optional pre-run scripts, composes prompts, invokes the core agent runner, and delivers results to messaging platforms (scheduler.py). It matters because it bridges persistent schedule state, on-disk scripts, the LLM-driven agent runtime, and multi-platform delivery; correctness is required to avoid duplicate firings, unintended side-effects, or leaking raw script/prompt artifacts to users.
key_behaviors: Persist, parse and compute next-run times for scheduled jobs (supports cron/interval/ISO/duration syntaxes and grace windows).
Periodic tick that loads jobs, acquires a file lock to avoid concurrent ticks, determines due jobs and advances/marks next_run_at appropriately to avoid replays.

For each job: optionally run a constrained pre-run script from HERMES_HOME/scripts (with a timeout), build an LLM prompt including referenced skills and delivery guidance, execute the job via the core agent runner, and post-process the agent response for delivery.
Resolve delivery target (origin/home channel/explicit) and deliver results either via a running gateway adapter or a standalone send helper, handling media attachments and suppression markers.

insights.title: SILENT marker must suppress delivery wherever present
insights.problem: Cron responses containing a [SILENT] marker in positions other than the very start were still being delivered to recipients.
insights.root_cause: Delivery suppression previously checked only startswith('[SILENT]'), missing occurrences later in the response.
insights.solution: When deciding whether to deliver, check for the SILENT marker anywhere in the response text and suppress delivery if present. Treat presence semantics consistently across all delivery paths.

insights.title: MEDIA: tags were sent as literal text instead of attached media
insights.problem: Cron-delivered messages contained literal MEDIA: lines instead of the scheduler attaching files to the outgoing message.
insights.root_cause: Scheduler passed raw text containing MEDIA: markers directly to the send helper without extracting and attaching referenced files.
insights.solution: Extract MEDIA: tags from job output before sending, resolve and attach referenced files via the gateway/send helper API, and remove or replace MEDIA markers in the delivered text so recipients don't see raw tag lines.

insights.title: Cron-run agents must not call send_message when scheduler will deliver
insights.problem: Agents executed for cron jobs attempted to call send_message tools, leading to unnecessary tool invocations and failures when send_message is disabled or redundant.
insights.root_cause: Prompts given to the model did not explicitly indicate that the scheduler will deliver results; the agent therefore tried to use the send_message tool itself.
insights.solution: Include explicit delivery guidance in the job prompt so the model avoids calling send_message; ensure this guidance is preserved and considered when composing prompts and when caching prompts.

insights.title: jobs.json loading can fail silently on control characters
insights.problem: jobs.json containing bare control characters (e.g., literal newlines in stored prompts) caused json.load() to fail and load_jobs() to return an empty list, stopping scheduled jobs without obvious errors.
insights.root_cause: Unescaped control characters in persisted job fields lead to JSON parsing errors; load_jobs did not robustly handle or repair malformed JSON.
insights.solution: Auto-repair or robustly sanitize jobs.json on load (e.g., escape or remove invalid control characters) and surface parsing errors appropriately; ensure load_jobs recovers gracefully without silently dropping all jobs.

insights.title: Recurring jobs can re-fire on restart/crash if next_run_at isn't advanced atomically
insights.problem: If the process crashed or the gateway restarted while a job was running, the job could be seen as still due on restart and re-fire repeatedly.
insights.root_cause: next_run_at for recurring jobs was not advanced and persisted before execution began, so a crash during run left the job's next_run_at unchanged.
insights.solution: Advance and persist the job's next_run_at (or otherwise mark it as running) before executing the job so restarts do not immediately re-queue the same run; ensure persistence is atomic to avoid duplicate firings.

key_files.why: Core runtime for ticking and executing jobs: resolves origins/delivery targets, runs pre-run scripts, builds prompts, invokes the agent runner, post-processes output (SILENT/MEDIA handling), and performs delivery via gateway or send helper.

Persistent job model and scheduler data: parsing schedule expressions, computing next_run times, persisting jobs.json with secure permissions, and CRUD operations that determine which jobs the scheduler will see.
Public API surface for cron: exposes create/list/trigger/tick entrypoints that other subsystems (gateway, CLI, runloop) call to schedule and run jobs.

construct_relationships: scheduler.run_job uses jobs.load_jobs and compute_next_run to identify due jobs and update next_run_at around execution.
scheduler._run_job_script confines execution to HERMES_HOME/scripts, enforcing a timeout and returning script output that becomes part of the job prompt.
scheduler._build_job_prompt composes model prompts including referenced skills and delivery guidance; these prompts are passed to the core agent runner (run_job) for execution.
scheduler._deliver_result either routes through a live gateway adapter (if connected) or a standalone send helper; delivery logic must honor SILENT suppression and extract MEDIA attachments.
jobs.create_job/load_jobs/save_jobs are the authoritative persistence layer; scheduler relies on their file format, permissions, and auto-repair behavior when reading/writing jobs.json.
notable_relationships: Upstream: scheduler depends on the core agent runtime (agent runner/prompt builder/skill loader) to execute jobs and produce responses; prompt composition must remain compatible with agent prompt APIs.
Downstream: delivery uses gateway adapters (gateway/platforms/*) or send helpers; any change to delivery semantics affects messaging adapters and platform-specific formatting.

Environment: scheduler executes on-disk scripts from HERMES_HOME/scripts and persists jobs under HERMES_HOME; changes must respect HERMES_HOME configuration, file permissions, and host filesystem semantics.
Optional dependency: jobs parsing integrates with croniter when available; behavior must remain acceptable when croniter is absent.
Operational: file-based locking prevents concurrent ticks; modifications must preserve locking semantics to avoid race conditions or double-execution.
recommendations: When editing scheduler or jobs code, preserve the public API exported by cron/__init__.py (create_job, list_jobs, trigger_job, tick) to avoid breaking callers.
Respect HERMES_HOME boundaries: scripts must only execute from HERMES_HOME/scripts with enforced timeouts; do not widen script execution scope or remove confinement checks.
Maintain file-based locking around tick/run execution; ensure concurrent ticks cannot run and that lock acquisition/release remains robust across crashes.

Jobs persistence must remain durable and atomic: retain secure file permissions, auto-repair behavior for malformed JSON, and atomic updates to jobs.json (avoid windows where next_run_at is not persisted before execution).
Delivery logic must continue to (a) suppress delivery if a SILENT marker appears anywhere in an agent response, and (b) extract MEDIA: tags to attach files rather than sending literal tags.
Prompt composition must continue to include explicit delivery guidance so the model avoids using send_message tools during cron runs; preserve how skills are referenced/loaded in prompts.
Any change affecting schedule computation should preserve existing grace-window semantics and recurring job next_run_at advancement before execution to prevent duplicate firings on restart.

Run the unit test suite and any cron-related integration tests (gateway delivery paths, persistence) after changes; validate behavior with and without optional croniter present and with empty API-key CI-like environments if delivery uses external providers.

nix.description: This folder contains Nix expressions and helper scripts that define how Hermes is packaged, how developer environments are provisioned, and how the agent is deployed to NixOS or containers. It provides build-time validation, a reproducible dev-shell/virtualenv workflow, Python virtualenv derivation via uv2nix, and a NixOS module that emits runtime units and configuration files — all of which are critical for reproducible builds, deployment, and developer onboarding.
key_behaviors: Produce reproducible Nix package derivations for the Hermes Python project including bundled skills and runtime dependencies.
Generate a developer shell and stamp-based virtualenv so local development matches the Nix-built runtime environment.
Construct an uv2nix-based Python workspace/overlay that other Nix expressions (packages, devShell) consume.
Validate package and module configuration at build time via checks to catch cross-system or key drift.
Emit a NixOS module (container/native) that generates systemd unit(s) and configuration files and can be merged with runtime YAML via a deep-merge script.

insights.title: Package version drift
insights.problem: The Nix package version was previously hardcoded and drifted from pyproject.toml causing mismatched builds and releases.
insights.root_cause: Manual duplication of the package version between pyproject.toml and nix packaging expressions instead of single-source-of-truth.
insights.solution: Ensure packaging reads the authoritative project metadata (pyproject.toml) at build time or otherwise keep a single automated mapping; treat any change to pyproject version as requiring validation of nix outputs and downstream consumers.

insights.title: Stamp-based devShell/venv coordination
insights.problem: Dev shell relies on stamp files to coordinate Nix and virtualenv lifecycle; incorrect stamp handling can lead to stale environments or inconsistent dev/runtime dependencies.
insights.root_cause: Dual-world of Nix derivations and a Python virtualenv with synchronization markers (stamps) introduces a fragile lifecycle boundary.
insights.solution: Respect stamp semantics when updating devShell or python environment generation; treat stamps as canonical indicators of environment freshness and validate after changes that affect workspace/overlay or runtimeDeps.

insights.title: Merging Nix settings into runtime YAML
insights.problem: Deploy-time configuration requires deep merging between Nix-provided settings and the agent's YAML config, which can result in unexpected overrides if merge semantics are ambiguous.
insights.root_cause: Complex nested configuration structures require a deterministic deep-merge; ad-hoc or shallow merges can drop keys or silently replace structures.
insights.solution: Preserve the deep_merge semantics implemented in the script — callers must expect recursive merging of maps and consistent handling of lists/primitive values; validate the merged output before deployment to avoid runtime misconfiguration.

insights.title: perSystem and multi-platform checks
insights.problem: Package checks and module expectations vary across target systems; failures on one system can be masked if perSystem logic isn't exercised.
insights.root_cause: Nix expressions use perSystem conditionals (e.g., different dependencies or derivations per architecture) and the checks rely on these variations.
insights.solution: Treat perSystem branches in checks.nix as first-class: when editing Nix expressions, run checks across the intended target systems and ensure any configKeys or runtimeDeps changes are reflected in perSystem logic.

key_files.why: Primary package derivation that bundles runtimeDeps and packaged skills; it currently bridges pyproject metadata into Nix and affects produced artifacts used by deployment and Docker.
Provides uv2nix-based Python workspace/overlay and the python virtualenv set used by packages and devShells; changes propagate to all Python-related Nix outputs.
Defines the developer shell with stamp-based coordination for Nix and venv; used by contributors to reproduce development environments.
NixOS module that generates systemd units and configuration files for deployment in container or native modes; it is the integration point for runtime wiring.
Script implementing deep-merge semantics used at deployment time to combine Nix-provided settings with the agent's YAML configuration; critical for correct runtime config.

construct_relationships: packages.nix reads project metadata (version) originating from pyproject.toml and uses python.nix workspace/overlay and runtimeDeps to build the Hermes package derivation.
devShell.nix depends on python.nix to construct the Python environment and uses stamp files to synchronize the venv lifecycle with Nix derivations produced by packages.nix.
nixosModules.nix emits systemd unit definitions and a configFile which are intended to be merged with runtime YAML via configMergeScript.nix at deployment.
checks.nix validates configKeys and perSystem expectations against the outputs/inputs of packages.nix and nixosModules.nix to catch build-time mismatches.
notable_relationships: pyproject.toml is an authoritative upstream for package metadata used by nix/packages.nix; any change to pyproject (version, extras, dependencies) affects Nix derivations.

Dockerfile and external deployment tooling expect artifacts produced by nix/packages.nix and the systemd units from nix/nixosModules.nix — packaging changes can affect container images and runtime behavior.

uv2nix/python.nix must remain compatible with optional Python extras declared in the project (tools like Playwright or optional native bindings); changes to extras may require re-evaluation of overlays and runtimeDeps.

HERMES runtime configuration, persisted data directories, and systemd unit command lines are downstream consumers of the merged YAML produced by configMergeScript.nix; mismatches can cause runtime failures.
recommendations: When editing any nix expression, run the Nix checks (nix-build or checks.nix) and exercise perSystem variants to detect platform-specific failures.
If package metadata (version, dependencies, extras) in pyproject.toml is changed, update or validate nix/packages.nix (which reads project metadata) and re-run builds to avoid drift.
Preserve stamp semantics in devShell.nix: modifications that affect python.nix or runtimeDeps must update stamps or ensure venv recreation to avoid stale environments.
Do not alter deep_merge semantics without validating merged YAML output against expected runtime config; deployment consumers (systemd units, gateway) assume specific merged shapes.

When changing the NixOS module outputs (systemd unit or configFile format), verify downstream consumers (Docker/systemd, gateway startup scripts, runtime entrypoints) remain compatible and test a full deployment cycle.

environments.description: The environments package contains Atropos/Atropos-style RL environment integrations, multi-turn agent loop runtime logic, concrete evaluation environments (Terminal-Bench, TBLite, YC-Bench, SWE-bench), OPD/on-policy distillation plumbing, and a family of tool-call parsers. It provides the orchestration layer that runs Hermes agent rollouts, resolves toolsets and server modes, parses model tool-call markup into structured calls, and emits rollout/trajectory artifacts for training, evaluation, and logging (wandb/JSONL).
key_behaviors: Drive multi-turn agent rollouts that alternate model chat completions and tool executions, accumulate messages/reasoning, and return structured AgentResult/trajectories.
Resolve per-group toolsets and server modes (direct chat vs managed server/vllm), then run rollouts across dataset-defined tasks and persist JSONL/wandb-formatted trajectories and scores.
Parse varied model output formats into normalized tool_call objects via a registry of parsers (multiple regex/JSON strategies), tolerating truncated or mixed-content generations.
Run evaluation-specific sandboxed executions (Modal, Docker, terminal tools) to verify task results and compute rewards, including reading external artifacts (e.g., SQLite DBs) for scoring.
Perform OPD-specific flows: extract hindsight hints using a judge LLM, compute teacher logprobs, and emit distillation-ready ScoredDataGroup outputs.

insights.title: Atropos dependency is optional and imports are conditional
insights.problem: Parts of the package (Atropos integrations) must be importable only when the optional atroposlib is available; unconditional imports would break environments for users without that dependency.
insights.root_cause: Top-level initializer exposes RL components only when atroposlib can be imported; other modules in the package should remain importable without Atropos present.
insights.solution: Maintain conditional import patterns and lightweight fallbacks/no-ops (e.g., apply_patches) so the package remains safe to import in environments without Atropos. Any change must preserve graceful degradation and clear import-time behavior.

insights.title: Tool execution can be blocking and requires a shared thread pool
insights.problem: Tool callbacks may perform blocking I/O or compute; running them on the event loop without isolation can block rollouts and break async schedulers.
insights.root_cause: Tool implementations are heterogeneous (blocking subprocesses, Docker/Modal calls) and agent_loop maintains a thread pool resized at runtime to handle this.
insights.solution: Respect and preserve thread-pool lifecycle and resize semantics; ensure any modifications keep offloading blocking tool calls to the pool and maintain error capture/propagation behavior (ToolError capture).

insights.title: Tool-call parser registration depends on module imports
insights.problem: The registry populates available parsers by importing concrete parser modules; failing to import these modules or changing register semantics will leave PARSER_REGISTRY incomplete.
insights.root_cause: register_parser decorator and module-side imports are used to populate a global registry at import time.
insights.solution: Preserve register_parser semantics and module import-time side effects; any refactor that moves or lazily-loads parsers must ensure the registry remains discoverable to consumers synchronously or provide an explicit loader API.

insights.title: Parsers must tolerate truncation, mixed content and provider-specific markers
insights.problem: Model output often contains truncated/unclosed blocks, embedded JSON, or provider tokens; parsers implement bespoke regex and JSON-decoding fallbacks to remain robust.
insights.root_cause: Different model providers and model versions emit tool-call markup in many formats (tagged XML-like blocks, JSON blobs, concatenated name{args}, or section tokens).
insights.solution: When editing parsers or their patterns, maintain tolerant behavior (accept truncated tags, preserve pre-tool-call content, fallback-safe deserialization) and ensure unique id-generation behavior remains stable for downstream trace linking.

insights.title: Trajectories and ScoredDataGroup outputs have consumers expecting specific fields/formats
insights.problem: Downstream training/evaluation tooling (OPD distillation, wandb dashboards, replay buffers) depend on fields like distill_token_ids/distill_logprobs, teacher logprobs, or wandb-friendly trajectory shapes.
insights.root_cause: The environments format trajectories and scored items in a particular schema consumed by RL training or logging systems.
insights.solution: Preserve field names, nested structure, and serialization formats for trajectories and ScoredDataGroup outputs; any schema change must be coordinated with consumers or include migration compatibility.

insights.title: Bench environments rely on external runtimes and artifacts (Modal, Docker, SQLite, yc-bench)
insights.problem: Evaluation code executes sandboxed tasks, test suites, or CLI tools that require external binaries, Docker images, or access to generated DBs; missing runtimes will cause runtime failures during rollouts.
insights.root_cause: SWE/TB2/YC-Bench flows run tasks in Modal sandboxes or Docker images and read artifacts (e.g., SQLite DBs) to compute scores.
insights.solution: Keep runtime checks and graceful error messages where availability of Modal/Docker/yc-bench/DB files is assumed; ensure code paths that read external artifacts validate presence and handle errors without crashing the whole orchestration.

key_files.why: Core multi-turn runtime: drives model/chat completions, inspects/dispatches tool calls, accumulates reasoning, and produces AgentResult objects; central to all rollout flows.
Abstract orchestration for Hermes agent rollouts: resolves toolsets, switches server modes, formats trajectories for wandb, and is the shared base for concrete eval environments.
Registry and loader for tool-call parsers; module import-time behavior populates PARSER_REGISTRY which consumers use to select parsers by name.
OPD/on-policy distillation environment: extracts hindsight hints, computes teacher logprobs, and emits distillation-ready ScoredDataGroup outputs consumed by training pipelines.

Concrete SWE benchmark environment that interacts with Modal sandboxes, runs dataset-provided tests inside the sandbox, and computes test-based rewards—touches external sandbox runtime and test execution plumbing.

construct_relationships: HermesAgentLoop (agent_loop.py) orchestrates the chat/tool-call cycle and returns AgentResult objects consumed by HermesAgentBaseEnv to build trajectories and compute rewards.
HermesAgentBaseEnv (hermes_base_env.py) resolves per-group toolsets and server mode, then invokes HermesAgentLoop for each rollout group; it also formats trajectories for wandb and storage.

ToolCallParser registry (tool_call_parsers/__init__.py) is populated by importing concrete parser modules (deepseek_v3*, glm*, llama, mistral, hermes, longcat, kimi_k2); callers ask get_parser(name) to obtain a parser used to extract tool_calls from raw model text.

SWE/Terminal/YC/ TBLite eval environments subclass or use HermesAgentBaseEnv and call into HermesAgentLoop; they add dataset loading, sandbox/test orchestration, and domain-specific reward computation.

AgenticOPDEnv uses rollouts and next-state signals (via base env/agent loop), calls a judge LLM to extract hindsight hints, computes teacher logprobs, and packages ScoredDataGroup for distillation pipelines.
notable_relationships: Top-level environments package applies (now no-op) patches.apply_patches at import time to preserve backward compatibility with async tool behavior; callers assume safe import side effects.

Bench environments depend on external runtimes and artifacts: Modal sandboxes, Docker images, yc-bench CLI, and SQLite DBs; failures or absence affect evaluation runs but should not break package imports.
Wandb/JSONL logging and ScoredDataGroup outputs are consumed downstream by training/evaluation pipelines; schema expectations cross folder boundaries (training, logging, RL code).

Parser implementations target provider-specific output formats (GLM, Llama, Mistral, DeepSeek, Kimi, Hermes, Longcat) so changes can affect multi-provider compatibility across agent adapters in agent/ and gateway/ subsystems.

Agent loop's tool execution semantics (thread pool, ToolError capture) interact with various tools under tools/* and with sandbox execution in gateway/platforms or environments/*; changes may impact blocking behavior and error propagation.
recommendations: Preserve conditional import behavior in environments/__init__.py so the package remains importable when atroposlib is absent; do not assume Atropos presence unless guarded.
When modifying agent_loop, retain thread-pool offloading, ToolError capture semantics, and AgentResult shape; verify blocking tool calls stay isolated from the async event loop.

If touching the parser registry or any parser, ensure the register_parser decorator/import-time registration remains intact and that parsers tolerate truncated/unclosed blocks and mixed content; keep id-generation stable for traceability.

Any change to trajectory, scoring, or ScoredDataGroup schemas must account for downstream consumers (OPD distillation, wandb, training) — maintain field names and serialization shapes or coordinate migrations.

For eval environments that invoke external runtimes (Modal, Docker, yc-bench) or read files/DBs, preserve precondition checks and graceful error handling; run relevant integration/e2e evaluation flows when modifying orchestration or sandboxing code.

Run unit tests and relevant evaluation/integration scenarios after edits: parser unit tests, agent loop tool-invocation tests, OPD distillation unit tests, and end-to-end sandboxed evaluations (SWE/TB2/YC) where changes affect orchestration.

website.description: This folder contains the Docusaurus-based documentation site and the code that produces a searchable Skills Hub for Hermes Agent. It includes site configuration and build metadata (docusaurus.config.ts, package.json, sidebars), a script that extracts SKILL.md metadata into a site-consumable skills.json, and a React page that renders that skills index. The docs are treated as the canonical, human-facing contract for APIs, file formats, environment variables, and extension points used across the codebase, so site artifacts are tightly coupled to runtime behavior.
key_behaviors: Static site generation: configuration, plugin enablement (search, mermaid), and build scripts determine site output.
Documentation-as-contract: markdown docs codify APIs, env vars, and on-disk formats that contributors and operators rely on.
Skill metadata extraction and publication: extract-skills.py consolidates SKILL.md and cached external indexes into a skills.json artifact consumed by the Skills Hub UI.
Client-side skill browsing: the Skills Hub React page loads the consolidated skill index, provides search/filter/highlight/pagination, and surfaces install hints.
Navigation control: sidebars.ts and category metadata determine navigation structure and doc visibility in the generated site.

insights.title: Documentation must mirror runtime contracts
insights.problem: Docs in this folder are read and relied upon by contributors and external integrators; if they diverge from code (APIs, SKILL.md schema, env vars), users will be misled.
insights.root_cause: Docs are authored independently of code changes and there is no automatic enforcement ensuring documentation reflects code/API/format changes.
insights.solution: When editing code that changes APIs, on-disk formats, env vars, or SKILL.md conventions, update the corresponding docs and any site-generated artifacts (e.g., skills.json). Run a site build and link checks to validate published docs remain consistent.

insights.title: skills.json generation is a single point of truth for the Skills Hub
insights.problem: The Skills Hub page depends on a consolidated skills.json produced by scripts/extract-skills.py; stale or incompatible skill index files will break search, filtering, or installation hints in the UI.
insights.root_cause: extract-skills.py normalizes and merges local SKILL.md and cached external indexes; changes to SKILL.md schema or cache format are not auto-propagated without running the extractor.
insights.solution: Any change to SKILL.md format, skill directory layout, or external index caches requires running and verifying extract-skills.py to regenerate the site artifact consumed by the Skills Hub and committing the updated JSON to the site build inputs.

insights.title: Docusaurus config, plugins and Node tooling must remain aligned
insights.problem: site behavior (search, mermaid, theme options) and local dev/build scripts depend on package.json and docusaurus.config.ts; mismatches between config and installed plugins or Node engine/version can break local builds or CI site deploys.
insights.root_cause: docusaurus.config.ts references plugins and theme settings that require matching dependencies declared in package.json and a compatible Node environment.
insights.solution: When modifying site config or adding/removing plugins, update package.json dependencies and ensure the Node engine/version constraints are respected; validate by running the Docusaurus dev/build commands used by the project.

insights.title: Sidebar and category metadata drive site navigation and visibility
insights.problem: sidebars.ts and per-category _category_.json files determine ordering and grouping; adding, renaming, or deleting doc files without updating navigation metadata can lead to broken links or missing pages in the generated site.
insights.root_cause: Navigation is defined separately from doc content; file moves or renames are not automatically reflected in sidebars.
insights.solution: When editing docs, ensure sidebars.ts and category metadata are updated and run a site build/link checker to detect navigation regressions.

key_files.why: Central site configuration controlling themes, plugins (search/mermaid), and build-time behavior that affects all generated docs and pages.
Defines Node engine constraints, build/dev scripts, and dependencies required to run and build the Docusaurus site; changes here affect local/CI builds.
Produces the consolidated skills.json consumed by the Skills Hub; acts as the bridge between SKILL.md files/external caches and the website.
Client-side Skills Hub implementation that consumes the generated skill index and provides search/filter/install UI—breaking changes in the index shape will impact this page.
Drives documentation navigation and grouping visible in the site UI; edits to docs require corresponding sidebar adjustments to maintain navigation integrity.

construct_relationships: extract-skills.py reads SKILL.md files and cached external index JSON and writes a consolidated skills.json artifact that the Skills Hub React page consumes for rendering and client-side filtering.
docusaurus.config.ts references plugins and theme settings that depend on the dependencies declared in package.json for successful local/CI builds.
sidebars.ts and per-directory _category_.json files determine how markdown files under website/docs are organized, displayed, and ordered by the Docusaurus build pipeline.
website/src/pages/skills/index.tsx expects a specific skill metadata schema (normalized by extract-skills.py); changes in the schema require synchronized edits to the extractor or the UI component.
notable_relationships: Documentation files in website/docs encode runtime contracts (APIs, SKILL.md schema, environment variables) that must remain consistent with code in the agent, gateway, tools, and scripts elsewhere in the repo.

The skills extraction script operates over repository SKILL.md files and external cache JSONs (outside this folder); changes to skill storage locations or SKILL.md fields in the codebase impact site generation.

CI and contributor workflows rely on Node and Docusaurus build commands (package.json scripts); altering site dependencies or Node engine constraints may require CI updates and will affect developer reproduction steps.
Site search, mermaid and other plugin-dependent features in docusaurus.config.ts create an implicit dependency on plugin versions and capabilities declared in package.json.
recommendations: When editing docs, update corresponding navigation (sidebars.ts, _category_.json) and run a local Docusaurus build (npm/yarn install + site build) and link checks to detect regressions.

If changing SKILL.md schema, skill directory layout, or external index formats, run and validate website/scripts/extract-skills.py to regenerate the skills.json consumed by the Skills Hub; confirm the UI still renders and filters correctly.

When modifying docusaurus.config.ts or adding plugins, ensure package.json dependencies are updated and that the Node engine/version constraint is compatible with CI/dev environments before committing.
Validate changes by running the site dev server and a production build to surface TypeScript/React/runtime errors in website/src/pages and confirm search/mermaid functionality behaves as expected.

Treat the website docs as a canonical contract: any code changes affecting public APIs, env vars, or on-disk formats should be accompanied by coordinated documentation updates and regeneration of any derived site artifacts.

docs.description: This docs/ folder holds operational and design documentation used to integrate, theme, migrate to, and architect subsystems of Hermes. It contains setup guides for external integrations (ACP, process managers), migration advice from a predecessor project, a design proposal affecting runtime cost/accounting, and an example runtime UI skin. These artifacts are authoritative references for runtime behavior, integration points, and configuration formats that other code and deployment processes rely on.
key_behaviors: Describe and prescribe integration steps and expectations for external adapters and runtimes (ACP editor integration, honcho/process-manager usage).
Document migration mappings and compatibility notes for users coming from OpenClaw, clarifying how concepts map to Hermes subsystems.
Capture architecture-level design decisions that impact runtime accounting and provider routing (pricing accuracy design), potentially informing code in agent/model_metadata and credential routing.
Provide concrete configuration examples consumed by runtime components (example skin YAML used by the CLI theming system).

insights.title: ACP setup must reflect adapter entrypoints and options
insights.problem: ACP integration guide can become inconsistent with the acp_adapter code if entrypoints, flags, or protocols change.
insights.root_cause: Docs are authored separately and reference runtime entrypoints and options that evolve in code.
insights.solution: When relying on ACP docs, treat them as a contract: verify referenced entrypoints, CLI flags, and protocol details against acp_adapter.{entry,server} and CI/startup scripts to avoid divergence.

insights.title: Process manager comparison depends on actual run_agent behaviour
insights.problem: Honcho integration spec compares Hermes to another project's runtime behavior; if run semantics or start scripts change, the spec becomes misleading.
insights.root_cause: Spec presumes stable runtime entrypoints and service semantics that may change across releases.
insights.solution: Keep process-manager specs tied to concrete start commands and service behavior; cross-check the documented commands and env assumptions with run_agent entrypoints and packaged CLI scripts before relying on the spec.

insights.title: Migration doc links user expectations to internal abstractions
insights.problem: OpenClaw-to-Hermes migration guidance can mislead if internal abstractions (skills, gateway sessions, persistence locations) differ from what's described.
insights.root_cause: Mapping between projects is conceptual and risks drifting from implementation details over time.
