insights.solution: Treat migration docs as mappings that must be validated against current subsystem names, config keys, and HERMES_HOME persistence locations; verify any API or config keys referenced are still present in the codebase.

insights.title: Pricing-accuracy design impacts model metadata and routing
insights.problem: Design assumptions about pricing and accuracy influence model selection, billing calculations, and credential rotation; inconsistency between doc and code may produce incorrect cost accounting.
insights.root_cause: Design docs and runtime code (model_metadata, credential_pool, smart_model_routing) are edited independently.
insights.solution: Assume the architecture doc is a specification: any doc edits or references should be reconciled with agent/model_metadata, credential pools, and any telemetry/metrics collection code to ensure accounting and routing behavior remain consistent.

insights.title: Example skin YAML must match CLI skin loader schema
insights.problem: The example skin can be syntactically or semantically invalid relative to the CLI's theme loader, causing runtime errors when users copy it.
insights.root_cause: Example YAML is maintained separately from the schema code that consumes it (hermes_cli skin loader).
insights.solution: When using or editing the example YAML, validate it against the runtime schema and loader behavior (keys like name, colors, spinner, branding, tool_prefix) and ensure its field names and value formats remain compatible.

key_files.why: Authoritative setup and configuration instructions for the editor ACP integration; references runtime adapter entrypoints and protocol expectations.
Operational spec comparing Hermes to an alternative process manager; documents expected start/monitor semantics and how Hermes should behave under a process manager.
Migration guide mapping OpenClaw concepts to Hermes subsystems; used by users and maintainers to understand compatibility and feature mappings.
Design proposal that drives model selection, pricing, and telemetry concerns; affects agent/model_metadata, credential rotation, and billing logic.
Concrete example of the CLI theming configuration consumed by hermes_cli; serves as a template users copy into runtime config.

construct_relationships: The ACP setup doc describes how editor integrations should invoke the acp_adapter entry and the server protocol; changes in acp_adapter endpoints, flags, or message shapes must be reflected here.
The Honcho integration spec describes expected service lifecycle semantics which correspond to run_agent.startup and packaged CLI entrypoints used by system/service managers.

The OpenClaw migration doc establishes conceptual mappings: OpenClaw 'skills' and persistence map to Hermes 'skills' subsystem, HERMES_HOME, and gateway/session models in the gateway/ and agent/ packages.
The pricing accuracy architecture doc informs assumptions used by agent/model_metadata and credential_pool for cost estimation, provider selection, and routing logic.

The example skin YAML provides keys (name, colors, spinner, branding, tool_prefix) consumed by the hermes_cli skin loader and theming subsystem; schema drift between YAML and loader will break theme loading.
notable_relationships: Docs reference runtime code in acp_adapter, run_agent, hermes_cli, gateway, and agent/* — edits to documentation must consider those code locations as authoritative for behavior.

The pricing/accuracy design has downstream implications for telemetry, billing, and routing modules (agent/model_metadata, credential_pool, smart_model_routing) which may require tests and metrics updates when the design changes.
Example configuration files in docs (skins YAML) are user-facing and thus tightly coupled to CLI configuration loading and default HERMES_HOME paths used by packaging/Dockerfiles.

Migration and integration docs are consumed by users and automation (install scripts, CI), so filenames and referenced command examples may be relied on externally (e.g., onboarding scripts, README snippets).
recommendations: When editing any document that describes runtime behavior (ACP setup, Honcho spec, migration mappings, pricing architecture), cross-check the referenced code entrypoints, CLI commands, and config keys in the corresponding modules (acp_adapter, run_agent, hermes_cli, agent/model_metadata, credential_pool).
Validate the example skin YAML against the runtime skin loader/schema before committing (YAML syntax and key names must match hermes_cli expectations).
Treat design docs as specifications: if they describe metrics, billing or routing behavior, ensure associated unit/integration tests and telemetry pipelines remain aligned with any doc changes.
Do not assume docs are authoritative for implementation; confirm any behavioral claims against current code and update code or tests in tandem when doc edits change expected behavior.

Check for external references: examples and command snippets in these docs may be copied into README, install scripts, or CI; maintain relative paths, command accuracy, and date/version metadata to avoid breaking external consumers.

plugins.description: This subtree contains pluggable long‑term memory provider implementations and a lightweight discovery/loader. Plugins supply prefetch/inject context for prompts, durable ingestion and background sync of conversation turns, per‑provider tool schemas (query/store/curate/etc.), and provider‑specific lifecycle (threads, async loops, daemons) that directly affect agent context, privacy scoping, and shutdown correctness.
key_behaviors: Dynamic discovery and lazy loading of MemoryProvider implementations (supports plugin-style register(ctx) and MemoryProvider subclass patterns, plus CLI command registration without full init).
Prefetch/inject memory context into prompts: providers fetch relevant long‑term memories synchronously or asynchronously before model calls.
Durable ingestion and background sync: write‑behind queues, background threads or async loops, and non‑blocking sync patterns to persist conversation turns and files.
Tool schema exposure: each provider registers tool schemas and RPC/handler functions for recall/retain/search/curate/status/profile operations consumed by the agent.
Retrieval & ranking pipelines: local providers use FTS + Jaccard + optional HRR vector similarity and trust/temporal weighting for results.

insights.title: Flexible plugin loading must support light CLI registration
insights.problem: CLI commands need to be exposed without fully loading heavy provider code or starting provider daemons.
insights.root_cause: Some plugins perform expensive startup (daemon threads, event loops, SDK initialization) that is unnecessary for CLI metadata registration.
insights.solution: Discovery code separates discovery from full instantiation and supports registering CLI commands via a lightweight check or register(ctx) hook to avoid side effects on CLI invocations.

insights.title: Provider default scoping can leak memories between gateway users
insights.problem: Some providers used global or hardcoded user identifiers (e.g., mem0/honcho) causing memory sharing across different gateway users.
insights.root_cause: Providers default to a static user_id or path (e.g., 'hermes-user' or ~/.hermes) instead of using gateway session user_id or profile‑scoped HERMES_HOME.
insights.solution: Ensure providers accept and prefer gateway/user/session identifiers and profile‑scoped hermes home when available; respect explicit per‑session scoping when assembling requests or file locations.

insights.title: Provider code assumptions about upstream SDK/REST signatures break on API changes
insights.problem: Mem0 and RetainDB callers assumed legacy call signatures and response formats leading to errors (wrong params, unexpected response shapes).
insights.root_cause: Upstream SDK/REST API changed parameter names/response envelope (e.g., filters=, dict returns) while provider adapters retained old assumptions.
insights.solution: Adapters should normalize or detect provider API versions and unwrap/translate calls and responses; keep error handling and compatibility layers around network client calls.

insights.title: Background sync threads/queues can drop messages or leak resources at shutdown
insights.problem: Pending sessions/queued writes could be lost when shutdown logic didn't join background threads or commit pending sessions; prefetch threads and write queues leaked connections and threads under load.
insights.root_cause: Insufficient shutdown coordination: not joining threads, not draining write queues, creating and closing sqlite connections per operation, and ignoring turn_count or last‑message timing.
insights.solution: Providers must join/drain background threads/queues on shutdown, use thread‑local or pooled DB connections, and ensure final commit/flush logic runs even on abrupt termination paths (or expose explicit shutdown hooks invoked by loader).

insights.title: SQLite access across threads/processes requires careful connection and lifecycle handling
insights.problem: Write‑behind queue and FTS-backed stores opened/closed connections per op and did not use thread‑local connections, leading to race conditions and resource exhaustion under load.
insights.root_cause: Naive sqlite3 connection usage from multiple threads without thread‑local handling and missing joins on worker threads.
insights.solution: Use thread‑local sqlite connections, ensure connections are not shared across threads, and join worker threads on shutdown; avoid repeated open/close in tight loops.

insights.title: HRR phase vectors must be deterministic and serializable across processes
insights.problem: Phase vector encoding needs to produce identical vectors across runs/processes and be storable/queriable by the SQLite store and retrieval components.
insights.root_cause: HRR encoding relies on deterministic atom encodings and serialization to bytes; retrieval and store expect stable serialization/deserialization.
insights.solution: Ensure encode/serialize routines are deterministic, use a stable random seed or deterministic hashing, and store canonical serialized representations in the DB for cross‑process retrieval and HRR similarity.

key_files.why: Central discovery and loader for memory provider plugins; governs lazy loading, CLI registration without full plugin init, and selection/instantiation of active provider.
Primary local persistence layer for structured facts and HRR vectors (SQLite/FTS); responsible for storage schema, entity resolution, and rebuilds used by holographic retrieval.
Implements hybrid retrieval pipeline (FTS candidates, Jaccard rerank, optional HRR scoring) and probes/contradiction detection used when injecting relevant facts into prompts.
Contains write‑behind durable ingestion queue, client interactions and file ingest logic; critical for crash‑safe background syncing and provider reliability.
Represents a cloud provider adapter that surfaced API compatibility and scoping issues; changes here affect multi‑user scoping and network call semantics.

construct_relationships: plugins/memory.__init__.py discovers available provider directories and loads/instantiates selected MemoryProvider implementations (ByteRover, Hindsight, Holographic, Honcho, Mem0, OpenViking, RetainDB, Supermemory).

HolographicMemoryProvider uses holographic.holographic for deterministic HRR vector encoding and holographic.store.MemoryStore for persistence; holographic.retrieval.FactRetriever queries the MemoryStore and applies HRR scoring.
HonchoMemoryProvider composes with honcho.client.get_honcho_client and honcho.session.HonchoSession/Manager for session caching, async flush, and writing to Honcho service.

RetainDBMemoryProvider uses a local _WriteQueue and _Client to perform crash‑safe write‑behind ingestion; write queue threads interact with sqlite write store and must be coordinated with provider shutdown.

ByteRoverMemoryProvider resolves and invokes an external brv binary and exposes tool handlers that invoke synchronous queries or background curations; resolution and synchronous calls interact with agent prefetch flows.
notable_relationships: Memory plugins feed prefetch/injected context into the core agent prompt builder and tool invocation system; changes in provider tool schemas or timing can alter model inputs and behavior.
Many plugins read/write configuration or transient state under HERMES_HOME; profile isolation depends on using get_hermes_home() consistently across providers to avoid cross‑profile leakage.
Gateway user_id/session identifiers must be threaded into providers for correct multi‑user scoping (affects Honcho, Mem0, etc.); failing to do so results in shared memories across gateway users.
External dependencies (SDKs, REST endpoints, CLI binaries) and their version changes are external failure modes; provider adapters are the boundary that must normalize varying upstream behaviors.

Shutdown and process exit ordering in the runtime (CLI, gateway, run_agent) must call provider shutdown hooks to avoid losing pending writes or leaking threads; provider lifecycle interacts with the agent's overall shutdown sequence.
recommendations: Preserve the loader’s semantics: continue supporting both plugin.register(ctx) and MemoryProvider subclass patterns and the ability to expose CLI commands without full provider init.
When editing providers, ensure prefetch paths don’t block the hot prompt path (distinguish sync prefetch vs background async), and retain non‑blocking write patterns for ingestion.
Respect HERMES_HOME and profile scoping: avoid hardcoded ~/.hermes or global user_id defaults; use gateway session user_id or per‑profile directories where applicable.
Coordinate background workers and DB access: use thread‑local sqlite connections, join/drain worker threads/async tasks on shutdown, and ensure final commit/flush logic runs even in error paths.
Be mindful of upstream API/SDK changes: normalize request/response shapes and add robust error handling around network/SDK calls (e.g., filter vs user_id, dict envelopes).
Retain deterministic HRR encoding and stable serialization for cross‑process storage and retrieval; tests relying on vector equality expect reproducibility.

When changing provider tool schemas or handlers, check the agent tool invocation flow and any callers that expect specific RPC signatures; run unit tests and, for provider integrations, run integration/e2e tests with appropriate API keys or mocks.

..description: This repository is the Hermes Agent: a Python 3.11+ distributed agent runtime that exposes a CLI, a messaging gateway, scheduled job runner (cron), plugin surface, and model/provider integrations. The code coordinates persistent user state under HERMES_HOME (auth.json, plugins, scripts, config), provider credentials and model metadata, prompt construction and caching, and platform-adapter orchestration so the same agent runtime can run interactively, as a gateway, or as a remote ACP-driven service.
key_behaviors: Bootstrap and environment wiring: apply profile overrides, load .env, ensure HERMES_HOME and defaults, and initialize logging before importing heavyweight subsystems.
Credential lifecycle and selection: seed credentials from environment/legacy stores, persist auth.json with locking, select/lease/rotate credentials at runtime for provider calls.

Model discovery and routing: fetch/cached model metadata (remote registries and local endpoints) and use that to size prompts, select providers, and gate UI model lists (Nous free-tier, Gemini updates).

Prompt composition and caching: stateless builders assemble system prompts (identity, platform hints, scanned project files, skills index) with truncation/sanitization and multi-layer caching to avoid heavy imports at cold start.

Gateway session and adapter orchestration: load gateway config, create platform adapters, construct deterministic session keys and dynamic session prompts (PII redaction), manage reconnection/backoff and pending command lifecycles.

Scheduled job execution: cron tick acquires a file lock, runs optional constrained scripts, composes job prompts including delivery guidance, executes jobs via core runner, and post-processes and delivers results to platforms.
Plugin discovery and lifecycle: discover plugins from multiple sources (user/project/entry-points), load them into an isolated namespace, and invoke hooks through a constrained PluginContext.

insights.title: Credential seeding and multi-endpoint Z.AI handling
insights.problem: Seeded credentials could fail on first call because Z.AI keys were seeded with an incorrect default base_url; multi-endpoint providers were not respected.
insights.root_cause: Seeding logic and some login code assumed a single hardcoded Z.AI endpoint and ignored environment overrides or alternate endpoints present in credential sources.
insights.solution: Seed and resolve credentials using the resolved base_url for the provider and respect HERMES_PORTAL_BASE_URL and other env overrides when probing/authenticating; ensure credential seeding preserves endpoint metadata so first calls target the correct server.

insights.title: Credential pool race/exhaustion due to missing leases
insights.problem: Concurrent child agents/threads could all pick and use the same credential leading to rate-limits and rapid exhaustion.
insights.root_cause: Credential pool lacked a soft leasing API and robust thread-safety guarantees for concurrent selection/marking of exhausted entries.
insights.solution: Credential selection must support soft leases and atomic marking of exhaustion with cooldowns; callers should respect soft-lease semantics to avoid concurrent usage of the same credential.

insights.title: models.dev disk cache corruption and atomic writes
insights.problem: Disk cache for models.dev could become corrupt if a process was killed mid-write and subsequent loads silently returned empty data.
insights.root_cause: Cache writes were not atomic (no write-to-temp-and-rename), so partial JSON could be left behind.
insights.solution: Write caches atomically (write temp file then rename) and detect/repair corrupted cache loads so transient failures don't permanently hide cached metadata.

insights.title: Outdated model default context sizes and provider prefixes
insights.problem: Default context length and provider-prefix mappings for some model families (e.g., Gemini/Gemma) were stale, causing prompt sizing and provider resolution errors.
insights.root_cause: Model capability defaults were hardcoded and not updated to reflect new model capabilities and provider naming conventions.
insights.solution: Model metadata layer must normalize provider prefixes, preserve vendor-specific tags (Ollama), and provide updated fallback context lengths while allowing override via fetched metadata.

insights.title: Expensive skills indexing and cold-start costs
insights.problem: Building the skills system prompt pulled heavy tool registries at prompt-time, causing slow cold starts.
insights.root_cause: Skills prompt generation imported heavy registries directly and lacked a multi-layer caching/snapshot mechanism.
insights.solution: Prompt builder should avoid heavy imports at call-time by relying on precomputed/cached snapshots and two-layer caching to keep runtime prompt assembly lightweight.

insights.title: Streamed reasoning vs response ordering inversion
insights.problem: When show_reasoning and streaming were enabled, response content could appear before the reasoning block finished rendering, creating visual inversion.
insights.root_cause: Streaming output was not deferred or synchronized with reasoning box completion, so chunks of final response could be interleaved with reasoning UI updates.
insights.solution: CLI streaming must coordinate reasoning-block lifecycle and ensure ordering guarantees (for display) when composing streamed events.

insights.title: Session key semantics changed for thread handling
insights.problem: Session key construction previously keyed threads per participant; it was changed to shared-by-default threading, affecting routing and thread isolation.
insights.root_cause: Thread handling semantics were updated but session-keying and downstream assumptions were not consistently updated across gateway and tools.
insights.solution: Session key generation, session reset policies, and any thread-specific behavior must be kept in sync and configuration-exposed (thread_sessions_per_user) so adapters and persistence layers remain consistent.

insights.title: Cron delivery semantics leaking MEDIA tags and send thrash
insights.problem: Raw MEDIA: tags and [SILENT] markers could leak into deliveries; cron-run agents attempted to call send_message even though scheduler already handled delivery.
insights.root_cause: Post-processing of agent responses for delivery was incomplete: MEDIA tags were not extracted/attached and silent markers were only checked at string start. The agent-run job prompt did not include clear delivery guidance.
insights.solution: Scheduler must strip/detect MEDIA tags and [SILENT] anywhere in response, inject delivery guidance into job prompts to prevent redundant send_message calls, and ensure attachments are delivered rather than left as tags.

insights.title: Plugin manifest and hook invocation inconsistencies
insights.problem: Plugin manifests supported richer env-var declarations than the manifest type hinted, and plugin hooks could not return data to callers because invoke_hook discarded non-None results.
insights.root_cause: Manifest typing was too narrow and hook invocation API did not collect return values; request-scoped hooks previously exposed large payloads and used conflicting names.
insights.solution: Plugin manifest parsing must tolerate rich formats for required env vars, and invoke_hook should collect non-None returns. Hook payloads should be narrowed and request-scoped interfaces made explicit to avoid accidental coupling.

key_files.why: Central implementation of credential seeding, selection, rotation, exhaustion marking and soft-leasing — changes here affect all provider calls and auth persistence (auth.json).
Canonical source for model capability metadata and endpoint probing; influences prompt sizing, model routing and pre-flight decisions.
Top-level CLI bootstrap, profile/application environment wiring and early initialization; edits influence HERMES_HOME selection, logging, and pre-import state for the whole process.
Gateway lifecycle and adapter orchestration; responsible for SSL cert handling, adapter startup/reconnect and pending-command lifecycle that affect message ordering and safety.
Scheduled-job execution and delivery post-processing; coordinates running scripts from HERMES_HOME/scripts, prompt-building for jobs, and final delivery to platforms.

construct_relationships: hermes_cli/auth.py and agent/credential_pool.py interact: auth resolves provider-specific credentials and seeds/updates the persistent credential pool in auth.json which the pool persists and rotates at runtime.

agent/model_metadata.py is consulted by hermes_cli/model_switch.py and hermes_cli/models.py for model alias resolution, pricing/tier gating, and for determining context-length defaults used by prompt sizing.

agent/prompt_builder.py composes system prompts that include skills indexes produced by the plugin subsystem (hermes_cli/plugins.py) and must avoid importing heavy tool registries to prevent cold-start latency.

gateway/session.py builds deterministic session keys and dynamic session context prompts which gateway/run.py uses to route messages into sessions and to decide per-session PII redaction and reset behavior.

cron/scheduler.py uses agent prompt-building utilities and the core agent runner to execute jobs, then interacts with gateway/run.py or send helpers to deliver results, requiring coordination about delivery semantics and attachment handling.
notable_relationships: Persistent state under HERMES_HOME (auth.json, plugins/, scripts/, config.yaml, .env) is authoritative across CLI, gateway, cron, and plugin subsystems; edits that change formats or keys must preserve backward compatibility or provide migration paths.

Networked metadata sources (models.dev, OpenRouter/OpenAI endpoints, Nous portal, Z.AI) are fetched and cached by agent/model_metadata.py and agent/models_dev.py; changes that cause extra fetches or format shifts can affect startup latency and gating logic (Nous free-tier).

Tests depend on environment isolation and temporary HERMES_HOME directories; modules that change file-locking behavior, atomic writes, or cache formats will break tests unless test fixtures are updated accordingly.

ACP adapter (acp_adapter/server.py) exposes internal session lifecycle externally; changes to in-process session semantics, prompt building, or tool-call event shapes must be considered for ACP clients that expect a stable wire behavior.

Plugin discovery/loading touches both hermes_cli/plugins.py and runtime hooks that inject context into prompts or register tools; plugin API surface and hook payload shapes are cross-cutting and must remain stable for third-party plugins.
recommendations: When editing code that touches persisted files under HERMES_HOME (auth.json, models.dev cache, plugins, config), preserve atomic write semantics and file-locking; run the test suite with temporary HERMES_HOME to validate migrations.

Respect thread-safety and soft-lease semantics for credential selection and marking exhausted; concurrent unit/integration scenarios must be exercised to avoid race conditions and leaking a single credential to many callers.
Avoid introducing blocking or heavy imports during early bootstrap (hermes_cli/main.py and prompt_builder); preserve two-layer caching or snapshotting behavior to prevent slow cold starts.

Any change affecting session-keying, thread/session semantics, or PII redaction must be validated against gateway adapters and session persistence; run gateway unit tests and simulate adapter message flows to ensure routing/metadata correctness.

Modifications that alter model metadata defaults, provider prefixes, or alias resolution must be validated against model selection flows (model_switch, models) and UI gating (Nous free-tier); update cached registries and ensure backward-compatible normalization.

Cron/job delivery changes must preserve existing delivery semantics: extract MEDIA tags, honor [SILENT] anywhere in output, and avoid duplicate send_message calls by agents. Run cron integration tests with sample jobs and attachments.

When changing plugin manifest parsing or hook invocation APIs, ensure manifest backward compatibility and collect non-None hook returns; run plugin discovery and sample plugin registration flows to confirm behavior.
Avoid making real external network/provider calls in unit tests; follow CI pattern of blanking provider env vars and use cached fixtures/mocks for models.dev, OpenRouter, Nous, and Z.AI interactions.
